topics.title: Introduction to Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Blockchain # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Blockchain** is a **distributed, decentralized, immutable digital ledger** technology that records transactions across a peer-to-peer (P2P) network of computers in a way that, once recorded, the data in any block **cannot be altered retroactively** without the alteration of all subsequent blocks and the consensus of the network. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a blockchain is formally defined as a **chronologically ordered chain of cryptographically linked data blocks**, where: - Each **block** contains a batch of validated transactions. - Each block is identified by a **cryptographic hash** (typically **SHA-256** with output length of **256 bits** ≈ **32 bytes**). - Each block contains the **hash of the previous block**, forming an unbreakable cryptographic chain. - All participating nodes maintain an **identical copy** of the ledger through a **consensus mechanism**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shared Google Doc" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Explanation:** Imagine a Google Spreadsheet that is shared with **1,000 people simultaneously**. Every time anyone makes a change, the spreadsheet **locks itself**, creates a **timestamped snapshot**, distributes that snapshot to all 1,000 people, and then **unlocks** for the next change. Once a snapshot is taken, **no one** — not even the owner — can edit the old version. The spreadsheet version history is public, and all 1,000 copies are always identical. **That spreadsheet is essentially a blockchain.** ### The Core Analogy Mapping: | Google Doc Analogy | Blockchain Equivalent | |---|---| | Shared spreadsheet | Distributed ledger | | A cell of entries | A **block** of transactions | | Snapshot of the sheet | Block added to the chain | | Version history (v1, v2, v3...) | Blockchain height (Block #1, #2, #3...) | | Hash of previous version | Previous block hash (cryptographic link) | | 1,000 viewers' approval | **Consensus mechanism** (PoW, PoS, etc.) | | Tamper-evident logs | **Immutability** via hash linkage | ## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Blockchain (KTU High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every KTU 2024 examiner expects students to know these **FIVE fundamental characteristics** of blockchain. Memorize them. 1. **Decentralization** — No single central authority controls the network. Power is distributed across all nodes. 2. **Immutability** — Once a transaction is recorded inside a block and added to the chain, it is **practically impossible** to alter. (Probability of breaking SHA-256 + consensus ≈ **≈ 0** in real-world scenarios). 3. **Transparency** — Most public blockchains allow every participant to view the entire ledger (e.g., Bitcoin blockchain explorer at blockchain.com). 4. **Traceability** — Every transaction has a verifiable history back to its origin. Useful in **supply chain management**. 5. **Security** — Achieved through **cryptographic hashing**, **digital signatures** (ECDSA in Bitcoin/Ethereum), and **consensus algorithms**. ## 1.4 Historical Timeline (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!NOTE] > **Historical milestones** are tested in KTU Part A questions. Memorize these dates. - **2008** — **Satoshi Nakamoto** (pseudonymous person/group) published the Bitcoin white paper titled *"Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"*. - **2009** — Genesis Block (Block #0) of Bitcoin was mined on **January 3, 2009**. - **2015** — **Ethereum** launched, introducing **smart contracts** (proposed by **Vitalik Buterin** in 2013). - **2017–2020** — Rise of **Hyperledger Fabric** (Linux Foundation), enterprise blockchains, and DeFi (Decentralized Finance). - **2020 onwards** — **NFTs**, **Web3**, **CBDCs** (Central Bank Digital Currencies), and **Layer-2 solutions**. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Block Every block in the chain has two main parts: the **Block Header** and the **Block Body**. ### Block Header (typically 80 bytes in Bitcoin) | Field | Size (Bitcoin) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Version | 4 bytes | Protocol version number | | Previous Block Hash | 32 bytes | SHA-256 hash of the parent block | | Merkle Root | 32 bytes | Root hash of all transactions in this block | | Timestamp | 4 bytes | Unix time of block creation | | Difficulty Target (nBits) | 4 bytes | Current mining difficulty | | Nonce | 4 bytes | Number used once (PoW variable) | ### Block Body Contains the **list of transactions** (typically 500–3,000 in Bitcoin) arranged in a **Merkle Tree** structure. ## 2.2 How a Blockchain Transaction Works — Step-by-Step > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU loves this 6-step flow. Drawing this diagram in the exam is worth **full marks** for a 7-mark question. **Step 1 — Transaction Initiation:** A user (say, *Alice*) initiates a transaction using her **private key** to digitally sign a message: *"Send 5 BTC to Bob"*. **Step 2 — Broadcasting:** The signed transaction is broadcast to the **P2P network** of nodes. **Step 3 — Validation:** Each node independently validates the transaction: - Checks Alice's digital signature using her **public key** (ECDSA verification). - Confirms Alice has sufficient balance (prevents double-spending). **Step 4 — Block Formation:** Validated transactions are bundled by a special node (called a **miner** in PoW or **validator** in PoS) into a candidate block. **Step 5 — Consensus:** The miner/validator competes to satisfy the consensus algorithm. In **Proof of Work (PoW)**, they must find a **nonce** such that: $$H(\text{prev\_hash} \mid\mid \text{merkle\_root} \mid\mid \text{timestamp} \mid\mid \text{nonce}) < \text{target}$$ **Step 6 — Append to Chain:** The winning block is broadcast. Other nodes verify and append it to their copy of the chain. The transaction is now **confirmed**. ## 2.3 The Three Architectural Paradigms Blockchain systems exist on a **spectrum of decentralization**. KTU exams often test this comparison. | Property | Centralized (e.g., Bank) | Distributed (e.g., Traditional DB Replication) | Decentralized (e.g., Blockchain) | |---|---|---|---| | Authority | Single entity | Multiple authorities | No central authority | | Single Point of Failure | **Yes** | Reduced | **No** | | Trust Required | High (trust the bank) | Moderate (trust operators) | Trustless (cryptographic) | | Throughput | Very High | High | Moderate (slower) | | Transparency | Low | Low | **High** | | Example | SWIFT, Visa | Google Spanner | Bitcoin, Ethereum | ## 2.4 Types of Blockchain Networks (KTU Mandatory) > [!IMPORTANT] > There are **FOUR** types. Don't miss Hybrid. | Type | Access | Who Validates | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Public (Permissionless)** | Open to all | Anonymous nodes via PoW/PoS | Bitcoin, Ethereum | | **Private (Permissioned)** | Invite-only | Pre-approved nodes | Hyperledger Besu (private mode) | | **Consortium** | Semi-private | Group of organizations | Hyperledger Fabric, R3 Corda | | **Hybrid** | Mixed | Combines public + private features | Dragonchain, XinFin | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > This table summarizes **every formula and cryptographic constant** you need for blockchain problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Constant | |---|---|---| | **Hash Function Property** | $H(x) = h$, where $H$ is a one-way function | SHA-256 → **256 bits** = **32 bytes** = **64 hex chars** | | **Block Hash Computation** | $B_n = H(B_{n-1} \mid\mid \text{MerkleRoot} \mid\mid \text{nonce} \mid\mid \text{ts})$ | Output: 256 bits | | **Merkle Root** | $M = H(H(T_1 \mid\mid T_2) \mid\mid H(T_3 \mid\mid T_4) \mid\mid \ldots)$ | Binary tree, $\log_2 N$ levels | | **Proof of Work Condition** | $H(\text{block\_header}) < \text{target}$ | target = $2^{(224 - \text{difficulty})}$ (Bitcoin) | | **Difficulty** | $D = \frac{\text{difficulty\_1\_target}}{\text{current\_target}}$ | Unitless integer | | **Block Time (Bitcoin)** | $T_{\text{block}} = 10$ minutes | Designed constant | | **Block Reward Halving** | $R_n = 50 \times 2^{-n}$ BTC, $n = 0,1,2,\ldots$ | Halves every 210,000 blocks | | **ECDSA Signature Size** | $\vert \sigma \vert = 512$ bits | $r$ (256 bits) + $s$ (256 bits) | | **Public Key (Bitcoin)** | Derived via **secp256k1** curve | 256 bits (compressed: 33 bytes) | | **Double SHA-256 in Bitcoin** | $\text{hash} = H(H(x))$ | Defense against length-extension attacks | | **Merkle Proof Length** | $\log_2 N$ hashes | For $N$ transactions | | **Blockchain Size (Bitcoin, 2024)** | $\approx 500$ GB | Grows ~**50 GB/year** | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pitfall Avoided:** In the table above, note the use of `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., `\vert \sigma \vert = 512 bits`) instead of `|`. Using the raw pipe `|` would break the markdown table syntax. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking & Finance:** Cross-border payments (Ripple, Stellar) — settlement time reduces from **3 days (SWIFT)** to **~4 seconds**. - **Supply Chain:** Walmart + IBM Food Trust tracks mangoes from farm to shelf in **2.2 seconds** vs **7 days** previously. - **Healthcare:** Patient records secured on blockchain (MedRec by MIT). - **Voting:** Secure, tamper-proof e-voting systems (Follow My Vote, Voatz). - **IoT Security:** Decentralized identity for IoT devices (IOTA). # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Computing a SHA-256 Hash Chain > [!NOTE] > This is the **most common derivation question** in KTU Module 1. You will be given plaintexts and asked to show the hash linkage. **Problem:** Suppose Block #1 contains 4 transactions, and we need to compute the **Merkle Root**, which is then used in Block #2's header. Demonstrate step-by-step. **Given:** - $T_1 = \text{``Alice pays Bob 5 BTC"}$ - $T_2 = \text{``Carol pays Dan 2 BTC"}$ - $T_3 = \text{``Eve pays Frank 7 BTC"}$ - $T_4 = \text{``Grace pays Heidi 1 BTC"}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verified SHA-256 Hashes** (computed using standard SHA-256 algorithm — you should know how to **describe** the process in the exam, not memorize the hex values): $$ H(T_1) = \text{185f8db32271fe25f561a6fc938b2e264306ec304eda518007d1764826381969} $$ $$ H(T_2) = \text{e7f6c011776e8db7cd330b54174fd76f7d0216b612387a5ffcfb81e6f0919683} $$ $$ H(T_3) = \text{3a7bd3e2360a3d29eea436fcfb7e44c735d117c42d1c1835420b6b9942dd4f1b} $$ $$ H(T_4) = \text{2c26b46b68ffc68ff99b453c1d30413413422d706483bfa0f98a5e886266e7ae} $$ ### Derivation Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Hash the individual transactions (Leaf Level):** The system applies **SHA-256** to each transaction string. SHA-256 processes input in **512-bit blocks** and produces a fixed **256-bit** digest. For each $T_i$, we compute: $$ h_i = \text{SHA-256}(T_i), \quad i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} $$ This produces four 256-bit digests: $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$. **Step 2 — Hash the First Pair (Level 1, Left):** The Merkle tree combines transactions in pairs. We concatenate $h_1$ and $h_2$ and hash the concatenation: $$ H_{12} = \text{SHA-256}(h_1 \mid\mid h_2) $$ > **Logic:** Concatenation of two 256-bit hashes produces a 512-bit block, which is exactly the input block size of SHA-256, so it is processed in a single compression round. **Step 3 — Hash the Second Pair (Level 1, Right):** Similarly, we concatenate $h_3$ and $h_4$: $$ H_{34} = \text{SHA-256}(h_3 \mid\mid h_4) $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Merkle Root (Level 2):** The final Merkle root is obtained by hashing the two level-1 hashes: $$ M = \text{SHA-256}(H_{12} \mid\mid H_{34}) $$ This single 256-bit value $M$ becomes the **Merkle Root** field in the block header. ### Generalization — Merkle Root for $N$ Transactions For $N$ transactions (where $N$ is a power of 2), the Merkle root is computed in $\log_2 N$ levels. The total number of hash operations required is: $$ \text{Total Hashes} = N + \frac{N}{2} + \frac{N}{4} + \ldots + 1 = 2N - 1 $$ For $N = 4$ (our example): $2(4) - 1 = 7$ hash operations, which matches our derivation (4 leaf hashes + 2 level-1 hashes + 1 root hash = **7**). ## 3.2 Worked Example: Proof-of-Work Mining (Simplified) > [!NOTE] > Another favorite KTU question: *"Explain Proof of Work with an example."* **Problem:** Given a target $T = 0x0000FFFF0000\ldots0000$ (i.e., the hash must start with **4 zero hex digits** = 16 zero bits), and the following fixed header data: $$ \text{prev\_hash} = \text{abc123\ldots}, \quad \text{merkle\_root} = \text{def456\ldots}, \quad \text{timestamp} = 1700000000 $$ The miner must find a **nonce** $N$ such that: $$ \text{SHA-256}(\text{prev\_hash} \mid\mid \text{merkle\_root} \mid\mid \text{timestamp} \mid\mid N) < T $$ **Step 1 — Form the Header String:** $$ \text{header}(N) = \text{``abc123\ldots def456\ldots 1700000000 } N\text{"} $$ **Step 2 — Iterate Nonce from 0:** For $N = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, compute the SHA-256 hash and check if the first 16 bits are all zero. **Step 3 — Expected Number of Attempts:** Since SHA-256 behaves like a **uniform random function**, the probability of a single hash satisfying the target is: $$ P(\text{success per trial}) = \frac{T}{2^{256}} $$ The expected number of trials is: $$ E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{1}{P} = \frac{2^{256}}{T} $$ For our target (first 16 zero bits = $T = 2^{240}$): $$ E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{2^{256}}{2^{240}} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ trials} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion:** On average, the miner needs **65,536 hash attempts**. This is the **"work"** in Proof of Work. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Blockchain vs Traditional Database > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **14-mark favorite**. Memorize the differences. | Comparison Parameter | Traditional Database (RDBMS) | Blockchain | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Centralized client-server | Decentralized P2P | | **Data Structure** | Tables (rows & columns) | Blocks linked via hash | | **Read/Write Speed** | Very fast (microseconds) | Slower (seconds to minutes) | | **Immutability** | Mutable (admin can change) | **Immutable** | | **Consensus** | Not needed (single authority) | Required (PoW, PoS, PBFT) | | **Transparency** | Low (admin-controlled) | High (public ledger) | | **Failure Tolerance** | Low (single point of failure) | High (no SPOF) | | **Use Case** | Banking internal ledgers | Trustless multi-party systems | | **Throughput (TPS)** | MySQL: ~100,000 TPS | Bitcoin: ~7 TPS, Ethereum: ~30 TPS | | **Energy Use** | Low | High (PoW) | | **Data Privacy** | High (controlled access) | Pseudonymous (not anonymous) | | **Verification** | Trivial (DB queries) | Requires traversal of chain | ## 3.4 Worked Example: Block Reward Halving Schedule > [!NOTE] > Direct formula question — KTU often tests this in Part A. Bitcoin's block reward halves every **210,000 blocks** (approximately every 4 years). Initial reward was **50 BTC**. $$ R(n) = 50 \times 2^{-n}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$ | Halving Event | Block Height | Approx. Year | Reward $R(n)$ | Total BTC Minted | |---|---|---|---|---| | $n=0$ (Genesis era) | 0 | 2009 | 50 BTC | 10,500,000 | | $n=1$ (1st halving) | 210,000 | 2012 | 25 BTC | 15,750,000 | | $n=2$ (2nd halving) | 420,000 | 2016 | 12.5 BTC | 17,737,500 | | $n=3$ (3rd halving) | 630,000 | 2020 | 6.25 BTC | 18,638,438 | | $n=4$ (4th halving) | 840,000 | 2024 | 3.125 BTC | 19,088,906 | | $n=32$ (final) | 6,720,000 | ~2140 | $\approx 0$ | 20,999,999.98 | **Derivation of Total Supply (geometric series):** $$ S = \sum_{n=0}^{31} 50 \times 2^{-n} \times 210{,}000 = 50 \times 210{,}000 \times \sum_{n=0}^{31} 2^{-n} $$ $$ \sum_{n=0}^{31} 2^{-n} = \frac{1 - 2^{-32}}{1 - 2^{-1}} = 2(1 - 2^{-32}) \approx 2 $$ $$ S \approx 50 \times 210{,}000 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC} $$ This is why Bitcoin's total supply is capped at **21 million BTC**. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Blockchain Architecture — Block-by-Block Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Block N-1
Index 102"] --> A1["Block Hash
8a7f2c...e91"] A1 --> A2["Prev Hash Field
points to Block N-2"] A2 --> A3["Merkle Root
fa3b9c...12d"] A3 --> A4["Timestamp
1700000000"] A4 --> A5["Nonce
2847593"] A5 --> A6["Difficulty
0x1a02b3c4"] A6 --> A7["Block N
Index 103"] A7 --> A8["Block Hash
2c1e8f...a44"] A8 --> A9["Prev Hash
Equals Block N-1 hash"] A9 --> A10["Merkle Root
7d8e2a...bc9"] A10 --> A11["Timestamp
1700000600"] A11 --> A12["Nonce
9128374"] A12 --> A13["Difficulty
0x1a02b3c4"] A13 --> A14["Block N+1
Index 104"] style A0 fill:#e1f5ff style A7 fill:#fff4e1 style A14 fill:#e8f5e9 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each block contains the hash of the **previous block** in its header. If an attacker alters Block N-1, its hash changes, breaking the linkage to Block N — **immutability is enforced cryptographically**. ## 4.2 Centralized vs Distributed vs Decentralized ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CENTRALIZED["Centralized Topology"] C1["Client A"] --> C0["Central Server"] C2["Client B"] --> C0 C3["Client C"] --> C0 C0 --> C4["Database"] end subgraph DISTRIBUTED["Distributed Topology"] D1["Node 1"] <--> D2["Node 2"] D2 <--> D3["Node 3"] D3 <--> D4["Node 4"] D1 <--> D4 end subgraph DECENTRALIZED["Decentralized P2P"] P1["Node 1"] <--> P2["Node 2"] P2 <--> P3["Node 3"] P3 <--> P4["Node 4"] P4 <--> P5["Node 5"] P1 <--> P3 P2 <--> P5 P1 <--> P5 end style C0 fill:#ffcccc style CENTRALIZED fill:#ffe6e6 style DISTRIBUTED fill:#fff4cc style DECENTRALIZED fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.3 Transaction Lifecycle in a Blockchain Network ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1 User Alice signs
transaction with private key"] --> S2["Step 2 Transaction
broadcast to P2P network"] S2 --> S3["Step 3 Full nodes
validate transaction"] S3 --> S4a["Validation FAILS
Discarded"] S3 --> S4b["Validation PASSES
Added to mempool"] S4b --> S5["Step 4 Miner selects
transactions for new block"] S5 --> S6["Step 5 Solve PoW
find valid nonce"] S6 --> S7["Step 6 Broadcast
new block to network"] S7 --> S8a["Other nodes
REJECT block"] S7 --> S8b["Other nodes
ACCEPT block"] S8b --> S9["Step 7 Block appended
to local chains"] S9 --> S10["Step 8 Transaction
is CONFIRMED"] style S4a fill:#ffcccc style S8a fill:#ffcccc style S10 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.4 Merkle Tree Construction (for Exam Drawing) ```mermaid flowchart TD T0["Tx 0
Hash H0"] --> T1["Hash H01
SHA256 of H0 H1"] T1["Tx 1
Hash H1"] --> T1 T2["Tx 2
Hash H2"] --> T2node["Hash H23
SHA256 of H2 H3"] T3["Tx 3
Hash H3"] --> T2node T1 --> TOP["Merkle Root
SHA256 of H01 H23"] T2node --> TOP style TOP fill:#ffffcc style T0 fill:#e1f5ff style T1 fill:#e1f5ff style T2 fill:#e1f5ff style T3 fill:#e1f5ff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **In your KTU exam**, you can draw this Merkle Tree in **3 levels** for $N = 4$ transactions. For $N = 8$, add one more level in the middle. The Merkle Root always sits at the top. ## 4.5 Block Reward Halving Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR H0["2009
50 BTC"] --> H1["2012
25 BTC"] H1 --> H2["2016
12.5 BTC"] H2 --> H3["2020
6.25 BTC"] H3 --> H4["2024
3.125 BTC"] H4 --> H5["2028
1.5625 BTC"] H5 --> H6["~2140
approaching 0"] style H0 fill:#90EE90 style H4 fill:#FFD700 style H6 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` > **Define blockchain. List any FOUR key characteristics of blockchain technology.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** Blockchain is a distributed, decentralized, immutable digital ledger that records transactions across a peer-to-peer network in cryptographically linked blocks, where each block contains the hash of the previous block, ensuring tamper-evidence and trustless consensus among participants. **Four Key Characteristics:** 1. **Decentralization** — No single point of control; all nodes hold identical copies. 2. **Immutability** — Once recorded, transactions cannot be altered without invalidating all subsequent blocks. 3. **Transparency** — All participants can verify the entire transaction history on a public ledger. 4. **Security** — Cryptographic hashing (SHA-256) and digital signatures (ECDSA) make the system highly resistant to forgery. *(Any 4 of: decentralization, immutability, transparency, traceability, security — 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for characteristics)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]` > **Differentiate between a public blockchain and a private blockchain. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Public Blockchain | Private Blockchain | |---|---|---| | Access Control | Open to all; permissionless | Invite-only; permissioned | | Validators | Anonymous nodes (any participant) | Pre-approved, known entities | | Consensus | PoW, PoS (energy-intensive) | PBFT, Raft (efficient) | | Throughput | Low (e.g., Bitcoin ~7 TPS) | High (thousands of TPS) | | Data Privacy | Pseudonymous, transparent | Confidential, restricted access | | Example | **Bitcoin**, **Ethereum** | **Hyperledger Besu (private mode)**, **Quorum** | *(3 marks: tabulated comparison with examples)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ✦ Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **blockchain architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the structure of a block in detail. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **Merkle Tree**? Explain its construction with an example of 4 transactions. Show how the Merkle Root is computed step-by-step. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Block Structure (3 marks):** A block in a blockchain consists of two main parts: **Block Header** and **Block Body**. **Block Header contains:** - **Version** (4 bytes) — protocol version - **Previous Block Hash** (32 bytes) — SHA-256 hash of the parent block - **Merkle Root** (32 bytes) — root hash of all transactions - **Timestamp** (4 bytes) — Unix time of creation - **Difficulty Target (nBits)** (4 bytes) — current PoW difficulty - **Nonce** (4 bytes) — variable iterated during mining **Block Body contains:** - A list of validated transactions (typically 500–3000 in Bitcoin) organized into a **Merkle Tree**. - A special **coinbase transaction** that credits the miner with the block reward. **Architecture Diagram (2 marks):** *(Draw the flowchart from Section 4.1)* **Key Property — Cryptographic Chain (2 marks):** Each block stores the SHA-256 hash of its predecessor, forming a one-way cryptographic chain. Any modification to Block N-1 would change its hash, invalidating the *Previous Block Hash* field of Block N, which would invalidate all subsequent blocks. This makes tampering **computationally infeasible** (probability $\approx 2^{-256}$ per block attempt). #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Definition (2 marks):** A **Merkle Tree** (also called a binary hash tree) is a data structure used in blockchain to efficiently summarize and verify the integrity of large sets of transactions. It is named after **Ralph Merkle** (1979). **Construction Steps (3 marks):** *(Draw the Merkle Tree diagram from Section 4.4)* For 4 transactions $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3$: 1. **Leaf Level:** Hash each transaction: $H_0 = H(T_0), H_1 = H(T_1), H_2 = H(T_2), H_3 = H(T_3)$. 2. **Level 1:** Hash pairs: $H_{01} = H(H_0 \mid\mid H_1)$ and $H_{23} = H(H_2 \mid\mid H_3)$. 3. **Root Level:** $M = H(H_{01} \mid\mid H_{23})$ → this is the **Merkle Root**, stored in the block header. **Significance (2 marks):** - **Efficient Verification:** To prove a transaction is included, only $\log_2 N$ hashes are needed (Merkle Proof), not all $N$ transactions. - **Tamper Detection:** Any change in any $T_i$ propagates upward, changing the Merkle Root, making tampering instantly detectable. --- ### ✦ Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **Proof of Work (PoW)** and **Proof of Stake (PoS)** consensus mechanisms. Which one is more energy-efficient and why? > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain any **FIVE real-world applications** of blockchain technology with suitable examples. #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] | Parameter | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | |---|---|---| | **Resource Used** | Computational power (electricity) | Economic stake (locked coins) | | **Validator Selection** | First to solve cryptographic puzzle | Weighted by stake size | | **Energy Consumption** | Very high (Bitcoin: ~150 TWh/year) | **Low (~99.95% less)** | | **Block Time** | ~10 min (Bitcoin) | ~12 sec (Ethereum post-Merge) | | **Security Model** | Cost of 51% attack ≈ hardware cost | Cost of 51% attack ≈ buying 51% of coins | | **Penalty for Misbehavior** | Wasted electricity | **Slashing** (loss of staked coins) | | **Example** | Bitcoin, Litecoin (pre-2019) | Ethereum (post-Sep 2022 Merge), Cardano | **Energy Efficiency Discussion (3 marks):** PoS is **vastly more energy-efficient** than PoW. In PoW, miners must continuously perform trillions of hash computations, consuming enormous electricity. In PoS, validators are chosen probabilistically based on their stake, requiring no significant computation. Ethereum's transition from PoW to PoS (the "Merge" in **September 2022**) reduced its energy consumption by approximately **99.95%**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Five Real-World Applications:** 1. **Cryptocurrency & Payments (1.5 marks):** Bitcoin, Ethereum enable peer-to-peer digital cash without banks. Settlements take seconds, not days. 2. **Supply Chain Management (1.5 marks):** **Walmart + IBM Food Trust** uses blockchain to trace food origin. Time to trace a mango from farm to shelf reduced from **7 days to 2.2 seconds**. 3. **Smart Contracts (1.5 marks):** **Ethereum** executes self-enforcing contracts (e.g., Vyper, Solidity code) that auto-trigger on conditions, removing intermediaries. 4. **Healthcare Records (1 mark):** **MedRec (MIT)** securely stores patient data with patient-controlled access, ensuring privacy and interoperability. 5. **Digital Identity & Voting (1.5 marks):** **Sovrin** and **Estonia's e-Residency** programs use blockchain for self-sovereign identity and tamper-proof voting. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:** > > 🔴 **Mistake 1:** Confusing **distributed** with **decentralized**. Distributed has multiple nodes but may have a central coordinator. Decentralized has **no center at all** (true P2P). > > 🔴 **Mistake 2:** Writing that blockchain is "100% anonymous" — **WRONG**. Blockchain is **pseudonymous** (addresses are public, identities are not directly linked, but can be traced via analysis). > > 🔴 **Mistake 3:** Confusing **SHA-256 output size (256 bits)** with **input block size (512 bits)**. SHA-256 processes 512-bit blocks internally but produces 256-bit digests. > > 🔴 **Mistake 4:** In Merkle Tree questions, students often forget that the **last single transaction is duplicated** to maintain an even number at each level (when $N$ is odd). > > 🔴 **Mistake 5:** Saying "blockchain cannot be hacked" — **WRONG**. Blockchains can be attacked via **51% attacks**, **Sybil attacks**, or **smart contract bugs** (e.g., the **DAO hack, 2016, $60M stolen**). The protocol is secure, but implementations may have vulnerabilities. > > 🔴 **Mistake 6:** Drawing Merkle Trees with **more than 2 children per parent node** — it must be a **binary** tree, period. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:** - 🔹 **Definition:** Blockchain = Decentralized + Distributed + Immutable + Cryptographic ledger of linked blocks. - 🔹 **Block Header Fields (6):** Version, Prev Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Difficulty (nBits), Nonce. - 🔹 **SHA-256:** Input block = 512 bits, Output hash = 256 bits = 32 bytes = 64 hex characters. - 🔹 **Bitcoin uses DOUBLE SHA-256** (hash of hash) for defense against length-extension attacks. - 🔹 **Merkle Tree is a BINARY tree** with $\log_2 N$ levels for $N$ transactions. Total hashes = $2N - 1$. - 🔹 **Bitcoin Block Time:** 10 minutes. **Block Reward Halving:** Every 210,000 blocks (~4 years). **Max Supply:** 21 million BTC. - 🔹 **Four Types of Blockchain:** Public, Private, Consortium, Hybrid. - 🔹 **Three Architectures:** Centralized, Distributed, Decentralized. - 🔹 **Five Characteristics:** Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Traceability, Security. - 🔹 **Genesis Block:** Block #0, mined on **January 3, 2009** by Satoshi Nakamoto, contains the famous headline *"The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks"*. - 🔹 **Ethereum:** Proposed by **Vitalik Buterin** in 2013, launched in 2015. Introduced **Smart Contracts** and **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)**. - 🔹 **Consensus Mechanisms:** PoW (Bitcoin), PoS (Ethereum post-Merge), PBFT (Hyperledger), PoA (Proof of Authority), PoH (Proof of History - Solana). - 🔹 **Difference:** Blockchain is **pseudonymous**, NOT anonymous. Blockchain is **immutable** at protocol level, but applications built on it can have bugs. - 🔹 **Digital Signature:** Uses **ECDSA** with **secp256k1** curve in Bitcoin/Ethereum. Signature = 512 bits. - 🔹 **Smart Contract:** Self-executing code on blockchain, runs on EVM, written in **Solidity**. - 🔹 **Year of key events:** 2008 (Bitcoin white paper), 2009 (Genesis), 2015 (Ethereum), 2022 (Ethereum Merge to PoS). - 🔹 **Immutability Formula:** Probability of forging $k$ blocks in a row in PoW $\approx (q/p)^k$ where $q$ is attacker hash rate and $p$ is honest hash rate. - 🔹 **Web3:** Decentralized internet built on blockchain. **DeFi:** Decentralized Finance. **NFT:** Non-Fungible Token (ERC-721 standard). - 🔹 **Energy:** Bitcoin PoW consumes ~**150 TWh/year** (comparable to Poland's annual electricity use).

topics.title: Objective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Objective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for **PCCGT601 – Blockchain Technologies and Applications**, the topic **"Objective"** within **Module 1 (Introduction to Blockchain)** refers to the foundational goals, design aims, and the set of engineering problems that a **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** / **Blockchain** architecture is built to solve. It articulates *why* blockchain was invented, *what* properties the system guarantees, and *how* these properties are realized through cryptographic, decentralized, and consensus-driven mechanisms.

The blockchain objective can be formally expressed as a tuple of **system goals** and **system invariants**:

$$
\text{Blockchain Objective} = \{ \text{Decentralization},\ \text{Immutability},\ \text{Transparency},\ \text{Trust Minimization},\ \text{Security},\ \text{Consensus} \}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The "Objective" topic is the very first conceptual building block of the course. Examiners frequently test whether the student can *list* and *briefly explain* the core objectives of blockchain. A strong answer must cover the **7–8 primary objectives** (sometimes called the "Design Goals of Blockchain") and connect them to real-world problems in finance, supply chain, and identity management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> *Blockchains are NOT databases.* While both store information, a blockchain is engineered with very specific objectives — trustless consensus, tamper-evidence, and distributed agreement — that a traditional centralized database does not provide.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **public notice board** in a small village, placed right in the middle of the town square. Anyone in the village can:

1. **Walk up and pin a new notice** on the board (this is like *adding a transaction*).
2. **Read every notice ever pinned** (this is *transparency*).
3. **Never remove, alter, or tear down** any old notice — the board is magically indestructible and the village watchman enforces this (this is *immutability*).
4. **No single person owns the board** — it belongs to *everyone* collectively (this is *decentralization*).
5. **All villagers must agree** that the new notice is genuine before it gets pinned (this is *consensus*).

Now imagine this notice board is **digital**, **worldwide**, **duplicated on millions of computers**, and protected by **unbreakable math (cryptography)**. That is, in essence, what a **blockchain** is — and the **"Objectives"** of blockchain are the rules governing this magical, community-owned digital notice board.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Trick:** Whenever you forget the objectives, recall the acronym **"D-I-T-T-S-C-A"** — Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Trust minimization, Security, Consensus, Auditability.

---

## 1.3 Why Was the Blockchain Objective Created? (The Origin Story)

The **objective** of blockchain was originally formulated by the pseudonymous figure **Satoshi Nakamoto** in the **2008 whitepaper titled *"Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System"***. The core problem being solved:

> *"How can two strangers, who do not trust each other and have no intermediary, transact value directly and safely over the internet?"*

This problem is known in academic literature as the **"Double-Spend Problem"** and the **"Byzantine Generals' Problem"** in distributed systems. The blockchain objective is the *engineering answer* to these classical computer science challenges.

| Standard Metric (Bold) | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Year of Origin** | **2008** (whitepaper) / **2009** (genesis block) |
| **Original Creator** | **Satoshi Nakamoto** (pseudonymous) |
| **First Implementation** | **Bitcoin** (cryptocurrency) |
| **Underlying Problem** | **Double-spending** in digital cash |
| **Classical CS Problem Solved** | **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** |
| **Key Innovation** | **Proof-of-Work (PoW) consensus** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Objectives of Blockchain (Structured Breakdown)

The engineering goals of a blockchain system can be decomposed into the following structured hierarchy. Each objective answers a specific "**Why**" and is achieved through a specific "**How**" mechanism.

### 🎯 Objective 1: Decentralization
- **Why:** Traditional systems (banks, governments, social media platforms) concentrate power in a single authority, creating single points of failure, censorship risk, and corruption potential.
- **How:** The ledger is replicated across **N peers (nodes)** in a Peer-to-Peer (P2P) network. No central server is required.
- **Real-world utility:** Enables censorship-resistant money (Bitcoin), uncensorable journalism, and decentralized social networks.

### 🎯 Objective 2: Immutability
- **Why:** Once a record is committed (e.g., a land title, a degree certificate), it must be unalterable to prevent fraud.
- **How:** Each block contains the **cryptographic hash of the previous block** (typically **SHA-256**, producing a **256-bit / 64-hex-character** digest). Tampering with one block breaks the chain — to rewrite history, an attacker must recompute every subsequent block.
- **Real-world utility:** Medical records, audit trails, supply-chain provenance.

### 🎯 Objective 3: Transparency (with Pseudonymity)
- **Why:** All participants should be able to verify the system state independently, eliminating the need for blind trust.
- **How:** In public blockchains, every transaction is broadcast to the entire network and stored on every full node.
- **Real-world utility:** Public charities, government tenders, election voting.

### 🎯 Objective 4: Trust Minimization (Trustless Systems)
- **Why:** Counterparties should not need to trust each other (or a third party) to transact safely.
- **How:** Trust is replaced by **mathematical proof** — cryptographic signatures, hash functions, and consensus algorithms.
- **Real-world utility:** Cross-border payments without correspondent banks, peer-to-peer lending.

### 🎯 Objective 5: Security & Cryptographic Integrity
- **Why:** Digital systems are vulnerable to hacking, replay attacks, and identity spoofing.
- **How:** Use of **asymmetric cryptography** (e.g., **ECDSA — Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm** with the **secp256k1** curve in Bitcoin/Ethereum), **SHA-256 hashing**, and **Merkle trees**.
- **Real-world utility:** Digital identity, secure IoT communication, anti-counterfeiting.

### 🎯 Objective 6: Consensus & Agreement
- **Why:** In a distributed system, all honest nodes must agree on a single version of the truth, even when some nodes are malicious (the **Byzantine Generals' Problem**).
- **How:** Consensus algorithms such as **Proof-of-Work (PoW)**, **Proof-of-Stake (PoS)**, **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**, etc.
- **Real-world utility:** Multi-party computation, federated consortiums (e.g., banking consortiums).

### 🎯 Objective 7: Auditability & Traceability
- **Why:** Every action in the system must be provable and traceable for compliance and accountability.
- **How:** The append-only chain structure, combined with timestamped blocks, provides a complete and chronological history.
- **Real-world utility:** Pharmaceutical drug tracking (FDA DSCSA compliance), carbon credit accounting.

### 🎯 Objective 8: Programmability & Automation (in modern blockchains)
- **Why:** Beyond simple transactions, users want to encode business logic on-chain.
- **How:** **Smart contracts** (Solidity on Ethereum, etc.) — self-executing code that runs deterministically on every node.
- **Real-world utility:** Decentralized Finance (DeFi), NFTs, Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Objective | The "Why" (Problem Solved) | The "How" (Mechanism Used) | KTU Standard Metric (Bold) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Decentralization** | Single point of failure | **P2P network** with $\mathbf{N \geq 1}$ nodes | Resilience increases with node count |
| 2 | **Immutability** | Tampering & fraud | **SHA-256 hash chain** | Hash length = $\mathbf{256\ \text{bits}}$ |
| 3 | **Transparency** | Opaque intermediaries | **Public ledger replication** | Full-node count: $\mathbf{\sim 15{,}000+}$ (Bitcoin) |
| 4 | **Trust Minimization** | Counterparty risk | **Cryptographic proofs** | **ECDSA** key size = $\mathbf{256\ \text{bits}}$ |
| 5 | **Security** | Cyberattacks | **Asymmetric cryptography** | Collision resistance: $\mathbf{2^{128}}$ operations |
| 6 | **Consensus** | Disagreement among nodes | **PoW / PoS / PBFT** | Fault tolerance: $\mathbf{f < n/3}$ (PBFT) |
| 7 | **Auditability** | Lack of traceability | **Append-only timestamped log** | Block time: $\mathbf{\sim 10\ \text{min}}$ (Bitcoin) |
| 8 | **Programmability** | Manual contract execution | **Smart contracts** (Turing-complete) | Gas limit per block: $\mathbf{30M}$ (Ethereum) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Mathematical Symbols in Tables:**
> All vertical bar symbols ($\vert$) for absolute value or set cardinality are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. KTU examiners reward students who quote **standard metrics in bold** with their units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The objectives of blockchain are not abstract — they map directly to **high-impact engineering applications**:

- **Banking & Finance (FinTech):** Cross-border remittance (Ripple, Stellar), real-time settlement.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Walmart–IBM Food Trust tracks mangoes in **seconds** (down from **6 days**).
- **Healthcare:** Patient-controlled medical records (MedRec, MIT).
- **Government:** Estonia's e-Residency program uses blockchain for digital identity.
- **Energy:** Peer-to-peer solar energy trading (Power Ledger, Brooklyn Microgrid).
- **Legal:** Smart-contract-based escrow, automated royalty distribution for artists.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Answers:** Always link *each* objective to a *concrete use case*. A generic answer like *"blockchain is secure"* scores low; *"blockchain provides immutability via SHA-256 hash chaining, which is critical for pharmaceutical drug provenance tracking"* scores high.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of the Objective

## 3.1 Mathematical Formalization of the Blockchain Objective

The blockchain objective can be expressed as a set of **desirable system properties** that the protocol must guarantee under adversarial conditions. Let us formalize each one.

---

### 3.1.1 Formalization of Decentralization

A blockchain network is a distributed system comprising a set of nodes:

$$
\mathcal{N} = \{ n_1,\ n_2,\ n_3,\ \dots,\ n_k \}
$$

where each $n_i$ is a full or light node maintaining a local copy of the ledger $\mathcal{L}$. Decentralization demands:

$$
\forall\ n_i \in \mathcal{N},\quad \mathcal{L}_{n_i} \equiv \mathcal{L}_{n_j}\quad \text{for all honest } n_j
$$

**Explanation of logic:** Every honest node in the network must converge to the *same* ledger state. This is called **consistency** in distributed systems terminology. There is no privileged node $\mathbf{n_{central}}$ that issues commands — instead, authority is distributed across all $k$ nodes.

---

### 3.1.2 Formalization of Immutability

Each block $B_t$ (at height $t$ in the chain) contains the cryptographic hash of the previous block:

$$
B_t = \{\ \text{MerkleRoot}_t,\ \text{Timestamp}_t,\ \text{Nonce}_t,\ H(B_{t-1})\ \}
$$

where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function (typically **SHA-256**). Immutability means:

$$
\text{If } B_t \text{ is altered, then } H(B_t) \neq H(B_t)_{\text{stored}} \implies \text{Chain is broken.}
$$

**Explanation of logic:** Because each block's identity is derived from the previous block, modifying *any* historical block would require recomputing *all* subsequent blocks. The probability of an attacker successfully doing this in a PoW system is:

$$
P_{\text{attack}} = \sum_{r=0}^{z} \binom{z}{r} \left( \frac{q}{p} \right)^{z-r} \left( 1 - \frac{q}{p} \right)^{r}
$$

where $p$ is the honest mining power fraction, $q = 1 - p$ is the attacker's power, and $z$ is the number of confirmation blocks the attacker must outrun. For $p > 0.5$ and large $z$, this probability becomes **vanishingly small**.

---

### 3.1.3 Formalization of Trust Minimization

Trust minimization is quantified by the **number of trusted intermediaries** required for a transaction. In traditional finance:

$$
T_{\text{traditional}} = \{\ \text{Sender Bank},\ \text{Correspondent Bank},\ \text{Receiver Bank},\ \text{Payment Processor},\ \text{Regulator}\ \}
$$

$$
|T_{\text{traditional}}| \geq 3
$$

In a blockchain:

$$
T_{\text{blockchain}} = \{\ \text{Sender Wallet},\ \text{Receiver Wallet},\ \text{Network Consensus}\ \}
$$

$$
|T_{\text{blockchain}}| \approx 0
$$

**Explanation of logic:** "$\approx 0$" means the user does not need to trust any *specific* counterparty — they only need to trust the **mathematics, the protocol, and the assumption that the majority of hashing power is honest**.

---

### 3.1.4 Formalization of Security (Cryptographic Integrity)

Each transaction is signed using an **ECDSA** signature. The verification equation is:

$$
\text{Verify}(m,\ \sigma,\ P_K) = \text{True}\ \iff\ \sigma \cdot G = z \cdot G + r \cdot P_K
$$

**Explanation of logic:**

- $m$ is the message (transaction data)
- $\sigma$ is the signature (a pair of integers $(r, s)$)
- $P_K$ is the public key of the sender
- $G$ is the generator point of the elliptic curve **secp256k1**
- $z$ is the hash of the message

Forgery is computationally infeasible because solving the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** requires approximately $\mathbf{2^{128}}$ operations, far beyond the reach of any classical computer.

---

### 3.1.5 Formalization of Consensus

The **Byzantine Generals' Problem** states that consensus is achievable only if:

$$
n \geq 3f + 1
$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of nodes (generals)
- $f$ = number of faulty / malicious nodes

**Explanation of logic:** To tolerate $f$ traitors among $n$ generals and still reach agreement, the network must have at least $\mathbf{3f + 1}$ participants. This is why PBFT-based blockchains (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) require a minimum cluster of $\mathbf{4}$ nodes to tolerate $\mathbf{1}$ faulty node.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How Objectives Solve Classical CS Problems

| Classical Problem | Engineering Pain | Blockchain Objective That Solves It | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Double-Spend Problem** | Same digital coin spent twice | Consensus + Immutability | UTXO model + longest-chain rule |
| **Byzantine Generals' Problem** | Disagreement among distributed nodes | Consensus | PoW, PoS, PBFT |
| **Single Point of Failure** | Central server crashes | Decentralization | P2P replication |
| **Data Tampering** | Records altered after-the-fact | Immutability | SHA-256 hash chain |
| **Identity Forgery** | Fake digital signatures | Security | ECDSA cryptography |
| **Lack of Auditability** | Opaque record-keeping | Transparency + Auditability | Append-only public ledger |
| **Counterparty Risk** | Other party defaults | Trust Minimization | Smart-contract escrow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Eight Objectives of Blockchain

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **eight core objectives of blockchain** to the **classical problems** they solve and the **cryptographic/distributed-systems mechanisms** they employ. This is the exact kind of "Objective ↔ Problem ↔ Mechanism" mapping that KTU examiners reward in 14-mark answers.


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `O1`, `M1`) — **no reserved keywords** like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` used as node names.
> - Subgraph IDs are named `ClassA`, `ClassB`, `ClassC` (alphanumeric only).
> - Node labels contain only **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** with spaces — no bold, italics, or HTML formatting that would break the Mermaid parser.

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Objectives (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid

    C --> C2[Merkle Tree]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define the term "blockchain" and list any four of its core objectives.** *(Modeled on KTU University Exam - May 2024)*

**Model Answer:**

A **blockchain** is a distributed, append-only data structure consisting of cryptographically linked blocks of transactions, replicated across a peer-to-peer network and validated through a consensus mechanism, eliminating the need for a central trusted authority.

The four core objectives of blockchain are:

1. **Decentralization** — removal of central control via a peer-to-peer network.
2. **Immutability** — historical records cannot be altered without breaking the cryptographic hash chain.
3. **Transparency** — all participants can independently verify the public ledger.
4. **Trust Minimization** — counterparty risk is replaced by mathematical proof and protocol rules.

> [!TIP]
> **3-Mark Strategy:** A perfect 3-mark answer in KTU expects a 1-line definition + 4 distinct objectives (each in one line). Do not write paragraphs — bullet points score higher.

---

### **Question 2: What is the "Double-Spend Problem" in digital cash, and how does blockchain's consensus objective solve it?** *(Modeled on KTU University Exam - Dec 2023)*

**Model Answer:**

The **Double-Spend Problem** is the challenge of preventing a user from spending the same digital token (coin) more than once. Since digital files can be trivially duplicated, a digital cash system without a central authority would be trivially defrauded.

Blockchain's **consensus objective** solves this by requiring all nodes in the network to **collectively agree** on the chronological order of transactions through a consensus algorithm (e.g., **Proof-of-Work**). Once a transaction is included in a block and added to the longest chain, attempting to re-spend the same coin in a competing block is rejected by the honest majority. The **Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO) model** in Bitcoin ensures that every coin is consumed exactly once.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions for Module 1 typically follow the internal-choice pattern. Below are TWO fully independent 14-mark question alternatives. A student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain in detail the **eight core objectives of blockchain technology** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The eight core objectives of blockchain are:

1. **Decentralization:** Blockchain distributes the ledger across all participating nodes in a P2P network, eliminating single points of failure. *Example:* Bitcoin operates on ~15,000+ full nodes worldwide with no central server.

2. **Immutability:** Each block contains the SHA-256 hash of the previous block, making retroactive changes computationally infeasible. *Example:* A land title once recorded on a blockchain cannot be silently altered by a corrupt registrar.

3. **Transparency:** All transactions on a public blockchain are visible to every participant. *Example:* Every Bitcoin transaction can be audited on block explorers like Blockchain.com.

4. **Trust Minimization:** Counterparties do not need to trust each other — they trust the cryptographic protocol. *Example:* Two strangers can transact on Ethereum without an escrow service, using a smart contract.

5. **Security:** Cryptographic primitives (SHA-256, ECDSA) protect data integrity and authenticity. *Example:* Brute-forcing a Bitcoin private key requires ~2^128 operations, computationally infeasible.

6. **Consensus:** Network participants agree on a single chain state despite malicious actors. *Example:* Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work requires honest nodes to control >50% hash power to maintain security.

7. **Auditability:** The append-only structure provides a complete chronological history. *Example:* Walmart uses IBM Food Trust blockchain to trace mangoes in seconds.

8. **Programmability:** Smart contracts encode business logic on-chain. *Example:* Decentralized Finance (DeFi) applications like Uniswap run entirely as smart contracts on Ethereum.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** For a 7-mark sub-question, the student should cover *all 8* objectives briefly OR *4–5* objectives in depth. KTU rewards **examples** heavily — every objective without a real-world example typically loses 0.5–1 mark.

#### (b) Discuss how the **Byzantine Generals' Problem** is addressed by blockchain's consensus objective. Derive the condition $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**The Byzantine Generals' Problem** is a classical distributed-systems dilemma: several generals surrounding a Byzantine army must unanimously decide to *attack* or *retreat* via messengers, but some generals may be traitors sending conflicting messages. The problem asks: *how can loyal generals reach consensus despite traitors?*

**Formal derivation of the condition $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$:**

Consider $n$ generals, of which $f$ are traitors. We want a protocol where all $n - f$ loyal generals reach the same decision.

- **Step 1:** The loyal generals must each make a decision based on the messages received.
- **Step 2:** A traitor can send different messages to different generals, creating confusion.
- **Step 3:** With $f$ traitors, the worst case is that the loyal generals receive $f$ contradictory signals per round.
- **Step 4:** For the loyal generals to outvote the traitors, the number of loyal generals must exceed the number of traitors by a safe margin.
- **Step 5:** The minimum safe configuration is:

$$
n - f > 2f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n > 3f \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n \geq 3f + 1
$$

**Explanation of logic:** With $3f + 1$ generals, even if $f$ are traitors, the $2f + 1$ loyal generals constitute a strict majority. Any message from a traitor can be cross-validated against the majority view of loyal generals.

**Application in Blockchain:** Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work consensus tolerates up to 49.9% malicious hash power. PBFT-based blockchains (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric) use the $n \geq 3f + 1$ rule directly — with 4 nodes, 1 traitor is tolerated.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often write $\mathbf{n \geq 2f + 1}$ (the *crash-fault* condition) instead of $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$ (the *Byzantine-fault* condition). This is a **fatal error** that loses 2–3 marks. Remember: **Byzantine faults require 3f+1; crash faults require only 2f+1.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of blockchain** and identify which layer realizes each of the following objectives: (i) decentralization, (ii) immutability, (iii) consensus. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The blockchain architecture is typically organized into **five conceptual layers**, each realizing specific objectives:

1. **Application Layer:** User-facing applications (wallets, DeFi, NFTs) — realizes the **Programmability** objective.
2. **Smart Contract Layer:** Business-logic code (Solidity, etc.) — realizes the **Programmability and Automation** objective.
3. **Consensus Layer:** Algorithms like PoW, PoS, PBFT — realizes the **Consensus** objective.
4. **Network Layer (P2P):** Node-to-node message propagation — realizes the **Decentralization** objective.
5. **Data Layer:** Blocks, hash chains, Merkle trees, digital signatures — realizes the **Immutability, Security, and Auditability** objectives.

**Block diagram representation (textual):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|          LAYER 5 : APPLICATION LAYER             |
|   (Wallets, DeFi apps, NFT marketplaces)         |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|          LAYER 4 : SMART CONTRACT LAYER          |
|   (Solidity / Vyper on EVM)                      |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|          LAYER 3 : CONSENSUS LAYER                |
|   (PoW / PoS / PBFT)                             |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|          LAYER 2 : NETWORK LAYER (P2P)           |
|   (Gossip protocol, node discovery)              |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|          LAYER 1 : DATA LAYER                    |
|   (Blocks, SHA-256 hash chain, Merkle trees,     |
|    ECDSA signatures)                             |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**Mapping of objectives to layers:**

- **(i) Decentralization** → **Network Layer (P2P)**
- **(ii) Immutability** → **Data Layer** (SHA-256 hash chain)
- **(iii) Consensus** → **Consensus Layer** (PoW/PoS/PBFT)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always *draw* the layered diagram in the answer booklet. KTU awards 1–2 marks purely for a clean, well-labeled block diagram even if the explanation is partial.

#### (b) Explain in detail the **trust minimization objective** of blockchain. How does it differ from the trust model of a **traditional centralized banking system**? Use a comparative table. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Trust minimization** is the objective of replacing interpersonal or institutional trust with **mathematical and algorithmic trust**. In a blockchain, users do not need to trust any specific counterparty, intermediary, or authority — they only need to trust:

- The correctness of the cryptographic algorithms (SHA-256, ECDSA).
- The open-source protocol code (verifiable by anyone).
- The honest-majority assumption (that >50% of the network is honest).

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Traditional Centralized Banking | Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| **Trusted Parties** | Bank, SWIFT, correspondent bank, regulator, payment processor | No trusted party — only math and protocol |
| **Trust Anchor** | Institutional reputation, legal contracts, government backing | Cryptographic proof, consensus algorithm |
| **Number of Intermediaries** | 3 to 5 per transaction | 0 (peer-to-peer) |
| **Transaction Settlement Time** | 1–5 business days (cross-border) | ~10 minutes (Bitcoin) to ~12 seconds (Ethereum) |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible (chargebacks, refunds) | Irreversible once confirmed (immutability) |
| **Censorship Resistance** | Low (bank can freeze accounts) | High (no single entity can block a valid transaction) |
| **Auditability** | Internal audits, regulator-driven | Public, real-time, cryptographically verifiable |
| **Cost per Transaction** | $25–$50 (cross-border remittance) | $0.10–$2.00 (Bitcoin), $0.50–$5.00 (Ethereum) |

**Conclusion:** Blockchain's trust-minimization objective represents a **paradigm shift** from "trust the institution" to "trust the math." It does not eliminate trust entirely — it redistributes it to verifiable open systems rather than fallible human organizations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Many students mistakenly claim *"blockchain eliminates trust"* — this is wrong. The correct phrasing is *"blockchain minimizes trust"* or *"blockchain replaces institutional trust with cryptographic trust."* Writing *"zero trust"* or *"no trust"* loses 1 mark in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Objective" Topic**

- ✅ The **"Objective"** topic refers to the **design goals of blockchain** — what the technology is engineered to achieve.
- ✅ The **8 core objectives** are: **Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Trust Minimization, Security, Consensus, Auditability, Programmability**.
- ✅ Blockchain was created to solve the **Double-Spend Problem** and the **Byzantine Generals' Problem**.
- ✅ **Immutability** is achieved via **SHA-256 hash chaining** (256-bit output).
- ✅ **Decentralization** is achieved via **P2P networks** with no central server.
- ✅ **Security** is achieved via **ECDSA cryptography** on the **secp256k1** curve.
- ✅ **Consensus** tolerates up to **$f < n/3$** Byzantine faults in PBFT, requiring $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$ nodes.
- ✅ **Trust minimization** = replacing institutional trust with **cryptographic trust** (not zero trust).
- ✅ **Smart contracts** on platforms like Ethereum realize the **programmability** objective.
- ✅ Real-world use cases: **Bitcoin (decentralized money), Ethereum (smart contracts), Hyperledger (enterprise BFT), Walmart–IBM Food Trust (supply chain)**.
- ✅ Always quote **standard metrics in bold with units** (e.g., **256 bits**, **10 minutes**, **~$0.50/tx**).
- ✅ **Do NOT confuse** $\mathbf{n \geq 3f + 1}$ (Byzantine) with $\mathbf{n \geq 2f + 1}$ (crash fault).
- ✅ The **layered architecture** maps objectives to layers: **Data Layer (immutability) → Network Layer (decentralization) → Consensus Layer (agreement) → Smart Contract Layer (programmability) → Application Layer (user interface)**.
- ✅ **Year of invention:** **2008** (whitepaper by Satoshi Nakamoto); **2009** (genesis block of Bitcoin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blockchain Terminologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Blockchain Terminologies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are Blockchain Terminologies? **Blockchain Terminologies** constitute the foundational vocabulary of distributed ledger technology (DLT). They are the precise technical terms, acronyms, and conceptual definitions that every blockchain engineer, smart-contract developer, and KTU examiner expects a student to define, differentiate, and apply correctly. Mastering these terms is the gateway to understanding subsequent modules (cryptography, consensus, smart contracts, and decentralized applications). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The 2024 scheme explicitly lists *Distributed Ledger, Blockchain, Blocks, Transactions, Hashing, Merkle Tree, Nonce, Mining, Consensus (PoW, PoS, PBFT), Smart Contracts, Wallets, Digital Signatures, and Forks* as mandatory glossary items. Every definition below is mapped to a syllabus bullet. ## 1.2 The Core Vocabulary — Formal Definitions | # | Term | Formal KTU-Style Definition | |---|------|-----------------------------| | 1 | **Distributed Ledger** | A replicated, shared, and synchronised digital data structure spread across multiple nodes/geographies, with no central administrator. | | 2 | **Block** | A container data structure that holds a batch of validated transactions, a timestamp, the previous block's hash, and its own nonce/hash. | | 3 | **Blockchain** | A cryptographically linked, append-only sequence of blocks, where each block references the hash of its predecessor. | | 4 | **Transaction (Tx)** | The smallest atomic unit of state change signed by a private key and broadcast to the network. | | 5 | **Hash Function** | A deterministic, one-way mathematical function $H(\cdot)$ mapping arbitrary input to a fixed-length output (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256). | | 6 | **Merkle Tree** | A binary hash tree where leaves are transaction hashes and each internal node is the hash of its two children, enabling efficient membership verification. | | 7 | **Nonce** | "Number used ONCE" — a counter field in the block header that miners vary to satisfy the difficulty target. | | 8 | **Mining** | The competitive process of finding a valid nonce that produces a block hash below the network's target. | | 9 | **Consensus Protocol** | A fault-tolerant algorithm enabling all honest nodes to agree on a single canonical chain state. | | 10 | **Smart Contract** | Self-executing code deployed on the blockchain that runs deterministically when its conditions are met. | | 11 | **Wallet** | A software/hardware abstraction managing a user's key-pair and enabling transaction signing. | | 12 | **Digital Signature** | A cryptographic proof generated using a private key to authenticate the origin and integrity of a message. | | 13 | **Fork** | A divergence in the chain caused by simultaneous block discovery, leading to two competing histories. | | 14 | **Genesis Block** | Block #0 — the hard-coded first block with no parent, from which the entire chain descends. | > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Shared Notary Notebook":** > Imagine a classroom where every student has an **identical notebook** (the *distributed ledger*). When a new agreement is made, a **page** (a *block*) is added. Each new page contains a unique **fingerprint** (the *hash*) of the previous page — so if anyone tries to rip out or alter an old page, the fingerprints will not match and the whole class will reject the cheating. The class must all **agree** on which page to add next (*consensus*), and the slowest student still has the **same exact notebook** as the fastest one. ## 1.3 Why These Terms Matter in KTU Examinations - **Definitions (2 marks):** Examiners frequently test precise wording — "Define *Merkle Tree*" or "What is a *nonce*?". - **Comparisons (7–14 marks):** Classic long-answer questions: *Compare PoW vs PoS*, *Public vs Private Blockchain*, *Hard Fork vs Soft Fork*. - **Diagram-based (7 marks):** Drawing a block structure, Merkle tree, or a full blockchain is a guaranteed part-B question topic. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for the KTU Big-8 Terms:** > **D-B-T-H-M-N-Mi-C** → *Distributed ledger, Block, Transaction, Hash, Merkle Tree, Nonce, Mining, Consensus.* Memorise this first cluster; it covers ~60% of Module 1 definitions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Anatomical Hierarchy of a Blockchain A blockchain is best understood as a **stack of five abstractions**, each one consuming the layer below it. 1. **Cryptographic Layer (Bottom):** Hashing + Digital Signatures provide *integrity* and *authenticity*. 2. **Data Layer:** Transactions, Merkle Trees, and Blocks give the system *structure*. 3. **Network Layer:** Peer-to-peer gossip protocol spreads *transactions* and *blocks* to every node. 4. **Consensus Layer:** PoW / PoS / PBFT ensures every node *agrees* on one canonical chain. 5. **Application Layer (Top):** Smart Contracts, Wallets, and DApps provide *user-facing utility*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind each layer:** > - Why hashing? → **Tamper-evidence** (change 1 bit → avalanche). > - Why Merkle Tree? → **$O(\log n)$** membership proof (SPV). > - Why consensus? → **Byzantine fault tolerance** in an untrusted environment. > - Why digital signatures? → **Non-repudiation** + **authentication** without a central CA. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Property | Engineering Use | |---------|-------------------|-----------------| | SHA-256 Output | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ | Block & tx hashing in Bitcoin | | Avalanche Effect | $\Delta H \gg \Delta I$ for 1-bit flip of $I$ | Tamper detection | | Block Hash | $H_{i} = SHA\text{-}256(\text{Header}_{i})$ | Chain linking | | Difficulty Target | $H_{i} \leq T$ where $T$ is the $n$-bit target | PoW mining | | Merkle Root | $R = H(H(Tx_1 \vert\vert Tx_2) \vert\vert H(Tx_3 \vert\vert Tx_4))$ | Single-root summary of $n$ txs | | Mining Probability | $P = \dfrac{\text{hash-power}_{\text{miner}}}{\text{hash-power}_{\text{network}}}$ | 51% attack threshold | | Digital Signature | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, m) ;\; \text{Verify}(pk, m, \sigma) \in \{0,1\}$ | Tx authentication | | Byzantine Fault Tolerance | Tolerates $f$ faulty nodes if $n \geq 3f+1$ | PBFT | | Block Reward (Bitcoin) | Initially **50 BTC**, halved every **210,000 blocks** | Monetary policy | | Block Time | Bitcoin ≈ **10 min**, Ethereum ≈ **12–15 s** | Throughput tuning | | Throughput (TPS) | $\dfrac{\text{Block size}}{\text{Block time} \times \text{Tx size}}$ | Scalability metric | > [!NOTE] > **Critical substitution note:** In the Merkle Root formula above, the symbol $\vert\vert$ denotes bitwise **concatenation**, not logical OR. KTU examiners are strict on this distinction. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Terminology | Industry Application | |-------------|---------------------| | **Distributed Ledger** | Cross-border bank settlements (Ripple, JPMorgan Onyx) | | **Smart Contracts** | Decentralised Finance (DeFi), NFT marketplaces, supply-chain escrow | | **Merkle Tree** | Git version control, IPFS content addressing, certificate transparency logs | | **Consensus (PBFT)** | Hyperledger Fabric enterprise consortiums | | **Digital Signature** | E-passports, Aadhaar eSign, software code signing | | **Wallets** | MetaMask, Ledger Nano — managing $\sim$\$3 trillion in global crypto assets | ## 2.4 Properties Guaranteed by Cryptographic Hashing A function $H(x)$ is blockchain-grade only if it satisfies: 1. **Determinism:** $H(x) = H(x)$ always. 2. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $y$, finding $x$ such that $H(x)=y$ is infeasible. 3. **Second pre-image resistance:** Given $x_1$, finding $x_2 \neq x_1$ with $H(x_1)=H(x_2)$ is infeasible. 4. **Collision resistance:** Finding *any* $x_1 \neq x_2$ with $H(x_1)=H(x_2)$ is infeasible. 5. **Avalanche effect:** A 1-bit change in input changes ~50% of output bits. > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's favourite:** If asked *"Why is SHA-256 used and not MD5?"* — answer with **collision resistance** + **256-bit output space ($2^{256}$ candidates)**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Merkle Root Collapses $n$ Transactions into a Single 256-bit Hash **Given:** A block contains $n = 2^k$ transactions $\text{Tx}_1, \text{Tx}_2, \dots, \text{Tx}_n$. **Goal:** Construct a single root hash $R$ such that any tampering in **any** $\text{Tx}_i$ propagates up to change $R$. ### Step 1 — Leaf level (Level 0) Hash each transaction to obtain the leaf hashes $L_i^{(0)}$. $$L_i^{(0)} = H(\text{Tx}_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$ ### Step 2 — Internal node (Level 1) Each internal node is the hash of the concatenation of its two child hashes. $$L_j^{(1)} = H\Big(L_{2j-1}^{(0)} \;\Vert\; L_{2j}^{(0)}\Big) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, \tfrac{n}{2}$$ ### Step 3 — Induction step (Level $l$) We inductively define every level $l \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$. $$L_j^{(l)} = H\Big(L_{2j-1}^{(l-1)} \;\Vert\; L_{2j}^{(l-1)}\Big) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \dots, \tfrac{n}{2^l}$$ ### Step 4 — The Merkle Root The root is the single hash at level $k$ (only one node remains). $$R = L_1^{(k)} = H\Big(L_1^{(k-1)} \;\Vert\; L_2^{(k-1)}\Big)$$ ### Step 5 — Complexity analysis - **Number of levels:** $k = \log_2 n$. - **Hashes computed per block construction:** $n - 1$. - **SPV membership proof size:** $k$ hashes (i.e., $\log_2 n$), not $n$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** A Bitcoin block with **$n = 2{,}500$ transactions** still allows a light client to prove one transaction's inclusion with only **$k = \log_2 2500 \approx 11.3 \Rightarrow 12$ hashes** — a massive bandwidth saving. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: PoW vs PoS vs PBFT | Parameter | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) | |-----------|---------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------| | **Resource consumed** | Computational power (electricity) | Economic stake (coins locked) | Network messages | | **Block proposer** | First miner to find valid nonce | Validator chosen weighted by stake | Pre-determined primary rotates | | **Finality** | Probabilistic (~6 blocks confirmation) | Probabilistic / sometimes absolute (Ethereum Casper) | Absolute (immediate after $2f+1$ replies) | | **Throughput** | Low (Bitcoin ≈ 7 TPS) | Medium (Ethereum ≈ 30 TPS) | High (thousands TPS) | | **Energy cost** | Very high | Low | Negligible | | **Attack vector** | 51% hashrate | 51% stake acquisition | Sybil via cheap identities (mitigated by permissioning) | | **Tolerated faults** | $< 50\%$ hashrate | $< 50\%$ stake | $f$ Byzantine out of $n \geq 3f+1$ | | **Used by** | Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin | Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano, Polkadot | Hyperledger Fabric, Cosmos (Tendermint) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The $3f+1$ rule in PBFT:** With $n = 4$ nodes, $f = 1$ is tolerated. With $n = 7$, $f = 2$. This is because you need $\geq 2f+1$ agreeing replies to outvote the $f$ faulty nodes, and at most $f$ nodes can be unreachable → $2f+1 + f + f = 4f+1$ — wait, the standard derivation shows $n \geq 3f+1$ when including the primary. **Memorise $3f+1$**; it is the most-tested PBFT constant in KTU. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Validating a Block Hash > **Question (Model):** A Bitcoin-style block header has the following fields: > Version = `0x20000000`, Previous Hash = `0000000000000000000a3b...`, Merkle Root = `9b7c...`, Timestamp = `1700000000`, Bits = `0x1a03ffff`, Nonce = `2083236893`. > Show the step-by-step SHA-256 double-hash to obtain the block hash. **Solution (analytical walk-through):** 1. **Step 1 — Serialise header (80 bytes little-endian).** The header bytes $B$ are the concatenation: version $\Vert$ prev_hash $\Vert$ merkle_root $\Vert$ time $\Vert$ bits $\Vert$ nonce. 2. **Step 2 — First SHA-256.** $$H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(B)$$ 3. **Step 3 — Second SHA-256 (Bitcoin quirk).** $$H_{\text{block}} = \text{SHA-256}(H_1)$$ 4. **Step 4 — Compare with target.** If $H_{\text{block}} \leq T$ (where $T$ is derived from `bits`), the nonce is valid; the miner broadcasts the block. > [!NOTE] > **KTU marking note:** Students often forget that Bitcoin applies **double SHA-256** (SHA-256d). Examiners specifically test this in 14-mark questions. ## 3.4 Mining Probability Derivation Let $h_m$ be a miner's hash-rate and $H$ be the total network hash-rate. $$P(\text{miner mines next block}) = \frac{h_m}{H}$$ For a **51% attack** to succeed, the attacker must control: $$\frac{h_m}{H} > 0.5 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; h_m > 0.5 \cdot H$$ > [!WARNING] > A 51% attacker **cannot** steal coins from arbitrary addresses (private keys are still required), but **can double-spend** by re-mining a parallel chain that overtakes the honest one. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Structure (ASCII + Mermaid Flow) ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ BLOCK (n + 1) │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Version (4 bytes) │ │ Previous Hash (32 bytes) │ │ Merkle Root (32 bytes) │ │ Timestamp (4 bytes) │ │ Difficulty Bits (4 bytes) │ │ Nonce (4 bytes) │ ├────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ... │ │ │ Tx1 │ │ Tx2 │ │ Tx3 │ │ Tx4 │ │ │ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ │ │ (Merkle Tree inside) │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 4.2 Blockchain as a Hash-Chain (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR G["Genesis Block
Hash 00000a"] --> B1["Block 1
Hash 7f2b91"] B1 --> B2["Block 2
Hash 3a8e44"] B2 --> B3["Block 3
Hash c102ab"] B3 --> B4["Block 4
Hash 9d77e0"] B4 --> B5["Block N
Latest Block"] G -. prev_hash .-> B1 B1 -. prev_hash .-> B2 B2 -. prev_hash .-> B3 B3 -. prev_hash .-> B4 B4 -. prev_hash .-> B5 ``` ## 4.3 Merkle Tree Construction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Tx A"] --> AH["H_A = SHA256 A"] B["Tx B"] --> BH["H_B = SHA256 B"] C["Tx C"] --> CH["H_C = SHA256 C"] D["Tx D"] --> DH["H_D = SHA256 D"] AH --> AB["H_AB = SHA256 H_A H_B"] BH --> AB CH --> CD["H_CD = SHA256 H_C H_D"] DH --> CD AB --> ROOT["MERKLE ROOT
H_ABCD"] CD --> ROOT ``` ## 4.4 Distributed Ledger Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NET["Peer-to-Peer Network"] N1["Node 1
Full Copy of Ledger"] N2["Node 2
Full Copy of Ledger"] N3["Node 3
Full Copy of Ledger"] N4["Node 4
Full Copy of Ledger"] end TX["New Transaction
Signed by Wallet"] --> N1 TX -. gossip .-> N2 TX -. gossip .-> N3 TX -. gossip .-> N4 N1 -. validate .-> CONS["Consensus Engine
PoW PoS PBFT"] N2 -. validate .-> CONS N3 -. validate .-> CONS N4 -. validate .-> CONS CONS --> BLK["New Block Appended
to Every Node Copy"] ``` ## 4.5 Public vs Private vs Consortium vs Hybrid — Visual Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart LR BC["Blockchain Types"] --> PUB["Public
Anyone can read write mine
e.g. Bitcoin Ethereum"] BC --> PRV["Private
Single organisation controls
e.g. Hyperledger Besu"] BC --> CNS["Consortium
Group of organisations
e.g. R3 Corda TradeLens"] BC --> HYB["Hybrid
Public base + private channels
e.g. Dragonchain"] ``` ## 4.6 Transaction Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR W["Wallet
signs Tx"] --> BCAST["Broadcast to P2P Network"] BCAST --> MP["Mempool
Unconfirmed Txs"] MP --> MINER["Miner Validator
selects Txs"] MINER --> BUILD["Build Block Header
Merkle Root Nonce"] BUILD --> CONS["Consensus Check
PoW PoS PBFT"] CONS -->|valid| APP["Block Appended
to Chain"] CONS -->|invalid| REJ["Rejected Dropped"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram-rendering tip:** If a Mermaid block fails to render on GitHub/Confluence, replace node IDs with single-word tokens (e.g., `wallet1` instead of `"Wallet"`-style IDs) and remove any bold/italic markup inside label strings. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a *Merkle Tree*. Why is its root stored in the block header instead of the full list of transactions? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Merkle Tree** is a binary hash tree in which every leaf node is the cryptographic hash of a transaction, and every internal node is the hash of the concatenation of its two child hashes, recursively up to a single root hash $R$. It is stored in the block header because (i) it gives a **single 256-bit summary** of *all* transactions in the block, drastically reducing header size, and (ii) it enables **$O(\log n)$ Simplified Payment Verification (SPV)** — a light client can prove a transaction's inclusion with only $\log_2 n$ hashes instead of transmitting all $n$ transactions. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a *nonce* in the context of Proof-of-Work mining? What happens when the nonce space is exhausted? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **nonce** ("number used once") is a 32-bit field in the block header that miners iteratively vary to search for a block hash below the network's difficulty target. When the **$2^{32} \approx 4.29$ billion** nonce values are exhausted, miners modify an **extra-nonce** field inside the coinbase transaction (which alters the Merkle root) and restart the nonce search, effectively expanding the search space to $2^{64}$ or more. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** #### (a) Explain the structure of a blockchain in detail, with a neat diagram. List any **six** key terminologies associated with it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A **blockchain** is an append-only, cryptographically linked sequence of blocks replicated across a peer-to-peer network. Each block is composed of two parts — a **header** (metadata) and a **body** (transactions). **Block Header Fields (refer to §4.1 diagram):** 1. **Version** — protocol version (4 bytes). 2. **Previous Block Hash** — 32-byte SHA-256d hash of the prior block's header; this is the **chain link**. 3. **Merkle Root** — single hash summarising all transactions in the current block. 4. **Timestamp** — Unix time of block creation. 5. **Difficulty Bits** (`nBits`) — compact encoding of the current mining target. 6. **Nonce** — counter adjusted during mining. **Block Body:** Contains a list of validated transactions, serialised into a Merkle tree, with the **coinbase transaction** (mining reward) as the first tx. **Six Key Terminologies:** | # | Term | Meaning | |---|------|---------| | 1 | **Distributed Ledger** | Replicated, shared database across all nodes | | 2 | **Hash Function** | One-way mapping to fixed-length digest | | 3 | **Digital Signature** | Authenticates transaction origin | | 4 | **Consensus** | Agreement protocol among nodes | | 5 | **Smart Contract** | Self-executing on-chain code | | 6 | **Wallet** | Manages keys & signs transactions | **Marking split:** Structure (3) + Diagram (2) + Six terminologies (2). --- #### (b) Differentiate between **Public, Private, Consortium, and Hybrid** blockchains.
