At $z = 6$, the probability is approximately: $$ P(0.1, 6) \approx 0.000021 $$ i.e., roughly **1 in 47,619** — economically infeasible to attack. > [!NOTE] **KTU Examiner Insight** > If the question asks "Why does Bitcoin use 6 confirmations?", the expected answer references the **exponential decay** of attack success probability with $k$ — making reversal computationally infeasible after ~60 minutes. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: TPS Calculation Across Blockchain Types **Given:** A blockchain processes $N_{tx}$ transactions in a time period $T$ (seconds). $$ TPS = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{T} $$ **Comparison Table for Common KTU Values:** | Blockchain Type | Block Time $t_{block}$ | Tx per Block | TPS | |-----------------|:----------------------:|:------------:|:---:| | Bitcoin (Public PoW) | 600 s | ~3000 | ~5 | | Ethereum (Public PoS) | 12 s | ~300 | ~25 | | Hyperledger Fabric (Private) | <1 s | configurable | ~3000 | | Corda (Consortium) | <1 s | configurable | ~1700 | **Engineering Trade-off Equation:** A common KTU conceptual question asks: *"Why can't a public blockchain simply increase block size to improve TPS?"* The answer lies in the **Bandwidth-Latency-Block Size Triangle**: $$ t_{propagation} \propto \dfrac{S_{block}}{B_{network}} $$ Where $S_{block}$ is block size and $B_{network}$ is bandwidth. Increasing $S_{block}$ increases $t_{propagation}$, which increases the **orphan rate (forks)**, which reduces effective security. Hence public chains are **inherently throughput-limited** by their decentralized topology. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing Blockchain Type This is a high-yield KTU exam matrix. Use it to answer *"Which blockchain type should be used for X scenario?"*: | Scenario / Requirement | Recommended Type | Justification | |------------------------|------------------|---------------| | Public cryptocurrency | **Public** | Censorship resistance, no central failure | | Cross-bank settlement | **Consortium** | Shared trust, regulatory compliance | | Internal company audit | **Private** | High throughput, low cost | | Supply chain with public audit | **Hybrid** | Confidentiality + notarization | | National land registry | **Hybrid / Public** | Public verifiability, restricted write | | NFT marketplace | **Public** | Open marketplace, composability | | Pharmaceutical traceability | **Consortium** | Multi-party (manufacturers, distributors, pharmacies) | | Employee payroll | **Private** | Centralized HR control | | DAO governance | **Public** | Trustless voting | | Healthcare records | **Hybrid** | Public audit, private PHI | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — High-Level Classification of Blockchain Types ```mermaid graph TD A[BLOCKCHAIN TYPES] --> B[PUBLIC Permissionless] A --> C[PRIVATE Permissioned] A --> D[CONSORTIUM Federated] A --> E[HYBRID Semi-Decentralized] B --> B1[Open Read, Open Write, Open Consensus] B --> B2[Example: Bitcoin, Ethereum] B --> B3[Consensus: PoW, PoS, DPoS] C --> C1[Restricted Read, Restricted Write] C --> C2[Example: Hyperledger Fabric] C --> C3[Consensus: PBFT, Raft, CFT] D --> D1[Restricted Read, Group Write] D --> D2[Example: R3 Corda, Besu] D --> D3[Consensus: PBFT, Tendermint] E --> E1[Selective Public plus Private Layers] E --> E2[Example: Dragonchain, XinFin] E --> E3[Architecture: Public Anchor plus Private Sidechain] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Architectural Flow of a Hybrid Blockchain ```mermaid graph LR User[User or Application Layer] -->|Submit Tx| PrivateLayer subgraph PrivateSide["PRIVATE CHAIN (Permissioned)"] PrivateLayer[Private Validator Nodes] PrivateLayer --> ExecuteTx[Execute Transaction Off Chain] ExecuteTx --> GenHash[Generate Cryptographic Digest] end GenHash --> AnchorTx[Anchor Hash to Public Ledger] subgraph PublicSide["PUBLIC CHAIN (Trust Anchor)"] AnchorTx --> PublicNodeA[Public Node 1] AnchorTx --> PublicNodeB[Public Node 2] AnchorTx --> PublicNodeC[Public Node N] PublicNodeA --> Consensus[Decentralized Consensus PoS or PoW] PublicNodeB --> Consensus PublicNodeC --> Consensus end Consensus --> FinalHash[Immutable Public Proof] FinalHash --> Audit[Public Auditability and Notarization] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Access Control Comparison Matrix ```mermaid graph TB subgraph AccessControl["ACCESS CONTROL SPECTRUM"] direction LR FullyOpen[Fully Open] -->|Decrease Decentralization| SemiOpen[Semi Open] SemiOpen -->|Decrease Decentralization| SemiClosed[Semi Closed] SemiClosed -->|Decrease Decentralization| FullyClosed[Fully Closed] end FullyOpen -. maps to .-> PubType[PUBLIC BLOCKCHAIN] SemiOpen -. maps to .-> HybridType[HYBRID BLOCKCHAIN] SemiClosed -. maps to .-> ConsortiumType[CONSORTIUM BLOCKCHAIN] FullyClosed -. maps to .-> PrivateType[PRIVATE BLOCKCHAIN] style FullyOpen fill:#90EE90 style FullyClosed fill:#FFB6C1 style PubType fill:#87CEEB style PrivateType fill:#FFA07A style HybridType fill:#DDA0DD style ConsortiumType fill:#F0E68C ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flowchart for Choosing Blockchain Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([New Project Requirement]) --> Q1{Is full
censorship resistance
required?} Q1 -->|Yes| PublicPath[Use Public Blockchain] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is there a single
trusted owner
of the data?} Q2 -->|Yes| PrivatePath[Use Private Blockchain] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is there a known
group of organizations
to share trust?} Q3 -->|Yes| ConsortiumPath[Use Consortium Blockchain] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Do you need both
public audit and
private data?} Q4 -->|Yes| HybridPath[Use Hybrid Blockchain] Q4 -->|No| DefaultPath[Re-evaluate requirements] PublicPath --> End1[Examples: Bitcoin, Ethereum] PrivatePath --> End2[Examples: Hyperledger Fabric] ConsortiumPath --> End3[Examples: R3 Corda] HybridPath --> End4[Examples: Dragonchain] ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Consensus Mechanism Mapping ```mermaid graph TD Consensus[Consensus Mechanism] --> PubCons[Public Consensus] Consensus --> PrivCons[Permissioned Consensus] PubCons --> PoW[Proof of Work] PubCons --> PoS[Proof of Stake] PubCons --> DPoS[Delegated Proof of Stake] PrivCons --> PBFT[Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance] PrivCons --> Raft[Raft] PrivCons --> IBFT[Istanbul BFT] PrivCons --> Tendermint[Tendermint BFT] PoW --> BTC[Bitcoin] PoS --> ETH[Ethereum] PBFT --> HF[Hyperledger Fabric] IBFT --> QB[Quorum Blockchain] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]** *Define a public blockchain. List two distinguishing features and one example.* **Model Answer (10–15 lines expected):** A **public blockchain** is a permissionless, distributed ledger that is openly accessible to anyone with an internet connection. Every node can read the ledger, submit transactions, and participate in the consensus process without needing prior authorization. Consensus is reached through competitive mechanisms such as **Proof of Work (PoW)** or **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, and the system operates without a central authority. **Distinguishing features:** 1. **Permissionless access** — anyone can join, validate, and transact. 2. **Trustless environment** — security is guaranteed by cryptography and economic incentives, not by a central party. 3. **High transparency** — all transactions are publicly verifiable on the ledger. **Example:** Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin. > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > Students frequently lose 1 mark by writing only "it is decentralized" without specifying *who* can access the network. The phrase **"permissionless"** is mandatory for full marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** *Differentiate between a private blockchain and a consortium blockchain. Give one real-world example of each.* **Model Answer (10–15 lines expected):** | Parameter | Private Blockchain | Consortium Blockchain | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------| | **Authority** | Single organization | Group of pre-selected organizations | | **Access** | Restricted to invited members | Restricted to member organizations | | **Read** | Often restricted | Shared among members | | **Write** | Single organization | Multi-party | | **Consensus** | PBFT, Raft | PBFT, IBFT, Tendermint | | **Throughput** | Very high | Very high | | **Example** | Hyperledger Fabric (single org) | R3 Corda (banking consortium) | In a **private blockchain**, a single entity (e.g., a hospital) controls all validator nodes. In a **consortium blockchain**, multiple organizations (e.g., a group of banks) collectively operate the validator set and share governance. --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Module Choice A, Dec 2024]** **(a)** Explain the four major types of blockchain with their key characteristics, access control mechanisms, and suitable use cases. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Compare Proof of Work (PoW) and Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) consensus mechanisms in terms of node requirement, fault tolerance, throughput, and ideal blockchain type. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** The four major types of blockchain are classified based on **read access, write access, and consensus participation**: **1. Public Blockchain (Permissionless)** *(2 marks)* - **Access:** Fully open — anyone can read, write, and validate. - **Consensus:** PoW, PoS, DPoS. - **Throughput:** Low (~5–30 TPS). - **Use Case:** Cryptocurrencies, DeFi, NFTs, public voting. - **Example:** Bitcoin, Ethereum. **2. Private Blockchain (Permissioned)** *(2 marks)* - **Access:** Restricted — single organization controls participation. - **Consensus:** PBFT, Raft, CFT. - **Throughput:** Very high (>1000 TPS). - **Use Case:** Internal corporate audit, internal asset tracking. - **Example:** Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum. **3. Consortium Blockchain (Federated)** *(1.5 marks)* - **Access:** Restricted to a pre-selected group of organizations. - **Consensus:** Multi-party PBFT, IBFT, Tendermint. - **Throughput:** Very high. - **Use Case:** Cross-bank settlement, supply chain consortium, trade finance. - **Example:** R3 Corda, Hyperledger Besu, Marco Polo Network. **4. Hybrid Blockchain (Semi-Decentralized)** *(1.5 marks)* - **Access:** Selective — private chain with public anchor. - **Consensus:** Mixed (private BFT + public PoS anchoring). - **Throughput:** Variable, depends on private layer. - **Use Case:** Healthcare records, IP protection, notarization. - **Example:** Dragonchain, XinFin. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** | Parameter | PoW | PBFT | |-----------|-----|------| | **Validator requirement** | Thousands of anonymous nodes | $n \ge 3f+1$ known validators | | **Fault tolerance** | Tolerates up to 49% hash power | Tolerates up to $f$ Byzantine nodes | | **Throughput** | Low (~5–30 TPS) | High (>1000 TPS) | | **Energy consumption** | Very high (mining) | Minimal | | **Finality** | Probabilistic (6 confirmations) | Immediate / deterministic | | **Ideal blockchain type** | Public | Private / Consortium | | **Examples** | Bitcoin, Ethereum (pre-Merge) | Hyperledger Fabric, Zilliqa | **Key Distinctions:** - **PoW** is **permissionless** — anyone can mine. Security derives from computational work and economic incentives. - **PBFT** is **permissioned** — validators are pre-vetted. Security derives from cryptographic voting and the $3f+1$ quorum property. - PoW is **open and trustless** but slow and energy-intensive. - PBFT is **closed and trust-based** but fast and efficient. **Conclusion:** PoW suits public blockchains requiring censorship resistance; PBFT suits private/consortium blockchains requiring high throughput and immediate finality. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Module Choice B, June 2024]** **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of a hybrid blockchain. Discuss its advantages over purely public or private blockchains. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A consortium of 7 banks wishes to deploy a permissioned blockchain using PBFT. If at most 2 banks may act maliciously (Byzantine), verify whether $n=7$ satisfies the PBFT safety condition. If not, what is the minimum number of validators required? *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** **Architecture of a Hybrid Blockchain:** *(3 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation)* A hybrid blockchain has **two layers**: 1. **Private Layer (Permissioned):** Handles sensitive business logic, confidential transactions, and high-throughput operations. 2. **Public Layer (Permissionless):** Acts as a trust anchor and notarization layer. Stores cryptographic digests (hashes) of private layer state. **Working Steps:** 1. A user submits a transaction to the private chain. 2. The private chain's validator nodes execute the transaction using smart contracts. 3. A **Merkle root** or transaction hash is computed. 4. This hash is submitted as a transaction to the public chain (e.g., Ethereum). 5. The public chain confirms the hash via its decentralized consensus. 6. Anyone can now verify the existence and timestamp of the private transaction by checking the public chain, without exposing the underlying data. **Advantages over Public/Private:** *(2 marks)* - **Over Public:** Confidentiality of sensitive data; higher throughput. - **Over Private:** Public verifiability; cryptographic proof of integrity; better stakeholder trust. - **Combined:** Best of both worlds — privacy + transparency. **Example Use Cases:** Healthcare records, IP registration, supply chain audit, government-enterprise collaboration. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Recall the PBFT Safety Condition** *(1 mark)* The PBFT consensus is safe if and only if: $$ n \ge 3f + 1 $$ Where $n$ is total validators and $f$ is the maximum number of Byzantine (malicious) nodes. **Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values** *(2 marks)* - Given: $f = 2$, $n = 7$. - Required minimum: $n_{\min} = 3f + 1 = 3(2) + 1 = 7$. **Step 3 — Verify the Condition** *(2 marks)* $$ n = 7 \ge n_{\min} = 7 \;\; \checkmark $$ The condition is **just satisfied** — $n = 7$ validators can tolerate up to $f = 2$ Byzantine nodes. **Step 4 — Interpretation** *(2 marks)* This is a **tight bound** — the system operates at the absolute minimum. Any further Byzantine behavior beyond 2 nodes (e.g., $f = 3$) would require at least $n = 10$ validators for safety. Practically, the consortium should plan to scale to $n = 10$ to maintain a safety margin. **Final Answer:** $n = 7$ **just satisfies** the PBFT condition. Recommended safety margin: $n = 10$. --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing "Private" with "Confidential":** Students often describe private blockchains as "anonymous." They are not — they are **permissioned** with **known identities**. Anonymous = public pseudonymous. > > 2. **Forgetting the Consensus Mapping:** Marks are awarded for linking the *type* to its *consensus*. Saying "private blockchain uses PoW" is **factually incorrect** and costs 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Omitting the Hybrid Chain "Anchor" Concept:** Hybrid blockchains are *defined* by the public anchor. Skipping this loses 2 marks in a 7-mark question. > > 4. **PBFT Formula Mistake:** Writing $n \ge 2f + 1$ instead of $n \ge 3f + 1$ is a frequent error. The factor of 3 is **non-negotiable** for BFT safety. > > 5. **Not Mentioning Use Cases:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *application-level understanding*. A perfect theoretical answer without a real-world use case is capped at ~80% marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Types of Blockchain** - **Four Types:** Public, Private, Consortium, Hybrid — distinguished by **read, write, and consensus access**. - **Public (Permissionless):** Open to all. Uses **PoW/PoS**. Examples: **Bitcoin, Ethereum**. Low TPS (~5–30). Vulnerable to 51% attacks. High energy. - **Private (Permissioned):** Single organization controls access. Uses **PBFT/Raft**. High TPS (>1000). Example: **Hyperledger Fabric**. Known identities, no anonymity. - **Consortium (Federated):** Pre-selected group of organizations share validation. Uses **PBFT, IBFT, Tendermint**. Examples: **R3 Corda, Hyperledger Besu**. Used in **banking, supply chain, trade finance**. - **Hybrid (Semi-Decentralized):** Combines private chain (for confidentiality) with public chain (for notarization/audit). Example: **Dragonchain, XinFin**. - **PBFT Safety Bound:** $n \ge 3f + 1$ — to tolerate $f$ Byzantine nodes. - **PoW Finality:** Probabilistic; Bitcoin uses **6 confirmations** (~60 min). - **TPS Comparison:** Public <<< Permissioned. Public is throughput-limited by decentralized propagation. - **51% Attack:** Occurs when an entity controls **>50%** of consensus power. - **Decision Rule:** If censorship resistance matters → Public. If single owner → Private. If group of known parties → Consortium. If you need both privacy and public proof → Hybrid. - **Memorable Acronym (KTU trick):** **"PPCH"** — **P**ublic, **P**rivate, **C**onsortium, **H**ybrid. - **Examiner's favorite question:** *"Differentiate between private and consortium blockchain"* — always pair with a **real-world example** and the **consensus mechanism** to score full marks.

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Blockchain Architecture

**Blockchain Architecture** refers to the layered, decentralized, and cryptographically-secured structural framework that defines how data (transactions) is grouped, validated, chained, replicated, and propagated across a distributed peer-to-peer (P2P) network of nodes. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCCGT601), the architecture is best understood as a **stack of five functional layers** — Application, Execution, Consensus, Data, and Network — each with a specific responsibility that collectively produces an immutable, append-only ledger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Architecture):**
> The architecture of a blockchain is *not* just a chain of blocks. It is a **distributed data structure + replicated state machine + consensus protocol** running on top of an overlay P2P network. Examiners expect students to identify each layer and explain its function.

The five canonical layers of a blockchain architecture (as per the widely-adopted academic model — e.g., the IBM / NIST layered reference model) are:

1. **Application Layer** – Smart contracts, decentralized applications (DApps), wallets, and user-facing scripts.
2. **Execution Layer** – The "virtual machine" (e.g., EVM) that executes smart-contract code deterministically.
3. **Consensus Layer** – The protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT) by which nodes agree on the next valid block.
4. **Data Layer** – The chain of blocks, Merkle trees, hash pointers, and cryptographic primitives (SHA-256).
5. **Network Layer** – The P2P topology, gossip protocol, and message propagation mechanics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Public Notice Board" Model

Imagine a **public notice board in a college campus** that everyone can read but no one can tamper with:

- The **board** = the blockchain ledger.
- Each **notice pinned to the board** = a **block** containing many small entries (transactions).
- Each new notice is stapled (chained) to the previous one with a **unique fingerprint** (hash) — if anyone tries to remove or edit an old notice, the fingerprint breaks and everyone notices.
- A **photocopy of the board** is maintained by every student (full node). When a new notice is added, every student verifies it independently and updates their copy.
- A **proctor** (consensus algorithm) decides whose notice goes up next if two students submit one simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Blockchain Architecture = *How blocks are built* (Data) + *How they are linked* (Hash pointers) + *How they are broadcast* (P2P Network) + *How agreement is reached* (Consensus) + *How users interact* (Application/Execution).

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **Block Time (T_b):** Average time to produce a new block (Bitcoin ≈ **10 minutes**, Ethereum ≈ **12–14 seconds**).
- **Block Size (S_b):** Maximum data a block can hold (Bitcoin ≈ **1 MB**, Bitcoin Cash ≈ **32 MB**).
- **Hash Output Length (n):** SHA-256 produces **256 bits** = **64 hex characters**.
- **Merkle Tree Height (h):** For **N** transactions, h = **⌈log₂(N)⌉**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Block — The Atomic Unit of the Architecture

A **block** is a container data structure that batches validated transactions. Structurally, a block has two parts:

### (a) Block Header (≈ 80 bytes in Bitcoin)
| Field | Size (Bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 | Protocol version for soft-fork signaling |
| Previous Block Hash | 32 | Hash pointer linking to parent block |
| Merkle Root | 32 | Root of the Merkle tree of all TX in this block |
| Timestamp | 4 | Unix time of block creation |
| nBits (Difficulty Target) | 4 | Encoded difficulty threshold |
| Nonce | 4 | Variable counter for PoW puzzle |

### (b) Block Body
- Contains the **transaction counter** (varint) and the **list of transactions** encoded in a **Merkle Tree** structure.
- The first transaction in a block is typically a **coinbase transaction** that mints new coins and pays the block reward to the winning miner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently test the *relationship* between fields — e.g., "How does changing one transaction invalidate the entire chain?" The answer lies in the **Merkle Root → Block Hash chain**.

## 2.2 The Chain — Hash Pointers Explained

Each block B_i contains a cryptographic hash of the previous block B_{i-1}'s header. This is called a **hash pointer** and it is the structural reason for immutability.

$$H_i \;=\; H(\text{Header}_i) \;=\; \text{SHA-256}\bigl(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i)\bigr)$$

If an attacker changes a single byte in transaction TX_5 of block B_{i-1}:
- The **Merkle root** of B_{i-1} changes.
- The **header hash H_{i-1}** changes.
- Block B_i (which stores H_{i-1}) is **no longer valid**.
- Every subsequent block must therefore be **re-mined**, which is computationally infeasible beyond a few blocks deep — this is the basis of the **"six-block confirmation rule"** in Bitcoin.

## 2.3 Merkle Tree — The Backbone of Block-Level Verification

A **Merkle Tree** is a binary hash tree where:
- **Leaves** = hash of each transaction H(TX_k).
- **Internal nodes** = hash of the concatenation of their two children: H(Left $\Vert$ Right).
- **Root** = single hash summarizing all transactions in the block.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Hash Output Length (SHA-256) | $n = \mathbf{256 \; bits} = \mathbf{64 \; hex}$ | Defines collision resistance ≈ $2^{128}$ (birthday bound) |
| Merkle Root | $R = H\bigl(H(T_1 \Vert T_2) \Vert H(T_3 \Vert T_4)\bigr)$ | Single hash for block-level integrity |
| Tree Height | $h = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | Determines max TX per block |
| Number of Hashes for SPV Proof | $\approx \log_2 N$ | Light-client (SPV node) verification cost |
| Block Hash Pointer | $H_i = \text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_i)$ | Links block $i$ to $i-1$ |
| Chain Immutability Depth | $\text{Six blocks} = \mathbf{60 \; minutes}$ in Bitcoin | Probabilistic finality |
| Difficulty Target | $D = \text{0x00000000FFFF0000} \cdot \frac{2016 \text{ blocks}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$ | Retargets every 2016 blocks ≈ 2 weeks |
| Block Reward Halving | $\frac{B_0}{2^{k}}$ after every **210,000 blocks** | Bitcoin's monetary policy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> The Merkle tree is used **outside blockchain** in Git (commit graph), certificate transparency logs, IPFS, and AWS DynamoDB's Merkle-tree-based anti-entropy replication. It is one of the most important data structures in modern distributed systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Merkle Root of 4 Transactions

Let us derive the Merkle root for a block containing **4 transactions** T1, T2, T3, T4. Each transaction is first hashed with SHA-256 (we use $H_{TX}$ for clarity).

**Step 1 — Hash the leaves (transactions):**
Each leaf is the SHA-256 double-hash of the raw transaction data.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= H(T_1) \\
L_2 &= H(T_2) \\
L_3 &= H(T_3) \\
L_4 &= H(T_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The leaves of a Merkle tree are the cryptographic digests of the underlying data, not the data itself. This ensures that the tree is **collision-resistant** and **commitment-binding**.

**Step 2 — Compute the first level of internal nodes (Level 1):**
We pair adjacent leaves and hash their concatenation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{12} &= H(L_1 \Vert L_2) \\
H_{34} &= H(L_3 \Vert L_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The operator $\Vert$ denotes **byte-string concatenation**. The order matters: swapping L1 and L2 produces a *completely different* hash. This makes the tree **order-sensitive**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Merkle Root (Level 0):**
Finally, we hash the concatenation of H_12 and H_34.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= H(H_{12} \Vert H_{34}) \\
  &= H\Bigl(H\bigl(H(T_1) \Vert H(T_2)\bigr) \;\Vert\; H\bigl(H(T_3) \Vert H(T_4)\bigr)\Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion Logic:* This **single 256-bit value R** is what is stored in the block header. It acts as a **fingerprint of all transactions in the block**. Any modification to any T_k will cascade upward and change R.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Verifying a Single Transaction (SPV Proof)

Suppose a light client (SPV node) wants to verify that transaction **T_2** is included in a block, without downloading all transactions. The full node provides the **Merkle path** (also called the *authentication path*).

For a 4-leaf tree, the path for T_2 consists of **2 sibling hashes**: $L_1$ and $H_{34}$.

**Step 1 — Reconstruct the leaf hash:**
$$L_2 = H(T_2)$$

**Step 2 — Reconstruct the parent using the sibling:**
$$H_{12} = H(L_1 \Vert L_2)$$

**Step 3 — Reconstruct the root using the other sibling:**
$$R' = H(H_{12} \Vert H_{34})$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**
$$\text{Verified} \iff R' = R_{\text{stored in header}}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The SPV client only needs **log₂(N) hashes** for verification. For a Bitcoin block with ≈ 2,500 transactions, that is only **≈ 12 hashes** — a massive bandwidth and storage saving.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost of Architectural Operations

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Network Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Add a new block | $O(1)$ local + $O(N)$ gossip | $O(B)$ per node (B = block size) | $O(N \cdot B)$ total |
| Verify block hash chain | $O(1)$ (one hash compare) | $O(1)$ | 0 |
| Merkle root compute | $O(N)$ hashes | $O(N)$ | 0 |
| SPV proof verification | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ | $O(\log N)$ |
| Full chain validation | $O(B \cdot \text{chain length})$ | $O(B \cdot \text{chain length})$ | 0 |
| Re-org to depth $d$ | $O(d \cdot B)$ | $O(d \cdot B)$ | $O(d \cdot B)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The "Architecture" topic often yields derivation-style questions on the Merkle root or the hash-pointer chain. Always state the **time complexity** and the **size of the authentication path** — examiners reward that explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Layered Blockchain Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **five-layer architecture** of a blockchain system and the directional flow of data between layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[End User DApp Browser] --> appL
    subgraph LAYERS[Blockchain Architecture Stack]
        direction TB
        appL["LAYER 5 APPLICATION LAYER\nSmart Contracts DApps Wallets"]
        execL["LAYER 4 EXECUTION LAYER\nEVM Deterministic State Transition"]
        consL["LAYER 3 CONSENSUS LAYER\nPoW PoS PBFT Block Finality"]
        dataL["LAYER 2 DATA LAYER\nBlocks Merkle Tree Hash Pointers"]
        netL["LAYER 1 NETWORK LAYER\nP2P Gossip TCP UDP Discovery"]
    end
    appL --> execL
    execL --> consL
    consL --> dataL
    dataL --> netL
    netL --> consL
```

> *Reading the diagram:* A user request originates at **Layer 5**, executes at **Layer 4**, competes for consensus at **Layer 3**, is committed to the data structure at **Layer 2**, and is broadcast across the network at **Layer 1**. The reverse arrow (Net → Cons) shows that newly received blocks from peers also re-enter the consensus layer for validation.

## 4.2 Block Structure Schematic

The following diagram maps the **internal anatomy of a single block**, showing how the Merkle root summarizes the transactions in the body and how the header is hash-linked to the previous block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrevBlock["PREVIOUS BLOCK B i minus 1"]
        pHdr["Header B i minus 1\nVersion PrevHash MerkleRoot Time Bits Nonce"]
    end
    subgraph CurBlock["CURRENT BLOCK B i"]
        cHdr["Header B i\nVersion PREV HASH MerkleRoot Time Bits Nonce"]
        cBody["Block Body\nTX1 TX2 TX3 TX4 ... TXn"]
    end
    subgraph MT["MERKLE TREE"]
        root["Merkle Root R"]
        n12["H 12"]
        n34["H 34"]
        l1["H T1"]
        l2["H T2"]
        l3["H T3"]
        l4["H T4"]
    end
    pHdr -- "hash pointer" --> cHdr
    cBody --> l1
    cBody --> l2
    cBody --> l3
    cBody --> l4
    l1 --> n12
    l2 --> n12
    l3 --> n34
    l4 --> n34
    n12 --> root
    n34 --> root
    root --> cHdr
```

> *Reading the diagram:* The **hash pointer** from B_{i-1} to B_i is what makes the blockchain immutable. The **Merkle root R** is the single field in the header that commits to every transaction in the body.

## 4.3 Distributed P2P Network Topology

This diagram illustrates how **full nodes, light (SPV) nodes, and miners** interact in a typical blockchain network. Each peer maintains independent copies of the chain and propagates new blocks via a **gossip protocol**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FN["Full Nodes"]
        n1["Node A"]
        n2["Node B"]
        n3["Node C"]
        n4["Node D"]
    end
    subgraph SPV["Light SPV Nodes"]
        s1["Mobile Wallet"]
        s2["Web Wallet"]
    end
    subgraph MIN["Miners Validators"]
        m1["Miner 1"]
        m2["Miner 2"]
    end
    n1 <--> n2
    n2 <--> n3
    n3 <--> n4
    n4 <--> n1
    n1 <--> m1
    n3 <--> m2
    s1 -. "merkle proof only" .-> n2
    s2 -. "merkle proof only" .-> n4
```

> *Reading the diagram:* **Full nodes** (dotted in academic diagrams as solid squares) store the entire chain and validate every transaction. **SPV (light) nodes** store only headers and request **Merkle proofs** from full nodes to verify their own transactions. **Miners/validators** are full nodes that additionally produce new blocks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term "block" in a blockchain. List the main components of a Bitcoin-style block header.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **block** is a data structure that batches a set of validated transactions and binds them to the previous block using a cryptographic hash pointer, forming a chronological, append-only chain. In Bitcoin, the **80-byte block header** contains the following six fields:

1. **Version** (4 bytes) – Protocol version number.
2. **Previous Block Hash** (32 bytes) – SHA-256 hash of the parent block's header.
3. **Merkle Root** (32 bytes) – Root hash of the Merkle tree of all transactions in the current block.
4. **Timestamp** (4 bytes) – Unix epoch time of block creation.
5. **nBits / Difficulty Target** (4 bytes) – Encoded compact representation of the mining difficulty.
6. **Nonce** (4 bytes) – The arbitrary counter that miners vary to satisfy the PoW puzzle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a Merkle tree? Why is it preferred over a simple hash list for summarizing transactions in a block?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Merkle tree** is a binary tree of cryptographic hashes in which the leaves are hashes of individual data items (transactions) and every internal node is the hash of the concatenation of its two child hashes, culminating in a single **Merkle root** that represents all leaves.
It is preferred over a flat hash list because:
- **Efficient membership proof (SPV):** A client can prove a transaction is in a block using only $\log_2 N$ hashes, rather than the full list of N hashes.
- **Tamper evidence:** Any single-bit change in any transaction cascades up to the root, giving a strong integrity guarantee.
- **Parallelism:** Subtrees can be hashed independently, enabling concurrent computation.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> For 14-mark questions on "Architecture," students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
> 1. Confusing the **Merkle root** (a data-structure concept) with the **block hash** (a PoW concept) — these are *different* hashes computed over *different* inputs.
> 2. Forgetting to mention that the Merkle root sits **inside the block header**, not in the body.
> 3. Omitting the **order-dependence** of hash concatenation ($H(L_1 \Vert L_2) \ne H(L_2 \Vert L_1)$).
> 4. Not relating architectural features to a **real-world use case** (e.g., SPV in mobile wallets). Examiners specifically reward "engineering utility" statements.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of a blockchain system**. Discuss the role of each layer in ensuring decentralization, immutability, and consensus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **internal structure of a block** in a blockchain. With a step-by-step example, show how the **Merkle root** is computed for a block containing **4 transactions** T1, T2, T3, T4. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) – 7 marks:**

The blockchain architecture is a **five-layer stack** that decouples concerns of application logic, execution, agreement, storage, and networking.

- **Layer 1 – Network (P2P) Layer:** Uses an unstructured overlay (often Kademlia in Ethereum) and a **gossip protocol** to broadcast new transactions and blocks. Each node maintains a set of peers and discovers new ones via seed nodes. Role: **decentralization** (no central server).
- **Layer 2 – Data Layer:** Stores the **chain of blocks**, the **Merkle tree** of transactions, and the **hash pointers** linking consecutive blocks. Role: **immutability** — altering one byte changes the Merkle root, which changes the block hash, which invalidates all descendants.
- **Layer 3 – Consensus Layer:** Implements the agreement protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT, Raft). Role: **Byzantine fault tolerance** — ensures all honest nodes agree on a single ordering of blocks.
- **Layer 4 – Execution Layer:** Runs the **state machine** (e.g., EVM) that deterministically processes transactions and smart contracts. Every node must arrive at the **identical post-state** given the same pre-state and TX set.
- **Layer 5 – Application Layer:** Hosts **DApps, wallets, and smart-contract frontends** that users interact with. This layer is the only one the end user directly experiences.

**Model Solution (b) – 7 marks:**

The internal structure of a block consists of a **header (80 bytes)** and a **body (variable, up to block size limit)**. The header contains version, previous block hash, **Merkle root**, timestamp, difficulty, and nonce. The body contains the **coinbase transaction** (first) and a list of regular transactions encoded as a Merkle tree.

**Step-by-step Merkle root computation for T1, T2, T3, T4:**

**Step 1 — Hash the leaves:**
$$
L_1 = H(T_1),\quad L_2 = H(T_2),\quad L_3 = H(T_3),\quad L_4 = H(T_4)
$$

**Step 2 — Hash the pairs (Level 1):**
$$
H_{12} = H(L_1 \Vert L_2),\quad H_{34} = H(L_3 \Vert L_4)
$$

**Step 3 — Hash the pair of pairs (Root):**
$$
R = H(H_{12} \Vert H_{34})
$$

The 256-bit value $R$ is then written into the **Merkle Root field** of the block header. If an attacker later alters $T_2$, then $L_2$ changes → $H_{12}$ changes → $R$ changes → the block header hash changes → the chain breaks. The immutability of the chain is therefore a direct consequence of this nested hashing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **hash pointer** and how it forms the structural basis of a blockchain. Illustrate with a small chain of 3 blocks $B_1 \to B_2 \to B_3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is an **SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) node**? Show, with a worked example on a Merkle tree of 4 transactions, how a light client can prove inclusion of transaction $T_2$ using only $\log_2 4 = 2$ hashes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) – 7 marks:**

A **hash pointer** is a pointer (i.e., a reference to where data is stored) that is augmented with a cryptographic hash of the data at the time of the pointer's creation. In a blockchain, every block $B_i$ stores in its header the hash of the previous block $B_{i-1}$'s header. Formally:

$$
\text{Ptr}_i = (H_{i-1}, \text{location of } B_{i-1})
$$

**Three-block chain illustration:**

| Block | Header Hash | Stores Previous Hash Of |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ (Genesis) | $H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(Hdr_1)$ | — (no parent) |
| $B_2$ | $H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(Hdr_2)$ | $B_1$ |
| $B_3$ | $H_3 = \text{SHA-256}(Hdr_3)$ | $B_2$ |

If an attacker modifies $B_1$ even slightly, $H_1$ changes. But $B_2$ still stores the *old* $H_1$ in its header, so the hash of $B_2$'s header (computed afresh) will no longer match what $B_3$ expects as its "previous hash" — the chain becomes inconsistent from that point forward. To successfully tamper, the attacker must rewrite $B_2$ and $B_3$ as well, and out-race the honest network — computationally infeasible beyond a small depth. This is the structural basis of **immutability**.

**Model Solution (b) – 7 marks:**

An **SPV node** (proposed by Satoshi Nakamoto in the Bitcoin whitepaper) is a lightweight client that:
- Stores **only block headers**, not full transactions.
- Relies on full nodes to provide **Merkle proofs** (authentication paths) to verify that a specific transaction is included in a block.

**Worked example — Proving $T_2$ in a 4-transaction Merkle tree:**

Given Merkle root $R$ stored in the block header, the full node provides the **Merkle path** for $T_2$, which is the set of sibling hashes: $\{L_1, H_{34}\}$.

**Step 1 — Hash the target transaction to get the leaf:**
$$L_2 = H(T_2)$$

**Step 2 — Combine with the first sibling ($L_1$) to obtain the parent:**
$$H_{12} = H(L_1 \Vert L_2)$$

**Step 3 — Combine with the second sibling ($H_{34}$) to obtain the reconstructed root:**
$$R' = H(H_{12} \Vert H_{34})$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**
$$\text{Transaction is included} \iff R' = R_{\text{header}}$$

The SPV client needed only **2 hashes** from the network, not the full transaction set — a logarithmic reduction critical for mobile and IoT devices.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Architecture is layered:** Application → Execution → Consensus → Data → Network. Memorize the order top-down and the responsibility of each.
- **Block = Header + Body.** The header is 80 bytes in Bitcoin and contains the **Merkle root**, not the full transactions.
- **Hash pointer** = hash of previous block header stored in current block. This is the structural mechanism of immutability.
- **Merkle tree** is a binary hash tree enabling $O(\log N)$ inclusion proofs via **SPV**.
- **Order of concatenation matters:** $H(A \Vert B) \ne H(B \Vert A)$ — always state this in derivations to score full marks.
- **Merkle root ≠ Block hash.** Merkle root is the root of the transaction tree; block hash is SHA-256 of the entire header (used in PoW).
- **SPV cost:** For N transactions, the proof size and verification cost are $\log_2 N$ hashes.
- **Six-block rule** = probabilistic finality in Bitcoin (≈ 60 minutes at 10-min block time).
- **Real-world engineering utility:** Merkle trees are used in Git, IPFS, certificate transparency, and AWS — emphasize this in answers for full marks.
- **Diagram expected:** Every 14-mark question on architecture should include a labeled block/chain/Merkle-tree diagram; purely textual answers lose 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefits and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Benefits and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Benefits and Limitations of Blockchain* refers to the systematic enumeration of the architectural, cryptographic, and economic advantages that distributed ledger technology (DLT) provides over centralized systems, alongside the technological, regulatory, and operational constraints that currently restrict its widespread enterprise adoption. This topic is critical for KTU Module 1 because it contextualizes *why* the technology exists and *where* it fails.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Notice Board"

Imagine a small village that used to keep all its land records in **one locked cabinet** in the Tahsildar's office. The cabinet had a single keyholder, and people had to trust that one person wouldn't tamper with, lose, or secretly change the papers.

Now imagine the village replaces that cabinet with a **digital notice board**:
- The board is duplicated and **mirrored** on every villager's phone.
- Every new entry is **sealed with a unique cryptographic stamp** linked to the previous entry.
- If someone tries to scratch out an old entry, *every* phone in the village will instantly reject it as invalid.

> This is the essence of a blockchain: a **distributed, cryptographically chained, tamper-evident ledger** that removes the need for a single trusted authority.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Benefits and Limitations

> [!NOTE]
> **Benefits** are the *intrinsic properties* the system guarantees by design (decentralization, immutability, transparency, security, traceability). **Limitations** are the *trade-offs* that emerge when these properties are implemented at scale (TPS bottlenecks, energy footprint, storage bloat, regulatory ambiguity, and irreversibility).

The standard reference benchmark used by KTU examiners for blockchain performance is **Transactions Per Second (TPS)** — the throughput metric a network can sustainably process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric Reference Values (Approximate):**
> - **Bitcoin (PoW):** ~**7 TPS**
> - **Ethereum (PoS post-Merge):** ~**30 TPS** (Layer-1 base)
> - **Visa (Centralized):** ~**65,000 TPS** peak
> - **Average Block Time (Bitcoin):** ~**10 minutes**
> - **Average Block Time (Ethereum):** ~**12 seconds**
> - **Hash Function Standard:** **SHA-256** (Bitcoin), **Keccak-256** (Ethereum)

These numbers are the cornerstone of every "limitation" argument in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Blockchain Benefits

Below is the structured "Why" and "How" behind each benefit — the exact framing KTU examiners reward.

#### **Benefit 1: Decentralization**
- **Why:** Eliminates single point of failure and single point of control.
- **How:** Data is replicated across N nodes; consensus protocols (PoW, PoS, PBFT) ensure agreement without a central coordinator.
- **Engineering Utility:** Resilience in critical infrastructure (power grids, supply chain).

#### **Benefit 2: Immutability**
- **Why:** Once a block is added, altering it would invalidate all subsequent block hashes.
- **How:** Each block header contains the hash of the previous block, creating a cryptographic chain.
- **Engineering Utility:** Audit trails, legal evidence preservation, anti-counterfeiting.

#### **Benefit 3: Transparency & Traceability**
- **Why:** Public blockchains allow any participant to verify the entire transaction history.
- **How:** Every transaction is signed, timestamped, and permanently recorded.
- **Engineering Utility:** Provenance in pharmaceuticals, food supply, diamonds.

#### **Benefit 4: Enhanced Security**
- **Why:** Asymmetric cryptography (ECDSA, RSA) protects ownership; hash functions ensure integrity.
- **How:** Private keys sign transactions; SHA-256/Keccak-256 generate block identifiers.
- **Engineering Utility:** Identity management, secure IoT communication.

#### **Benefit 5: Cost & Time Efficiency (Disintermediation)**
- **Why:** Removes intermediaries (banks, notaries, clearinghouses).
- **How:** Peer-to-peer settlement via smart contracts.
- **Engineering Utility:** Cross-border remittances, micro-payments.

### 2.2 The Six Critical Limitations

#### **Limitation 1: Scalability (The Blockchain Trilemma)**
- **Why:** To increase decentralization and security, throughput is sacrificed.
- **How:** Block size and block time create a hard ceiling on TPS.
- **Engineering Utility (Why It Matters):** A KTU favorite — explain via the formula below.

#### **Limitation 2: Energy Consumption**
- **Why:** PoW requires massive computational effort to solve cryptographic puzzles.
- **How:** Miners race to find a nonce satisfying `H(nonce ∥ block) < target`.
- **Engineering Utility:** Environmental impact analysis.

#### **Limitation 3: Storage Bloat**
- **Why:** Full nodes must store the entire ledger.
- **How:** Bitcoin ledger currently exceeds **500 GB**; Ethereum exceeds **1 TB** (with archival nodes).
- **Engineering Utility:** Node onboarding cost.

#### **Limitation 4: Irreversibility (No Refund Button)**
- **Why:** Transactions cannot be reversed without consensus.
- **How:** Code is law; mistakes are permanent.
- **Engineering Utility:** Loss prevention UX design.

#### **Limitation 5: Regulatory & Legal Uncertainty**
- **Why:** Jurisdictions disagree on whether crypto is property, currency, or security.
- **How:** No universal KYC/AML framework for decentralized systems.
- **Engineering Utility:** Compliance architecture.

#### **Limitation 6: Complexity & Lack of Standardization**
- **Why:** Multiple competing protocols (Ethereum, Hyperledger, Corda, Solana) prevent interoperability.
- **How:** No universal standard for smart contract languages, consensus, or token models.
- **Engineering Utility:** Enterprise integration cost.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE and Internal exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Units | Boundary / Reference |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Block Time (avg)** | $T_{block} = \dfrac{\text{Difficulty Target}}{\text{Network Hashrate}}$ | seconds | Bitcoin: ~600 s |
| 2 | **Throughput (TPS)** | $TPS = \dfrac{\text{Block Size (bytes)}}{\text{Avg Tx Size (bytes)} \times T_{block}}$ | tx/sec | Bitcoin ≈ 7 TPS |
| 3 | **Mining Probability** | $P_{mine} = \dfrac{\text{Hashrate of Node}}{\text{Total Network Hashrate}}$ | dimensionless | 0 ≤ P ≤ 1 |
| 4 | **Cryptographic Hash** | $h = H(m)$ where $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ | bits | SHA-256 output = 256 bits |
| 5 | **Energy Cost (PoW)** | $E_{block} = P_{rig} \times T_{block} \times n_{miners}$ | kWh | Bitcoin: ~**150 TWh/year** |
| 6 | **51% Attack Cost** | $C_{attack} > 0.51 \times \text{Total Hashrate} \times \text{Time}$ | USD | Defends decentralization |
| 7 | **Block Reward Halving** | $R_n = \dfrac{R_0}{2^{\lfloor n/210000 \rfloor}}$ | BTC | Halves every 210,000 blocks |
| 8 | **Merkle Root** | $R = H(H(T_1 \Vert T_2) \Vert H(T_3 \Vert T_4))$ | bits | Logarithmic verification |

**Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> The TPS formula is the most weaponized equation in KTU valuation. An examiner can instantly verify whether a student understands *why* Bitcoin is slow by plugging in:
> $$\text{Block Size} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}, \quad \text{Avg Tx} = 250 \text{ bytes}, \quad T_{block} = 600 \text{ s}$$
> $$\Rightarrow TPS = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{250 \times 600} \approx 6.67 \text{ TPS}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section mathematically proves the core limitation claims and constructs a formal decision matrix for KTU answers.

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The TPS Bottleneck (Scalability Limitation)

**Given:**
- Block size limit: $B = 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes (Bitcoin)
- Average transaction size: $S_{tx} = 250$ bytes
- Average block time: $T_{block} = 600$ seconds

**Step 1 — Number of transactions per block:**
$$N_{tx} = \dfrac{B}{S_{tx}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{250} = 4{,}000 \text{ transactions/block}$$

**Step 2 — Transactions per second:**
$$TPS = \dfrac{N_{tx}}{T_{block}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{600} \approx 6.67 \text{ TPS}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Visa:**
$$\dfrac{TPS_{Visa}}{TPS_{BTC}} = \dfrac{65{,}000}{6.67} \approx 9{,}745\times$$

**Conclusion:** Bitcoin processes transactions roughly **9,745 times slower** than Visa. This is the **scalability limitation**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: 51% Attack Threshold (Security Trade-off)

**Given:** Total network hashrate $H_{total}$. A malicious entity controls $H_{mal}$.

**Step 1 — Probability of producing the next block:**
$$P_{honest} = \dfrac{H_{total} - H_{mal}}{H_{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of attacker outpacing the chain for $z$ blocks:**
$$P_{attack}(z) = \left(\dfrac{H_{mal}}{H_{total} - H_{mal}}\right)^z$$

**Step 3 — When $H_{mal} > 0.5 \times H_{total}$:**
$$P_{attack} \rightarrow 1 \text{ as } z \rightarrow \infty$$

**Conclusion:** Once an entity controls >50% of the hashrate, immutability collapses. This is the **security limitation** of small networks.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Benefits vs. Limitations (High-Yield for KTU)

| **Dimension** | **Benefit** | **Counter-Limitation** | **Trade-off Resolution** |
|---------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Architecture | Decentralization | Slow consensus at scale | Sharding, Layer-2 (Lightning, Rollups) |
| Data Integrity | Immutability | Irreversible user errors | Multi-sig, time-locked refunds |
| Security | Cryptographic hashing | 51% attack on small chains | PoS, permissioned consensus |
| Transparency | Public verifiability | Privacy leakage | Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZK-SNARKs) |
| Cost | No intermediaries | High energy for PoW | Migration to PoS (Ethereum Merge) |
| Storage | Full replication | Storage bloat | Pruning, archival nodes, IPFS |
| Trust | Trustless system | Smart contract bugs | Formal verification, audits |
| Regulation | Borderless | Legal uncertainty | Hybrid consortium chains |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The "**Trade-off Resolution**" column is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer. Always pair a benefit with its limitation and propose a solution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Benefit-Limitation Map (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Blockchain System] --> B[Benefits]
    A --> C[Limitations]

    B --> B1[Decentralization]
    B --> B2[Immutability]
    B --> B3[Transparency]
    B --> B4[Security]
    B --> B5[Disintermediation]

    C --> C1[Scalability TPS]
    C --> C2[Energy Cost]
    C --> C3[Storage Bloat]
    C --> C4[Irreversibility]
    C --> C5[Regulatory Risk]

    B1 -.mitigated by.-> S1[Sharding Layer2]
    B2 -.mitigated by.-> S2[Multi-Sig Wallets]
    B3 -.mitigated by.-> S3[ZK Proofs]
    B4 -.mitigated by.-> S4[PoS Consensus]
    B5 -.mitigated by.-> S5[Smart Contracts]

    C1 --> R1[Solution Rollups]
    C2 --> R2[Solution PoS]
    C3 --> R3[Solution Pruning]
    C4 --> R4[Solution Escrow]
    C5 --> R5[Solution Compliance Chains]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#d62728,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style S1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 The Blockchain Trilemma (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TRILEMMA[Blockchain Trilemma]
        DEC[Decentralization]
        SEC[Security]
        SCA[Scalability]
    end

    DEC --- SEC
    SEC --- SCA
    DEC --- SCA

    BTC[Bitcoin: High DEC + High SEC + Low SCA]
    ETH[Ethereum: High DEC + High SEC + Medium SCA]
    SOL[Solana: Medium DEC + High SEC + High SCA]
    HL[Hyperledger: Low DEC + High SEC + High SCA]

    BTC --> TRILEMMA
    ETH --> TRILEMMA
    SOL --> TRILEMMA
    HL --> TRILEMMA

    style DEC fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style SEC fill:#17becf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style SCA fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:22px,color:#fff
    style TRILEMMA fill:#e377c2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Trade-off Resolution Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Blockchain Application] --> Q1{Need Privacy?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| ZKP[Use ZK-SNARKs]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need Speed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| L2[Use Layer 2 Rollups]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need Energy Efficiency?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| POS[Migrate to PoS]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need Compliance?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| CC[Use Consortium Chain]
    Q4 -->|No| PUB[Use Public PoW Chain]

    ZKP --> OUT[Optimized Deployment]
    L2 --> OUT
    POS --> OUT
    CC --> OUT
    PUB --> OUT

    style IN fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style OUT fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q4 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** List any three limitations of blockchain technology.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Scalability:** Limited TPS (Bitcoin ≈ 7 TPS) due to block size and block time constraints.
> 2. **High Energy Consumption:** PoW consensus requires enormous electricity (~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin).
> 3. **Storage Bloat:** Full nodes must retain the entire ledger, currently exceeding 500 GB for Bitcoin.
> *(Acceptable alternatives: irreversibility, regulatory uncertainty, 51% attack vulnerability, lack of interoperability.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Define *immutability* as a benefit of blockchain.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Immutability** is the property that once a transaction is validated and added to a block, it cannot be altered or deleted without invalidating all subsequent blocks. This is achieved cryptographically by including the **previous block's hash** in the current block's header. Any tampering would change the hash and be immediately rejected by all network nodes. Immutability ensures **auditability, trust, and tamper-evidence** in applications such as land registries, supply chains, and legal contracts.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Benefits-Focused*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the five core benefits of blockchain technology with real-world examples for each.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With the help of the TPS formula, prove mathematically why blockchain scalability is limited. Compare Bitcoin's TPS with a centralized payment system.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Limitations-Focused*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the six major limitations of blockchain technology, citing technical reasons for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Blockchain Trilemma** with a neat diagram. Discuss how Layer-2 solutions (e.g., Lightning Network, Rollups) attempt to resolve the scalability trade-off.

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Five Core Benefits:**

| # | Benefit | Mechanism | Real-World Example |
|---|---------|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Decentralization** | Distributed ledger across nodes | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| 2 | **Immutability** | Cryptographic chaining via SHA-256 | Land registry in Sweden |
| 3 | **Transparency** | Public ledger visibility | Walmart food traceability |
| 4 | **Security** | ECDSA signatures, hash functions | Hardware crypto wallets |
| 5 | **Disintermediation** | Smart contracts replace middlemen | DeFi lending (Aave, Compound) |

**(b) Mathematical Proof of Scalability Limitation:**

**Step 1 — State the TPS formula:**
$$TPS = \dfrac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Avg. Transaction Size} \times \text{Block Time}}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in Bitcoin parameters:**
$$TPS = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}}{250 \text{ bytes} \times 600 \text{ s}} \approx 6.67 \text{ TPS}$$

**Step 3 — Plug in Visa parameters:**
$$TPS_{Visa} = \dfrac{\text{Throughput}}{1 \text{ s}} \approx 65{,}000 \text{ TPS}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the gap:**
$$\dfrac{65{,}000}{6.67} \approx 9{,}745\times \text{ slower}$$

**Conclusion:** Block size and block time create a hard ceiling. Bitcoin is **~9,745× slower** than Visa, proving scalability is a fundamental limitation.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Six Major Limitations:**

1. **Scalability:** TPS bottleneck (proven above).
2. **Energy Consumption:** PoW mining consumes ~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin, comparable to the annual electricity use of Poland.
3. **Storage Bloat:** Bitcoin full ledger > 500 GB; Ethereum archival > 1 TB.
4. **Irreversibility:** No "undo" for mistaken transactions; lost private keys = lost funds.
5. **Regulatory Uncertainty:** Inconsistent legal status across jurisdictions; no universal KYC/AML standard.
6. **Complexity & Interoperability:** Multiple protocols, lack of standardization, smart contract bugs (e.g., DAO hack 2016).

**(b) Blockchain Trilemma:**

The **Blockchain Trilemma**, coined by Vitalik Buterin, states that a blockchain can simultaneously achieve only **two of the three** properties: **Decentralization, Security, and Scalability**.

| System | Decentralization | Security | Scalability |
|--------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| Bitcoin | High | High | Low |
| Solana | Low | High | High |
| Hyperledger | Low | High | High |
| Ethereum (L1) | High | High | Medium |

**Layer-2 Resolution:**
- **Lightning Network (Bitcoin):** Off-chain payment channels settle on-chain only at opening/closing, boosting TPS to millions.
- **Rollups (Ethereum):** Bundle hundreds of transactions off-chain, submit a single proof on-chain (Optimistic or ZK Rollups), increasing TPS by 10–100×.
- **Sidechains:** Independent chains pegged to the main chain (e.g., Polygon for Ethereum).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** list benefits/limitations without a technical reason. *"Blockchain is slow"* gets 0.5 marks; *"Blockchain is slow because block size = 1 MB and block time = 600 s gives TPS ≈ 7"* gets full marks.
> 2. **Do NOT** confuse PoW limitations with general blockchain limitations. The energy issue is specific to PoW; PoS does not have it.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget the trilemma — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 ESE paper.
> 4. **Always** pair a benefit with its limitation in 14-mark answers. Marks are awarded for *trade-off analysis*, not just listing.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final 60-Second Recall Before Exam):**

- ✅ **Benefits (5):** Decentralization, Immutability, Transparency, Security, Disintermediation.
- ✅ **Limitations (6):** Scalability, Energy, Storage, Irreversibility, Regulatory, Complexity.
- ✅ **Key TPS Values:** Bitcoin ≈ 7 TPS | Ethereum ≈ 30 TPS | Visa ≈ 65,000 TPS.
- ✅ **Block Times:** Bitcoin = 10 min | Ethereum = 12 sec.
- ✅ **Hash Functions:** Bitcoin = **SHA-256** | Ethereum = **Keccak-256**.
- ✅ **TPS Formula:** $TPS = \dfrac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Avg. Tx Size} \times \text{Block Time}}$
- ✅ **51% Attack Condition:** $H_{mal} > 0.5 \times H_{total}$.
- ✅ **Trilemma:** Can achieve only 2 of {Decentralization, Security, Scalability}.
- ✅ **Layer-2 Solutions:** Lightning (Bitcoin), Rollups, Sidechains (Ethereum).
- ✅ **Energy Benchmark:** Bitcoin ≈ **150 TWh/year**.
- ✅ **Storage Benchmark:** Bitcoin ledger > **500 GB**.
- ✅ **Examiner Loves:** Pairing a benefit with its limitation and proposing a solution (Layer-2, ZK, PoS).
- ✅ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Listing without reasoning, confusing PoW issues with general blockchain issues.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consensus - Definition and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Consensus - Definition and Types # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **Consensus** in a distributed blockchain network is a fault-tolerant agreement protocol that enables a set of geographically dispersed, mutually distrusting nodes to collectively validate transactions, agree on the order in which they are appended to the distributed ledger, and maintain a single, immutable, canonical state of the system — even in the presence of malicious (Byzantine) actors and network failures. In simpler KTU-board terminology: **Consensus = a rulebook + a referee** that allows thousands of strangers on the internet to update a shared database (the ledger) without needing a central authority like a bank or Google. ### 1.2 The Problem Consensus Solves (The Byzantine Generals Problem) The famous **Byzantine Generals Problem** (formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease, 1982) describes the situation where several divisions of an army surround a city. They must coordinate an attack (or retreat) using only messengers, but some generals may be traitors who send conflicting messages. The loyal generals must reach agreement despite the traitors. In blockchain, the "generals" are the **nodes**, the "city" is the **next block**, and the "traitors" are **malicious nodes or attackers**. | Real-World Analogy | Blockchain Equivalent | |---|---| | Group of friends splitting a bill | Network nodes verifying a transaction | | One friend tries to cheat by claiming they paid more | Malicious node sending false transactions | | Everyone votes on correct amount | Consensus mechanism (voting/validation) | | Majority decision is final | Byz. fault-tolerant agreement reached | > [!NOTE] Why It Matters > Without consensus, a blockchain would just be a slow, redundant database. Consensus is the **engine of trustlessness** — it is the reason Bitcoin works without a central bank. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) - **Validator / Miner:** The node that proposes or validates the next block. - **Block Reward:** The crypto-economic incentive paid to the successful validator. - **Finality:** The point at which a block is considered irreversible. - **Fork:** A divergence in chain history (can be accidental or malicious). - **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT):** Ability of a system to reach consensus even if some nodes fail or act maliciously (typically up to **< 33.3%** malicious nodes). - **Sybil Attack Resistance:** Defense against an attacker creating many fake identities. - **Sybil Attack:** An attack where one adversary controls many pseudo-identities to subvert the network. - **51% Attack:** When a single entity controls more than half the hashing power (PoW) or stake (PoS). - **Probabilistic Finality:** Confidence in a block grows as more blocks are added on top of it (Bitcoin uses this). - **Absolute / Deterministic Finality:** Once agreed, the block is mathematically irreversible (PBFT, Tendermint). ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Consensus Analysis - **Throughput (TPS):** Transactions per second. Bitcoin ≈ **7 TPS**, Ethereum PoS ≈ **~30 TPS**, Solana (PoH) ≈ **65,000 TPS**. - **Latency:** Time from transaction submission to confirmation. - **Energy per Transaction:** Bitcoin PoW ≈ **1,173 kWh/tx**; PoS ≈ **0.03 kWh/tx**. - **Decentralization Index (Nakamoto Coefficient):** Minimum number of entities needed to halt the network. - **Tolerance Threshold (f):** Maximum number of faulty nodes the system tolerates out of `n` total nodes. For BFT systems: `n ≥ 3f + 1`. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Core Properties a Consensus Mechanism Must Guarantee 1. **Safety (Agreement):** All honest nodes agree on the same value/chain. No two honest nodes finalize different blocks at the same height. 2. **Liveness (Progress):** The system keeps producing new blocks; honest transactions are eventually included. 3. **Fault Tolerance:** The system works despite nodes crashing, going offline, or acting maliciously (Byzantine behavior). > [!IMPORTANT] CAP Theorem Trade-off > A distributed system can simultaneously guarantee only **two** out of three: **Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.** Consensus mechanisms make deliberate trade-offs here. ## 2.2 Classification of Consensus Types (KTU Board Favorite) ``` CONSENSUS MECHANISMS | ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐ │ │ │ PROBABILISTIC DETERMINISTIC HYBRID (Finality grows) (Instant finality) (Mixed) │ │ │ PoW, PoC PBFT, Tendermint PoH + PoS ``` ### A. Proof of Work (PoW) - **Used by:** Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin (historically Ethereum). - **Idea:** Miners solve a computationally hard puzzle (find a nonce `N` such that `H(block || N) < target`). - **Incentive:** First to solve gets block reward + transaction fees. - **Security:** Attacker needs **> 50%** of total network hash power. - **Drawback:** Massive electricity consumption; low TPS. ### B. Proof of Stake (PoS) - **Used by:** Ethereum 2.0 (The Merge), Cardano (Ouroboros), Polkadot (NPoS). - **Idea:** Validators are chosen to propose blocks proportional to the number of coins they "stake" (lock up). - **Incentive:** Earn staking rewards; lose stake (slashing) if they act dishonestly. - **Security:** Attacker needs **> 50%** of total staked coins. - **Advantage:** Energy-efficient; faster finality. ### C. Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) - **Used by:** EOS, Tron, BitShares. - **Idea:** Coin holders **vote** to elect a small fixed set of delegates (witnesses/block producers). - **Advantage:** Very high throughput (thousands of TPS). - **Drawback:** More centralized — only 21–101 producers. ### D. Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) - **Used by:** Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint, Zilliqa. - **Idea:** Three-phase protocol: **Pre-Prepare → Prepare → Commit.** Tolerates `f` faulty nodes out of `3f + 1`. - **Advantage:** Deterministic finality; no mining. - **Drawback:** Communication-heavy `O(n²)`; not scalable to thousands of public nodes. ### E. Proof of Authority (PoA) - **Used by:** VeChain, private Ethereum networks. - **Idea:** Approved, identity-verified authorities validate blocks. - **Advantage:** Extremely fast and efficient. - **Drawback:** Fully centralized; suited only for permissioned/consortium chains. ### F. Proof of Burn (PoB) - **Used by:** Slimcoin, Counterparty. - **Idea:** Miners "burn" coins (send to an unspendable address) to earn the right to mine. ### G. Proof of Capacity / Proof of Space (PoC) - **Used by:** Chia, Burstcoin. - **Idea:** Miners allocate hard-disk space (plotting) to store cryptographic solutions. ### H. Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET) - **Used by:** Hyperledger Sawtooth (Intel SGX). - **Idea:** Trusted hardware picks a random wait time; the node that "wakes up" first wins the block. ### I. Proof of History (PoH) - **Used by:** Solana. - **Idea:** Cryptographic clock that proves the passage of time between events; pairs with PoS for ordering. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Meaning / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H(\text{Block Header} \,\|\, N) < T$ | $H$=hash, $N$=nonce, $T$=target | PoW mining puzzle | | 2 | $\text{Difficulty} = \frac{D_{\max}}{T}$ | $D_{\max}$ = max target | Bitcoin difficulty metric | | 3 | $E_{\text{block}} = \text{Tx fees} + \text{Block subsidy}$ | Subsidy halves every 210,000 blocks | Miner reward (BTC) | | 4 | $\text{Nakamoto Coeff.} = \min(\text{entities needed to halt})$ | Integer | Decentralization metric | | 5 | $P(\text{selected}) = \frac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}$ | $s_i$ = stake of node $i$ | PoS validator selection probability | | 6 | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | $n$ total, $f$ faulty | BFT safety condition | | 7 | $P(\text{attack success after } k \text{ blocks}) = (q/p)^k$ | $p$ honest, $q$ attacker hash | PoW double-spend probability | | 8 | $\text{TPS} = \frac{\text{Block size}}{\text{Block time} \times \text{Avg. tx size}}$ | | Throughput formula | | 9 | $\text{Finality time} \approx 6 \times 10 \text{ min} = 60 \text{ min}$ (Bitcoin) | | Probabilistic finality | | 10 | $\text{Slashed amount} = \alpha \cdot s_i$ | $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ | PoS penalty coefficient | > [!NOTE] KTU Pitfall > Students often confuse the Byzantine fault tolerance threshold. The correct bound is **`n ≥ 3f + 1`** (NOT `2f + 1` as some textbooks incorrectly state for the original Byzantine problem with signed messages). For **asynchronous BFT** with **unauthenticated** links, you need `3f + 1`. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Consensus - **Cryptocurrencies:** Decentralized money (Bitcoin, Ethereum). - **Supply Chain:** Hyperledger Fabric (PBFT) — Walmart, Maersk tracking. - **Healthcare:** Patient record sharing across hospitals (PoA). - **Voting / DAOs:** DPoS for high-throughput governance. - **Cross-border Payments:** Ripple / XRP Consensus Ledger. - **IoT:** Lightweight PoA / PoET for device networks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PoW Mining Difficulty Target Bitcoin adjusts its difficulty every **2,016 blocks** (~2 weeks) so that average block time stays at **10 minutes**. Let: - $T_{\text{actual}}$ = time taken for last 2,016 blocks (in seconds) - $T_{\text{expected}}$ = expected time = $2016 \times 600 = 1,209,600$ seconds (10 min × 2016) - $D_{\text{old}}$ = previous difficulty - $D_{\text{new}}$ = new difficulty The Bitcoin protocol updates difficulty as follows: $$ D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \left( \frac{T_{\text{expected}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} \right) $$ **Step-by-step reasoning:** 1. If miners found 2,016 blocks in less than 2 weeks, $T_{\text{actual}} < T_{\text{expected}}$, so the ratio $> 1$ → difficulty **increases**. 2. If miners were too slow, $T_{\text{actual}} > T_{\text{expected}}$, so the ratio $< 1$ → difficulty **decreases**. 3. The target is then computed as: $$ T_{\text{target}} = \frac{T_{\max}}{D_{\text{new}}} $$ where $T_{\max} = 0x00000000FFFF0000\ldots$ is a protocol constant. 4. A 4× cap is applied to prevent extreme jumps: the ratio is clamped to $[1/4, 4]$. **Worked Numerical Example:** Suppose the last 2,016 blocks took 10 days (864,000 s) instead of 14 days. $$ D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{1{,}209{,}600}{864{,}000} = D_{\text{old}} \times 1.4 $$ **Meaning:** Mining became 1.4× harder to slow block production back to 10 minutes. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Probabilistic Finality in PoW (Double-Spend Attack) Consider an attacker with hashing power fraction $q$, and honest network with $p = 1 - q$. The probability that the attacker catches up from a deficit of $z$ blocks is given by the **Binomial/Poisson distribution**: $$ P(\text{catch-up}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q \geq p \\[6pt] \left( \dfrac{q}{p} \right)^{z} & \text{if } q < p \end{cases} $$ **Derivation sketch:** 1. Attacker and honest nodes race to extend two competing chains. 2. The probability attacker advances $k$ blocks while honest nodes advance $k + z$ follows a negative binomial process. 3. Summing the infinite series and applying $q < p$, the closed-form solution collapses to $(q/p)^z$. 4. The number of confirmations needed to make this probability vanishingly small defines "finality." **Example:** For $q = 0.1$ (10% attacker hash power) and $z = 6$ confirmations (Bitcoin default): $$ P = (0.1/0.9)^6 = (0.1111)^6 \approx 1.77 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000177\% $$ So a merchant can wait 6 blocks to be **99.9998% sure** the transaction is safe. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PoS Validator Selection Probability In pure Proof of Stake, the chance a validator is selected to propose the next block is **proportional to their stake**. Let total staked coins be $S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j$. The probability of selecting validator $i$ in any given slot is: $$ P_i = \frac{s_i}{S_{\text{total}}} $$ **Property check:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \frac{\sum s_i}{S_{\text{total}}} = 1$ ✓ (valid probability distribution) **Example:** Suppose 4 validators stake [40, 30, 20, 10] ETH. $$ P_1 = \frac{40}{100} = 0.4, \quad P_2 = 0.3, \quad P_3 = 0.2, \quad P_4 = 0.1 $$ Over 100 slots, expected blocks proposed ≈ [40, 30, 20, 10]. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — BFT Quorum Intersection In PBFT, agreement is reached when a quorum of size $2f + 1$ is reached, where $f$ is the number of Byzantine (faulty) nodes. Why? **Total nodes:** $n = 3f + 1$ **Quorum size:** $Q = 2f + 1$ Two quorums $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ intersect in at least: $$ \vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert = 2Q - n = 2(2f+1) - (3f+1) = f + 1 $$ Since there are only $f$ Byzantine nodes, the intersection of $f + 1$ nodes is guaranteed to contain **at least one honest node**. This honest node propagates the true value, ensuring **consistency (safety)**. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table) | Property | PoW | PoS | DPoS | PBFT | PoA | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Sybil Resistant?** | ✅ (energy) | ✅ (stake) | ✅ (vote weight) | ✅ (identity) | ✅ (identity) | | **Finality Type** | Probabilistic | Probabilistic / Economic | Probabilistic | Deterministic | Deterministic | | **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7–100 | ~100–10,000 | ~1,000–100,000 | ~1,000–10,000 | ~1,000+ | | **Energy Use** | Very High | Low | Very Low | Low | Very Low | | **Decentralization** | High | Medium-High | Low | Low (permissioned) | Very Low | | **Tolerance (f)** | < 50% hash | < 50% stake | < 50% votes | < 33% nodes | Depends on authority | | **Permissioned/Public** | Public | Public | Public | Both | Permissioned | | **Example** | Bitcoin | Ethereum 2.0 | EOS | Hyperledger | VeChain | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Consensus Mechanism Taxonomy ```mermaid graph TD Root["Consensus Mechanisms"] Root --> Prob["Probabilistic Finality"] Root --> Det["Deterministic Finality"] Root --> Hybrid["Hybrid Finality"] Prob --> PoW["Proof of Work
Bitcoin, Litecoin"] Prob --> PoS["Proof of Stake
Ethereum 2.0, Cardano"] Prob --> DPoS["Delegated Proof of Stake
EOS, Tron"] Prob --> PoB["Proof of Burn
Slimcoin"] Prob --> PoC["Proof of Capacity
Chia, Burst"] Det --> PBFT["Practical BFT
Hyperledger Fabric"] Det --> Tend["Tendermint BFT
Cosmos"] Det --> PoA["Proof of Authority
VeChain"] Det --> PoET["Proof of Elapsed Time
Hyperledger Sawtooth"] Hybrid --> PoH["Proof of History + PoS
Solana"] Hybrid --> AuRa["Authority Round
Kovan testnet"] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: PBFT Three-Phase Protocol Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as Client participant L as Leader Primary participant R1 as Replica 1 participant R2 as Replica 2 participant R3 as Replica 3 C->>L: REQUEST L->>R1: PRE-PREPARE L->>R2: PRE-PREPARE L->>R3: PRE-PREPARE R1->>L: PREPARE R1->>R2: PREPARE R1->>R3: PREPARE R2->>L: PREPARE R2->>R1: PREPARE R2->>R3: PREPARE R3->>L: PREPARE R3->>R1: PREPARE R3->>R2: PREPARE Note over L,R3: 2f+1 PREPARE messages received R1->>L: COMMIT R1->>R2: COMMIT R1->>R3: COMMIT R2->>L: COMMIT R2->>R1: COMMIT R2->>R3: COMMIT R3->>L: COMMIT R3->>R1: COMMIT R3->>R2: COMMIT Note over L,R3: 2f+1 COMMIT messages received L->>C: REPLY ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level PoW Mining Architecture ```mermaid graph LR TxPool["Mempool of
Pending Transactions"] TxPool --> BlockDraft["Block Draft Assembly"] PrevBlock["Previous Block Hash"] --> BlockHeader["Block Header"] Merkle["Merkle Root of Tx"] --> BlockHeader Timestamp["Current Timestamp"] --> BlockHeader DifficultyBits["Difficulty Target Bits"] --> BlockHeader BlockHeader --> Mining["Mining Process
Iterate Nonce N"] Nonce["Nonce N"] --> Mining Mining --> Check{HASH < Target?} Check -- No --> Mining Check -- Yes --> Broadcast["Broadcast New Block
to Network"] Broadcast --> Verify["Other Nodes Verify"] Verify --> ChainAppend["Append to Local Ledger"] ChainAppend --> Reward["Pay Block Reward + Fees"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: PoS Epoch and Slot Validation ```mermaid graph TD Epoch["Epoch
e.g. 32 Slots in Ethereum"] Epoch --> S1["Slot 1"] Epoch --> S2["Slot 2"] Epoch --> S3["Slot 3"] Epoch --> Sn["Slot n"] S1 --> VRF["VRF Random Selection
P_i = stake_i / total_stake"] VRF --> V1["Selected Validator Proposes Block"] V1 --> Attest["Other Validators Attest"] Attest --> Justify["Justification Check"] Justify --> Final["Finalized Block
after 2 Epochs"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define consensus in blockchain. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Model Answer (3 marks):** Consensus in blockchain is a fault-tolerant protocol that enables all honest nodes in a distributed, peer-to-peer network to agree on the validity and ordering of transactions, and to maintain a single canonical state of the shared ledger. It eliminates the need for a trusted central authority by ensuring that even in the presence of malicious or faulty nodes (Byzantine faults), all honest participants converge to the same chain. Consensus mechanisms also enforce Sybil resistance and provide economic incentives for honest behavior. Examples include Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), and PBFT. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Byzantine tolerance, 1 mark for example.)* --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Proof of Work and Proof of Stake.
