[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | |---|---|---| | Resource Used | Computational power (electricity) | Coin stake (locked capital) | | Selection | First to solve puzzle wins | Probability proportional to stake | | Attack Cost | 51% of hash power (hardware + electricity) | 51% of total staked coins | | Energy | Very high | Negligible | | Example | Bitcoin | Ethereum 2.0 | *(Examiner's note: Tabular comparison scores full marks; mention attack cost explicitly.)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the **Byzantine Generals Problem**. How does PBFT solve it? Derive the condition $n \geq 3f + 1$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **PoW, PoS, and DPoS** consensus mechanisms in detail with respect to security model, throughput, and decentralization. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Byzantine Generals Problem & PBFT (7 marks) **Byzantine Generals Problem (3 marks):** - Formulated by Lamport, Shostak, Pease in 1982. - A group of generals surrounding a city must collectively decide to **attack or retreat**. - Communication is only by messenger, and some generals may be **traitors** who send conflicting messages. - The goal: all **loyal** generals must agree on the same plan. - In blockchain: generals = nodes, city = next block, traitors = malicious nodes. **How PBFT Solves It (2 marks):** PBFT uses a three-phase protocol — **Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit** — with a designated primary (leader) node: - The primary broadcasts a pre-prepare message. - Replicas broadcast prepare messages. - After collecting $2f+1$ prepare messages, replicas broadcast commit messages. - After collecting $2f+1$ commit messages, the request is executed and a reply sent to the client. - This achieves **deterministic finality** even with up to $f$ Byzantine nodes. **Derivation of $n \geq 3f + 1$ (2 marks):** Two quorums of size $Q = 2f+1$ each in a system of $n$ nodes must intersect: $$ \vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert = 2Q - n = 2(2f+1) - n $$ For safety, the intersection must contain **at least one honest node**, so it must exceed $f$ (the number of Byzantine nodes): $$ 2(2f+1) - n \geq f + 1 $$ $$ 4f + 2 - n \geq f + 1 $$ $$ n \leq 3f + 1 $$ So the **minimum** $n$ that guarantees safety is $n = 3f + 1$. #### Part (b) — Comparison of PoW, PoS, DPoS (7 marks) | Criterion | PoW | PoS | DPoS | |---|---|---|---| | **Selection Mechanism** | Hash power competition | Stake-weighted random selection | Voting by token holders | | **Security Model** | Cost of computation | Cost of stake (slashing) | Reputation + cost of votes | | **Attack Cost** | 51% hash power | 51% total stake | 51% of votes | | **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7 (Bitcoin) | ~30–10,000 | ~1,000–100,000 | | **Decentralization** | High (open mining) | Medium-High | Low (limited delegates) | | **Finality** | Probabilistic (~60 min) | Economic + probabilistic | Probabilistic (~1–3 s) | | **Energy** | Very high | Low | Very low | | **Example** | Bitcoin | Ethereum 2.0 | EOS, Tron | **Conclusion (1 mark):** PoW prioritizes decentralization at the cost of energy and speed; PoS offers a balanced trade-off; DPoS optimizes for throughput at the cost of centralization. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Explain **Proof of Work** consensus in detail. Derive the formula for **probabilistic finality** and compute the probability of a successful double-spend attack with $q = 0.1$ and $z = 6$ confirmations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What is **Proof of Authority (PoA)**? Discuss its advantages, disadvantages, and use cases. How does it differ from **Proof of Burn (PoB)**? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — PoW and Double-Spend Probability (7 marks) **PoW Working (3 marks):** - Miners collect pending transactions into a candidate block. - They repeatedly hash the block header with different nonces $N$ until: $$ H(\text{Block Header} \,\|\, N) < T $$ where $T$ is the difficulty target. - The first miner to find a valid $N$ broadcasts the block; other nodes verify and append it. - Incentive = block subsidy + transaction fees. **Probabilistic Finality Derivation (2 marks):** Let $q$ = attacker hash power, $p = 1-q$ = honest hash power, $z$ = required confirmations. From the negative binomial distribution summing to infinity, the probability the attacker catches up from $z$ blocks behind is: $$ P(\text{attack succeeds}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } q \geq p \\[6pt] \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^{z} & \text{if } q < p \end{cases} $$ **Numerical Computation (2 marks):** Given $q = 0.1$, $p = 0.9$, $z = 6$: $$ P = \left(\frac{0.1}{0.9}\right)^{6} = (0.1111)^{6} $$ **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$ (0.1111)^2 = 0.01234 $$ $$ (0.1111)^3 = 0.001371 $$ $$ (0.1111)^6 = \left((0.1111)^3\right)^2 = (0.001371)^2 \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-6} $$ So $P \approx 0.000188\%$, i.e., the merchant is **99.99981%** safe. #### Part (b) — PoA and PoB (7 marks) **Proof of Authority (PoA) (3 marks):** - Validators are pre-approved, identity-verified authorities (not anonymous). - Each authority takes turns producing blocks at fixed time intervals. - Used in **permissioned/consortium** blockchains. - Examples: VeChain, Kovan testnet, Epirus (private Ethereum). **Advantages:** Very high TPS, very low energy, fast finality, no mining hardware needed. **Disadvantages:** Centralized; trust shifts to authorities; not suitable for public trustless networks. **Use Cases:** Enterprise consortia, supply chains, banking internal ledgers, government records. **Proof of Burn (PoB) (2 marks):** - Miners **destroy** (send to verifiably unspendable address) coins to earn the right to mine. - The more coins burned, the higher the chance of being selected. - Concept: burning coins proves commitment and is itself a cost (anti-Sybil). - Examples: Slimcoin, Counterparty. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Aspect | PoA | PoB | |---|---|---| | Selection Basis | Identity & authority | Coins destroyed | | Decentralization | Very Low | Medium | | Energy | Very Low | Low | | Trust Anchor | Real-world identity | Destroyed wealth | | Public/Private | Permissioned | Public | | Example | VeChain | Slimcoin | --- > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > 1. **Do NOT confuse BFT bounds:** The condition is $n \geq 3f + 1$ for **unauthenticated** Byzantine fault tolerance. Signed-message BFT allows $n \geq f + 1$, but the question is about the standard blockchain PBFT context. > 2. **Probabilistic vs. Deterministic Finality:** Many students write "Bitcoin has finality after 6 blocks" — this is incorrect phrasing. It is **probabilistic**, not absolute. State the residual probability. > 3. **DPoS ≠ PoS:** DPoS uses **voting** to elect delegates; it is not the same as direct PoS. Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused. > 4. **Show derivation steps:** Writing the final formula $(q/p)^z$ alone is worth only 1 mark; show that it comes from the negative binomial/Poisson summation. > 5. **Mention Sybil resistance** explicitly in PoW (computational cost) and PoS (stake cost) answers — it is a frequently missed marking point. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Consensus** = agreement protocol among distributed, distrustful nodes on the state of the ledger. - **Three guarantees:** Safety, Liveness, Fault Tolerance. - **Byzantine Generals Problem** is the foundational problem consensus solves. - **BFT safety condition:** $n \geq 3f + 1$; quorum size = $2f + 1$. - **PoW** uses hashing puzzles; energy-intensive; probabilistic finality; $(q/p)^z$ double-spend formula. - **PoS** uses stake-weighted selection; energy-efficient; slashing for misbehavior. - **DPoS** elects delegates via voting; high TPS; lower decentralization. - **PBFT** gives deterministic finality; 3 phases (Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit). - **PoA** is permissioned with approved validators. - **PoH, PoET, PoB, PoC** are specialized mechanisms. - **CAP Theorem:** Distributed systems trade off Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. - **Throughput (TPS)** varies wildly: Bitcoin ~7, Ethereum PoS ~30, Solana ~65,000, PBFT ~1,000–10,000. - **51% Attack:** The fundamental threat to PoW and PoS systems. - **Finality types:** Probabilistic (PoW, PoS) vs. Deterministic (PBFT, PoA, Tendermint). - **Incentive structures** (block reward, fees, slashing) are the economic engine that makes consensus work in trustless environments. - **Use the right formula for the right question:** Nakamoto Coefficient, validator probability $P_i = s_i / S_{\text{total}}$, and difficulty adjustment ratio $T_{\text{expected}}/T_{\text{actual}}$.

topics.title: Consensus Interaction with Architectural Layers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consensus Interaction with Architectural Layers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Consensus Interaction with Architectural Layers** refers to the systematic, protocol-defined coordination and communication flow that occurs between a blockchain's **consensus mechanism** (the algorithm by which distributed nodes agree on the validity and ordering of transactions) and the **other architectural layers** of the blockchain stack — namely the **Application Layer, Contract/Execution Layer, Network/Propagation Layer, and Data Layer**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this interaction defines how a transaction transitions from a user's request down through the layered stack, gets validated by consensus, gets propagated across the peer-to-peer (P2P) network, and finally gets immutably written into the distributed ledger — and how the reverse signal (blocks, confirmations, forks) flows back upward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Consensus is **not a standalone component** — it is a *cross-cutting concern* that spans all five architectural layers. Understanding this interaction is essential for answering 14-mark KTU ESE questions on blockchain architecture.

### 1.2 The Five Architectural Layers (KTU Reference Model)

A standard blockchain system, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, is decomposed into the following **5 logical layers**:

| Layer # | Layer Name | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Application Layer** | User-facing dApps, wallets, APIs |
| L2 | **Contract / Execution Layer** | Smart contracts, transaction logic, EVM |
| L3 | **Consensus Layer** | Agreement protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT, etc.) |
| L4 | **Network / Propagation Layer** | P2P gossip, block/transaction broadcast |
| L5 | **Data Layer** | Merkle trees, blocks, chain, cryptographic storage |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Parliamentary Bill System"**
>
> Imagine a country where laws (transactions) must be passed before they become history (the blockchain):
>
> 1. A citizen (user) drafts a proposal via an app (Application Layer).
> 2. A legal team translates it into enforceable code (Contract Layer).
> 3. Parliament votes on whether to accept it (Consensus Layer).
> 4. Messengers carry the draft between chambers (Network Layer).
> 5. The approved law is bound into the official record book (Data Layer).
>
> Consensus is the **voting session** — but it cannot function without the messengers (network), the drafted bill (contract), the binding (data), or the citizen's voice (application). The interaction is *holistic*.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Block Time ($\tau_b$)**: Average time to produce a new block. **Bitcoin: 10 min**, **Ethereum (PoS): 12 s**.
- **Finality Time ($\tau_f$)**: Time until a transaction is considered irreversible.
- **Throughput (TPS)**: Transactions per second. **Bitcoin: ~7 TPS**, **Ethereum: ~30 TPS**.
- **Fork Choice Rule**: Determines which chain is canonical (e.g., *Nakamoto consensus* uses **longest chain rule**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Interaction with Consensus

#### 2.1.1 Application Layer ↔ Consensus Layer

- **Downward Flow:** Users submit transactions (TX) via wallets/dApps. These TXs are signed with the user's **private key** and broadcast to the network.
- **Upward Flow:** Once consensus confirms a TX, the application receives a **transaction receipt** (status, block number, gas used).
- **Interaction Type:** *Transaction origination & result feedback.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The Application Layer does **not** directly communicate with consensus. It interacts via the **JSON-RPC / Web3 API** interface, which forwards TXs to the Execution Layer.

#### 2.1.2 Contract/Execution Layer ↔ Consensus Layer

- **Downward Flow:** Smart contracts execute state transitions. The EVM (or equivalent) produces a **state root** ($\sigma_{root}$) representing the new global state.
- **Upward Flow:** Consensus nodes verify that the state transition is valid before voting/accepting the block.
- **Interaction Type:** *State validation & execution verification.*

#### 2.1.3 Consensus Layer ↔ Network/Propagation Layer

- **Downward Flow:** Once a leader (or miner/validator) produces a candidate block, the consensus layer requests the network layer to **gossip/broadcast** it.
- **Upward Flow:** The network layer collects incoming blocks/validations from peers and feeds them back to the consensus layer for **fork resolution**.
- **Interaction Type:** *Block dissemination & vote collection.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** In **PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)**, the consensus layer depends on the network layer to perform a **three-phase broadcast** (pre-prepare, prepare, commit). Without proper network propagation, consensus fails.

#### 2.1.4 Consensus Layer ↔ Data Layer

- **Downward Flow:** Validated blocks are **serialized into the Merkle tree structure** and appended to the chain. Each block header contains a reference to the **previous block hash** ($H_{prev}$).
- **Upward Flow:** The data layer returns the **current chain state**, enabling consensus to verify $H_{prev}$ continuity.
- **Interaction Type:** *Block persistence & chain continuity check.*

### 2.2 Cross-Layer Data Flow Sequence

The standard KTU-recommended flow for a single transaction lifecycle is:

1. **TX Creation** (App Layer) → Signed TX broadcast via RPC.
2. **TX Validation & Execution** (Contract Layer) → EVM runs the code, updates state trie.
3. **TX Pooling** (Network Layer) → Mempool propagates TX to all nodes.
4. **Block Proposal** (Consensus Layer) → Miner/Validator assembles block.
5. **Consensus Voting** (Consensus + Network Layer) → Nodes vote; BFT rounds or PoW hash race.
6. **Block Finalization** (Data Layer) → Block is appended; Merkle root committed.
7. **Confirmation Receipt** (App Layer) → User receives TX hash + block number.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H_b$ | $H_b = \text{SHA-256}(\text{Block Header})$ | Block hash identifier | bits |
| $H_{prev}$ | $H_{prev} = H_{b-1}$ | Hash linking to previous block | bits |
| $M_{root}$ | $M_{root} = \text{MerkleRoot}(TX_1, TX_2, ..., TX_n)$ | Merkle root of transactions | bits |
| $\tau_b$ | $\tau_b = \frac{D_{target}}{H_{rate}}$ | Block time (PoW) | seconds |
| $D_{target}$ | Difficulty target (Bitcoin: adjusts every 2016 blocks) | Mining difficulty | dimensionless |
| $n$ | $n = 3f + 1$ | Min. nodes for BFT (PBFT) | nodes |
| $f$ | Max. Byzantine faulty nodes tolerated | Fault tolerance | nodes |
| $TPS$ | $TPS = \frac{\text{Block Size}}{\tau_b \times \text{TX Size}}$ | Throughput | tx/sec |
| $\tau_f$ | $\tau_f = k \cdot \tau_b$ | Finality (k confirmations) | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In **all** markdown tables above and below, the vertical pipe symbol is represented as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax. For absolute value, write $\vert x \vert$, never $\|x\|$ inside a table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This layered consensus interaction model is the foundation for:
- **Cryptocurrency design** (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana)
- **Enterprise blockchain** (Hyperledger Fabric, Corda)
- **Cross-chain bridges** (Polkadot's relay chain + parachains)
- **Layer-2 scaling** (Rollups, sidechains) — where consensus is delegated but must still interact with the L1 data layer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Block Hash Chaining (Data Layer ↔ Consensus)

The block hash establishes the cryptographic link that consensus mechanisms verify. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the chain validity check.

**Step 1 — Block Header Construction**

Each block $B_i$ contains a header with the following fields:

$$H_i = \text{Header}_i = \{ \text{Version}, H_{prev}, M_{root}, \text{Timestamp}, D_{target}, \text{Nonce} \}$$

**Step 2 — Hash Computation**

The block hash is computed by applying a cryptographic hash function (SHA-256 in Bitcoin) twice:

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i))$$

**Step 3 — Chain Linkage Condition**

For block $B_i$ to be valid, the consensus rule requires:

$$H_{prev} = H_{i-1}$$

**Step 4 — Nakamoto Consensus Validity Function**

A node validates a chain as canonical when:

$$V(\text{Chain}) = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{N} \left[ H_{prev}^{(i)} = H_{i-1} \;\land\; H_i < D_{target} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Explanation of Each Term**

- $H_{prev}^{(i)}$: The "previous block hash" field stored in block $i$'s header.
- $H_{i-1}$: The actual computed hash of block $i-1$.
- $H_i < D_{target}$: The Proof-of-Work condition (for PoW chains).
- $\bigwedge$: Logical AND across all $N$ blocks in the chain.

**Step 6 — Finality Check (Probabilistic, PoW)**

After $k$ confirmations, the probability of a successful attack (reversal) is:

$$P_{attack} = \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \left( 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{r} \lambda^k e^{-\lambda} / k! \right)$$

For large $k$ and attacker hash power $q < 0.5$:

$$P_{attack} \approx 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{r} \binom{r+k-1}{k} (q/p)^k (1-q/p)$$

where $p$ is honest hash power and $q$ is attacker hash power.

### 3.2 Derivation: PBFT Quorum Requirement (Network ↔ Consensus)

**Step 1 — Byzantine Fault Tolerance Constraint**

To tolerate $f$ Byzantine (malicious) nodes out of $n$ total, the system must satisfy:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

**Step 2 — Derivation Explanation**

A BFT system must have:
- At least $f + 1$ honest nodes to outvote $f$ malicious ones (simple majority).
- Plus an additional $f$ to handle the network delays during voting.
- Plus 1 to break ties.

Hence: $n = f + 1 + f = 2f + 1$? **No** — this is for *non-Byzantine* crash faults. For **Byzantine** faults (where malicious nodes can lie), we need overlapping quorums of size $2f + 1$ from two different rounds, giving:

$$n_{total} = (2f+1) + f = 3f + 1$$

**Step 3 — Quorum Size**

Each prepare and commit phase requires a quorum of:

$$Q = 2f + 1$$

**Step 4 — Safety Condition**

A value is committed only when at least $2f + 1$ nodes have sent matching prepare and commit messages.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Consensus × Layer Interaction Summary

| Consensus Type | App Layer | Contract Layer | Network Layer | Data Layer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PoW (Bitcoin)** | TX submission via wallet | Script execution (limited) | Gossip protocol (inv/getdata) | Longest chain rule |
| **PoS (Ethereum 2.0)** | TX via MetaMask | EVM execution | GossipSub (libp2p) | LMD-GHOST + Casper FFG |
| **PBFT (Hyperledger)** | Chaincode invocation | Docker execution | gRPC broadcast | Block validation by orderer |
| **DPoS (EOS)** | TX via dApp | WASM execution | P2P broadcast | 21 producer rotation |
| **PoH (Solana)** | TX via Phantom | Sealevel parallel exec | Turbine (UDP-based) | Verifiable Delay Function |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: PBFT Node Count

**Problem:** A consortium blockchain uses PBFT. If the system must tolerate $f = 3$ Byzantine nodes, determine:
(a) Minimum total nodes $n$
(b) Quorum size $Q$
(c) Number of nodes that can fail without halting the system

**Solution:**

**(a)** Applying the BFT condition:

$$n = 3f + 1 = 3(3) + 1 = 10 \text{ nodes}$$

**(b)** Quorum size for prepare/commit:

$$Q = 2f + 1 = 2(3) + 1 = 7 \text{ nodes}$$

**(c)** The system can tolerate $f = 3$ Byzantine faults (includes both crash and malicious failures).

**Verification:** With 10 nodes, if 3 are Byzantine, the remaining 7 honest nodes form a quorum of exactly $2f+1 = 7$, satisfying the prepare/commit safety condition. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Five-Layer Blockchain Architecture with Consensus Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App1[APPLICATION LAYER\nWallets dApps Web3 APIs]
    Con1[CONTRACT LAYER\nEVM Smart Contracts State Machine]
    Cns1[CONSENSUS LAYER\nPoW PoS PBFT BFT]
    Net1[NETWORK LAYER\nP2P Gossip Libp2p GossipSub]
    Dat1[DATA LAYER\nMerkle Tree Blockchain Cryptography]

    A1[User TX Signed] --> App1
    A2[RPC Call Web3 JSON] --> App1
    App1 --> B1[Transaction Hash]
    B1 --> Con1
    Con1 --> C1[State Root Transition]
    C1 --> Cns1
    Cns1 --> D1[Block Proposal Voting]
    D1 --> Net1
    Net1 --> E1[Block Broadcast Gossip]
    E1 --> Dat1
    Dat1 --> F1[Block Appended to Chain]
    F1 --> G1[Confirmation Receipt TX Hash Block Number]
    G1 --> App1

    H1[Chain State Read] --> Cns1
    H1 --> Dat1
    I1[Mempool Propagation] --> Net1
    J1[Prev Block Hash Check] --> Cns1
    J1 --> Dat1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Detailed PBFT Consensus Flow with Layer Annotations

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client App Layer
    participant Node0 as Primary Node Consensus L3
    participant Node1 as Replica 1 Network L4
    participant Node2 as Replica 2 Network L4
    participant Node3 as Replica 3 Network L4
    participant DataL as Data Layer L5

    Client->>Node0: Send TX Request
    Node0->>Node1: PrePrepare Block B Contract L2 State
    Node0->>Node2: PrePrepare Block B
    Node0->>Node3: PrePrepare Block B
    Node1->>Node0: Prepare Vote
    Node2->>Node0: Prepare Vote
    Node3->>Node0: Prepare Vote
    Node1->>Node2: Prepare Broadcast
    Node1->>Node3: Prepare Broadcast
    Node2->>Node1: Prepare Broadcast
    Node2->>Node3: Prepare Broadcast
    Node3->>Node1: Prepare Broadcast
    Node3->>Node2: Prepare Broadcast
    Note over Node1,Node3: 2f+1 Prepare Quorum Reached
    Node1->>Node0: Commit Vote
    Node2->>Node0: Commit Vote
    Node3->>Node0: Commit Vote
    Note over Node0,Node3: 2f+1 Commit Quorum Reached
    Node0->>DataL: Append Block to Chain
    DataL->>Client: TX Receipt Confirmation
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block Validation Flowchart (Consensus ↔ Data Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Block Received] --> B{Verify H_prev Matches}
    B -->|No| X1[Reject Block]
    B -->|Yes| C{Verify Merkle Root}
    C -->|No| X1
    C -->|Yes| D{Verify Consensus Signature}
    D -->|No| X1
    D -->|Yes| E{Verify TX Validity}
    E -->|No| X1
    E -->|Yes| F[Append to Local Chain]
    F --> G[Broadcast to Peers]
    G --> H[Update UTXO Set or State Trie]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid `flowchart TB` represents the *downward* transaction flow (App → Data) and the *upward* confirmation flow. The `sequenceDiagram` represents the time-ordered PBFT message exchange with layer annotations. The `flowchart LR` depicts the validation gate sequence at the Consensus ↔ Data boundary.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the five architectural layers of a blockchain system and state the primary function of the Consensus Layer.*

**Model Answer:**
The five architectural layers of a blockchain system are:
1. **Application Layer** – User-facing dApps, wallets, APIs.
2. **Contract/Execution Layer** – Smart contracts and EVM execution.
3. **Consensus Layer** – Agreement protocol among distributed nodes.
4. **Network/Propagation Layer** – P2P gossip-based message dissemination.
5. **Data Layer** – Merkle trees, block storage, cryptographic chain.

**Primary function of the Consensus Layer:** To ensure that all honest nodes in the distributed network **agree on a single, valid ordering of transactions** and a **single canonical chain state**, even in the presence of faulty or malicious nodes. Examples include PoW, PoS, PBFT, and DPoS. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "fork choice rule" and explain its role in the Consensus Layer's interaction with the Data Layer.*

**Model Answer:**
A **fork choice rule** is a deterministic algorithm used by nodes to select the *correct chain* among multiple competing forks in the blockchain.

**Role in Consensus ↔ Data Layer interaction:**
When two miners/validators produce blocks at nearly the same time, the data layer stores both branches as a temporary fork. The consensus layer applies the fork choice rule (e.g., **longest chain rule** in Bitcoin, **GHOST rule** in Ethereum) to determine which branch is canonical. The chosen branch is then persisted as the official chain, and the orphaned branch is discarded. This interaction ensures **single, agreed-upon history** across all nodes. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on this topic for the following reasons:
> 1. **Treating consensus as isolated** — Failing to mention its interaction with all 5 layers (loses 3-4 marks).
> 2. **Confusing fork choice rules** — Writing "longest chain" for Ethereum (incorrect; Ethereum uses LMD-GHOST).
> 3. **Missing the role of Merkle trees** — Forgetting that consensus uses Merkle roots to verify TX inclusion efficiently.
> 4. **Skipping network layer dependence** — PBFT cannot function without the 3-phase network broadcast.
> 5. **No diagrams** — KTU examiners award 2-3 marks specifically for well-labeled architectural diagrams.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five architectural layers of a blockchain system**. Describe how the **Consensus Layer interacts with each of the other four layers**, with specific reference to transaction flow and data persistence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Proof of Work (PoW)** and **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** consensus mechanisms with respect to their **interaction with the Network and Data Layers**. Use suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Architectural Layers and Consensus Interaction:**

**1. Application Layer ↔ Consensus:** Users submit signed transactions via wallets/dApps. The application layer packages these TXs and forwards them via JSON-RPC. Consensus receives the validated TXs (after Contract Layer execution) and includes them in candidate blocks. **Interaction type:** TX origination and result feedback.

**2. Contract/Execution Layer ↔ Consensus:** Smart contracts (Solidity, etc.) run on the EVM. The EVM produces a new **state root** ($\sigma_{root}$). Consensus nodes verify that this state transition is valid according to the protocol rules before voting on the block. **Interaction type:** State validation.

**3. Consensus Layer (self):** Implements PoW, PoS, or PBFT. The leader/validator proposes a block; other nodes vote. A block is finalized when the consensus rule (e.g., longest chain, 2f+1 quorum) is satisfied. **(7 marks distributed across these 5 interactions + consensus self-description)**

**(b) PoW vs PBFT — Network & Data Layer Interaction:**

| Aspect | PoW (Bitcoin) | PBFT (Hyperledger) |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Layer** | Uses *gossip protocol* (inv/getdata/headers) for unstructured broadcast | Uses *gRPC* for structured 3-phase broadcast (pre-prepare, prepare, commit) |
| **Network Overhead** | Low coordination; nodes don't need identities | High coordination; requires known validator set |
| **Data Layer Rule** | *Longest chain* — nodes follow the chain with most cumulative work | *BFT finality* — once 2f+1 commits, block is immediately final |
| **Fork Handling** | Probabilistic; forks resolved by accumulated work | Deterministic; no forks in normal operation |
| **Block Propagation Delay** | Tolerates high latency | Sensitive to network latency (timeout issues) |
| **Throughput** | ~7 TPS (limited by 1MB blocks + 10min time) | ~3000+ TPS (with batching) |

**Diagram Requirement:** Draw the 5-layer stack with arrows showing PoW's data layer following longest-chain, and PBFT's consensus layer performing explicit 3-phase network messages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a **detailed block diagram**, explain how a **transaction flows through the five architectural layers** from user submission to final confirmation. Annotate the role of the Consensus Layer at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum node count formula** $n = 3f + 1$ for PBFT consensus. Explain how this formula governs the **Network Layer's quorum requirements** and the **Data Layer's finality condition**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transaction Flow Through 5 Layers:**

*Step 1 (App Layer):* User signs TX with private key → wallet broadcasts via RPC.

*Step 2 (Contract Layer):* TX is parsed; smart contract code executes on EVM → produces state change + logs.

*Step 3 (Network Layer):* TX enters the mempool; gossip protocol propagates to all peers.

*Step 4 (Consensus Layer):* Miner/validator selects TXs from mempool, assembles a candidate block, performs consensus (PoW hash race OR PBFT voting). Block is **validated** by other nodes.

*Step 5 (Data Layer):* Validated block is appended; Merkle root computed; previous block hash linked. New chain state is committed.

*Feedback (Upward):* TX receipt (status, block number) is sent back to the application layer for user notification.

**Role of Consensus at each stage:** Consensus **gates** the transition from Step 3 → Step 4 → Step 5. No block is written to the data layer without consensus approval. **(7 marks — including the block diagram)**

**(b) Derivation of $n = 3f + 1$:**

**Premise:** A distributed system with $n$ nodes can tolerate $f$ Byzantine (malicious) faults.

**Quorum Requirement:** Every decision requires agreement from at least $2f + 1$ honest nodes to outvote $f$ malicious ones.

**Safety Argument:** Consider two different rounds of consensus. Each round needs a quorum of $2f + 1$ nodes. To prevent conflicting decisions, the quorums of two rounds must intersect in at least one honest node:

$$Q_1 \cap Q_2 \geq 1 \text{ honest node}$$

Since $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ can each contain up to $f$ Byzantine nodes:

$$Q_1 + Q_2 - n \geq f + 1$$
$$2(2f + 1) - n \geq f + 1$$
$$4f + 2 - n \geq f + 1$$
$$n \leq 3f + 1$$

Therefore, the **minimum** total nodes is:

$$\boxed{n = 3f + 1}$$

**Network Layer Quorum:** Each prepare and commit phase requires $Q = 2f + 1$ matching messages. The network layer must successfully deliver these messages to satisfy the quorum.

**Data Layer Finality:** Once $2f + 1$ commit messages are received, the block is **deterministically final** — no fork is possible, because at most $f$ nodes can be malicious, and $2f+1 > f$ ensures no conflicting block can gather sufficient votes. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Blockchain has **5 layers**: Application, Contract, Consensus, Network, Data.
- ✅ Consensus is a **cross-cutting concern** — not a standalone component.
- ✅ **PoW** uses longest chain rule; **PoS (Ethereum)** uses **LMD-GHOST + Casper FFG**.
- ✅ **PBFT** requires $n \geq 3f + 1$ nodes; quorum $Q = 2f + 1$.
- ✅ Block hash: $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_i))$.
- ✅ Chain validity: $H_{prev}^{(i)} = H_{i-1}$ must hold for all blocks.
- ✅ Merkle root ($M_{root}$) enables **efficient TX inclusion proof** — consensus uses it.
- ✅ **Block time** ($\tau_b$) and **Finality time** ($\tau_f$) are the two key timing metrics.
- ✅ **Fork choice rule** lives at the **Consensus ↔ Data Layer** boundary.
- ✅ PBFT's 3-phase broadcast (pre-prepare → prepare → commit) requires the **Network Layer**.
- ✅ Bitcoin TPS ≈ 7, Ethereum PoS TPS ≈ 30, Hyperledger PBFT TPS ≈ 3000+.
- ✅ The **state root** $\sigma_{root}$ is the contract layer's output, verified by consensus.
- ✅ **Mempool** lives in the Network Layer; it feeds TXs to the Consensus Layer.
- ✅ **Deterministic finality** (PBFT) vs **Probabilistic finality** (PoW) — $k$ confirmations rule.
- ✅ KTU ESE questions on this topic **always** expect a labeled architectural diagram (2-3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consensus Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consensus Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consensus Algorithm** is a fault-tolerant mechanism used in distributed blockchain networks to achieve agreement among distributed, untrusting nodes on a single, canonical state of the ledger. It ensures that all honest participants in a peer-to-peer network agree on the validity and ordering of transactions, even in the presence of malicious actors, network delays, or partial system failures.

A consensus algorithm is the **cryptoeconomic heart** of any blockchain. It replaces the trusted central authority of traditional systems (banks, governments) with a deterministic mathematical protocol combined with economic incentives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The course outcome *CO2* explicitly maps consensus algorithms to the analysis of distributed agreement, Byzantine fault tolerance, and the security-performance trade-offs of modern distributed ledgers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine **ten friends in a WhatsApp group** deciding where to eat dinner:

- **No one is the leader.**
- Some friends may lie about their preferences.
- Some messages may be delayed or lost.
- They must all **independently arrive at the SAME restaurant** without a central coordinator.

A **consensus algorithm** is the rulebook they agree upon beforehand:
- Who proposes restaurants?
- How is a restaurant "voted" in?
- What happens if a friend is malicious or offline?

In blockchain terms, the "friends" are **nodes**, the "dinner" is the **next block**, and the "rulebook" is the **consensus protocol**.

### 1.3 The Three Sacred Properties of Consensus

| Property | Meaning in Blockchain | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Safety** | All honest nodes agree on the same chain (no forks). | Prevents **double-spending** and history rewriting. |
| **Liveness** | The chain keeps producing new valid blocks. | Ensures transactions are eventually confirmed. |
| **Fault Tolerance** | The system survives crashes or malicious nodes. | Quantified by the fraction of faulty nodes tolerated (e.g., $f$ Byzantine nodes out of $n = 3f + 1$). |

### 1.4 The CAP Theorem — A Foundational Constraint

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):** In any distributed data store, you can guarantee at most **two of the three** properties simultaneously — **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition tolerance (P)**. Since network partitions are inevitable in real distributed systems, every blockchain must consciously choose between **strong consistency + reduced availability** (BFT-style chains) or **high availability + eventual consistency** (PoW-style chains like Bitcoin).

### 1.5 The Byzantine Generals Problem (Foundational Motivation)

The **Byzantine Generals Problem**, formalised by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in **1982**, is the canonical thought experiment that motivates consensus research.

**The Problem:** Several divisions of the Byzantine army surround an enemy city. The generals must agree on a common plan of action (attack or retreat) by exchanging messages via messengers. However, **some generals may be traitors** who send conflicting messages. The loyal generals must still reach a unanimous correct decision.

**The Core Result:** The problem is unsolvable with fewer than $3f + 1$ total generals if $f$ are traitors when using only oral messages. With signed (cryptographic) messages, the bound improves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bitcoin's Whitepaper Insight (Nakamoto, 2008):** Satoshi Nakamoto solved the Byzantine Generals Problem **probabilistically** by combining Proof of Work with the longest-chain rule and economic incentives (block rewards + transaction fees). Consensus is achieved not by voting, but by **computational expenditure and economic cost**.

### 1.6 Classification of Consensus Algorithms (High-Level)

- **Probabilistic Finality (PoW family):** Blocks become more final as more confirmations are added.
- **Absolute Finality (BFT family):** Once committed, a block is mathematically irrevocable.
- **Leader-Based (Raft, PBFT, PoS variants):** A single (rotating) node drives consensus.
- **Leaderless (PoW mining race):** Any miner can propose the next block.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Failure Model Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern consensus protocols are designed against one of two failure models, and this single choice dictates the entire protocol structure.

| Failure Model | Failure Type | Examples | Tolerates |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT)** | Nodes halt and stop responding. | Raft, Paxos | Up to $\lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ crashes. |
| **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** | Nodes behave arbitrarily (lie, equivocate, collude). | PBFT, PoW, PoS | Up to $\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ byzantine nodes. |

**Why the 1/3 bound?** For a deterministic protocol to guarantee agreement with $f$ Byzantine nodes among $n$ total nodes, the **honest majority** must be able to outvote the traitors. In the worst case, the traitors coordinate to produce the maximum possible conflicting votes. Mathematically, this requires $n - f > f + f$, which simplifies to $n > 3f$, hence $n \geq 3f + 1$.

### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' of Each Major Family

#### 2.2.1 Proof of Work (PoW) — Used by Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin

- **Why:** Converting digital votes into physical (computational) cost makes Sybil attacks economically infeasible.
- **How:**
  1. Miners collect pending transactions into a candidate block.
  2. They repeatedly hash the block header with a changing **nonce** until the hash falls below a target value.
  3. The first miner to find a valid nonce broadcasts the block; the network verifies it in **O(1)** time per node.
  4. The chain with the **most cumulative work** is the canonical chain (Nakamoto consensus).
- **Consensus Rule:** $H(\text{nonce} \parallel \text{prev\_hash} \parallel \text{merkle\_root} \parallel \dots) < T$, where $T$ is the difficulty target.
- **Probabilistic Finality:** The probability of a successful double-spend attack after $k$ confirmations decays exponentially as $(q/z)^k$, where $q$ is attacker hash power and $z$ is network hash power.

#### 2.2.2 Proof of Stake (PoS) — Used by Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano

- **Why:** Replaces energy waste with **capital lockup**. To attack, you must own and risk the network's native asset.
- **How:**
  1. Validators **stake** (lock) native tokens as collateral.
  2. A pseudo-random selection algorithm picks a validator to propose the next block, weighted by stake size.
  3. Other validators **attest** to the validity of the proposed block.
  4. Misbehaviour triggers **slashing** — the validator loses a portion of their stake.
- **Consensus Rule:** Selection probability $P_i = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}$, where $s_i$ is validator $i$'s stake.
- **Finality:** Modern PoS chains (Ethereum) use **Casper FFG** to overlay BFT-style finality on the PoS chain.

#### 2.2.3 Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — Used by EOS, Tron, BitShares

- **Why:** Achieve high throughput by limiting the validator set to a small, elected group.
- **How:**
  1. Token holders **vote** to elect a fixed number of *block producers* (witnesses/delegate nodes).
  2. Elected producers take turns producing blocks in a deterministic round-robin.
  3. Misbehaving producers are voted out in the next epoch.
- **Trade-off:** Higher speed and efficiency, but more **centralised** and prone to cartel formation.

#### 2.2.4 Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) — Used by Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint

- **Why:** Provide deterministic finality in permissioned (known validator) networks.
- **How:** Three-phase voting protocol:
  1. **Pre-Prepare:** The leader broadcasts a proposed block order.
  2. **Prepare:** Validators broadcast the prepared message; each waits for $2f$ matching prepares.
  3. **Commit:** Validators broadcast the commit message; each waits for $2f + 1$ matching commits before executing the block.
- **Safety Bound:** Tolerates up to $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ Byzantine validators.
- **Scalability:** Communication complexity is $O(n^2)$, limiting practical deployments to $\leq 100$ validators.

#### 2.2.5 Proof of Authority (PoA) — Used by VeChain, POA Network, xDai

- **Why:** Consortium blockchains where validators are **pre-approved, identified entities**.
- **How:** A fixed set of *authority nodes* (e.g., known companies) take turns sealing blocks. Their **reputation**, not stake or work, is the economic bond.
- **Trade-off:** Extremely fast and efficient, but **fully trusted** model — not truly decentralised.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This formula sheet contains every equation a KTU examiner can test in the **ESE (End Semester Examination)** for Module 2.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PoW hash target | $H(\text{nonce} \parallel \text{header}) < T$ | $T$ is a 256-bit integer | Defines the difficulty of the mining puzzle. |
| PoW difficulty | $D = \dfrac{T_{\max}}{T}$ | $D$ is dimensionless (integer) | Higher $D$ = lower $T$ = harder puzzle. |
| PoW expected attempts | $E[N] = \dfrac{2^{256}}{T}$ | Number of hashes | Average mining time = $E[N] \cdot t_{\text{hash}}$. |
| Bitcoin block time | $t_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H}$ | $H$ = network hash rate (H/s) | Target is readjusted every 2016 blocks ($\approx$ 2 weeks). |
| PoS selection probability | $P_i = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_j s_j}$ | $s_i$ = stake of node $i$ | Wealthy validators are chosen more often. |
| BFT safety bound | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | $n$ = total nodes, $f$ = faulty | Minimum quorum: $2f + 1$ honest votes. |
| PBFT quorum | $Q = 2f + 1$ | A subset of validators | Required agreement for commit. |
| Double-spend attack probability | $P_{\text{success}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \binom{\lambda}{k} \cdot p^k \cdot (1-p)^{\lambda - k}$ | $p = q/z$ (attacker ratio) | From Nakamoto's whitepaper, simplified form. |
| PoW block reward (Bitcoin) | $R(t) = 50 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\lfloor t / 210000 \rfloor}$ | $t$ = block height | **Halving event** every 210,000 blocks ($\approx$ 4 years). |
| CAP trade-off metric | $\text{CAP}(S) = \alpha \cdot C + \beta \cdot A + \gamma \cdot P$ | $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ | Conceptual scoring for protocol design. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bitcoin and Ethereum (historical):** PoW underpins the **\$1+ trillion cryptocurrency market**.
- **Enterprise Blockchains (Hyperledger Fabric):** PBFT enables **supply chain traceability** with deterministic finality.
- **Banking Settlement Layers (JPM Coin, Quorum):** PoA / BFT variants enable **central bank digital currencies (CBDCs)**.
- **IoT and Edge Networks (IOTA, Hedera):** Modified consensus (Hashgraph, Tangle) supports **high-throughput micropayments**.
- **AI/ML Model Registries:** Blockchain consensus is used to **provably timestamp and version AI models**, preventing data and model tampering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BFT Safety Bound $n \geq 3f + 1$

**Goal:** Prove that a deterministic consensus protocol can tolerate up to $f$ Byzantine failures only if at least $3f + 1$ total participants exist.

**Step 1 — Define the honest and Byzantine sets.**
Let $n$ be the total number of nodes. Partition into honest set $H$ and Byzantine set $B$, with $\vert H \vert = h$ and $\vert B \vert = f$, so $n = h + f$.

**Step 2 — Safety requirement (agreement).**
For the honest nodes to agree, they must all reach the same decision. In the worst case, the Byzantine nodes can:
- Send a value $v_0$ to one honest subset and value $v_1$ to another.
- Coordinate to maximise the disagreement among honest nodes.

**Step 3 — Quorum intersection requirement.**
The protocol proceeds in rounds, and in each round, a **quorum** $Q$ of nodes votes. For the system to be safe, **any two quorums must intersect in at least one honest node**. Otherwise, a Byzantine node could vote one way in one quorum and the opposite way in another.

Mathematically, for any two quorums $Q_1$ and $Q_2$:

$$
\vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert > n
$$

This is the **quorum intersection property** (also called *quorum overlap*).

**Step 4 — Apply the Byzantine assumption.**
Each quorum of size $q$ can contain at most $f$ Byzantine nodes in the worst case. So the honest part of any quorum is at least $q - f$. For two quorums to share an honest node, we need:

$$
(q - f) + (q - f) > n
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
2q - 2f > n
$$

We also know that a quorum of size $q$ must be reachable even when $f$ nodes are Byzantine, so $q \leq n - f$. The optimal choice is $q = \lceil (n + f) / 2 \rceil$. Substituting:

$$
2 \cdot \frac{n + f}{2} - 2f > n
$$

$$
n + f - 2f > n
$$

$$
-f > 0
$$

This is impossible. To resolve, we require the quorum to be **larger** so that the intersection is guaranteed. The classic BFT result uses $q = 2f + 1$, which gives:

$$
(2f + 1) + (2f + 1) > 3f + 1
$$

$$
4f + 2 > 3f + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f > -1 \;\;\checkmark
$$

This holds for all $f \geq 0$. Therefore, the minimum $n$ is:

$$
n = 3f + 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This derivation is a *favourite KTU question*. You should be able to derive $n \geq 3f + 1$ from quorum intersection in an exam. If $f = 1$ Byzantine node, you need at least $n = 4$ total nodes (so 3 honest). This is why PBFT deployments start at **4 validators**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bitcoin's Difficulty Adjustment

**Goal:** Show how Bitcoin keeps the average block time at $\approx$ 10 minutes despite fluctuating hash power.

**Step 1 — Define the actual and target times.**

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{2016} t_i
$$

where $t_i$ is the time taken to mine the $i$-th block in the current 2016-block epoch.

**Step 2 — Compute the new difficulty.**

$$
D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{target}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{target}} = 2016 \cdot 10 \cdot 60 = 1,209,600$ seconds (two weeks).

**Step 3 — Apply bounds (anti-gaming rule).**
To prevent wild oscillations due to luck, the protocol **clamps** the adjustment to a factor of 4:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = \min\!\left(D_{\text{old}} \cdot 4, \; \max\!\left(\dfrac{D_{\text{old}}}{4}, \; D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{target}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}\right)\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style question):** Suppose the last 2016 blocks took 3 weeks ($T_{\text{actual}} = 1,814,400$ s). The new difficulty becomes:
> $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot (1,209,600 / 1,814,400) \approx 0.667 \cdot D_{\text{old}}$.
> Difficulty **decreases by ~33%** to bring block time back to 10 minutes.

### 3.3 Derivation: Proof of Stake Selection (Algorithmic Pseudocode-as-Logic-Flow)

Although this course is a CS-elective and pseudocode is allowed, we present the **logic as a numbered algorithm** without code syntax (no Python is used per the protocol rules).

**Algorithm: PoS Block Proposer Selection**

1. Let $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ be the active validator set, each with stake $s_i$.
2. Compute the **total stake**: $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i$.
3. Assign to each validator an interval on the real line $[0, S)$:
   $I_i = \left[\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} s_j, \; \sum_{j=1}^{i} s_j\right)$.
4. Generate a **verifiable random function (VRF)** output $r$ using the previous block's seed.
5. Select validator $v_k$ such that $r \mod S \in I_k$.
6. Validator $v_k$ is the proposer for slot $s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why VRF?** A naive approach using only the previous block hash allows the last proposer to **grind** through many candidate hashes to bias the next selection. VRFs (as in Algorand and Ethereum's RANDAO) make the output unpredictable yet verifiable, eliminating the grinding attack.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance Trade-Offs

| Algorithm | Throughput (TPS) | Finality | Energy | Decentralisation | Sybil Resistance | Trust Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PoW (Bitcoin)** | 5–7 | Probabilistic ($\sim$ 1 hr for high confidence) | Very High | Very High | Computational cost | Trustless |
| **PoS (Ethereum)** | 30–60 | Absolute (Casper FFG) | Low | Medium–High | Capital lockup + slashing | Trustless |
| **DPoS (EOS)** | 1,000–4,000 | Absolute (instant) | Very Low | Low (cartel risk) | Delegated voting | Semi-trustless |
| **PBFT (Fabric)** | 1,000–10,000 | Absolute (instant) | Very Low | Low (permissioned) | Identity-based | Permissioned |
| **PoA (VeChain)** | 1,000+ | Absolute (instant) | Very Low | Very Low | Reputation | Trusted authorities |
| **PoH (Solana)** | 50,000+ | Probabilistic | Low | Medium | PoS + clock | Trustless |
| **PoSpace (Chia)** | 50–100 | Probabilistic | Medium | High | Storage cost | Trustless |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: High-Level Classification of Consensus Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Consensus Algorithms] --> B[Probabilistic Finality]
    A --> C[Deterministic Finality]
    A --> D[Hybrid Models]

    B --> B1[Proof of Work - PoW]
    B --> B2[Proof of Burn - PoB]
    B --> B3[Proof of Capacity - PoC]

    C --> C1[Byzantine Fault Tolerant - BFT]
    C --> C2[Crash Fault Tolerant - CFT]
    C --> C3[Proof of Authority - PoA]

    C1 --> C1a[PBFT]
    C1 --> C1b[Tendermint BFT]
    C1 --> C1c[HotStuff]

    C2 --> C2a[Raft]
    C2 --> C2b[Paxos]

    D --> D1[Proof of Stake - PoS]
    D --> D2[Delegated PoS - DPoS]
    D --> D3[Proof of History - PoH]
    D --> D4[Hybrid PoW plus PoS]

    B1 --> B1a[Bitcoin]
    B1 --> B1b[Ethereum Classic]
    D1 --> D1a[Ethereum 2.0]
    D1 --> D1b[Cardano Ouroboros]
    D2 --> D2a[EOS]
    D2 --> D2b[Tron]
    C3 --> C3a[VeChain]
    C3 --> C3b[POA Network]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Proof of Work Mining Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Miner node receives transactions]) --> step1[Bundle valid transactions into mempool]
    step1 --> step2[Construct candidate block header with Merkle root and previous hash]
    step2 --> step3[Set nonce equal to 0]
    step3 --> step4[Compute SHA-256 double hash of header]
    step4 --> decision1{Hash less than target T?}
    decision1 -- No --> step5[Increment nonce by 1]
    step5 --> step4
    decision1 -- Yes --> step6[Broadcast winning block to network]
    step6 --> step7[Other nodes verify block in O of 1 time]
    step7 --> step8[Honest nodes append block to longest chain]
    step8 --> step9[Failed miners switch to mining on top of new block]
    step9 --> endA([Consensus achieved via longest chain rule])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PBFT Three-Phase Consensus Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ClientA as Client
    participant LeadB as Leader Node
    participant RepC as Replica 1
    participant RepD as Replica 2
    participant RepE as Replica 3
    participant RepF as Replica 4

    ClientA ->> LeadB: Send request R
    Note over LeadB: Leader assigns sequence number n
    LeadB ->> RepC: Pre-Prepare PRE-PREPARE n v m
    LeadB ->> RepD: Pre-Prepare PRE-PREPARE n v m
    LeadB ->> RepE: Pre-Prepare PRE-PREPARE n v m
    LeadB ->> RepF: Pre-Prepare PRE-PREPARE n v m

    RepC ->> RepC: Verify pre-prepare
    RepD ->> RepD: Verify pre-prepare
    RepE ->> RepE: Verify pre-prepare
    RepF ->> RepF: Verify pre-prepare

    RepC ->> LeadB: Prepare PREPARE n v m
    RepC ->> RepD: Prepare PREPARE n v m
    RepC ->> RepE: Prepare PREPARE n v m
    RepC ->> RepF: Prepare PREPARE n v m

    RepD ->> LeadB: Prepare PREPARE n v m
    RepE ->> LeadB: Prepare PREPARE n v m
    RepF ->> LeadB: Prepare PREPARE n v m

    Note over RepC,RepF: Wait for 2f matching prepares
    Note over RepC,RepF: Prepared state reached

    RepC ->> LeadB: Commit COMMIT n v m
    RepC ->> RepD: Commit COMMIT n v m
    RepC ->> RepE: Commit COMMIT n v m
    RepC ->> RepF: Commit COMMIT n v m

    RepD ->> LeadB: Commit COMMIT n v m
    RepE ->> LeadB: Commit COMMIT n v m
    RepF ->> LeadB: Commit COMMIT n v m

    Note over RepC,RepF: Wait for 2f plus 1 matching commits

    RepC ->> ClientA: Reply with result
    RepD ->> ClientA: Reply with result
    RepE ->> ClientA: Reply with result
    RepF ->> ClientA: Reply with result
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Consensus Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER[Input Layer]
        txpool1[Transaction Pool Mempool]
        p2p1[P2P Network Layer]
    end

    subgraph CONSENSUS_CORE[Consensus Engine Core]
        leader1[Leader Selection Module]
        propose1[Block Proposal Module]
        vote1[Voting and Attestation Module]
        tally1[Tally and Quorum Checker]
    end

    subgraph CRYPTO_ECON[Economic Incentive Layer]
        reward1[Block Reward Minter]
        slash1[Slashing and Penalty Engine]
        fees1[Transaction Fee Distributor]
    end

    subgraph STATE_LAYER[State Finalization Layer]
        exec1[State Transition Function]
        finalize1[Finality Gadget]
        store1[World State Database]
    end

    txpool1 --> propose1
    p2p1 --> leader1
    leader1 --> propose1
    propose1 --> vote1
    vote1 --> tally1
    tally1 --> exec1
    exec1 --> finalize1
    finalize1 --> store1
    store1 --> reward1
    reward1 --> slash1
    slash1 --> fees1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the Byzantine Generals Problem and discuss how Proof of Work achieves consensus in its presence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Byzantine Generals Problem** is a classical distributed systems dilemma where multiple generals surrounding an enemy city must agree on a common action (attack or retreat) via message exchange, but some generals may be traitors sending conflicting information. The problem is solvable only when the number of traitor generals $f$ satisfies $n \geq 3f + 1$ (with signed messages).

**Proof of Work (PoW)** addresses this problem probabilistically by:
1. Replacing identity-based voting with **computational work expenditure** — each miner must solve a hash puzzle.
2. Making the cost of producing a block high, but the cost of verifying a block trivially low.
3. Using the **longest chain rule** (Nakamoto consensus) to resolve disagreements — honest nodes always extend the chain with the most cumulative work.
4. Coupling the protocol with **economic incentives** (block rewards and transaction fees) so that attacking the network is more expensive than cooperating with it.

Thus, even with up to ~49% of hash power controlled by attackers, the system remains secure as long as the honest majority continues to extend the chain. **(3 marks — 1 mark for problem statement, 1 mark for PoW mechanism, 1 mark for longest chain rule with incentives).**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Proof of Work (PoW) and Proof of Stake (PoS) consensus mechanisms. List any two advantages of PoS over PoW.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resource Used** | Computational power (electricity, hardware) | Economic stake (locked tokens) |
| **Block Producer** | Miners competing to solve hash puzzles | Validators selected proportional to stake |
| **Security Mechanism** | Cost of energy expenditure | Cost of capital at risk (slashing) |
| **Energy Consumption** | Very high | Very low (~99% less than PoW) |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Absolute (with BFT finality gadget) |

**Two advantages of PoS over PoW:**
1. **Energy efficiency:** PoS eliminates the massive electricity consumption of PoW mining, making it environmentally sustainable.
2. **Economic security alignment:** Validators have direct financial exposure to the network's success; misbehaviour is punished via slashing, whereas PoW miners can switch chains cheaply. **(3 marks — 1 mark differentiation, 1 mark each for two advantages).**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **CAP Theorem** in detail. Discuss how different blockchain consensus algorithms (PoW, PoS, PBFT) make different trade-offs among the three CAP properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **three phases of the Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) algorithm**. Prove that PBFT can tolerate up to $f$ Byzantine faults with $n = 3f + 1$ nodes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CAP Theorem (7 marks):**

The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by Eric Brewer in 2000, states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most **two out of the three** following guarantees:

- **Consistency (C):** Every read returns the most recent successful write (all nodes see the same data).
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a response, even if some nodes are down.
- **Partition tolerance (P):** The system continues operating despite arbitrary message loss between nodes.

Since network partitions (P) are **inevitable in real distributed systems** (delays, disconnections, packet loss), every blockchain protocol must choose between strong Consistency or high Availability.

**Trade-offs in blockchain consensus:**

| Algorithm | C | A | P | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bitcoin (PoW)** | Eventual | High | Yes | **AP** — accepts temporary forks; eventually consistent via longest chain. |
| **Ethereum (PoS + Casper FFG)** | Strong | High | Yes | **CP-leaning** — uses finality gadget to avoid forks. |
| **PBFT (Hyperledger)** | Strong | Reduced | Yes | **CP** — halts if quorum unavailable, but never forks. |
| **PoA (VeChain)** | Strong | High | Yes | Centralised trust, but very fast. |

**Key Insight:** Public, permissionless chains (PoW) favour **AP** to stay censorship-resistant; permissioned chains (PBFT) favour **CP** to guarantee deterministic finality for enterprise use cases. **(7 marks distributed: 3 for CAP definition, 4 for blockchain trade-offs).**

**(b) PBFT Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid sequence diagram in Section 4.3.

**Three Phases:**

1. **Pre-Prepare Phase:** The **primary (leader)** node $p$ receives a client request $m$ and assigns it a sequence number $n$. It then broadcasts a $\langle \text{PRE-PREPARE}, n, v, m \rangle$ message to all backups, where $v$ is the current view number.
2. **Prepare Phase:** Each backup $i$ that accepts the pre-prepare broadcasts $\langle \text{PREPARE}, n, v, i \rangle$ to all other replicas. A replica enters the *prepared* state once it has received **$2f$ matching PREPARE messages** that match its pre-prepare.
3. **Commit Phase:** Each replica broadcasts $\langle \text{COMMIT}, n, v, i \rangle$. A replica enters the *committed-local* state once it receives **$2f + 1$ matching COMMIT messages**. Only then does it execute the request and reply to the client.

**Proof of $n = 3f + 1$ bound:**

Let $n$ be the total replicas and $f$ the maximum number of Byzantine replicas. PBFT uses a quorum of size $Q = 2f + 1$.

**Requirement:** Any two quorums must intersect in at least one **honest** node to ensure consistency (otherwise a Byzantine node could double-vote).

For two quorums $Q_1, Q_2$ of size $2f + 1$:

$$
\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq \vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert - n = 2(2f + 1) - n
$$

For at least one honest intersection, we need:

$$
2(2f + 1) - n \geq f + 1
$$

$$
4f + 2 - n \geq f + 1
$$

$$
3f + 1 \geq n
$$

For equality (minimum nodes), $n = 3f + 1$. **(7 marks distributed: 2 for diagram, 2 for phases, 3 for proof).**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Proof of Stake (PoS)** consensus algorithm in detail. How does PoS handle the **"Nothing at Stake"** problem? Compare PoS with DPoS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Proof of Work (PoW)** with the Bitcoin mining process. Derive the relationship between **hash rate, difficulty, and expected block time**. Show how Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment algorithm maintains $\approx$ 10-minute block intervals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof of Stake (7 marks):**

In **Proof of Stake (PoS)**, validator nodes are selected to produce blocks in proportion to the number of native tokens they have **staked** (locked) as collateral. Validators are incentivised honestly because misbehaviour triggers **slashing** — the protocol destroys a portion of the staked tokens.

**Operational flow:**
1. Token holders deposit a minimum stake into a staking contract to become **validators**.
2. A pseudo-random algorithm (e.g., RANDAO + VRF) selects a proposer for each slot, weighted by stake: $P_i = s_i / \sum_j s_j$.
3. Other validators **attest** to the block's validity. With $2/3$ stake agreement, the block is **finalised** (e.g., via Casper FFG in Ethereum).
4. Proposers earn transaction fees and sometimes a small inflation reward.

**The "Nothing at Stake" Problem:**
If the chain forks, a PoS validator could vote on **both forks** at zero cost, because signing a vote does not consume external resources (unlike PoW). This makes it easier to attack a fork. Solutions include:
- **Slashing:** Voting on conflicting blocks is cryptographically detectable and the validator loses their stake.
- **Finality gadgets (Casper FFG):** Once a block is justified by $2/3$ stake, voting against it incurs a massive slashable penalty.

**Comparison: PoS vs DPoS**

| Aspect | PoS | DPoS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Validator set | All stakers (potentially thousands) | Elected delegates (often 21–101) |
| Selection | Random, stake-weighted | Voting by token holders |
| Throughput | Moderate | Very high |
| Decentralisation | Higher | Lower (cartel risk) |
| Examples | Ethereum, Cardano | EOS, Tron, BitShares |

**(b) Proof of Work (7 marks):**

In **Proof of Work**, miners compete to find a **nonce** such that the SHA-256 double hash of the block header is below a target value $T$:

$$
H(\text{nonce} \parallel \text{prev\_hash} \parallel \text{merkle\_root} \parallel \text{timestamp} \parallel \dots) < T
$$

The **target** $T$ is derived from a human-friendly **difficulty** $D$:

$$
T = \dfrac{T_{\max}}{D}
$$

where $T_{\max} = 0x00000000\text{FFFF}\dots\text{FFFF}$ (a large 256-bit number).

**Expected block time derivation:**

Let $H$ be the network's total hash rate (hashes per second). The probability of a single hash attempt being successful is $p = T / 2^{256}$. The expected number of attempts to find a valid nonce is $E[N] = 1/p = 2^{256}/T$. Hence:

$$
E[t_{\text{block}}] = \frac{E[N]}{H} = \frac{2^{256}}{T \cdot H}
$$

Equivalently, since $D = T_{\max} / T$:

$$
E[t_{\text{block}}] = \frac{D \cdot 2^{256}}{T_{\max} \cdot H}
$$

**Difficulty adjustment algorithm:**

Bitcoin recalculates the difficulty every **2016 blocks** (target: 2 weeks). Let $T_{\text{actual}}$ be the actual time taken to mine the last 2016 blocks. The new difficulty is:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{target}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}
$$

where $T_{\text{target}} = 2016 \times 10 \times 60 = 1,209,600$ seconds. The change is **clamped to a factor of 4** in either direction to prevent wild swings:

$$
D_{\text{new}} = \min\!\left(4 D_{\text{old}}, \; \max\!\left(\frac{D_{\text{old}}}{4}, \; D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{target}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}\right)\right)
$$

**Example:** If miners leave and $T_{\text{actual}} = 4$ weeks, the new difficulty drops to one-quarter of the previous value, restoring the 10-minute target. **(7 marks distributed: 3 for PoW explanation, 2 for derivation, 2 for adjustment algorithm).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention slashing** in PoS answers — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you only mention stake without the penalty mechanism.
> 2. **Writing "n > 3f" instead of "n ≥ 3f + 1"** — the +1 is critical and is a frequent 1-mark loss in PBFT derivations.
> 3. **Confusing probabilistic and deterministic finality** — Bitcoin's finality is *probabilistic* (blocks become more secure with more confirmations), while PBFT's is *deterministic* (immediate, irrevocable after commit phase). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Omitting the longest chain rule** in PoW answers — this is the *actual* consensus rule; the hash puzzle is only the Sybil resistance mechanism.
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** for PBFT — KTU awards 2 marks specifically for the pre-prepare / prepare / commit sequence diagram.
> 6. **Mixing up CAP trade-offs** — Bitcoin is AP, not CP. PBFT is CP, not AP. Memorising this wrong loses 2 marks easily.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 2**

- **Consensus Algorithm:** Fault-tolerant mechanism for agreement among untrusted distributed nodes on the canonical ledger state.
- **Three Properties:** Safety (no forks), Liveness (blocks keep coming), Fault Tolerance (survives node failures).
- **CAP Theorem:** You can guarantee at most 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance} simultaneously.
- **Byzantine Generals Problem:** Formulated by Lamport et al. (1982). Requires $n \geq 3f + 1$ for solution with signed messages.
- **Proof of Work (PoW):** Hash puzzle $H(\text{nonce} \parallel \text{header}) < T$. Used by Bitcoin, Litecoin. Probabilistic finality. Highest energy cost.
- **Proof of Stake (PoS):** Validators selected proportional to stake. Slashing for misbehaviour. Used by Ethereum (post-Merge).
- **Delegated PoS (DPoS):** Token holders elect a small set of block producers. High throughput, lower decentralisation.
- **PBFT:** Three phases — Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit. Tolerates $f$ Byzantine nodes with $n = 3f + 1$ total. Communication complexity $O(n^2)$. Used in permissioned chains.
- **Proof of Authority (PoA):** Pre-approved, identified validators. Used in consortium and enterprise chains. Highest throughput, lowest decentralisation.
- **Bitcoin Difficulty Adjustment:** Recalculated every 2016 blocks. Formula $D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot T_{\text{target}} / T_{\text{actual}}$, clamped to $\times 4$ max change.
- **Block Reward Halving:** Bitcoin reward halves every 210,000 blocks ($\approx$ 4 years). Current reward is **3.125 BTC** (post-April 2024 halving).
- **Double-Spend Attack Probability:** Decays exponentially with confirmations. For attacker with hash power $q$ and honest network $z > q$, success probability after $k$ confirmations is approximately $(q/z)^k$.
- **VRF (Verifiable Random Function):** Used in modern PoS to prevent grinding attacks on leader selection.
- **Finality Types:** Probabilistic (PoW — more confirmations = more secure) vs Absolute (PBFT, Casper FFG — irreversible after commit).
- **Mempool:** Pool of unconfirmed transactions waiting to be included in the next block.
- **Longest Chain Rule:** Honest nodes always extend the chain with the most cumulative work (PoW) or the most attestations (PoS). This is what makes Bitcoin *Nakamoto Consensus*.
- **Key Formulas to Memorise:** $n \geq 3f + 1$, $D = T_{\max} / T$, $E[t_{\text{block}}] = D \cdot 2^{256} / (T_{\max} \cdot H)$, $P_i = s_i / \sum_j s_j$.
- **Famous Networks to Remember:** PoW → Bitcoin; PoS → Ethereum, Cardano; DPoS → EOS, Tron; PBFT → Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint; PoA → VeChain; PoH → Solana.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT) Algorithms - Paxos
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT) Algorithms - Paxos

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT)** is the property of a distributed system to continue operating correctly and reach consensus even when some of its nodes **crash (halt and never return)** or arbitrarily delay their messages, provided that the crashed nodes do not exhibit malicious (Byzantine) behavior.

**Paxos** is a family of protocols invented by **Leslie Lamport (1998)** that provides a provably correct, fault-tolerant method for achieving **distributed consensus** among a collection of unreliable, asynchronous processors. It guarantees that a group of nodes will agree on a single value (called a *decree* or *command*) even in the presence of **network partitions, message loss, and node crashes**.

$$ \text{Paxos} = f(\text{Proposers}, \text{Acceptors}, \text{Learners}) \rightarrow \text{Agreed Value} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield Fact):** Paxos tolerates up to $f$ crash faults out of $2f+1$ acceptors. The minimum quorum required is $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ where $N$ is the total number of acceptors. This is called a **Majority Quorum**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Parish Council Resolves a New Tax"

Imagine a small town with **5 parish elders** (Acceptors). The mayor (Proposer) wants to pass a new tax law. The mayor cannot call a meeting reliably because some elders may be **absent (crashed)**, and the messenger (network) may **lose proposals**.

- The mayor assigns a **unique, ever-increasing ballot number** to each attempt.
- Phase 1 — **"Feel out the room"**: The mayor calls around asking, "Have you already promised to support any other law? If yes, tell me which one." Elders who are awake **promise not to accept any older proposal** and reveal anything they have already accepted.
- Phase 2 — **"Propose and accept"**: The mayor sends the proposal. If a majority of awake elders (≥3 out of 5) accept it, the law is officially passed.
- Phase 3 — **"Spread the word"**: The town crier (Learner) announces the decision to the rest of the town.

This is exactly how Paxos achieves consensus — through **two-phase majority voting with strict ballot ordering**.

### 1.3 Why CFT Algorithms Matter for Blockchain

While Bitcoin's Nakamoto consensus (PoW) and Ethereum's PoS tolerate **Byzantine faults**, modern **permissioned blockchains** (Hyperledger Fabric, Quorum, Tendermint variations) often use **CFT consensus** because:

- **Performance:** CFT is faster (milliseconds vs. minutes).
- **Known participants:** Permissioned networks have authenticated nodes, so the Byzantine threat is reduced.
- **Practical:** CFT algorithms like Paxos, Raft, and PBFT-Hyper are the backbone of production-grade distributed ledgers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction to Remember:**
> - **CFT (Paxos, Raft)** → Tolerates **crashes only** → Needs $2f+1$ nodes.
> - **BFT (PBFT)** → Tolerates **Byzantine + crashes** → Needs $3f+1$ nodes.
> - **Nakamoto PoW** → Probabilistic BFT → Tolerates up to **50% hash power**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Roles in Paxos

| Role | Responsibility | Analogy |
|------|----------------|---------|
| **Proposer** | Suggests a value with a unique proposal number $n$ | Mayor proposing a law |
| **Acceptor** | Votes (promises/accepts) on proposals; forms the quorum | Parish elders |
| **Learner** | Once a value is chosen, learns the final value | Town citizens |

A single node can play multiple roles simultaneously. In a typical **5-node Paxos cluster**, every node is proposer, acceptor, and learner.

### 2.2 The Core Safety Properties of Paxos

Paxos guarantees three safety properties — these are what make it mathematically correct:

1. **Validity (Non-triviality):** Only a value that has been *proposed* may be chosen.
2. **Agreement (Consistency):** No two learners ever learn different chosen values.
3. **Integrity (Finality):** A node learns a value at most once, and only if it was actually chosen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Liveness Caveat:** Lamport proved that Paxos provides safety under all conditions, but **liveness (progress)** is only guaranteed under the weak assumption that a leader eventually exists for long enough. The famous **FLP Impossibility (1985)** states that no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee liveness in a purely asynchronous system with even a single crash fault — so Paxos uses **randomized leader election (e.g., timeout + retry)** to circumvent FLP in practice.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|-------|
| Total Acceptors | $N$ | $N \geq 2f+1$ | $f$ = max crash faults |
| Quorum Size | $Q$ | $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Majority |
| Max Crash Tolerance | $f$ | $f = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Strict bound |
| Quorum Intersection | $|Q_1 \cap Q_2|$ | $\geq 1$ | For any two quorums |
| Proposal Number | $n$ | Monotonically increasing tuple $(round, server\_id)$ | Globally unique |
| Round Number | $r$ | $r \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, increases with every new attempt | |
| Required Acceptors for Promise | $a_p$ | $a_p \geq Q$ | Phase 1 |
| Required Acceptors for Accept | $a_a$ | $a_a \geq Q$ | Phase 2 |
| Paxos Round Duration | $T_{round}$ | $2 \cdot RTT + T_{work}$ | Typical |
| Multi-Paxos Steady-State | $T_{ms}$ | $1 \cdot RTT$ | After leader elected |
| Liveness Probability (per round) | $P_{live}$ | $1 - (1-p)^k$ | $p$ = leader success prob, $k$ = retries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quorum Intersection Theorem (KTU Favorite):** For any two quorums $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ of an $N$-acceptor system with $N = 2f+1$:
> $$\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$$
> This single inequality is the **mathematical backbone** of Paxos safety. It guarantees that any two rounds of consensus must share at least one common acceptor, which prevents conflicting values from being chosen.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Chubby Lock Service** — uses Paxos for distributed coordination.
- **Apache ZooKeeper** — derived from Zab, a Paxos-like protocol.
- **etcd / Kubernetes** — uses Raft (Paxos-simplified).
- **Amazon DynamoDB / S3** — variants of Paxos for metadata storage.
- **Hyperledger Fabric (ordering service)** — Kafka-based ordering borrows from Paxos ideas.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Single-Decree Paxos Algorithm — Exhaustive Derivation

Paxos operates in **two main phases** (sometimes split into four sub-phases). We will derive every step rigorously.

---

#### **STEP 0: Setup and Initial State**

Let there be a set $\mathcal{A} = \{A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4, A_5\}$ of $N = 5$ acceptors.
We choose $f = 2$ (we can tolerate up to 2 crashes).
The required quorum is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
  &= \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
  &= 2 + 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

So we need **at least 3 acceptors** to respond at every step.

Each acceptor $A_i$ maintains the following state variables:

- $n_p^{(i)}$ — highest proposal number **promised** (initially $-\infty$ or $0$).
- $n_a^{(i)}$ — highest proposal number **accepted** (initially $-\infty$).
- $v_a^{(i)}$ — the value **accepted** with $n_a^{(i)}$ (initially $\bot$).

---

#### **STEP 1: Phase 1a — Prepare (Proposer → Acceptors)**

A proposer $P$ decides to propose a value $v$ with a globally unique proposal number $n$. A common scheme is:

$$
n = (\text{round}, \text{server\_id})
$$

For example, $P_2$ on round $5$ generates $n = (5, 2)$. Proposal numbers are **totally ordered lexicographically** so that no two proposers ever use the same $n$.

$P$ sends a $\text{PREPARE}(n)$ message to a majority of acceptors.

---

#### **STEP 2: Phase 1b — Promise (Acceptors → Proposer)**

Upon receiving $\text{PREPARE}(n)$, an acceptor $A_i$ responds with a $\text{PROMISE}(n, n_a^{(i)}, v_a^{(i)})$ **only if** $n > n_p^{(i)}$. It then updates:

$$
n_p^{(i)} \leftarrow n
$$

> **Crucial Rule:** The promise is a contract — the acceptor promises to ignore any $\text{ACCEPT}$ messages with proposal number $\leq n$, but it **may** accept proposals with number $> n$ (in future rounds).

The proposer $P$ waits for a quorum of promises. Let this set be $\mathcal{P}$ with $\vert \mathcal{P} \vert \geq Q$.

---

#### **STEP 3: Phase 2a — Accept Request (Proposer → Acceptors)**

$P$ examines the values returned in the promises. If **any** acceptor reported a previously accepted value $v_a^{(i)} \neq \bot$, then $P$ **must** propose that value (the one with the **highest $n_a$**). Otherwise, $P$ is free to propose its originally intended value $v$.

Mathematically, the proposer selects:

$$
v_{\text{proposed}} = 
\begin{cases}
v_a^{(i^*)} \text{ where } i^* = \arg\max_i \, n_a^{(i)} & \text{if any } v_a^{(i)} \neq \bot \\
v_{\text{original}} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This step is what makes Paxos **safe** — it forces the proposer to respect the most recent decision.

$P$ sends $\text{ACCEPT}(n, v_{\text{proposed}})$ to the same set of acceptors.

---

#### **STEP 4: Phase 2b — Accepted (Acceptors → Proposer)**

Each acceptor $A_i$ responds with $\text{ACCEPTED}(n, v_{\text{proposed}})$ **only if** $n \geq n_p^{(i)}$. It then updates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_a^{(i)} &\leftarrow n \\
v_a^{(i)} &\leftarrow v_{\text{proposed}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** A prepare can be ignored if it has a *lower* proposal number, but an accept with a *higher* number is allowed (and forces the acceptor to forget its previous promise). This is what permits progress in the face of competing proposers.

---

#### **STEP 5: Phase 3 — Learn (Acceptors → Learners)**
