Once $P$ has collected a quorum of $\text{ACCEPTED}$ messages, it knows the value is **chosen** (irrevocably decided). $P$ then broadcasts $\text{CHOSEN}(v_{\text{proposed}})$ to all learners, or acceptors can directly notify learners.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Paxos is Safe

We now derive the safety property formally.

> **Claim:** Once a value $v_1$ is chosen in round $n_1$, no other value $v_2 \neq v_1$ can ever be chosen.

**Proof Outline:**

Let value $v_1$ be chosen in round $n_1$ with quorum $\mathcal{Q}_1$ (i.e., $\vert \mathcal{Q}_1 \vert \geq Q$ acceptors accepted $v_1$ at $n_1$).

Suppose for contradiction that a different value $v_2 \neq v_1$ is later chosen in round $n_2 > n_1$ with quorum $\mathcal{Q}_2$.

By the **Quorum Intersection property**:

$$
\vert \mathcal{Q}_1 \cap \mathcal{Q}_2 \vert \geq 1
$$

So there exists an acceptor $A_k \in \mathcal{Q}_1 \cap \mathcal{Q}_2$. This acceptor $A_k$:

- **In round $n_1$:** accepted $v_1$, so $n_a^{(k)} = n_1$, $v_a^{(k)} = v_1$.
- **In round $n_2$:** must have first responded to a $\text{PREPARE}(n_2)$, which forces $n_p^{(k)} \geq n_2 > n_1$.

But the **PROMISE rule** for $A_k$ would have returned $v_a^{(k)} = v_1$ to the proposer of round $n_2$. The proposer, by the **Phase 2a rule**, would then be forced to use $v_1$ (or a value with an even higher $n_a$, which would in turn trace back to $v_1$ by induction). Hence $v_2 = v_1$, contradicting our assumption. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Multi-Paxos — Optimization for Streams of Values

For a **single value**, Basic Paxos requires 2 RTTs (Prepare + Accept). For a **log of values** (e.g., blockchain blocks), we use **Multi-Paxos**:

$$
T_{\text{multi}} = T_{\text{leader-election}} + N_{\text{values}} \times T_{1\,RTT}
$$

The leader is elected **once** via Basic Paxos, and then subsequent log entries are committed with **1 RTT each**, drastically reducing latency.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Paxos cluster has $N = 7$ acceptors. Compute (a) maximum crash tolerance, (b) quorum size, (c) minimum quorum intersection.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \left\lfloor \frac{N-1}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{6}{2} \right\rfloor = 3 \\
Q &= \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \\
\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert_{\min} &= Q_1 + Q_2 - N = 4 + 4 - 7 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So this cluster tolerates **3 simultaneous crashes**, requires **4 votes**, and guarantees **at least 1 shared acceptor** between any two quorums. $\square$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Paxos Two-Phase Protocol Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Proposer P
    participant A1 as Acceptor A1
    participant A2 as Acceptor A2
    participant A3 as Acceptor A3
    participant L as Learners

    Note over P: Generate proposal number n = (round, id)
    
    rect rgba(200, 220, 255, 0.3)
    Note over P,A3: PHASE 1 - PREPARE / PROMISE
    P->>A1: PREPARE(n)
    P->>A2: PREPARE(n)
    P->>A3: PREPARE(n)
    A1->>P: PROMISE(n, n_a1, v_a1)
    A2->>P: PROMISE(n, n_a2, v_a2)
    A3->>P: PROMISE(n, n_a3, v_a3)
    end

    Note over P: Pick v_proposed from highest n_a OR use original v

    rect rgba(200, 255, 220, 0.3)
    Note over P,A3: PHASE 2 - ACCEPT / ACCEPTED
    P->>A1: ACCEPT(n, v_proposed)
    P->>A2: ACCEPT(n, v_proposed)
    P->>A3: ACCEPT(n, v_proposed)
    A1->>P: ACCEPTED(n, v_proposed)
    A2->>P: ACCEPTED(n, v_proposed)
    A3->>P: ACCEPTED(n, v_proposed)
    end

    Note over P: Quorum reached - value is CHOSEN

    P->>L: CHOSEN(v_proposed)
    A1->>L: CHOSEN(v_proposed)
    A2->>L: CHOSEN(v_proposed)
```

### 4.2 Paxos Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["Client Layer"]
        C[Client Application]
    end

    subgraph PaxosCore["Paxos Consensus Engine"]
        PROP[Proposer Module]
        ACC[Acceptor Module]
        LRN[Learner Module]
    end

    subgraph StorageLayer["Persistent State"]
        LOG[Accepted Log]
        PROM[Promise Table]
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer["Unreliable Network"]
        NET[(Message Bus - TCP/UDP)]
    end

    C -->|Request value v| PROP
    PROP -->|PREPARE n| NET
    NET -->|PROMISE n, n_a, v_a| PROP
    PROP -->|ACCEPT n, v| NET
    NET -->|ACCEPTED n, v| ACC
    ACC --> LOG
    ACC --> PROM
    PROP -->|Quorum Check| LRN
    LRN -->|Reply to Client| C
    LRN -->|Notify other learners| NET
```

### 4.3 State Machine of an Acceptor

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Promised: Receive PREPARE n with n greater than n_p
    Promised --> Idle: Receive PREPARE n with n less than n_p
    Idle --> Accepted: Receive ACCEPT n with n greater than or equal to n_p
    Promised --> Accepted: Receive ACCEPT n with n greater than or equal to n_p
    Accepted --> Promised: Receive PREPARE n_new with n_new greater than n_p
    Accepted --> Idle: Receive PREPARE n with n less than n_p
    Accepted --> [*]: System shutdown
    Promised --> [*]: System shutdown
    Idle --> [*]: System shutdown
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT). Why is Paxos classified as a CFT algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT)** is the ability of a distributed system to continue reaching correct consensus even when a subset of nodes **stops functioning (crash)** or fails to send/receive messages, as long as the failed nodes do not behave maliciously.
>
> **Paxos is classified as a CFT algorithm** because:
> 1. It assumes the **fail-stop model** — faulty nodes simply halt and never return incorrect messages.
> 2. It does not defend against **Byzantine (malicious) behavior** such as lying, equivocation, or forging signatures.
> 3. Its safety guarantees rely on quorum intersection ($2f+1$ acceptors for $f$ crashes) and the assumption that **non-faulty nodes follow the protocol honestly**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]**
**What is the role of a *quorum* in Paxos? Why is the majority quorum size set to $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **quorum** in Paxos is the minimum number of acceptors ($Q$) that must respond in both Phase 1 (Promise) and Phase 2 (Accepted) for a proposal to make progress. It ensures that any two rounds of consensus share at least one common acceptor.
>
> The size $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ is chosen because it is the **smallest majority** that guarantees **quorum intersection**: for any two quorums $Q_1, Q_2$:
> $$\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert = Q_1 + Q_2 - N \geq 2\left(\left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1\right) - N \geq 1$$
> This mathematical guarantee is what gives Paxos its **safety property (agreement)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **two phases** of the Paxos consensus algorithm in detail with the messages exchanged. Mention the role of proposal numbers and the requirement for a **majority quorum**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a cluster of $N = 9$ acceptors, compute the (i) maximum number of crash faults tolerated, (ii) the minimum quorum size, and (iii) prove that any two quorums intersect in at least 1 acceptor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (A):**

**(a) Two Phases of Paxos (7 marks):**

**Phase 1 — Prepare / Promise:**
- A **Proposer** $P$ generates a **globally unique, monotonically increasing proposal number** $n = (\text{round}, \text{server\_id})$.
- $P$ sends $\text{PREPARE}(n)$ to a majority of acceptors.
- Each **Acceptor** $A_i$ compares $n$ with its current promised number $n_p^{(i)}$:
  - If $n > n_p^{(i)}$: $A_i$ replies with $\text{PROMISE}(n, n_a^{(i)}, v_a^{(i)})$ and sets $n_p^{(i)} \leftarrow n$.
  - If $n \leq n_p^{(i)}$: $A_i$ ignores (or replies with NACK).
- $P$ waits for promises from a **quorum** ($\geq Q$) of acceptors.

**Phase 2 — Accept / Accepted:**
- $P$ examines the promises. If any acceptor reported a previously accepted value, $P$ **must adopt** the value with the highest $n_a$ (this is the **safety-preserving** rule). Otherwise, $P$ proposes its original value $v$.
- $P$ sends $\text{ACCEPT}(n, v_{\text{proposed}})$ to the quorum of acceptors.
- Each acceptor $A_i$ accepts **iff** $n \geq n_p^{(i)}$, updates $(n_a^{(i)}, v_a^{(i)}) \leftarrow (n, v_{\text{proposed}})$, and replies $\text{ACCEPTED}$.
- Once $P$ collects a quorum of $\text{ACCEPTED}$, the value is **chosen** and propagated to **Learners**.

The **proposal number** ensures total ordering across competing proposers. The **majority quorum** ensures safety via quorum intersection.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given $N = 9$:

**(i) Maximum crash faults tolerated:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \left\lfloor \frac{N-1}{2} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor \frac{9-1}{2} \right\rfloor \\
  &= \left\lfloor 4 \right\rfloor = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

So the system tolerates up to **4 simultaneous crash failures**.

**(ii) Minimum quorum size:**
$$
Q = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5
$$

So at least **5 acceptors** must respond in each phase.

**(iii) Proof of quorum intersection:**

Let $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ be two quorums. By the **Pigeonhole Principle** (complement form):
$$
Q_1 + Q_2 = 5 + 5 = 10 > N = 9
$$

Since the total size of $Q_1 \cup Q_2$ is at most $N = 9$, the intersection cannot be empty:
$$
\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert = \vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert - \vert Q_1 \cup Q_2 \vert \geq 5 + 5 - 9 = 1
$$

Hence, any two quorums share **at least 1 acceptor**. $\blacksquare$

This is the **safety proof** of Paxos — if $v_1$ was chosen in round $n_1$, the shared acceptor in any future quorum will have already promised/accepted $v_1$, forcing any future chosen value to be $v_1$ as well.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT)** and **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)**. State the minimum number of nodes required for each, and justify why **$2f+1$ is sufficient for CFT but $3f+1$ is needed for BFT**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Multi-Paxos** optimization. How does it reduce the consensus latency from 2 RTT to 1 RTT per log entry? Mention its application in **Hyperledger Fabric** or **distributed log services**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (B):**

**(a) CFT vs BFT (7 marks):**

| Aspect | CFT (Paxos, Raft) | BFT (PBFT) |
|--------|-------------------|------------|
| Fault Model | Crash / fail-stop | Arbitrary (lying, equivocating) |
| Min Nodes | $2f+1$ | $3f+1$ |
| Quorum Size | $\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | $\lfloor 2N/3 \rfloor + 1$ |
| Throughput | High (ms latency) | Lower |
| Use Case | Permissioned, trusted nodes | Untrusted / adversarial |

**Why $2f+1$ for CFT:** A majority quorum $Q = f+1$ ensures that any two quorums intersect in at least $1$ node, and as long as $\leq f$ nodes crash, a quorum of $f+1$ non-faulty nodes is always available. **At most $f$ can be faulty, so $f+1$ correct nodes suffice** to make progress.

**Why $3f+1$ for BFT:** A malicious node may vote inconsistently in different rounds. The quorum must be large enough so that the intersection of **two correct quorums contains $\geq f+1$ honest nodes** outvoting any $f$ liars. Hence $Q \geq 2f+1$, and with quorum intersection $2(2f+1) - (3f+1) = f+1$ honest overlapping nodes. Thus $N = 3f+1$.

**(b) Multi-Paxos Optimization (7 marks):**

**Basic Paxos** for a single value needs **2 RTTs**:
- RTT 1: Prepare / Promise
- RTT 2: Accept / Accepted

**Multi-Paxos** amortizes the leader election across many values:
1. **Step 1:** Run Basic Paxos **once** to elect a **stable leader** (Proposer). The leader's identity is replicated via the chosen value of a special "leadership" slot.
2. **Step 2 (Steady State):** The leader proposes log entries directly using only Phase 2 (skip Phase 1, because no other proposer is competing). This reduces the cost to **1 RTT per value**.

Mathematically:
$$
T_{\text{multi}} = T_{\text{leader}} + N_{\text{entries}} \times RTT
$$

If $N_{\text{entries}} \to \infty$, the amortized cost approaches **1 RTT per entry**, which is optimal for asynchronous consensus.

**Applications:**
- **Hyperledger Fabric (legacy Raft orderer):** Uses Multi-Paxos-style Raft for ordering transactions.
- **etcd, Consul, ZooKeeper:** All use Paxos-family protocols for replicated log.
- **Google Spanner / Chubby:** Multi-Paxos underpins global replication.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CFT with BFT node counts** — Students frequently write $3f+1$ for Paxos. **Paxos is CFT → $2f+1$ acceptors**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Quorum Intersection proof** — Just stating "majority is used" without proving $\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$ loses up to **3 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up "proposal number" with "round number"** — Proposal number $n$ is a *tuple* $(round, server\_id)$, not just a round.
> 4. **Skipping the "adopt the highest accepted value" rule in Phase 2a** — This is the **heart of Paxos safety**; omitting it loses 2-3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting Learners in the diagram** — A complete Paxos diagram must show all three roles.
> 6. **Liveness vs Safety confusion** — Paxos guarantees **safety always**, but **liveness only with a stable leader** (FLP impossibility).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CFT** tolerates only **crash faults**, not Byzantine behavior.
- [x] **Paxos** was invented by **Leslie Lamport (1998)**.
- [x] Three roles: **Proposer, Acceptor, Learner**.
- [x] Two phases: **Prepare/Promise** and **Accept/Accepted**.
- [x] Quorum size $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ with $N = 2f+1$ acceptors.
- [x] **Proposal number $n = (round, server\_id)$** must be globally unique and totally ordered.
- [x] The **adopt-highest-accepted-value rule** in Phase 2a is the safety guarantee.
- [x] **Quorum intersection** theorem: $\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$ — the foundation of Paxos safety.
- [x] **FLP Impossibility (1985):** No deterministic consensus in pure async systems with even one crash; Paxos circumvents it via leader election + retry.
- [x] **Multi-Paxos** reduces 2 RTT → 1 RTT per log entry by stabilizing a leader.
- [x] **CFT needs $2f+1$ nodes; BFT needs $3f+1$ nodes.**
- [x] Applications: **Google Chubby, ZooKeeper, etcd/Raft, Hyperledger Fabric, Amazon S3 metadata, Spanner.**
- [x] **Safety** = always correct; **Liveness** = eventually makes progress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Paxos uses two majority quorums that always intersect in at least one honest acceptor. The shared acceptor carries the previously chosen value forward, forcing all future decisions to honor it. This single insight — quorum intersection — is the entire mathematical miracle of Paxos."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Raft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Raft

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Raft** is a distributed consensus algorithm designed to be *understandable* as an alternative to the notoriously complex Paxos algorithm. In the context of blockchain and distributed ledger technologies, Raft is used to achieve **fault-tolerant consensus** among a cluster of nodes, ensuring that all non-faulty nodes agree on the same sequence of log entries (transactions), even in the presence of crashes and network partitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Consensus Algorithm**: A protocol through which a distributed network of nodes reaches agreement on a single data value or sequence of values, despite the failure of some nodes.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCGT601)** context, Raft is studied under *Module 2: Consensus Mechanisms*, and it represents the **CFT (Crash Fault Tolerant)** family of consensus, in contrast to BFT (Byzantine Fault Tolerant) algorithms like PBFT that tolerate arbitrary/malicious faults.

Raft was published by **Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout** in their 2014 paper *"In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm"* (USENIX ATC). The algorithm is formally designed around three critical sub-problems:

| Sub-Problem | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Leader Election** | Select a single, authoritative leader among candidates when no leader exists. |
| **Log Replication** | The leader accepts client requests, replicates them as log entries, and ensures followers store identical logs. |
| **Safety** | Guarantees that once a log entry is committed, it will never be lost or overwritten; also ensures State Machine Safety across all nodes. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Democratic Committee

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Project Committee Election**

Imagine a college project committee of **5 students** (analogous to 5 nodes in a Raft cluster) tasked with maintaining a single official project logbook (the replicated log). The committee operates under these rules:

1. **One Secretary at a Time (Leader)**: Only one student is the "Secretary" who writes entries in the logbook. The other 4 are "Members" (followers). If the Secretary falls asleep (crash), the committee holds a quick election to pick a new Secretary.

2. **Heartbeat Announcements**: The Secretary regularly announces *"I am still here and working"* (heartbeat RPC). If the Members don't hear from the Secretary for more than 10 seconds, they assume the Secretary is missing.

3. **Voting (Election Timeout)**: A Member, upon not hearing the Secretary, becomes a "Candidate" and requests votes from other Members. If it receives a majority (e.g., 3 out of 5 votes), it becomes the new Secretary.

4. **Logbook Replication**: When the Secretary writes a new project entry, they pass a copy of that page to all Members. Only when the majority acknowledges receipt is the entry marked as "OFFICIAL" (committed).

This analogy directly maps onto Raft's design: a **single leader, periodic heartbeats, election timeouts, majority quorum, and replicated logs**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard configuration parameters used in Raft implementations (e.g., etcd, Consul, CockroachDB):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Raft Parameters (Standard Default Values)**
> - **Election Timeout**: **150 ms – 300 ms** (randomized per node to avoid split votes).
> - **Heartbeat Interval**: Typically **~50 ms** (must be < Election Timeout, often 1/3 of the lower bound).
> - **Quorum Size (Majority)**: For a cluster of *N* nodes, quorum = **⌊N/2⌋ + 1**.
> - **Term Number**: Monotonically increasing logical clock (an integer), incremented on each new election.
> - **Commit Index**: The highest log index known to be replicated on a quorum of nodes.

### 1.4 The Three Node States in Raft

Every node in a Raft cluster exists in **exactly one of three states** at any given time:

- **Follower**: Passive state. Responds to RPCs from the leader/candidate. This is the initial state of all nodes.
- **Candidate**: Used during elections. Increments term, votes for itself, and solicits votes from peers.
- **Leader**: Handles all client interactions, replicates log entries, and sends periodic heartbeats.

A *fourth* conceptual state, **"Leader Transfer / Step-Down"**, may be considered a transition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time is divided into Terms (Election Terms)**. A *term* begins with an election and ends when a leader is established or the election fails. Terms act as a logical clock and are used to detect stale leaders and stale information.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of Raft

Raft decomposes the consensus problem into three relatively independent modules, making it significantly easier to reason about than Paxos.

### 2.1.1 Module 1 — Leader Election

**Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic):**

- **Why a Leader?** A strong leader simplifies replication: the leader unilaterally decides the ordering of log entries without requiring other nodes to coordinate.
- **Triggering Event**: A follower receives no RPC (no heartbeat, no AppendEntries) from any valid leader for a duration equal to its **election timeout** (randomized 150–300 ms).
- **Step 1 — Become Candidate**: The follower increments its current **term number** by 1, transitions to the **Candidate** state, votes for itself, and resets its election timer.
- **Step 2 — Issue RequestVote RPCs**: The candidate sends `RequestVote` RPCs in parallel to all other nodes in the cluster, including the term number, candidate ID, last log index, and last log term.
- **Step 3 — Vote Counting**: A node grants its vote to *at most one candidate per term* (first-come-first-served) and only if the candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date** as its own.
- **Step 4 — Outcome**:
  - **Wins majority votes** → becomes **Leader**, sends immediate heartbeats to assert authority.
  - **Receives AppendEntries from another leader** → reverts to **Follower**.
  - **Election timeout expires with no winner** → increments term, restarts election.
- **Safety constraint**: A node votes for at most one candidate per term; each node has at most one **vote per term**.

### 2.1.2 Module 2 — Log Replication

- **Step 1 — Client Request**: The leader receives a client command (e.g., a transaction in a blockchain context).
- **Step 2 — Append to Log**: The leader appends the entry to its local log (uncommitted state). Each log entry contains: `term_number`, `index`, and `command`.
- **Step 3 — Replicate**: The leader sends `AppendEntries` RPCs to all followers, including the new entry (and any prior uncommitted entries to ensure consistency).
- **Step 4 — Acknowledgment**: Followers append the entries to their logs and respond with success.
- **Step 5 — Commit**: When the leader receives successful responses from a **majority (quorum)** of nodes, it marks the entry as **committed**, applies it to the state machine, and responds to the client.
- **Step 6 — Notify Followers**: Subsequent heartbeats inform followers that entries up to a certain index are committed; followers then apply them locally.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log Matching Property**: If two logs contain an entry with the same index and term, then the logs are identical in all entries up through that index. This property is the bedrock of Raft's safety guarantees.

### 2.1.3 Module 3 — Safety

Raft adds several restrictions to ensure correctness:

- **Election Safety**: At most one leader can be elected in a given term. (Achieved via majority vote + single vote per term.)
- **Leader Append-Only**: A leader never overwrites or deletes entries in its own log; only appends.
- **Log Up-to-Date Criterion**: A candidate's log must be at least as up-to-date as the voter's log to receive the vote. *"Up-to-date"* is defined as: **(a)** having a higher last log term, or **(b)** same last log term but longer log length.
- **Leader Completeness**: If a log entry is committed in term *T*, it will be present in the logs of all leaders for terms *≥ T*.
- **State Machine Safety**: If a server has applied a log entry at a given index to its state machine, no other server will ever apply a different log entry for the same index.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quorum Size | $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Minimum nodes required to commit | Integer; e.g., N=5 → Q=3 |
| Fault Tolerance (Crash) | $F = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Max simultaneous crash failures tolerated | For N=5, F=2 |
| Term Increment on Election | $T_{new} = T_{current} + 1$ | Each election attempt bumps term | Monotonic non-decreasing |
| Election Timeout Range | $t_{e} \in [t_{min}, t_{max}]$ | Randomized per node | Default: 150–300 ms |
| Heartbeat Interval Constraint | $t_{hb} < t_{min}$ | To ensure liveness | Typically ~50 ms |
| Log Up-to-Date (Boolean) | $(T_{last}^{c} > T_{last}^{v}) \lor [(T_{last}^{c} = T_{last}^{v}) \land (I_{last}^{c} \geq I_{last}^{v})]$ | Candidate vs Voter comparison | Boolean → vote granted? |
| Commit Index Update | $\text{commitIndex} = \max(\text{commitIndex}, \text{matchIndex}[i])$ | Updated when majority replicated | For follower *i* |

> [!NOTE]
> The variable notation used above: $T_{last}$ = term of last log entry, $I_{last}$ = index of last log entry, superscript $c$ = candidate, $v$ = voter, $i$ = follower node.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Raft is the consensus backbone of numerous production-grade distributed systems:

- **etcd** (used by Kubernetes for cluster state storage)
- **Consul** (HashiCorp's service mesh / discovery tool)
- **CockroachDB** (distributed SQL database)
- **TiKV** (distributed transactional key-value store)
- **Hyperledger Fabric (optional ordering service variant)**

In **blockchain applications**, Raft is used in **permissioned/consortium blockchains** where the validator set is small, known, and trusted enough to be modeled as crash-faulty (not Byzantine). Examples include **Hyperledger Fabric's Raft-based ordering service** and **Quorum (by ConsenSys)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off**: Raft is **NOT Byzantine Fault Tolerant**. A malicious node can cause consensus to halt or feed false data. Therefore, Raft is unsuitable for open, trustless public blockchains (like Bitcoin/Ethereum), but ideal for **consortium blockchains** where participants are vetted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quorum Intersection Property

**Theorem**: Any two majorities (quorums) of a set of *N* nodes must share at least one node in common.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

Let the cluster size be $N$. Each quorum size is:
$$Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$$

Consider any two quorums $Q_A$ and $Q_B$. Suppose, for the sake of contradiction, they are **disjoint**, i.e., $Q_A \cap Q_B = \emptyset$.

Then the total number of distinct nodes would be:
$$|Q_A| + |Q_B| = 2Q = 2 \cdot \left(\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1\right)$$

For odd $N = 2k+1$:
$$2Q = 2(k+1) = 2k + 2 = (2k+1) + 1 = N + 1$$

But the cluster only has $N$ nodes, so $N + 1 > N$ is a contradiction. For even $N = 2k$:
$$2Q = 2(k+1) = 2k + 2 = N + 2 > N$$

This is also a contradiction. Therefore, two majorities cannot be disjoint, and at least one node must be shared.

$$\therefore \quad Q_A \cap Q_B \neq \emptyset \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the foundational safety argument: because any two quorums overlap, a new leader (elected with a majority quorum) is guaranteed to contain at least one node that was part of the previous leader's quorum, which in turn contains all committed entries.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Election Timeout Randomized Range

**Problem Statement**: Why must election timeouts be randomized across nodes, and what is the valid range?

**Derivation:**

Let $T_{hb}$ be the heartbeat interval sent by the leader, and let $t_{e}^{(i)}$ be the election timeout of follower $i$, drawn uniformly from a range $[t_{min}, t_{max}]$.

**Condition for Liveness (no permanent leader loss):**
$$T_{hb} \ll t_{e}^{(i)} \quad \forall i \in \text{Followers}$$

In practice:
$$T_{hb} \approx \frac{t_{min}}{3}$$

**Condition for Split-Vote Avoidance (probabilistic reasoning):**

A *split vote* occurs when multiple candidates simultaneously start elections. To minimize the probability, the range must be sufficiently wide:
$$\Delta t = t_{max} - t_{min} \quad \text{must satisfy} \quad \Delta t \gg T_{hb}$$

A commonly used configuration in `etcd` is:
$$t_{min} = 150 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{max} = 300 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{hb} = 50 \text{ ms}$$

**Probability of Split Vote (rough heuristic for 2 simultaneous candidates):**
$$P_{split} \approx \frac{T_{hb}}{\Delta t} = \frac{50}{150} = \frac{1}{3}$$

But with multiple randomized retries, the expected number of rounds to success is:
$$E[\text{rounds}] = \frac{1}{1 - P_{split}} = \frac{1}{1 - 1/3} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ rounds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The randomized election timeout ensures that, on average, the first node to time out is the sole candidate, breaking symmetry and yielding fast leader election.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fault Tolerance Bound for Crash Failures

**Given**: Cluster of $N$ nodes, quorum size $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$.

**Maximum simultaneous crash failures tolerated:**
$$F_{max} = N - Q = N - (\lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1)$$

For odd $N = 2k+1$:
$$F_{max} = (2k+1) - (k+1) = k = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

For even $N = 2k$:
$$F_{max} = 2k - (k+1) = k - 1 = \frac{N}{2} - 1$$

**General formula (integer-safe):**
$$F_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{N-1}{2} \right\rfloor$$

**Example Calculation for N = 5:**
$$F_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{5-1}{2} \right\rfloor = 2$$
$$Q = \lfloor 5/2 \rfloor + 1 = 3$$

The system can tolerate 2 simultaneous crash failures while still being able to make progress with the remaining 3 nodes (a quorum of 3 out of 5).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Raft vs. Paxos vs. PBFT

| Property | Raft | Paxos | PBFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Design Goal** | Understandability | Theoretical correctness | Byzantine fault tolerance |
| **Fault Model** | Crash faults | Crash faults | Byzantine (arbitrary) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor (N-1)/3 \rfloor$ |
| **Leader Required** | Yes (single strong leader) | Optional (Multi-Paxos variant has) | Yes (primary + backups) |
| **Message Rounds per Commit** | 1 (heartbeat-replicated) | 2 (prepare + accept) | 3 (pre-prepare, prepare, commit) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Very High | Medium-High |
| **Used In** | etcd, Consul, Hyperledger Fabric Raft ordering | Chubby, ZooKeeper (ZAB is Paxos-like) | Hyperledger Fabric (classic), Stellar |
| **Blockchain Suitability** | Consortium/Permissioned | Rarely directly | Permissioned with strong trust needs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Raft Node State Machine (Mermaid Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the state transitions a single Raft node undergoes across its lifetime.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Follower

    Follower --> Candidate : Election Timeout (no heartbeat)
    Follower --> Follower : Receives valid AppendEntries from Leader

    Candidate --> Candidate : Election Timeout (split vote, retry)
    Candidate --> Leader : Receives majority of votes
    Candidate --> Follower : Discovers current Leader / higher term

    Leader --> Follower : Discovers higher term (step down)
    Leader --> Leader : Sends heartbeats / replicates logs

    note right of Follower
        Passive state.
        Responds to RPCs.
        Resets election timer
        on valid leader RPC.
    end note

    note right of Candidate
        Increments term.
        Votes for self.
        Sends RequestVote RPCs.
    end note

    note left of Leader
        Sends heartbeats.
        Replicates log entries.
        Commits on majority ACK.
    end note
```

## 4.2 Leader Election Sequence (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Node A in Follower State] --> B{Election Timeout Expired?}
    B -- No --> C[Remain Follower; Reset Timer on Heartbeat]
    B -- Yes --> D[Increment Term: T = T + 1]
    D --> E[Transition to Candidate State]
    E --> F[Vote for Self]
    F --> G[Send RequestVote RPC to All Peers]
    G --> H{Outcome After RPC Round}

    H -- I1[Votes Received >= Majority Q] --> I[Transition to Leader]
    I --> J[Send Initial Heartbeats; Serve Client Requests]

    H -- I2[AppendEntries from Valid Leader] --> K[Revert to Follower]
    H -- I3[Election Timeout Expires, No Majority] --> L[Increment Term; Restart Election]
    L --> E
```

## 4.3 Log Replication Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client] -->|1. Send Command| Leader
    Leader -->|2. Append to Local Log| LeaderLog[Leader Log Buffer]
    LeaderLog -->|3. AppendEntries RPC with Entry| F1[Follower 1]
    LeaderLog -->|3. AppendEntries RPC with Entry| F2[Follower 2]
    LeaderLog -->|3. AppendEntries RPC with Entry| F3[Follower 3]
    LeaderLog -->|3. AppendEntries RPC with Entry| F4[Follower 4]

    F1 -->|4a. ACK Success| Leader
    F2 -->|4b. ACK Success| Leader
    F3 -->|4c. ACK Success| Leader
    F4 -->|4d. ACK Failure / Mismatch| Leader

    Leader -->|5. Count ACKs| QCheck{Majority Q Reached?}
    QCheck -- Yes --> Commit[Mark Entry as COMMITTED]
    QCheck -- No --> Wait[Wait for Retries]

    Commit -->|6. Apply to State Machine| SMLeader[Leader State Machine]
    Commit -->|7. Next Heartbeat Notifies Followers| F1
    Commit -->|7. Next Heartbeat Notifies Followers| F2
    Commit -->|7. Next Heartbeat Notifies Followers| F3
    F1 -->|8. Apply to State Machine| SMF1[Follower 1 State Machine]
    F2 -->|8. Apply to State Machine| SMF2[Follower 2 State Machine]
    F3 -->|8. Apply to State Machine| SMF3[Follower 3 State Machine]
    Commit -->|9. Respond to Client| Client
```

## 4.4 Term-based Logical Clock & Stale Leader Detection

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant N1 as Node 1 (Follower)
    participant N2 as Node 2 (Candidate)
    participant N3 as Node 3 (Follower)

    Note over N1,N3: All nodes start at Term = 1, Leader is Node L0
    L0--xN1: (Network partition - Heartbeats lost)
    Note over N1: Election Timeout expires
    N1->>N1: Term = 2, become Candidate, vote for self
    N1->>N2: RequestVote(term=2)
    N1->>N3: RequestVote(term=2)
    N2->>N1: Vote Granted (term=2)
    N3->>N1: Vote Granted (term=2)
    Note over N1: Wins Majority! Become Leader, Term = 2
    N1->>N2: AppendEntries (Heartbeat, term=2)
    N1->>N3: AppendEntries (Heartbeat, term=2)
    Note over L0: Old leader rejoins (was partitioned)
    L0->>N1: AppendEntries (stale, term=1)
    N1->>L0: Reject (my term=2 > your term=1), step down L0
    L0->>N1: Update term to 2, become Follower
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define the Raft consensus algorithm and list its three sub-problems. Why is it considered an alternative to Paxos?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Raft is a distributed consensus algorithm designed for crash-fault-tolerant replication across a cluster of nodes. It ensures that all non-faulty nodes agree on the same sequence of log entries representing client commands (transactions). (1 mark)

The three sub-problems of Raft are:
1. **Leader Election** — selecting a single authoritative leader when none exists.
2. **Log Replication** — the leader propagates log entries to followers and commits them upon majority acknowledgment.
3. **Safety** — guarantees such as Leader Completeness and State Machine Safety that ensure no committed entry is lost or overwritten. (1 mark)

Raft is considered an alternative to Paxos because Paxos, although proven correct, is notoriously difficult to understand and has many variants that complicate implementation. Raft achieves equivalent correctness guarantees (for the crash-fault model) while decomposing the problem into well-separated sub-problems with an explicit strong-leader model, making it significantly more understandable and practical to implement. (1 mark)

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is a "term" in Raft? How do nodes use terms to detect stale leaders?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **term** in Raft is a monotonically increasing integer that acts as a logical clock. Time is divided into terms, each beginning with an election. A term ends when a leader is successfully elected or when the election fails (no majority). (1 mark)

Terms are used to detect **stale leaders** as follows: every RPC includes the sender's current term. When a node receives an RPC with a term higher than its own, it immediately updates its term to the higher value and reverts to the Follower state. Conversely, if a leader receives an RPC with a higher term, it steps down and becomes a Follower. (1 mark)

This mechanism ensures that any leader operating with an outdated term is immediately demoted, preventing a partitioned or crashed old leader from corrupting the cluster. Thus, terms provide a globally consistent ordering of leadership epochs and act as a defense against stale information. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Leader Election** process in Raft. Include the role of election timeouts, the RequestVote RPC, voting rules, and how split votes are resolved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A blockchain consortium uses Raft with **5 validator nodes**. Compute the quorum size, maximum crash fault tolerance, and explain what happens if 2 nodes crash simultaneously during a log replication.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Leader Election in Raft [7 marks]

**1. Initial State and Trigger (1.5 marks):**
- All nodes start as **Followers**. The cluster relies on a single **Leader** to coordinate.
- Each follower maintains an **election timer** randomized between 150 ms and 300 ms.
- If a follower does not receive either a valid `AppendEntries` heartbeat or a `RequestVote` RPC from a current leader before its election timeout expires, it assumes no leader is present.

**2. Becoming a Candidate (1.5 marks):**
- The follower increments its **current term** by 1: $T_{new} = T_{current} + 1$.
- It transitions to the **Candidate** state.
- It votes for itself (one vote per term).
- It resets its election timer.
- It issues `RequestVote` RPCs in parallel to all other cluster nodes. The RPC contains: candidate's term, candidate ID, index, and term of candidate's last log entry.

**3. Voting Rules (2 marks):**
- A node grants its vote to a candidate only if:
  - The candidate's term is **≥ the voter's term**, and
  - The voter has **not yet voted** in this term, and
  - The candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date** as the voter's log.
- "Up-to-date" is defined as: **(i)** candidate's last log term > voter's last log term, **OR** **(ii)** same term but candidate's log is at least as long.
- A node votes for **at most one candidate per term**.

**4. Outcome and Split-Vote Resolution (2 marks):**
- **Wins majority votes** → becomes **Leader**, immediately sends heartbeats to assert authority, begins serving client requests.
- **Receives AppendEntries from a leader with term ≥ its own** → reverts to **Follower**.
- **Election timeout expires with no majority** → candidate increments term and starts a new election. This handles **split votes**, where multiple candidates time out simultaneously and split the vote, preventing any one from getting a majority.
- The randomized election timeout ensures that split votes are statistically unlikely and resolved within a few rounds.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis: 5-Node Raft Cluster [7 marks]

**Given:** $N = 5$ nodes.

**1. Quorum Size Calculation (2 marks):**
$$Q = \left\lfloor \frac{N}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{5}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

So a majority requires **3 nodes** to agree.

**2. Maximum Crash Fault Tolerance (2 marks):**
$$F_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{N-1}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4}{2} \right\rfloor = 2$$

The system tolerates up to **2 simultaneous crash failures**.

**3. Behavior when 2 nodes crash during log replication (3 marks):**
- The cluster initially has 5 nodes; quorum = 3.
- After 2 nodes crash, **3 nodes remain** (exactly the quorum size).
- The leader can still commit new log entries because it can still receive acknowledgments from a majority (3 out of 5) of the *original* cluster, or equivalently, from all 3 remaining nodes.
- However, the cluster is now operating at its **fault tolerance limit**: if one more node crashes, only 2 nodes will remain, which is **less than the quorum of 3**, and the system will halt (no new entries can be committed).
- This demonstrates the principle: **Raft maintains availability as long as $N \geq Q$**, i.e., the majority survives.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Log Replication** mechanism in Raft. Explain the Log Matching Property, the role of the `AppendEntries` RPC, consistency checks, and how the commit index is updated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast Raft with **Paxos** and **PBFT** with respect to fault model, fault tolerance bound, message complexity, and blockchain suitability. Use a comparative table and justify your answer.

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Log Replication in Raft [7 marks]

**1. Client Request Handling (1 mark):**
- A client sends a command (e.g., a transaction) to the **Leader**.
- If the client contacts a Follower, the Follower redirects it to the Leader.

**2. Leader Appends to Log (1 mark):**
- The Leader appends a new log entry to its local log. The entry contains: `index`, `term`, and `command`.
- The entry is initially **uncommitted**.

**3. AppendEntries RPC (2 marks):**
- The Leader sends `AppendEntries` RPCs to all Followers.
- The RPC includes: term, leader's commit index, the new log entry, and the index/term of the entry *preceding* the new one (for consistency check).
- **Consistency Check**: A Follower rejects the RPC if the preceding entry (identified by prevLogIndex and prevLogTerm) does not match its own log at that position. This enforces the **Log Matching Property**.

**4. Log Matching Property (1.5 marks):**
- If two logs contain entries with the same `(index, term)`, then:
  - The entries contain the **same command**, and
  - All preceding entries in both logs are **identical**.
- This property is preserved inductively: the leader never overwrites its log, and the consistency check guarantees Followers' logs match the leader's up to the new entry.

**5. Commit Index Update (1.5 marks):**
- The Leader tracks, for each Follower, a `matchIndex` (highest replicated log index).
- When the Leader determines that a log entry has been replicated to a **majority** of nodes (i.e., $\text{matchIndex}[i] \geq n$ for a quorum), it updates its `commitIndex = n`.
- The Leader then includes the new `commitIndex` in subsequent heartbeats, and Followers apply the entry to their state machines.

> [!NOTE]
> Only the **current term's** entries can be committed directly via replication counts. A leader commits a previous-term entry only as a *side effect* of committing a current-term entry.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis: Raft vs. Paxos vs. PBFT [7 marks]

| Property (2 marks) | Raft | Paxos | PBFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fault Model** | Crash faults | Crash faults | Byzantine (arbitrary/malicious) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$ | $\lfloor (N-1)/3 \rfloor$ |
| **Leader Required** | Yes (single strong leader) | Optional; Multi-Paxos uses one | Yes (primary + replicas) |
| **Message Rounds per Commit** | 1 (AppendEntries + ACK) | 2 (Prepare + Accept) | 3 (Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit) |
| **Understandability** | High (modular design) | Low (deceptively simple but tricky) | Medium |
| **Blockchain Suitability (2 marks)** | Consortium/Permissioned (e.g., Hyperledger Fabric Raft) | Rarely used directly; ZAB (ZooKeeper) is a variant | Permissioned with strong trust needs (e.g., classic Hyperledger Fabric) |

**Justification (3 marks):**

- **Raft vs. Paxos**: Both tolerate crash faults and have identical fault tolerance bound $\lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$. However, Raft decomposes the problem into leader election, log replication, and safety, with a strong leader model. This makes Raft significantly more understandable and practically implementable. Paxos, in contrast, is notoriously subtle and has many variants. For blockchain, Raft is preferred for its simplicity in consortium networks.

- **Raft vs. PBFT**: PBFT tolerates Byzantine faults (malicious nodes), making it suitable for untrusted environments. However, PBFT requires $3f+1$ nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine failures, has higher message complexity (3 rounds vs. 1 in Raft), and is more complex. Raft, restricted to crash faults, has lower overhead and is preferred when all participants are known and trusted (consortium settings).

- **Public Blockchains (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum)**: Use neither Raft, Paxos, nor PBFT directly. They use **Nakamoto Consensus** (Proof of Work) or **PoS-based BFT protocols** because they require tolerance to Sybil attacks and open participation. Raft's design assumes a fixed, known set of nodes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "term" with "round"**: A *term* in Raft spans an entire leadership epoch, not a single message round. Students frequently lose 1 mark by treating terms as round numbers.
> 2. **Quorum calculation error**: For an *even* N, students often write $N/2$ instead of $N/2 + 1$. **Always**: $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$. For $N=4$, $Q = 3$, *not* 2.
> 3. **Fault tolerance confusion**: Raft is **Crash Fault Tolerant (CFT)**, not Byzantine. Writing "Raft tolerates malicious nodes" is a factual error worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the "log up-to-date" criterion**: Forgetting that a candidate's log must be at least as up-to-date as the voter's to win a vote costs marks in leadership election answers.
> 5. **Misstating commit rule**: A leader commits an entry only when a *majority* of nodes (not all) have replicated it. Saying "all followers" instead of "majority" is incorrect.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams**: KTU examiners explicitly reward diagrams (state machines, RPC flows). A 14-mark answer without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Raft Consensus Algorithm**

- **Raft = "Understandable Paxos"** for crash fault tolerance. (1 mark question)
- **Three States**: Follower ⇄ Candidate → Leader. (state machine diagram is a favorite KTU question)
- **Three Sub-Problems**: Leader Election, Log Replication, Safety.
- **Term = Logical Clock**; increments on every election attempt. Higher terms override lower terms.
- **Election Timeout**: Randomized per node, typically 150–300 ms, to prevent split votes.
- **Heartbeat Interval**: ~50 ms, must be **< election timeout** to maintain leadership.
- **Quorum**: $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$. **Majority is mandatory** for both leader election and commit.
- **Crash Fault Tolerance**: $F = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$. For N=5, F=2, Q=3.
- **Log Replication**: Leader sends `AppendEntries`; commits when majority ACKs.
- **Log Matching Property**: Same `(index, term)` ⇒ same command and identical prefix.
- **Up-to-Date Criterion**: Used to grant votes; ensures Leader Completeness.
- **Raft is CFT, not BFT** — suitable for permissioned blockchains, not public ones.
- **Raft is used in**: etcd (Kubernetes), Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, **Hyperledger Fabric (Raft ordering service)**.
- **Comparison**: Raft < PBFT in fault tolerance class but > Paxos in understandability. Raft has lower message overhead than PBFT (1 round vs. 3 rounds).
- **Safety Properties to memorize**: Election Safety, Leader Append-Only, Log Matching, Leader Completeness, State Machine Safety.
- **Draw it**: Always draw the state transition diagram and the AppendEntries flow in 14-mark answers — KTU examiners award marks for clear diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) Algorithms - Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) Algorithms - Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** is the property of a distributed computing system that enables it to reach **consensus** (a single, agreed-upon value or state) and continue operating correctly even when some of its participating nodes fail or act maliciously — sending conflicting, incorrect, or deliberately deceptive information to different parts of the network. A node exhibiting such arbitrary misbehavior is called a **Byzantine Faulty node** (or simply *Byzantine*).

**Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** is a state-machine replication algorithm introduced by **Miguel Castro and Barbara Liskov (1999)** that solves the Byzantine Generals Problem in an *asynchronous* distributed system. It guarantees safety (all honest nodes agree on the same sequence of operations) and liveness (honest clients eventually receive replies) provided the total number of nodes is at least **3f + 1**, where **f** is the maximum number of simultaneously Byzantine (malicious/faulty) nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PBFT is a *permissioned/consortium* blockchain consensus mechanism. It is **NOT** typically used in public, anonymous chains like Bitcoin/Ethereum (which use Proof of Work / Proof of Stake) because PBFT requires known, identified validators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Byzantine Fault** — Any arbitrary deviation from the protocol (crash, omission, timing error, malicious intent).
> - **Byzantine Generals Problem** — The classical problem formulation by Lamport, Shostak, Pease (1982).
> - **State Machine Replication** — Running identical deterministic services on multiple replicas.
> - **Quorum** — A subset of replicas whose agreement is treated as global agreement.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine **ten generals surrounding an enemy city at night**, communicating only by messengers. They must all agree on a single plan: **ATTACK** or **RETREAT**. If even one traitor general sends "ATTACK" to some generals and "RETREAT" to others, the loyal generals may attack while others retreat — total disaster.

**The problem:** How many generals can be traitors and *still* let the loyal generals reach unanimous agreement?

**The answer (Lamport et al.):** With *n* generals, you can tolerate up to *f* traitors only if:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

So **4 generals can tolerate 1 traitor, 7 generals can tolerate 2 traitors, 10 generals can tolerate 3 traitors**, and so on.

**PBFT is the practical solution to this problem.** It gives generals (replicas) a step-by-step voting protocol so that, despite traitors, all loyal generals end up with the *same* decision in their logbook — and clients outside the city (end-users) trust that decision.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world PBFT analog:** A bank transaction that is simultaneously processed by 4 internal servers. Even if one server is hacked and tries to approve a fake transfer, the other 3 honest servers will outvote it and reject the malicious action. PBFT is the protocol that orchestrates this voting.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants to Remember

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
|-----------|---------------|---------|
| Minimum replicas | **3f + 1** | Smallest set that tolerates *f* Byzantine faults |
| Maximum tolerated faults | **f = ⌊(n − 1) / 3⌋** | Floor function of one-third minority |
| PBFT phases | **3 phases** | Pre-Prepare → Prepare → Commit |
| Views | Rotating | Each *view* has a designated primary |
| Message complexity | **O(n²)** per request | Communication grows quadratically |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why We Need BFT — The Failure Spectrum

Distributed systems can experience failures ranging from benign to fully malicious:

| Failure Class | Behavior | Examples | Tolerated By |
|---------------|----------|----------|--------------|
| **Crash Fault** | Node stops sending messages | Power loss, OS crash | Paxos, Raft |
| **Omission Fault** | Node skips some messages | Network drop | Crash-tolerant protocols |
| **Byzantine Fault** | Node sends *arbitrary* messages (lies, contradicts) | Hacked server, buggy code, malicious actor | **PBFT only** |

PBFT is the *strictest* correctness model — anything that works under BFT automatically works under simpler failure models.

### 2.2 The Three Phases of PBFT (Operational Logic)

PBFT progresses through a deterministic sequence. Let **n** = total replicas, **f** = max Byzantine faults, **v** = current view number, **n** = sequence number, **m** = client request, **d** = message digest.

#### Phase 1 — PRE-PREPARE (Primary proposes)
1. **Client** sends request *⟨REQUEST, o, t, c⟩* to the **primary** (leader).
2. The **primary** assigns a sequence number *n* and broadcasts a **PRE-PREPARE** message: *⟨⟨PRE-PREPARE, v, n, d⟩, m⟩* to all backup replicas.
3. A backup accepts the PRE-PREPARE only if:
   - Its signature verifies.
   - It is in view *v* (no conflicting PRE-PREPARE accepted earlier for same *v, n*).
   - Sequence number *n* is within acceptable bounds `[h, H]`.

#### Phase 2 — PREPARE (Backups broadcast agreement)
1. Each backup that accepted the PRE-PREPARE broadcasts **PREPARE** message: *⟨PREPARE, v, n, d, i⟩* to all replicas (including primary).
2. Replicas collect PREPARE messages. They mark the request as **prepared** only when they have received **2f valid matching PREPARE messages** from *different* replicas (in addition to the PRE-PREPARE).

#### Phase 3 — COMMIT (Backups confirm execution)
1. Each replica that reached "prepared" state broadcasts **COMMIT** message: *⟨COMMIT, v, n, d, i⟩* to all replicas.
2. Replicas mark the request as **committed** when they collect **2f + 1 valid matching COMMIT messages** (including their own).
3. Each replica then **executes** the request and sends **REPLY** *⟨REPLY, v, t, c, i, r⟩* directly to the client.
4. The client accepts the result when it receives **f + 1 matching REPLYs** from different replicas with the same result *r* and timestamp *t*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **"Pre-Prepare is by Primary. Prepare and Commit are by All. Reply is to Client."** The number sequence to remember is **2f → 2f+1 → f+1** for Prepare-threshold, Commit-threshold, Client-threshold respectively.

### 2.3 View Changes (Recovering from a Faulty Primary)

If the primary fails or acts maliciously, a **view change** is triggered:

1. Backup detects timeout or inconsistency → broadcasts **VIEW-CHANGE** *⟨VIEW-CHANGE, v+1, n, C, P, i⟩* where C is the prepared-certificate set.
2. New primary for view *v+1* collects **2f + 1 valid VIEW-CHANGE** messages.
3. New primary broadcasts **NEW-VIEW** *⟨NEW-VIEW, v+1, V, X⟩* where V is the set of VIEW-CHANGE messages and X is the new pre-prepare messages.
4. Backups verify, accept the new view, and resume normal operation.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum replicas to tolerate *f* Byzantine faults | Standard KTU 2-mark question |
| $f_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n-1}{3} \right\rfloor$ | Max Byzantine faults a system of size *n* can survive | Common derivation question |
| $Q_{\text{prepare}} = 2f$ | Threshold of matching PREPARE messages | Phase analysis |
| $Q_{\text{commit}} = 2f + 1$ | Threshold of matching COMMIT messages | Phase analysis |
| $Q_{\text{client}} = f + 1$ | Threshold of matching REPLY messages for client | Client-side finality |
| $n \geq 2f + 1$ | Minimum for Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT — Paxos/Raft) | Contrast question |
| $\text{Messages per request} = O(n^2)$ | Communication complexity | Frequently asked |
| $v$ | View number (epoch / leadership term) | Used in view change |
| $n$ | Sequence number assigned to a request | Used in pre-prepare |
| $\sigma_i$ | Digital signature of replica *i* | Authenticity |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hyperledger Fabric** — Uses PBFT (or its variants like SBFT) for ordering transactions in permissioned enterprise blockchains.
- **Banking & Finance** — Cross-border settlement systems (e.g., JPMorgan's Quorum, Visa's BFT-based ledgers).
- **Air Traffic Control** — Where incorrect coordination (a Byzantine-like fault) is catastrophic.
- **Replicated State Machines** — Google's Chubby, etcd-Raft (a CFT cousin), distributed databases.
- **Digital Asset Custody** — Institutional crypto custodians (Custodia, Anchorage) use BFT-based consensus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Exactly 3f + 1 Replicas?

This is the **most important derivation for KTU exams**. We must prove that in an asynchronous system, **n ≥ 3f + 1** is both *necessary* and *sufficient* for Byzantine agreement.

#### Setup
Let **N** = total replicas, **H** = honest replicas, **F** = Byzantine faulty replicas, so **N = H + F**. To make progress, honest replicas must have a quorum **Q** that:
1. Contains at least one honest majority (to avoid being misled by F).
2. Intersects any other quorum **Q′** (so all quorums agree on one decision).

#### Step 1: Quorum must contain at least one honest replica
A quorum is trusted only if at least one honest replica is in it, otherwise *every* quorum could be entirely Byzantine and the system would accept a malicious value.

So: $\vert Q \vert \geq F + 1$

#### Step 2: Two quorums must intersect in at least one honest replica
If two quorums **Q₁** and **Q₂** were disjoint (no overlap), a Byzantine coalition could populate one with lie-A and the other with lie-B, splitting the honest nodes into two camps. So:

$$\vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert - \vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \leq N$$

Since we need $\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$ (to prevent inconsistency), and assuming symmetric quorums $\vert Q \vert = q$:

$$2q - 1 \leq N \quad \Rightarrow \quad q \leq \dfrac{N+1}{2}$$

#### Step 3: Combine both requirements
The quorum must satisfy BOTH:
- $q \geq F + 1$ (at least one honest member — Step 1)
- $q \leq \dfrac{N+1}{2}$ (Step 2 upper bound)

Therefore:

$$F + 1 \leq \dfrac{N+1}{2}$$

Solving for **N**:

$$\begin{aligned}
2(F + 1) &\leq N + 1 \\
2F + 2 &\leq N + 1 \\
N &\geq 2F + 1
\end{aligned}$$

So a quorum system only needs *2F + 1* replicas — but that is the bound for **Crash Fault Tolerance**, not Byzantine. PBFT must defeat *active* liars, not just silent ones.

#### Step 4: Byzantine adversary requires an extra honest margin
In a Byzantine setting, the adversary can:
- Send **different** messages to different honest replicas (equivocation).
- Vote dishonestly in *every* phase.

So an honest quorum must overlap with *another* honest quorum even if the adversary controls *F* extra votes. The quorum itself must contain **strictly more honest members than the entire faulty set can muster** — meaning the quorum's honest count must exceed *F*.

If $\vert Q \vert = 2F + 1$ (Crash bound), and all *F* faulty nodes vote against the honest decision, the honest votes inside *Q* are only $\vert Q \vert - F = F + 1$, which can be *equally* counter-balanced by F lying votes. No strict majority.

We need *strictly more* honest voters inside Q than total voters outside:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{in Q}} &> \text{Total votes possible outside honest cooperation} \\
(2F + 1) - F &> F \\
F + 1 &> F \quad \checkmark \text{ (only just one extra — not safe under asynchrony)}
\end{aligned}$$

To get a *provable* strict majority under asynchrony, the quorum must grow to **3F + 1**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } N &= 3F + 1 \\
\text{Honest replicas } H &= N - F = 2F + 1 \\
\text{Quorum size } q &= 2F + 1 \quad \text{(so any two quorums intersect in at least } F+1 \text{ replicas)} \\
\text{In any quorum of } 2F+1 \text{ nodes, honest count} &= q - F = F + 1 \\
\text{Adversary max votes} &= F \\
F + 1 &> F \quad \checkmark \text{ strict honest majority guaranteed}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 5: Tight bound proof
Suppose $N = 3F$ (one less than needed). Then $H = 2F$. A quorum of $2F$ chosen adversarially could contain **all F faulty + F honest**, isolating the remaining $F$ honest nodes — no quorum can guarantee a strict honest majority. Thus $N \geq 3F + 1$ is **tight (necessary and sufficient)**.

$$\boxed{\,N_{\min} = 3f + 1\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A permissioned blockchain consortium wants to deploy PBFT. If the consortium requires surviving up to **5 simultaneously malicious nodes**, how many replicas must be deployed? Verify the quorum thresholds.

**Solution:**

Given $f = 5$.

Total replicas:

$$n = 3f + 1 = 3(5) + 1 = \mathbf{16 \text{ replicas}}$$

Prepare threshold (needed for "prepared" state):

$$Q_{\text{prepare}} = 2f = 2(5) = 10 \text{ matching messages}$$

Commit threshold (needed for "committed" state):

$$Q_{\text{commit}} = 2f + 1 = 11 \text{ matching messages}$$

Client reply threshold (for client acceptance):

$$Q_{\text{client}} = f + 1 = 6 \text{ matching replies}$$

Honest replicas guaranteed:

$$H = n - f = 16 - 5 = 11 \text{ honest replicas}$$

Verification — any quorum of 11 contains at least $11 - 5 = 6$ honest nodes, strictly exceeding the 5 adversarial nodes. ✅

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | PBFT | Paxos / Raft (CFT) | PoW (Nakamoto) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fault model | Byzantine (worst) | Crash-only | Crash + rational adversary |
| Replicas needed | **3f + 1** | 2f + 1 | Unlimited miners |
| Throughput | **High (~thousand TPS)** | High | Low (~7 TPS for Bitcoin) |
| Latency | Low (sub-second) | Low | High (minutes) |
| Finality | Immediate at commit | Immediate | Probabilistic (6+ confirmations) |
| Energy | Low | Low | Extremely high |
| Permissioning | **Permissioned (identity known)** | Permissioned | Permissionless |
| Message complexity | O(n²) | O(n) | O(n) broadcast |
| KTU typical score | 14-mark full question | 7-mark contrast | 7-mark contrast |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PBFT Phase Flow Diagram (Block-Level Architecture)

This diagram maps the full life-cycle of a single client request through the PBFT state machine, including view change fallback.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CLIENT: Send Request to Primary"] --> B["PRIMARY: Assign Sequence Number n"]
    B --> C["PRIMARY: Broadcast PRE-PREPARE v,n,d"]

    C --> D1["BACKUP 0: Verify PRE-PREPARE"]
    C --> D2["BACKUP 1: Verify PRE-PREPARE"]
    C --> D3["BACKUP i: Verify PRE-PREPARE"]

    D1 --> E1["BACKUP 0: Broadcast PREPARE"]
    D2 --> E2["BACKUP 1: Broadcast PREPARE"]
    D3 --> E3["BACKUP i: Broadcast PREPARE"]

    E1 --> F["COLLECT 2f matching PREPARE messages"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> G["STATE: PREPARED reached"]

    G --> H1["REPLICA: Broadcast COMMIT"]
    G --> H2["REPLICA: Broadcast COMMIT"]
    G --> H3["REPLICA: Broadcast COMMIT"]

    H1 --> I["COLLECT 2f+1 matching COMMIT messages"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J["STATE: COMMITTED-LOCAL reached"]
    J --> K["EXECUTE request and produce result r"]

    K --> L1["REPLICA 0: Send REPLY to Client"]
    K --> L2["REPLICA 1: Send REPLY to Client"]
    K --> L3["REPLICA i: Send REPLY to Client"]

    L1 --> M["CLIENT: Collect f+1 matching REPLYs"]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M

    M --> N["REQUEST ACCEPTED BY CLIENT"]

    G -.timeout or fault detected.-> O["TRIGGER VIEW CHANGE"]
    O --> P["BACKUPS: Broadcast VIEW-CHANGE v+1"]
    P --> Q["NEW PRIMARY: Collect 2f+1 VIEW-CHANGE"]
    Q --> R["NEW PRIMARY: Broadcast NEW-VIEW v+1"]
    R --> S["SYSTEM RESUMES IN NEW VIEW"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Replica State Machine — Replica Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Replica initialized

    Idle --> PrePrepared: Receive valid PRE-PREPARE from primary
    PrePrepared --> Prepared: Collect 2f matching PREPARE messages
    Prepared --> CommittedLocal: Collect 2f+1 matching COMMIT messages
    CommittedLocal --> Executed: Run operation on state machine
    Executed --> Replied: Send REPLY to client

    Replied --> Idle: Ready for next request

    Idle --> ViewChange: Timeout or detect faulty primary
    PrePrepared --> ViewChange: Timeout or detect faulty primary
    Prepared --> ViewChange: Timeout or detect faulty primary

    ViewChange --> NewView: Receive NEW-VIEW from new primary
    NewView --> Idle: Resume normal operation

    Executed --> [*]: Replica shut down
```

### 4.3 Byzantine vs Crash Fault Tolerance — Architectural Contrast

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRASH_FAULT_TOLERANCE["CRASH FAULT TOLERANCE (CFT)"]
        A1["Replica 1 - Honest"] --> Q1["QUORUM 2f+1"]
        A2["Replica 2 - Honest"] --> Q1
        A3["Replica 3 - CRASHED Silent"] --> Q1
        Q1 --> OUT1["Agreement Reached"]
    end

    subgraph BYZANTINE_FAULT_TOLERANCE["BYZANTINE FAULT TOLERANCE (BFT)"]
        B1["Replica 1 - Honest"] --> Q2["QUORUM 3f+1"]
        B2["Replica 2 - Honest"] --> Q2
        B3["Replica 3 - Honest"] --> Q2
        B4["Replica 4 - LIAR sending false msgs"] --> Q2
        B5["Replica 5 - LIAR equivocating"] --> Q2
        Q2 --> OUT2["Agreement still reached"]
    end

    style A3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 PBFT Communication Pattern (Round-by-Round)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant P as Primary
    participant B1 as Backup 1
    participant B2 as Backup 2
    participant B3 as Backup 3
    participant B4 as Backup 4

    C->>P: REQUEST operation o
    P->>B1: PRE-PREPARE v,n,digest
    P->>B2: PRE-PREPARE v,n,digest
    P->>B3: PRE-PREPARE v,n,digest
    P->>B4: PRE-PREPARE v,n,digest

    B1->>P: PREPARE v,n,digest,1
    B1->>B2: PREPARE v,n,digest,1
    B1->>B3: PREPARE v,n,digest,1
    B1->>B4: PREPARE v,n,digest,1

    B2->>P: PREPARE v,n,digest,2
    B2->>B3: PREPARE v,n,digest,2
    B2->>B4: PREPARE v,n,digest,2

    B3->>P: COMMIT v,n,digest,3
    B3->>B4: COMMIT v,n,digest,3

    B4->>C: REPLY v,t,c,result
    B3->>C: REPLY v,t,c,result
    B2->>C: REPLY v,t,c,result

    C->>C: Accept result on f+1 matching REPLYs
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The 3f + 1 formula is BFT-specific.** Writing 2f + 1 here will be marked WRONG (that is Paxos/Raft CFT).
> 2. **Don't confuse the three thresholds.** Prepare = 2f, Commit = 2f+1, Client-Reply = f+1. Students frequently swap them.
> 3. **Phase names are case-sensitive in KTU answers** — Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit (three separate words, hyphenated Pre-Prepare).
> 4. **View Change vs New View** — VIEW-CHANGE is the *request* sent by backups; NEW-VIEW is the *response* broadcast by the new primary. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for confusing these.
> 5. **PBFT is synchronous in latency assumption but asynchronous in message delivery model** — write both halves if asked.

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the Byzantine Generals Problem. State the minimum number of generals required to tolerate *f* traitors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Byzantine Generals Problem**, formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982), describes the challenge of reaching agreement among distributed parties when some of them may act maliciously and send contradictory information. In the classic formulation, several divisions of an army surround an enemy city and must unanimously agree to either *attack* or *retreat*, communicating only via messengers, while some generals may be traitors who try to prevent loyal generals from reaching agreement.

The minimum number of generals required to tolerate *f* traitors is:

$$n_{\min} = 3f + 1$$

For example, to tolerate 2 traitors, at least **7 generals** are required (since 3·2 + 1 = 7). This is a tight bound — proven both necessary and sufficient under asynchronous message delivery with authenticated messages.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — example / tight bound explanation)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT) and Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT). Give one protocol example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT) | Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault Model** | Nodes only stop (crash) silently | Nodes can act arbitrarily — lie, equivocate, send contradictory data |
| **Minimum Replicas** | 2f + 1 | 3f + 1 |
| **Adversary Capability** | Passive (silent failures) | Active (malicious / arbitrary behavior) |
| **Protocol Examples** | Paxos, Raft | **PBFT**, HotStuff, Tendermint |
| **Use Case** | Datacenter replication, Kubernetes (etcd) | Blockchain consortiums, financial ledgers |

*(1 mark — CFT definition + example, 1 mark — BFT definition + example, 1 mark — replica difference)*

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Operations & Mechanism Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]** Explain in detail the **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** algorithm with a neat diagram. Your answer must cover:

**(a)** [7 marks] The three phases of PBFT — Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit — with the message thresholds and the role of the primary.

**(b)** [7 marks] View change protocol when the primary is faulty, and the derivation showing why PBFT requires **3f + 1** replicas to tolerate *f* Byzantine faults.

---

##### Model Solution

### (a) The Three Phases of PBFT

PBFT operates on a **state-machine replication** model. The system consists of one **primary (leader)** and *n − 1* **backups (followers)**, all replicas executing the same deterministic service.

**Phase 1 — Pre-Prepare**
The client *c* sends a request *⟨REQUEST, o, t, c⟩* to the primary. The primary assigns a sequence number *n*, computes a digest *d = D(m)* of the request message *m*, and broadcasts:

$$\langle \text{PRE-PREPARE}, v, n, d \rangle, m$$

to all backups. A backup accepts the PRE-PREPARE only if it has not accepted another PRE-PREPARE for the same view *v* and sequence number *n* with a different digest, and the signature verifies.

**Phase 2 — Prepare**
Each backup that accepted the PRE-PREPARE broadcasts:

$$\langle \text{PREPARE}, v, n, d, i \rangle$$

to all other replicas (including the primary), where *i* is the sender's replica ID. The replica is marked **prepared** when it has gathered **2f PREPARE messages** matching the PRE-PREPARE (from different replicas with verified signatures).

**Phase 3 — Commit**
Each "prepared" replica broadcasts:

$$\langle \text{COMMIT}, v, n, d, i \rangle$$

It transitions to the **committed-local** state upon collecting **2f + 1 matching COMMIT messages**. At this point, every honest replica is guaranteed to eventually reach the same state.

**Execution and Reply**
Each committed replica executes the operation *o* on its state machine, obtains result *r*, and sends a direct REPLY:

$$\langle \text{REPLY}, v, t, c, i, r \rangle$$

to the client. The client accepts *r* when it receives **f + 1 matching REPLYs** with the same result and timestamp from distinct replicas.

> **Summary table of thresholds:**
>
> | State Transition | Threshold |
> |---|---|
> | Request → Pre-Prepared | 1 valid PRE-PREPARE |
> | Pre-Prepared → Prepared | **2f** matching PREPAREs |
> | Prepared → Committed-Local | **2f + 1** matching COMMITs |
> | Client acceptance | **f + 1** matching REPLYs |

---

### (b) View Change Protocol & 3f + 1 Derivation

**View Change Procedure:**
If a backup times out waiting for a request or detects that the primary is sending conflicting messages, it triggers a **view change**:

1. The suspecting backup broadcasts $\langle \text{VIEW-CHANGE}, v+1, n, C, P, i \rangle$, where *C* is the set of prepared-certificates it holds, and *P* is the corresponding set of PRE-PREPARE + matching PREPAREs.
2. The new primary for view *v + 1* waits until it collects **2f + 1 valid VIEW-CHANGE** messages.
3. The new primary broadcasts $\langle \text{NEW-VIEW}, v+1, V, X \rangle$ where *V* is the set of VIEW-CHANGE messages and *X* is the set of new PRE-PREPARE messages it constructs for valid sequence numbers.
4. Backups verify, accept the new view, and resume normal operation in view *v + 1*.

**Why 3f + 1 — Compact Derivation:**

Let *N* = total replicas, *F* = max Byzantine faults, *H = N − F* = honest replicas. For PBFT to satisfy both safety (all honest agree) and liveness (progress happens) under asynchrony:

**Requirement 1: Any two quorums must intersect in at least one honest replica.**
$$\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq F + 1$$

**Requirement 2: The quorum's honest members must form a strict majority to override the Byzantine adversary.**
A quorum of size *q* contains $q - F$ honest members (worst case all F faults are inside). For honest majority:

$$q - F > F \quad \Rightarrow \quad q > 2F \quad \Rightarrow \quad q \geq 2F + 1$$

**Combining:**
Any quorum of size $q \geq 2F + 1$ must intersect with every other such quorum. By the pigeonhole principle, two quorums of size $2F + 1$ in a universe of *N* nodes intersect in at least:

$$\begin{aligned}
\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert &= \vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert - N \\
&= (2F + 1) + (2F + 1) - N \\
&= 4F + 2 - N
\end{aligned}$$

For this intersection to contain at least $F + 1$ honest nodes:

$$\begin{aligned}
4F + 2 - N &\geq F + 1 \\
N &\leq 3F + 1
\end{aligned}$$

But $N$ must be *at least* $3F + 1$ for the inequality to be satisfiable as a system size. Therefore:

$$\boxed{N = 3F + 1}$$

This bound is **tight**: with $N = 3F$, an adversary could partition honest nodes into two non-intersecting quorums of size $2F$ each, breaking consensus. Hence $N \geq 3F + 1$ is both necessary and sufficient.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Application & Analysis Focus*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Answer the following:

**(a)** [7 marks] Compare and contrast **PBFT** with **Proof of Work (PoW)** consensus along the dimensions: fault model, energy consumption, throughput, finality, and permissioning. Explain why PBFT cannot be used directly in Bitcoin.

**(b)** [7 marks] A Hyperledger Fabric network has **10 ordering service nodes** running PBFT. **(i)** Compute the maximum number of simultaneously malicious (Byzantine) nodes that can be tolerated. **(ii)** Determine the prepare, commit, and client-reply thresholds. **(iii)** If a client sends a transaction and receives 6 matching REPLYs from distinct replicas with identical result and timestamp, does the client accept the result? Justify.

---

##### Model Solution

### (a) PBFT vs Proof of Work (PoW) — Comparative Analysis

| Dimension | PBFT | Proof of Work (Bitcoin) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault Model** | Byzantine — explicit handling of malicious, equivocating nodes | Rational adversary — economic incentives deter cheating; technically Byzantine but mitigated by 51% threshold |
| **Energy Consumption** | Negligible — only message passing and digital signatures | Extreme — billions of hash computations per block (~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin) |
| **Throughput** | **~1000–10000 TPS** (sub-second finality) | **~7 TPS** for Bitcoin, ~15–30 TPS for Ethereum (pre-merge) |
| **Finality** | **Deterministic** — once committed, cannot be reverted | **Probabilistic** — finality probability grows with confirmations; ~6 blocks ≈ 99.9% safe |
| **Permissioning** | **Permissioned** — all validator identities known and authorized | **Permissionless** — anyone can join as a miner without revealing identity |
| **Communication** | O(n²) — grows quadratically with replicas | O(n) — each block propagated once |
| **Scalability** | Limited by n² messaging | Scales to thousands of nodes but slow |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise consortium, banking, supply chain | Public cryptocurrency, censorship-resistant ledgers |

**Why PBFT cannot be used directly in Bitcoin (3 key reasons):**

1. **Identity Requirement:** PBFT requires each replica's identity to be known, verified, and authenticated via digital signatures. Bitcoin is permissionless — anyone can join anonymously with just a public key, with no Sybil-resistance without PoW.
2. **Sybil Vulnerability:** In a permissionless setting, an attacker could spawn millions of fake replicas and overwhelm honest ones. PBFT's 3f + 1 bound assumes *f* is bounded by honest identity provisioning.
3. **Asynchronous Global Network:** Bitcoin's global peer-to-peer network has unbounded message delays. PBFT assumes eventual delivery but uses timeouts; a global-scale PBFT would suffer constant view changes.

*(Marks: 1 mark per dimension × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for the Bitcoin explanation)*

---

### (b) Numerical Computation on Hyperledger Fabric

**Given:** Total replicas $n = 10$, PBFT consensus.

**(i) Maximum tolerated Byzantine nodes:**

$$f_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{n - 1}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{10 - 1}{3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3 \right\rfloor = \mathbf{3}$$

So the network can survive up to **3 simultaneously malicious nodes**.

Verification: $3f + 1 = 3(3) + 1 = 10 = n$ ✅ (tight bound — meets exactly the minimum).

**(ii) Quorum thresholds:**

$$Q_{\text{prepare}} = 2f = 2(3) = \mathbf{6 \text{ matching PREPARE messages}}$$

$$Q_{\text{commit}} = 2f + 1 = \mathbf{7 \text{ matching COMMIT messages}}$$

$$Q_{\text{client}} = f + 1 = \mathbf{4 \text{ matching REPLY messages}}$$

**(iii) Client acceptance analysis:**

The client received **6 matching REPLYs** from distinct replicas, all with identical result *r* and timestamp *t*.

Required threshold for client acceptance = **f + 1 = 4 matching REPLYs**.

Since the client has 6 ≥ 4, the **client accepts the result**. ✅

**Justification (3 points):**
1. 6 ≥ 4 → threshold satisfied.
2. f + 1 matching replies guarantee that at least one of them came from an honest replica, since at most *f = 3* replicas are Byzantine. With 6 distinct replicas responding identically, at least 3 must be honest, and honest replicas only reply after executing the committed operation — so the result is authentic.
3. The matching timestamps and results across multiple independent replicas rule out single-replica forking or replay attacks.
