*(Marks: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii) verdict + 2 marks for justification)*

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **BFT** = consensus under arbitrary / malicious failures; **CFT** = consensus under only crash failures.
- **PBFT** (Castro & Liskov, 1999) is the *first practical* BFT algorithm with polynomial message complexity.
- **Replica requirement:** $N \geq 3f + 1$ (NOT $2f + 1$ — that is Paxos/Raft).
- **Three phases:** Pre-Prepare (primary only) → Prepare (all replicas, 2f threshold) → Commit (all replicas, 2f + 1 threshold) → Reply (to client, f + 1 threshold).
- **View change** handles primary failures: VIEW-CHANGE (backups) → NEW-VIEW (new primary).
- **Client acceptance:** $f + 1$ matching REPLYs with same result and timestamp.
- **PBFT is permissioned** — cannot be used directly in public blockchains like Bitcoin due to identity, Sybil, and global-scale issues.
- **Communication complexity:** $O(n^2)$ per request — limits practical deployment to small consortiums (typically < 100 nodes).
- **Finality is deterministic** in PBFT (instant at commit) vs **probabilistic** in PoW.
- **Modern BFT successors:** HotStuff (used in Facebook Libra/Diem), Tendermint (Cosmos), SBFT, and Jolteon — all use 3f + 1 or similar bounds but optimize communication.
- **KTU-favorite questions:** (1) Derive 3f + 1. (2) Identify phase thresholds. (3) Compare PBFT with Raft/PoW. (4) Numerical: given n, find f, prepare/commit/client thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Work (PoW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proof of Work (PoW)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Proof of Work (PoW)** is a *consensus mechanism* originally conceptualized by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor in 1993 as a deterrence against denial-of-service attacks and email spamming, and later practically implemented by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008 as the foundational security pillar of the Bitcoin blockchain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Proof of Work (PoW) is a cryptographic consensus protocol in which network participants (miners) must expend significant computational energy to solve a mathematically hard, yet easily verifiable, hash-based puzzle. The first miner to find a valid solution is granted the right to append the next block to the blockchain and is rewarded with newly minted cryptocurrency (block subsidy) and transaction fees.*

Mathematically, the puzzle is reduced to finding a **nonce** (Number used ONCE) such that the double SHA-256 hash of the block header is less than a network-defined **target value**:

$$H(\text{BlockHeader}) = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Version} \mid\mid \text{PrevHash} \mid\mid \text{MerkleRoot} \mid\mid \text{Time} \mid\mid \text{Bits} \mid\mid \text{Nonce})) < T$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **Global Digital Lottery** played every 10 minutes:

1. The lottery master (the network) announces a target number. To win, you must roll a dice whose result, when combined with a chosen secret number (the **nonce**), produces a hash that begins with a specific number of zeros.
2. Every participant is essentially rolling a trillion-sided dice. There is **no shortcut or strategy** — the only method is brute-force trial.
3. Whoever finds a winning combination first shouts it out, and everyone can **instantly verify** the winning ticket by plugging the numbers into the official hash function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this design brilliant?**
> - **Asymmetric Cost:** Finding the solution is *hard* (requires ~10²⁰ hash operations for Bitcoin), but verifying it is *trivial* (one hash check).
> - **Sybil Resistance:** A malicious actor cannot create 1 million fake identities cheaply; they must spend real electricity per identity, making attacks prohibitively expensive.

---

## 1.3 Core Components & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical PoW Metrics (Bolded for KTU High-Yield Recall):**

| Component | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Target (T)** | 256-bit unsigned integer | Defines the puzzle difficulty |
| **Difficulty (D)** | Dimensionless ratio | Current target / genesis target |
| **Nonce** | 32-bit integer (0 to 2³² − 1) | Variable miner searches over |
| **Block Time** | **10 minutes (Bitcoin)** | Average time per block |
| **Difficulty Adjustment** | Every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) | Recalibration period |
| **Hash Function** | **SHA-256** (Bitcoin) | One-way, collision-resistant |
| **Block Reward (2024)** | **3.125 BTC** (post-4th halving) | Miner incentive |

---

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Timeline

- **1993:** Dwork & Naor — Original PoW for spam deterrence
- **1997:** Adam Back — *Hashcash* (precursor implementation)
- **2008:** Satoshi Nakamoto — Bitcoin whitepaper adopts PoW
- **2009:** Bitcoin Genesis Block mined
- **2015:** Ethereum launched (PoW-based, later migrated to PoS in 2022)
- **2024+:** Bitcoin continues PoW with the 4th halving event

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Proof of Work (Step-by-Step)

The PoW mechanism is governed by a sequence of deterministic and probabilistic operations:

1. **Transaction Aggregation:** Miners collect pending transactions from the **mempool** and verify digital signatures.
2. **Merkle Tree Construction:** Valid transactions are pairwise hashed repeatedly to produce a single **Merkle Root**, efficiently encoding all transactions.
3. **Block Header Assembly:** The miner constructs a 80-byte block header containing:
   - Software version (4 bytes)
   - Previous block hash (32 bytes)
   - Merkle root (32 bytes)
   - Timestamp (4 bytes)
   - Difficulty bits (4 bytes)
   - Nonce (4 bytes)
4. **Hashing Loop:** The miner iterates the nonce from 0 to 2³² − 1, recomputing the double SHA-256 hash of the header at each step.
5. **Target Comparison:** If the resulting hash interpreted as an integer is **strictly less than** the network target $T$, the puzzle is solved.
6. **Block Broadcast:** The winning miner broadcasts the block; other nodes verify the PoW in O(1) time and accept the block.
7. **Chain Extension:** The block is added to the chain, and the miner receives the **block subsidy** + **transaction fees**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - Steps 1–3 ensure **transaction integrity** (Merkle root binds them cryptographically).
> - Steps 4–5 implement the **computational cost** that makes Sybil attacks expensive.
> - Step 6 makes verification **cheap**, preserving decentralization.
> - Step 7 provides the **economic incentive** that aligns miner self-interest with network security.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most important** reference for solving KTU 2024 numerical problems on PoW. Note the use of `\mid` and `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hash Target Equation | $\text{SHA256}^2(\text{Header}) < T$ | $T$ is a 256-bit integer |
| 2 | Difficulty Definition | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{genesis}}}{T_{\text{current}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Target from Difficulty | $T = \dfrac{T_{\text{genesis}}}{D}$ | Genesis target = 0x1d00ffff |
| 4 | Expected Hashes per Block | $E[H] = \dfrac{2^{256}}{T}$ | Geometric distribution mean |
| 5 | Mining Probability per Hash | $p = \dfrac{T}{2^{256}}$ | $0 < p < 1$ |
| 6 | Average Time to Find Block | $E[t] = \dfrac{E[H]}{r} = \dfrac{2^{256}}{T \cdot r}$ | $r$ = hash rate (H/s) |
| 7 | Network Difficulty Update | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{\text{ActualTime}}{\text{2016} \times 600}$ | Adjustment every 2016 blocks |
| 8 | Block Reward (Halving) | $R_n = \dfrac{R_0}{2^n}$ | $R_0 = 50$ BTC, $n$ = halvings |
| 9 | Max Supply (Bitcoin) | $S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{32} 210000 \cdot \dfrac{50}{2^n} = 20999999.9769$ BTC | ~21 million cap |
| 10 | 51% Attack Cost | $C_{\text{attack}} \propto r_{\text{attacker}} \cdot t_{\text{attack}}$ | Proportional to sustained hash rate |
| 11 | Block Header Size | $4 + 32 + 32 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 80$ bytes | Fixed size in Bitcoin |
| 12 | Network Hash Rate | $r_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{2^{256} \cdot 2016}{T \cdot 1209600}$ | H/s, derived from difficulty |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The PoW paradigm, although energy-intensive, has direct applications beyond cryptocurrency:

- **Anti-Spam Systems:** Hashcash-style PoW attached to emails to deter bulk spamming.
- **DDoS Mitigation:** Web servers can require a small PoW from clients (e.g., **Anubis**, **Friendly Captcha**), forcing attackers to pay computational cost per request.
- **Decentralized Identity & Timestamping:** Document timestamping services like *OriginStamp* use PoW to prove a document existed at a particular time.
- **Cold Storage Security:** Hardware wallets use PoW challenges to prevent automated brute-force PIN extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The asymmetric cost structure of PoW is the *single most important* concept in this module. Always frame answers around the "**hard to compute, easy to verify**" property.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Hashes to Find a Valid Nonce

The probability that a uniformly random 256-bit hash falls below the target $T$ is given by the ratio of the valid hash space to the total hash space:

$$p = \Pr[\text{hash} < T] = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

Since each hash attempt is independent, the number of trials $H$ required follows a **Geometric Distribution** with success probability $p$. The expected value of a geometric random variable is:

$$E[H] = \frac{1}{p}$$

Substituting $p$:

$$E[H] = \frac{1}{T/2^{256}} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

**Interpretation:** For a difficulty of 1 (highest target, easiest puzzle), $E[H]$ is approximately $2^{32}$ hashes. For Bitcoin's current difficulty of ~$10^{13}$, $E[H]$ is astronomically large — roughly $2.4 \times 10^{22}$ hashes per block.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Network-Wide Average Block Time

Let $r_{\text{net}}$ be the **aggregate hash rate** of the entire network in hashes per second (H/s). The number of hashes the network performs in time $t$ seconds is:

$$N(t) = r_{\text{net}} \cdot t$$

Setting $N(t) = E[H]$ for the expected block time:

$$r_{\text{net}} \cdot t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

Solving for $t_{\text{avg}}$:

$$t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{256}}{T \cdot r_{\text{net}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant equation reveals that the **average block time is inversely proportional to both the target and the network hash rate.** If hash rate doubles, the network automatically finds blocks twice as often — unless difficulty adjusts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bitcoin Difficulty Adjustment (Every 2016 Blocks)

Bitcoin's protocol enforces a constant average block time of **600 seconds** (10 minutes) by recalibrating the target every 2016 blocks. The expected duration of 2016 blocks at perfect timing is:

$$t_{\text{expected}} = 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ seconds (14 days)}$$

Let $t_{\text{actual}}$ be the wall-clock time taken to mine the last 2016 blocks. The protocol applies the following adjustment:

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}$$

**Boundary Conditions (Hard-coded in Bitcoin Core):**

- The adjustment factor is **clamped** to a maximum of **4×** and minimum of **0.25×** per period to prevent extreme volatility from sudden hash rate changes.

$$\text{clamp}(x, 0.25, 4) = \max(0.25, \min(4, x))$$

- Therefore:

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \text{clamp}\left(\frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600},\ 0.25,\ 4\right)$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose a 2016-block epoch actually took only **604,800 seconds** (7 days, half the expected time). This means the network was twice as fast as expected:

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{604{,}800}{1{,}209{,}600} = T_{\text{old}} \times 0.5$$

The new target is halved, **doubling the difficulty** to restore the 10-minute average.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Attack Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "51% Attack" Cost-Benefit Analysis (KTU 2024 Hot Topic):**

| Parameter | Honest Mining | 51% Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Required Hash Power** | Any fraction $\geq 0$ | Strictly $> 0.5 \times r_{\text{net}}$ |
| **Block Reward (per block)** | $R + \text{fees}$ | $0$ (invalidated) |
| **Capital Cost (ASICs)** | Proportional to share | $\geq 0.5 \times \text{total network capex}$ |
| **Electricity Cost (per block)** | $\dfrac{E[H]}{r_{\text{attacker}}} \times P_{\text{per W}}$ | Same hardware, doubled |
| **Probability of Success** | Deterministic acceptance | Probabilistic; doublespend possible |
| **Net Outcome** | Block subsidy gained | Network devaluation $\Rightarrow$ mining hardware loses value |

The cost of a 51% attack on Bitcoin in 2024 is estimated to exceed **$20 billion USD** for hardware alone, plus sustained electricity costs in the range of **$1 million per hour**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Block Reward After Halving

The block reward is halved every **210,000 blocks** (~4 years). The reward in the $n$-th halving epoch is:

$$R_n = \frac{R_0}{2^n} = \frac{50}{2^n} \text{ BTC}$$

| Halving Event | Block Height | Year | Reward (BTC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Genesis) | 0 | 2009 | 50 |
| 1st | 210,000 | 2012 | 25 |
| 2nd | 420,000 | 2016 | 12.5 |
| 3rd | 630,000 | 2020 | 6.25 |
| **4th** | **840,000** | **2024** | **3.125** |
| 5th | 1,050,000 | ~2028 | 1.5625 |
| 32nd | 6,720,000 | ~2140 | ~0 (satoshi-level) |

The geometric series of total Bitcoin supply converges:

$$S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot \frac{50}{2^n} = 210000 \times 50 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

The factor of 2 arises from summing the geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (1/2)^n = 2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PoW Mining

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete mining pipeline from transaction ingestion to chain extension:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Mining Round] --> B[Collect Pending Transactions from Mempool]
    B --> C[Verify Digital Signatures on Each Transaction]
    C --> D[Construct Merkle Tree and Compute Merkle Root]
    D --> E[Assemble 80-byte Block Header with Nonce Init 0]
    E --> F[Compute Double SHA-256 Hash of Header]
    F --> G{Hash less than Target T}
    G -- No --> H[Increment Nonce by 1]
    H --> I{Nonce less than 2 to power 32}
    I -- Yes --> F
    I -- No --> J[Update Coinbase Extra Nonce and Restart]
    J --> F
    G -- Yes --> K[Solution Found]
    K --> L[Broadcast Block to Peers]
    L --> M[Network Verification of PoW]
    M --> N[Block Added to Chain]
    N --> O[Miner Receives Block Subsidy and Fees]
    O --> P[End of Mining Round]
```

## 4.2 Block Header Structure (80 Bytes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BH[Block Header 80 bytes]
        V[Version: 4 bytes]
        P[Previous Block Hash: 32 bytes]
        M[Merkle Root: 32 bytes]
        T[Timestamp: 4 bytes]
        D[Difficulty Bits: 4 bytes]
        N[Nonce: 4 bytes]
    end
    V --> P
    P --> M
    M --> T
    T --> D
    D --> N
    N --> H[Double SHA-256]
    H --> CMP{Compare with Target}
```

## 4.3 Consensus Flow: Honest Mining vs. 51% Attack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Honest[Honest Mining Path]
        H1[Miner with fraction x of network hash] --> H2[Find valid block with probability x]
        H2 --> H3[Receive block reward R and fees]
        H3 --> H4[Network accepts block]
    end

    subgraph Attack[51% Attack Path]
        A1[Attacker with hash power > 0.5] --> A2[Mine secret parallel chain]
        A2 --> A3[Send transaction to victim merchant]
        A3 --> A4[Wait for confirmations]
        A4 --> A5[Publish longer secret chain to invalidate payment]
        A5 --> A6{Doublespend successful?}
    end

    Attack --> Cost[Cost: hardware and electricity exceeded 50 percent of total network]
    Cost --> Risk[Risk: network devaluation collapses mining investment]
```

## 4.4 PoW Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Net[Bitcoin P2P Network]
        M1[Full Node and Miner A] --- M2[Full Node and Miner B]
        M2 --- M3[Full Node and Miner C]
        M3 --- M4[Full Node only]
        M1 --- M3
        M2 --- M4
    end
    M1 -->|Broadcast candidate block| M2
    M2 -->|Verify and relay| M3
    M3 -->|Verify and relay| M4
    M4 -->|Validate and add to local chain| Chain[(Local Blockchain Copy)]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define Proof of Work. List any three key properties that make PoW suitable as a blockchain consensus mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

Proof of Work (PoW) is a consensus protocol that requires network participants to solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle before they are allowed to propose a new block. The puzzle typically involves finding a nonce such that the SHA-256 hash of the block header is below a predetermined target value.

**Three Key Properties:**

1. **Asymmetric Cost Structure:** Solving the puzzle is computationally expensive, but verifying the solution requires only a single hash computation. This asymmetry is essential for decentralized verification.

2. **Sybil Resistance:** Since each attempt at the puzzle requires real energy expenditure, an attacker cannot create unlimited fake identities cheaply. The cost of influence on the network scales linearly with the attacker's hardware and electricity budget.

3. **Probabilistic Finality:** A block becomes exponentially more secure as more blocks are appended on top of it. Reversing a transaction with $k$ confirmations requires redoing an expected $\sum_{i=1}^{k} 2^{256}/T$ hash operations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is a nonce in PoW? Explain why the extra nonce field is required in modern Bitcoin mining.**

**Model Answer:**

A **nonce** (*Number used ONCE*) is a 32-bit field in the block header that miners vary to search for a valid hash below the target. The miner iterates the nonce from 0 to $2^{32} - 1$ (approximately 4.29 billion values) in an inner loop.

**Why the Extra Nonce is Required:**

Modern ASIC miners can exhaust the entire 32-bit nonce space in **milliseconds**, far faster than a new block template can be assembled. To extend the search space without changing the block header layout, miners modify the **coinbase transaction** (the first transaction in the block, which collects the mining reward). The coinbase contains a special field called the **extra nonce**, which alters the Merkle root. By changing the extra nonce, the miner effectively changes the header hash without altering the nonce field itself, providing an additional $\sim 2^{64}$ search space.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (1 × 14 = 14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the complete Proof of Work consensus algorithm with a block diagram. Describe the role of the Merkle root, previous block hash, and difficulty bits in the block header. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Bitcoin-like blockchain network has a target value $T$ and a total network hash rate of $120$ EH/s. If the current target is $T = 2^{220}$, calculate:
   - (i) The expected number of hashes required to find a valid block.
   - (ii) The average block discovery time in seconds.
   - (iii) The probability that exactly 3 valid blocks are found in a 30-minute window, assuming independent discoveries. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation (7 Marks)

The Proof of Work consensus algorithm operates as follows:

1. **Transaction Collection:** Miners collect unconfirmed transactions from their mempool. Each transaction is verified for correct signatures, double-spend prevention, and sufficient fees.

2. **Merkle Root Construction:** Valid transactions are organized into a binary Merkle tree. Each leaf is the SHA-256 hash of a transaction. Internal nodes are SHA-256 hashes of the concatenation of their two children. The single root hash (32 bytes) cryptographically binds all transactions in the block.

3. **Block Header Construction:** The miner builds an 80-byte header containing six fields: Version, Previous Block Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Difficulty Bits, and Nonce.

4. **Hash Puzzle:** The miner computes:

$$\text{Hash} = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}))$$

   The puzzle is solved if the hash, interpreted as a 256-bit integer, satisfies:

$$\text{Hash} < T$$

5. **Broadcast and Verification:** The winning block is broadcast. Other nodes verify the PoW by recomputing the header hash, taking only O(1) time.

**Roles of Key Header Fields:**

| Field | Role |
|---|---|
| **Previous Block Hash** | Links each block to its parent, forming an immutable chain. Modifying any historical block would require redoing all subsequent PoWs. |
| **Merkle Root** | Cryptographic summary of all transactions; any tampering with a single transaction invalidates the root and the entire block's PoW. |
| **Difficulty Bits** | Encoded representation of the target $T$. Determines the puzzle's hardness; recalibrated every 2016 blocks. |
| **Nonce** | The variable the miner iterates over; the only field that changes during the search. |

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1 and 4.2** above for the Mermaid schematic of the mining pipeline and block header structure.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Network hash rate: $r = 120 \text{ EH/s} = 120 \times 10^{18} \text{ H/s} = 1.2 \times 10^{20} \text{ H/s}$
- Target: $T = 2^{220}$

**(i) Expected Number of Hashes:**

The probability of a valid hash on a single attempt is:

$$p = \frac{T}{2^{256}} = \frac{2^{220}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-36}$$

Therefore, the expected number of hashes is:

$$E[H] = \frac{1}{p} = 2^{36} = 68{,}719{,}476{,}736 \approx 6.872 \times 10^{10} \text{ hashes}$$

**(ii) Average Block Discovery Time:**

$$t_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E[H]}{r} = \frac{6.872 \times 10^{10}}{1.2 \times 10^{20}} = 5.727 \times 10^{-10} \text{ seconds}$$

This unrealistically low figure simply reflects the chosen parameters; in real Bitcoin, the target is much smaller (difficulty much higher).

**(iii) Probability of Exactly 3 Blocks in 30 Minutes:**

The number of valid blocks found in time $t$ follows a **Poisson distribution** with rate parameter:

$$\lambda = \frac{t}{t_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{1800}{5.727 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 3.143 \times 10^{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The expected value here is astronomically large, indicating that the assumed parameters are not realistic for an actual blockchain. KTU examiners typically award full marks if the **Poisson formula is correctly applied** to a reasonable $\lambda$.

The Poisson probability of exactly $k = 3$ blocks is:

$$P(X = 3) = \frac{\lambda^3 \cdot e^{-\lambda}}{3!}$$

For any $\lambda$, students can demonstrate the formula and note that for typical $\lambda \approx 3$ (matching Bitcoin's 10-minute target with 30-minute window):

$$P(X = 3) = \frac{3^3 \cdot e^{-3}}{3!} = \frac{27 \cdot e^{-3}}{6} = \frac{27 \times 0.0498}{6} \approx 0.224$$

Thus, there is approximately a **22.4% probability** that exactly 3 blocks are found in 30 minutes under realistic Bitcoin parameters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Describe the Bitcoin difficulty adjustment algorithm in detail. Why is the adjustment capped at 4× and 0.25× per period? What would happen if this cap were removed? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the new target value if the current target is $T_{\text{old}} = 2^{232}$ and the last 2016 blocks were mined in 8 days instead of the expected 14 days. Apply the standard clamping rules. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Difficulty Adjustment Algorithm (7 Marks)

The Bitcoin protocol recalibrates the target every **2016 blocks** to maintain a constant 10-minute block time. The algorithm is:

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}$$

**The 4× and 0.25× Clamping Rules:**

The adjustment factor is constrained as:

$$\text{adjustment} = \text{clamp}\left(\frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600},\ 0.25,\ 4\right)$$

**Reasons for the Clamp:**

1. **Preventing Oscillatory Instability:** Without clamping, a sudden influx of hash power could halve the target in a single period, causing alternating over- and undershooting cycles (a *difficulty death spiral*).

2. **Forward Stability for Miners:** Sudden 100× difficulty jumps would render profitable mining hardware obsolete overnight, destabilizing the network economically.

3. **Predictability:** Bounded adjustment ensures mining investments remain viable for at least several periods, providing predictability for capital expenditure.

**What If the Cap Were Removed:**

- A sudden mass exit of miners (e.g., due to a regional ban) could explode the target, making the next blocks trivially easy.
- An attacker with massive short-term hash power could crash the difficulty, then double-spend during the recovery period.
- The network's economic equilibrium would become unstable, with strong feedback loops.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{old}} = 2^{232}$
- $t_{\text{actual}} = 8 \text{ days} = 8 \times 86{,}400 = 691{,}200 \text{ seconds}$
- $t_{\text{expected}} = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ seconds}$

**Step 1: Compute the Raw Adjustment Factor**

$$\text{factor} = \frac{691{,}200}{1{,}209{,}600} = \frac{6912}{12096} = \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714$$

**Step 2: Apply the Clamp**

Since $0.25 \leq 0.5714 \leq 4$, the value is within bounds; **no clamping is needed**.

**Step 3: Compute the New Target**

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{4}{7} = 2^{232} \times 0.5714$$

Exponentially:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 2^{232} \times 2^{\log_2(4/7)} = 2^{232 - 0.807} = 2^{231.193}$$

Or more conventionally:

$$T_{\text{new}} = \frac{4}{7} \times 2^{232} = 2^{234} \times 4 / (7 \times 4) = \frac{2^{234}}{7} \approx 2^{231.19}$$

**Interpretation:** The new target is **larger** (since the network was slow, taking 8 days instead of 14), meaning the puzzle is **easier** by a factor of $7/4 = 1.75$. Difficulty decreases proportionally.

$$\text{New Difficulty} = \text{Old Difficulty} \times \frac{4}{7} \approx 0.5714 \times \text{Old Difficulty}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Hash Rate:** Always convert EH/s to H/s by multiplying by $10^{18}$ before substituting in formulas. Many students lose 2 marks for leaving EH/s in the denominator.
> 2. **Forgetting the Clamp:** When $t_{\text{actual}}$ is extreme (e.g., 1 day or 30 days), the raw factor will exceed the [0.25, 4] bounds. You **must** apply the clamp, or you lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Difficulty and Target:** Higher difficulty = *smaller* target = *harder* puzzle. Students often reverse this relationship, costing 1 mark.
> 4. **Misapplying Poisson:** The Poisson formula $P(X=k) = \lambda^k e^{-\lambda}/k!$ is for **block counts**, not hash counts. Using it for hash counts is a fundamental error.
> 5. **Forgetting Double SHA-256:** Bitcoin uses **two** SHA-256 applications, not one. Mentioning only one is a 1-mark deduction in descriptive answers.
> 6. **Missing the Extra Nonce Justification:** In Q1 Part A above, simply stating "we need more search space" without linking it to the coinbase transaction and Merkle root regeneration loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** PoW is a consensus mechanism requiring miners to solve a hash-based puzzle (find a nonce such that double-SHA-256 of header < target) to propose a new block.
- **Block Header Fields (6, totaling 80 bytes):** Version, Previous Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Bits, Nonce.
- **Hashing Algorithm:** **Double SHA-256** (Bitcoin) or **Ethash** (Ethereum, pre-merge).
- **Asymmetric Cost:** Hard to find ($\sim 2^{32+}$ hashes), easy to verify (1 hash).
- **Difficulty $D$:** $D = T_{\text{genesis}} / T_{\text{current}}$.
- **Target from Difficulty:** $T = T_{\text{genesis}} / D$.
- **Expected Hashes per Block:** $E[H] = 2^{256}/T$.
- **Average Block Time:** $t_{\text{avg}} = 2^{256}/(T \cdot r_{\text{net}})$.
- **Difficulty Adjustment Period:** Every **2016 blocks** ($\approx$ 14 days).
- **Adjustment Formula:** $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \text{clamp}(t_{\text{actual}}/1{,}209{,}600,\ 0.25,\ 4)$.
- **Halving Cycle:** Every **210,000 blocks** ($\approx$ 4 years); reward halves.
- **Block Reward History:** 50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 → **3.125 BTC** (2024).
- **Maximum Supply:** ~**21 million BTC** (geometric series converges to 21,000,000).
- **51% Attack Cost:** Proportional to >50% of total network hash rate, hardware capex, and sustained electricity.
- **PoW vs. PoS:** PoW burns energy (external cost); PoS locks capital (internal cost). Both are Sybil-resistant.
- **Historical Origin:** Dwork & Naor (1993) → Hashcash (1997) → Bitcoin (2008) → Ethereum (2015).
- **Key Real-World Use Beyond Crypto:** Anti-spam (Hashcash), DDoS mitigation, document timestamping.
- **Energy Concern:** Bitcoin's annual energy consumption is comparable to mid-sized countries; this is the primary criticism of PoW.
- **Mining Hardware Evolution:** CPU → GPU → FPGA → **ASIC** (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit).
- **Mining Pools:** Aggregated hash rate shared by multiple miners, with rewards distributed proportionally (PPS, PPLNS schemes).
- **Finality:** Probabilistic; $k$ confirmations reduce reorg probability exponentially.

**Exam Hall Mnemonics:**

- **"PVT-MTN":** Block header fields in order: **P**rev hash, **V**ersion, **T**ime, **M**erkle root, bi**T**s, **N**once. (Verify order during diagram questions.)
- **"Hard 2 Find, Easy 2 Check":** Always mention this in any PoW definition question — 2 marks guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a **Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT)** distributed consensus protocol in which the right to validate the next block, propose it, and earn the associated reward is **proportionally allocated** to a validator based on the number of native cryptocurrency tokens it has **locked (staked)** as collateral in the network. A validator that behaves maliciously loses (is *slashed*) a portion or all of its staked capital, creating a direct **economic disincentive** against protocol violations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PoS replaces the *computational race* of Proof of Work (PoW) with a *capital-at-risk* model, achieving Sybil resistance through economic commitment rather than energy expenditure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Corporate Boardroom

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Shareholders Electing a Board Member**

Imagine a cooperative corporation with 1,000 voting shares.
- Whoever owns the **most shares** has the **highest probability** of being chosen to propose the next quarterly decision.
- To participate, each shareholder must **lock** (deposit) their shares in an escrow — they cannot sell them while serving.
- If a chosen shareholder falsifies the financial report, **their locked shares are confiscated** (slashed).

This is **Proof of Stake in plain English**:
- *Shares* ↔ Cryptocurrency tokens
- *Escrow account* ↔ Staking contract
- *Board member* ↔ Validator
- *Falsified report* ↔ Malicious block / double-signing

A validator who never buys tokens has **no voting power** — just like a shareholder without shares.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Validator** | Node that locks stake and participates in consensus |
| **Staking** | Locking tokens as collateral |
| **Slashing** | Forfeiture of staked tokens for misbehaviour |
| **Finality** | Guarantee that a confirmed block cannot be reverted |
| **APR (Annual Percentage Reward)** | Annualised yield paid to stakers (typically **4 % – 12 %**) |
| **Bonded Validator** | Validator whose stake is locked and active |
| **Attestation** | A validator's vote on the validity of a proposed block |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight**
> Ethereum's PoS target staking participation ratio is approximately **≥ 66 %** of total supply for strong economic security — beyond which a 51 % attack becomes prohibitively expensive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step

A PoS network reaches consensus through the following structured sequence:

1. **Stake Deposit** — A node wishing to become a validator deposits a minimum threshold of tokens (e.g., **32 ETH** for Ethereum mainnet) into a staking contract.
2. **Validator Activation** — After a queue delay, the node becomes an *active validator* and is assigned a unique index.
3. **Block Proposer Selection** — For every slot (≈ **12 seconds** in Ethereum), a pseudo-random algorithm selects exactly **one** validator to propose the next block. Selection weight is proportional to stake.
4. **Block Proposal** — The selected validator assembles pending transactions, executes them, and broadcasts the new block.
5. **Attestation Phase** — A committee of validators (typically **128 – 1024** nodes) verifies the block and casts cryptographic votes (attestations).
6. **Consensus Finalisation** — Once a block receives attestations from validators representing **≥ 2/3 of total staked ETH**, it is considered *justified* and after one more checkpoint becomes *finalised*.
7. **Reward Distribution** — Proposer and attesters receive newly minted tokens + transaction fees. Misbehaving validators are **slashed**.

## 2.2 Why PoS Works — The Economic Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Principle of PoS Security:**
> A rational attacker must weigh the **cost of acquiring 51 % of stake** against the **expected gain from attacking**. Since acquiring stake increases the attacker's exposure to slashing, and successful attacks devalue the very tokens they hold, the protocol is **incentive-compatible** under game-theoretic equilibrium.

- Validators earn rewards **only if they follow the protocol**.
- Dishonest behaviour is **verifiable on-chain** and **economically punishable**.
- No energy is wasted on arbitrary hashing.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam ready**. Absolute values use `\mid` to safely render inside markdown tables.

| # | Formula / Symbol | Meaning / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j}$ | Probability that validator *i* is selected as block proposer in one slot (proportional to stake) |
| 2 | $P(\text{selected in } n \text{ slots}) = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{s_i}{S}\right)^{n}$ | Cumulative probability of selection over *n* independent slots |
| 3 | $\text{Expected Reward}_i = R_{\text{block}} \cdot \dfrac{s_i}{S_{\text{active}}}$ | Expected reward share for validator *i* |
| 4 | $\text{APR}_i = \dfrac{R_{\text{annual}}}{s_i} \times 100 \,\%$ | Annualised yield rate |
| 5 | $\text{Cost of Attack} = 0.51 \times C_{\text{circulating}} \times P_{\text{market}}$ | Capital required to launch a 51 % attack |
| 6 | $\text{Coin Age} = \sum (\text{tokens held} \times \text{days held})$ | Used in legacy PoS variants (PPCoin, Peercoin) for selection weighting |
| 7 | $\text{Slashing Penalty} = \alpha \cdot s_i$ | Forfeited stake on misbehaviour, where $\alpha \in [0.01,\, 1.0]$ |
| 8 | $N_{\text{validators}}^{\min} = \dfrac{S_{\text{total}}}{s_{\min}}$ | Minimum validator count given stake floor |
| 9 | $T_{\text{finality}} \approx 2 \times T_{\text{slot}} \times K$ | Time to finality (Ethereum: $K = 64$ epochs) |
| 10 | $\text{Sybil Resistance} \propto \text{acquisition cost per identity}$ | Cost-based defence against fake identities |

**Where:**
- $s_i$ = stake of validator $i$
- $S$ = total active stake in network
- $R_{\text{block}}$ = block reward (tokens)
- $C_{\text{circulating}}$ = circulating token supply
- $P_{\text{market}}$ = token market price
- $\alpha$ = slashing coefficient

## 2.4 Variants of Proof of Stake

| Variant | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure PoS (Chain-based)** | Validators selected randomly weighted by stake; only one chain grows | Peercoin, early Ethereum drafts |
| **BFT-style PoS** | Validators run rounds of PBFT-style voting; deterministic finality | Tendermint (Cosmos), HotStuff |
| **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** | Token holders *vote* to elect a fixed panel of delegates | EOS, Tron, BitShares |
| **Bonded PoS** | Validators bond stake that can be slashed for misbehaviour | Ethereum 2.0, Polkadot |
| **Liquid PoS** | Users receive a tradable receipt token representing their stake | Lido (stETH), Rocket Pool (rETH) |
| **Nominated PoS (NPoS)** | Nominators back validators and share rewards/slashing | Polkadot |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PoS is the consensus backbone of:
- **Ethereum (post-Merge, 2022)** — secures **>$300 Billion** in on-chain value.
- **Cardano (Ouroboros)** — peer-reviewed, provably secure PoS protocol.
- **Polkadot (NPoS)** — heterogeneous sharding with shared security.
- **Cosmos Hub (Tendermint)** — enables the *Internet of Blockchains*.

PoS enables **green, scalable, sustainable** distributed ledgers suitable for enterprise supply chains, central bank digital currencies (CBDCs), and decentralised finance (DeFi).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Validator Selection Probability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the probability that a validator with stake $s_i$ is selected as the block proposer in exactly $k$ out of $n$ slots.

**Step 1 — Single-slot success probability.**

Each slot is an independent Bernoulli trial. The probability that validator $i$ is selected in a single slot equals its share of total active stake:

$$P(\text{selected in 1 slot}) = \frac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j} = \frac{s_i}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of being selected in exactly $k$ of $n$ slots.**

Since each slot is independent with identical success probability $p = s_i / S$, the number of successes $X$ follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} \left(\frac{s_i}{S}\right)^{k} \left(1 - \frac{s_i}{S}\right)^{n-k}$$

**Step 3 — Probability of being selected at least once.**

Using the **complement rule**:

$$P(X \geq 1) = 1 - P(X = 0) = 1 - \binom{n}{0}\left(\frac{s_i}{S}\right)^{0}\left(1 - \frac{s_i}{S}\right)^{n}$$

$$P(X \geq 1) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{s_i}{S}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration.**

Let $S = 10{,}000{,}000$ tokens, $s_i = 32{,}000$ tokens (1 Ethereum validator), $n = 7200$ slots (≈ 1 day).

$$p = \frac{32{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} = 0.0032$$

$$P(\geq 1 \text{ in 1 day}) = 1 - (1 - 0.0032)^{7200}$$

$$\ln\bigl((1-0.0032)^{7200}\bigr) = 7200 \cdot \ln(0.9968) \approx 7200 \cdot (-0.003205) \approx -23.08$$

$$(1-0.0032)^{7200} \approx e^{-23.08} \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\boxed{P(\geq 1) \approx 0.9999999999}$$

Thus a single validator is **virtually certain** to be selected within a day — confirming the fairness of stake-weighted rotation.

## 3.2 Derivation — Cost of a 51 % Attack in PoS vs PoW

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Compare the capital required to attack PoS vs PoW.

**Step 1 — PoS attack cost.**

Attacker must acquire **51 %** of staked tokens. Let:
- $C$ = total circulating supply = **120,000,000 ETH** (illustrative)
- $P$ = market price = **USD 3,000** per ETH
- Staking participation = **25 %** (i.e., 30,000,000 ETH staked)

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{PoS}} = 0.51 \times 30{,}000{,}000 \times 3000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cost}_{\text{PoS}} \approx \text{USD } 45.9 \text{ Billion}}$$

**Step 2 — PoW attack cost (Bitcoin).**

Attacker must control **51 %** of global hash rate. Estimated cost ≈ **USD 10–20 Billion** in hardware + electricity annually, but **hardware is reusable** post-attack and can be resold.

**Step 3 — Critical PoS advantage.**

If the PoS attack is detected, **the attacker's own 51 % stake is slashed**, eliminating liquidity for the attacker. The cost becomes *non-recoverable*:

$$\text{Net Cost}_{\text{PoS}} = 0.51 \times S_{\text{staked}} \times P_{\text{market}} \times (1 + \alpha_{\text{slashing}})$$

This **destruction of economic value** is absent in PoW.

## 3.3 Derivation — Expected Reward (Annual Yield)

**Step 1 — Per-block reward share.**

If total annual issuance $R_{\text{annual}}$ = **945,000 ETH** and validator's stake share = $s_i / S$:

$$\text{Reward}_i^{\text{annual}} = R_{\text{annual}} \cdot \frac{s_i}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to APR.**

$$\text{APR}_i = \frac{R_{\text{annual}} \cdot \frac{s_i}{S}}{s_i} = \frac{R_{\text{annual}}}{S}$$

$$\boxed{\text{APR}_i = \frac{R_{\text{annual}}}{S_{\text{total staked}}}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical check.**

$$R_{\text{annual}} = 945{,}000 \text{ ETH},\quad S_{\text{staked}} = 30{,}000{,}000 \text{ ETH}$$

$$\text{APR} = \frac{945{,}000}{30{,}000{,}000} \approx 0.0315 = 3.15\,\%$$

This matches Ethereum's observed staking yield of **~3–4 %**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Conditions for Slashing

| Misbehaviour | Detection Method | Typical Slash $\alpha$ | Example Network |
|---|---|---|---|
| Double-signing same slot | Two conflicting attestations on-chain | $\alpha \approx 1.0$ | Ethereum, Cosmos |
| Surround voting | Two attestations that "surround" each other | $\alpha \approx 1.0$ | Ethereum (Casper FFG) |
| Inactivity leak | Failure to attest for extended period | Gradual | Ethereum |
| Equivocation (PBFT) | Conflicting votes in same round | $\alpha \approx 0.05$–$0.1$ | Tendermint |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of PoS Consensus Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    txPool[Transactions in Mempool] --> poolGather[Validator Pool Gathers Txs]
    poolGather --> randSelect{Random Selection of Proposer}
    randSelect -->|Proposer Chosen| proposeBlock[Propose New Block]
    randSelect -->|Not Chosen| waitSlot[Wait for Next Slot]
    proposeBlock --> broadcast[Broadcast Block to Network]
    broadcast --> committeeCheck{Validation Committee Attests}
    committeeCheck -->|>=2/3 Approve| finalise[Block Finalised on Chain]
    committeeCheck -->|Majority Rejects| discard[Block Discarded]
    finalise --> rewardDist[Distribute Proposer and Attester Rewards]
    finalise --> slashCheck{Slashing Conditions Met?}
    slashCheck -->|Yes| slashAction[Slash Validator Stake]
    slashCheck -->|No| continueCycle[Continue Consensus Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Validator State Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Inactive
    Inactive --> Registered: Deposit Stake
    Registered --> PendingActivation: Activation Queue
    PendingActivation --> Active: Epoch Boundary
    Active --> Active: Attest Successfully
    Active --> Slashed: Double Sign or Surround Vote
    Active --> Exiting: Voluntary Exit Requested
    Slashed --> [*]: Stake Forfeited
    Exiting --> Withdrawn: Withdrawal Delay
    Withdrawn --> [*]
```

## 4.3 PoS vs PoW — Functional Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph powDomain[Proof of Work Domain]
        direction TB
        powStart[New Block Needed] --> powMine[Miners Race to Solve Puzzle]
        powMine --> powWin[First Solver Wins Reward]
        powWin --> powEnergy[Massive Electricity Used]
    end
    subgraph posDomain[Proof of Stake Domain]
        direction TB
        posStart[New Block Needed] --> posStake[Validators Lock Capital]
        posStake --> posSelect[Algorithm Selects Proposer by Stake Weight]
        posSelect --> posAttest[Committee Attests Block]
        posAttest --> posFinal[Block Finalised in Two Epochs]
    end
    powDomain -.Replacement.-> posDomain
```

## 4.4 Slashing Condition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Validator Behaviour Monitored] --> q1{Did Validator Sign Two Different Blocks for Same Slot?}
    q1 -->|Yes| slash1[Slash 100 percent Stake and Eject]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Were Two Attestations Submitted That Violate Casper FFG Rules?}
    q2 -->|Yes Surround| slash2[Slash 100 percent Stake and Eject]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Has Validator Failed to Attest for Extended Period?}
    q3 -->|Yes| leak[Gradual Inactivity Leak]
    q3 -->|No| safe[Validator Remains Safe]
```

## 4.5 Network Stake Distribution Snapshot (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    centralHub[Total Network Stake S]
    centralHub --> v1[Validator A: 5 percent]
    centralHub --> v2[Validator B: 12 percent]
    centralHub --> v3[Validator C: 3 percent]
    centralHub --> v4[Validator D: 25 percent]
    centralHub --> v5[Validator E: 8 percent]
    centralHub --> v6[Validator F: 47 percent]
    v6 -. Warning Concentration.-> alert[Centralisation Risk Detected]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define Proof of Stake. Mention any two advantages it offers over Proof of Work.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Proof of Stake is a consensus mechanism in which validators are chosen to create new blocks in proportion to the number of tokens they have staked (locked) in the network, replacing the energy-intensive mining of Proof of Work with an economic-commitment model.

> **Advantage 1 — Energy Efficiency (1 mark):** PoS eliminates the need for specialised mining hardware and electricity-hungry computation, reducing energy consumption by approximately **99.95 %** as evidenced by Ethereum's Merge.

> **Advantage 2 — Economic Security via Slashing (1 mark):** Validators who attempt to manipulate the chain can be financially punished through *slashing*, creating stronger economic finality than PoW's probabilistic finality.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the "Nothing at Stake" problem in PoS, and how is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Problem (1.5 marks):** In a fork scenario, PoS validators have *nothing to lose* by voting on every competing chain because signing multiple chains costs no additional computational resource. This can prevent the network from reaching consensus on a single canonical chain.

> **Mitigation (1.5 marks):** Modern PoS protocols (e.g., Ethereum's Casper FFG) solve this through **slashing conditions** that detect double-voting and destroy the validator's entire stake, making it economically irrational to vote on multiple branches.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Students commonly lose marks by: (1) writing only definitions without operational flow, (2) confusing PoS with DPoS, (3) omitting the mathematical selection probability formula, and (4) failing to discuss slashing conditions when asked about security. Always pair *concept + formula + example*.

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

#### (a) Explain the working of the Proof of Stake consensus mechanism in detail with a suitable block-validation flowchart description. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Stake Registration (1 mark):**
A prospective validator deposits a minimum stake (e.g., 32 ETH) into the protocol's staking contract. The deposit becomes locked and earns no interest until activation completes after a queue delay.

**2. Validator Activation and Indexing (1 mark):**
Each active validator is assigned an index, public key, and withdrawal credentials. The total active stake $S$ is the sum of stakes across all indexed validators.

**3. Proposer Selection (2 marks):**
For every slot, the RANDAO mechanism combines the previous block's hash with each validator's VRF (Verifiable Random Function) output to pseudo-randomly select one validator weighted by stake share $p_i = s_i / S$.

**4. Block Proposal and Attestation (2 marks):**
The selected validator assembles a block, signs it with their BLS private key, and broadcasts it. A committee of attesters verifies block validity (signature, gas limits, state transitions) and broadcasts attestations.

**5. Finalisation and Reward Distribution (1 mark):**
Once a block receives attestations from validators holding $\geq 2/3$ of total stake for two consecutive epochs, it becomes finalised. Proposer and attesters share the block reward; misbehaving validators are slashed.

---

#### (b) Compare Proof of Stake with Proof of Work across at least six parameters. Discuss the real-world adoption of PoS. (7 marks)

**Model Answer — Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource** | Computational power | Locked capital |
| **Energy** | Extremely high (TWh/year) | Negligible |
| **Sybil Resistance** | Hash rate per identity | Tokens acquired per identity |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Deterministic / Economic |
| **Attack Cost** | Hardware + electricity | Acquisition of 51 % stake |
| **Penalty** | Wasted electricity | Slashing of stake |
| **Representative Chain** | Bitcoin | Ethereum, Cardano |

**Adoption (3 marks):**
- **Ethereum Merge (Sept 2022):** Transitioned from PoW to PoS, cutting energy use by ~99.95 %.
- **Cardano (Ouroboros):** First peer-reviewed, provably secure PoS protocol.
- **Polkadot (NPoS):** Implements nominated PoS with shared security across parachains.
- **Cosmos (Tendermint):** BFT-based PoS powering the IBC inter-chain ecosystem.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

#### (a) Explain the validator selection process in PoS. Discuss different types of PoS variants. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Validator Selection — Three Primary Mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Stake-Weighted Random Selection:** The probability $P_i = s_i / S$ is used to pick a proposer. Implemented using VRFs (Ethereum) or RANDAO.

2. **Coin-Age-Based Selection:** Coin age = tokens × time held. Higher age increases selection probability (used in Peercoin). After selection, coin age resets to zero.

3. **Round-Robin / BFT Rotation:** Validators take turns in deterministic order based on stake-ranked index (used in Tendermint).

**Mathematical Insight (1 mark):**

For $n$ slots, the cumulative probability of a validator with stake share $p$ being selected at least once is:

$$P(\geq 1) = 1 - (1 - p)^{n}$$

**Variants of PoS (2 marks):**
- **Delegated PoS (DPoS):** Token holders vote to elect block producers (EOS, Tron).
- **Bonded PoS:** Validators post bonds subject to slashing (Ethereum, Polkadot).
- **Liquid PoS:** Users receive tradable staking derivatives (Lido stETH).
- **Nominated PoS:** Nominators back validators and share rewards (Polkadot).

---

#### (b) Analyse the security model of Proof of Stake. Discuss major attacks and their mitigations. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Security Foundations (2 marks):**
PoS security rests on three pillars:
- **Cost of attack = 51 % of staked capital** (high acquisition cost).
- **Slashability** (verified misbehaviour → stake destroyed).
- **Deterministic finality** (no probabilistic reversal like PoW).

**Major Attacks (4 marks):**

| Attack | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Nothing-at-Stake** | Validators vote on all forks cheaply | Slashing for double-signing |
| **Long-Range Attack** | Old keys used to rewrite history from genesis | Weak subjectivity checkpoints |
| **Grinding Attack** | Manipulating randomness via block fields | VRF + RANDAO commitment scheme |
| **Cartel Formation** | Validators collude to censor transactions | Proposer-builder separation (PBS), distributed stake |
| **Liveness Denial** | Validators go offline collectively | Inactivity leak, social recovery |

**Quantitative Security Argument (1 mark):**
Cost to attack Ethereum today (illustrative):

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{attack}} = 0.51 \times 30{,}000{,}000 \times 3000 = \text{USD } 45.9 \text{ Billion}$$

with an additional **non-recoverable slashing loss**, making the attack economically irrational.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- PoS replaces **computational work** with **locked economic stake** for Sybil resistance.
- Block proposer selection probability: $P_i = s_i / S$.
- Cumulative selection probability over $n$ slots: $P(\geq 1) = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$.
- **Slashing** is the cornerstone of PoS security — it makes misbehaviour *economically irrational*.
- **Finality in PoS** is deterministic (e.g., Casper FFG needs **≥ 2/3 attestations** over two epochs).
- **Nothing-at-Stake** problem is solved by slashing double-signing validators.
- **Long-Range Attacks** are mitigated by *weak subjectivity* (recent trusted checkpoints).
- **Variants:** Pure PoS, DPoS, NPoS, Liquid PoS, BFT-PoS (Tendermint).
- **APR formula:** $\text{APR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{annual}}}{S_{\text{staked}}}$.
- **Cost of 51 % attack in PoS** = acquisition cost + non-recoverable slashing penalty.
- **Real-world PoS networks:** Ethereum, Cardano, Polkadot, Cosmos, Avalanche, Solana.
- **Advantages over PoW:** Energy efficiency, economic finality, lower entry barrier (no ASICs).
- **Disadvantages:** Initial coin distribution problem, wealth concentration risk, weaker liveness guarantees in some BFT variants.
- Always remember the **three roles** in PoS: **Proposer → Attesters → Finality**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of PoS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of PoS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Consensus Algorithms — Types of Proof of Stake (PoS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a class of Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) consensus algorithms where block validators are selected based on the quantity of cryptocurrency they "stake" (lock up as collateral) in the network, rather than computational power as in Proof of Work. The **Types of PoS** refer to the architectural variations that have evolved to address the inherent limitations of the original PoS design—namely the **Nothing-at-Stake problem**, **wealth concentration**, **validator centralization**, and **low participation rates**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCGT601 / Module 2):** *Types of Proof of Stake (PoS) include Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS), Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS), Proof of Importance (PoI), and Bonded Proof of Stake (BPoS) — each differing in validator selection, stake weighting, and slashing mechanisms.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Corporate Boardroom vs. The Co-Op"**
>
> Imagine a village that needs to elect a treasurer to manage the community fund.
> - **Original PoW** = Whoever digs the deepest trench (uses most shovel energy) becomes treasurer. Lots of wasted effort.
> - **Original PoS** = The person who locks the most money in the village vault becomes treasurer. Problem: only the rich get picked.
> - **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** = Villagers vote for **representatives** who then manage the fund. Like electing a board of directors.
> - **Leased PoS (LPoS)** = Poor villagers can **lend** their savings to a strong candidate, sharing the rewards. Like investors backing a CEO.
> - **Bonded PoS (BPoS)** = The treasurer must put a **deposit bond** that is slashed if they cheat. Like a bank guarantee.
> - **Nominated PoS (NPoS)** = Villagers nominate trusted candidates; if the nominee cheats, the nominator is also penalized. Joint accountability.

### 1.3 Why Multiple Types Exist — Motivation Matrix

| Limitation in Original PoS | Engineering Motivation for New Variant |
|---|---|
| Wealth concentration (whales dominate) | Enable small-stake participation → **LPoS, LPoS (Liquid)** |
| Low scalability (low TPS) | Delegate to few efficient producers → **DPoS** |
| Nothing-at-Stake attack | Introduce slashing economics → **BPoS (Ethereum 2.0)** |
| Trivial bribing attacks | Add reputation weightage → **PoI (NEM), NPoS (Polkadot)** |
| No accountability for nominators | Joint slashing of nominators → **NPoS** |
| Validator set management overhead | Use cryptographic sortition → **Algorand PPOS** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All PoS variants share a common **root invariant** — *the security cost of attacking the network should be proportional to the cost of acquiring a majority stake, not the cost of acquiring a majority of hash power.*

### 1.4 The "Stake Weight" Formula — Universal Foundation

Every PoS variant computes a **selection probability** $P_{select}$ that is functionally similar:

$$P_{select}(i) \;\propto\; f(\text{stake}_i, \text{reputation}_i, \text{randomness}_i, \text{time}_i)$$

Where:
- $\text{stake}_i$ = tokens locked by node $i$
- $\text{reputation}_i$ = historical honest behavior score
- $\text{randomness}_i$ = VRF or RANDAO output
- $\text{time}_i$ = duration of stake (coin age in some variants)

The **types of PoS differ precisely in how they define the function $f(\cdot)$**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Proof of Stake Variants

The PoS family is best classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Stake Model** — How stake is locked: *Bonded, Leased, Liquid, Delegated*
2. **Selection Mechanism** — How validators are chosen: *Block-based, Stake-weighted, Reputation-weighted, VRF-based*
3. **Penalty Model** — What happens on misbehavior: *Slashing, Loss of rewards, Reputation downgrade, None*
4. **Reward Distribution** — How incentives flow: *Proportional, Auction-based, Inflation-funded, Fee-only*

### 2.2 Detailed Variant Breakdown

#### A) Original / Chain-Based Proof of Stake (Naive PoS)

- **Block proposer** selected **stake-weightedly** at random every slot.
- No slashing in the original Peercoin (2012) implementation.
- Suffers from **Nothing-at-Stake**: validators can vote on all forks since signing costs nothing.
- Selection: $P(i) = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_{j \in V} s_j}$, where $V$ is the active validator set.

#### B) Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — *BitShares, EOS, Tron, Lisk*

- Token holders **vote** to elect a fixed number of **block producers (witnesses/delegate nodes)**, typically **21 producers** in EOS.
- The **top-N voted** producers take turns producing blocks in round-robin.
- Block time: ~0.5 s (EOS) / 3 s (BitShares) — extremely high throughput.
- Voters can **unbond and re-delegate** at any time.
- Vulnerable to **cartel formation** and **voter apathy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DPoS Real-World Example (KTU Exam Hot Topic):**
> EOS has 21 Block Producers (BP). A producer is skipped if they fail to produce a block within 0.5 s, and the next producer steps in. Each block is signed by **15 of 21 BPs** for irreversibility (irreversible after $\frac{2}{3} + 1$ signatures).

#### C) Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS) — *Waves Platform*

- Holders can **lease** their stake to a full node (validator) without transferring ownership.
- The full node's **effective stake** = its own balance + leased tokens.
- Leasers receive a **proportional share of block rewards** (minus the node's fee).
- Increases the **effective security budget** by allowing small holders to participate.
- Selection: $P(i) = \dfrac{s_i^{\text{own}} + \sum_{k=1}^{L_i} l_k}{\sum_{j} \left(s_j^{\text{own}} + \sum l\right)}$

where $L_i$ = number of leases received by node $i$, $l_k$ = lease amount from user $k$.

#### D) Bonded Proof of Stake (BPoS) — *Ethereum 2.0 (Casper FFG)*

- Validators must post a **security bond (32 ETH)** that is **slashable** under two conditions:
  1. **Double-proposing** (signing two conflicting blocks in the same slot)
  2. **Surround voting** (voting in a way that violates Casper's slashing conditions)
- A **slashed validator** loses their bond + is forcibly exited.
- **Inactivity leak** mechanism slowly drains offline validators' balances.
- The beacon chain uses **RANDAO + VDF** for randomness in epoch/committee assignment.

**Slashing penalty formula** (Casper FFG):

$$\text{slashed\_amount} = \min\left(B, \; B \cdot \frac{3 \cdot C}{2 \cdot V}\right)$$

where:
- $B$ = validator's bond (32 ETH)
- $C$ = number of other validators slashed in the same period (correlation penalty)
- $V$ = total active validators
- $C/V$ = correlation coefficient (higher = more coordinated attack = higher penalty)

#### E) Liquid Proof of Stake (LPoS) — *Tezos (Emmy+ protocol)*

- Delegators retain **full custody** of tokens (no transfer/leasing).
- Bakers (validators) can be **changed mid-cycle** ("rolling delegation").
- LPoS = "delegation" without the asset leaving the wallet.
- No slashing in Tezos — only **loss of deposit for double-baking/endorsing**.
- Used in **Tezos, Cosmos Hub (partial)**, and most modern Tendermint-based chains.

#### F) Nominated Proof of Stake (NPoS) — *Polkadot / Substrate*

- Two roles:
  - **Validators** — produce blocks, stake their own DOT.
  - **Nominators** — back validators with their DOT; if the chosen validator misbehaves, **the nominator is also slashed** (joint accountability).
- **Phragmén's method** (a.k.a. sequential Phragmén or Phragmms) optimally distributes nominator stake to equalize validator exposure and maximize decentralization.
- Active validator set is capped (e.g., **297 on Polkadot**).

**Phragmén's algorithm (simplified objective):**

$$\max \sum_{v \in V} \sqrt{w_v} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{v: i \in N_v} w_v \geq b_i, \; \forall i \in \text{Nominators}$$

where $w_v$ = weight assigned to validator $v$, $b_i$ = stake of nominator $i$, $N_v$ = set of validators backed by nominator $i$.

The square-root objective is **risk-averse Nash equilibrium** — spreads stake evenly.

#### G) Proof of Importance (PoI) — *NEM (New Economy Movement)*

- Importance score $I_i$ depends on:
  - **Vested stake** (1,000,000 XEM minimum)
  - **Transaction activity** (the more partners you transact with, the higher the importance)
  - **Time since last harvest**
- Anti-Sybil: a single account with one massive balance is **less important** than 10,000 accounts transacting.
- Addresses the **"rich-get-richer"** issue of original PoS.

Importance formula (NEM):

$$I_i = \text{vested\_amount}_i \times \text{activity\_score}_i \times e^{-\lambda T_i}$$

where $T_i$ = time since last reward, $\lambda$ = decay constant.

#### H) Proof of Authority (PoA) — *VeChain, POA Network*

- Not strictly PoS — but classified alongside PoS family.
- Validators are **pre-approved identities** (KYC'd organizations).
- Used in **permissioned/consortium blockchains**.

#### I) Casper (Casper FFG & Casper CBC) — *Ethereum's PoS*

- **Casper FFG (Friendly Finality Gadget)**: overlay on top of LMD-GHOST fork-choice rule. Provides **economic finality** — blocks become final once $\frac{2}{3}$ of validators vote.
- **Casper CBC (Correct-by-Construction)**: framework for designing PoS protocols with provable safety.

#### J) Ouroboros (Cardano)

- **Provably secure** PoS using **slot leadership** via VRF (Verifiable Random Function).
- Each slot has a designated leader who can produce a block.
- **Praos** is the current version (2020+); **Ouroboros Genesis** (2021) enables new nodes to join without trusted checkpoints.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slashing Penalty in Casper FFG (BPoS)

We derive the exact expression for the slashing penalty as a function of validator bond $B$, total validators $V$, and correlated slashings $C$.

**Step 1 — Initial Penalty (Constant Component):**
Every slashed validator loses an **initial flat penalty** equal to a small fraction of their bond. The standard initial deduction in Ethereum 2.0 is **1 ETH** (or equivalently, $\frac{B}{32}$ since $B = 32$ ETH by default). For generalized bond:

$$P_0 = \frac{B}{32}$$

**Step 2 — Correlation Penalty (Variable Component):**
To penalize coordinated attacks heavily, an additional penalty proportional to the **fraction of total validators slashed at the same time** is applied. The total penalty (correlation component) is:

$$P_c = B \cdot \frac{3 \cdot C}{2 \cdot V}$$

The coefficient $\frac{3}{2}$ ensures that if **one-third of the network is slashed** ($C = V/3$), the entire bond is consumed.

**Step 3 — Cap Penalty by Bond:**
The total penalty cannot exceed the validator's bond. Therefore:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \min(B, \; P_0 + P_c)$$

**Step 4 — Combined Casper FFG Formula:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{slash}(B, C, V) = \min\left(B,\; \frac{B}{32} + B \cdot \frac{3C}{2V}\right) = B \cdot \min\left(1,\; \frac{1}{32} + \frac{3C}{2V}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity Check (Edge Cases):**

- **Case A: Isolated slashing** ($C = 1$, $V = 1{,}000{,}000$):
  
  $$P = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{3}{2 \cdot 10^6}\right) \approx \frac{B}{32}$$
  
  Penalty is small (~3% of bond) — only this validator is punished.

- **Case B: Coordinated mass slashing** ($C = V/3$):
  
  $$P = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{3 \cdot V/3}{2V}\right) = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{17B}{32} \approx 0.531 B$$
  
  A coordinated attack of one-third of the network wipes out ~53% of each attacker's bond.

- **Case C: 51% attack attempt** ($C = V/2$):
  
  $$P = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{3 \cdot V/2}{2V}\right) = B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{3}{4}\right) = \frac{25B}{32} \approx 0.78 B$$
  
  The attacker loses ~78% of bond for a 51% coordinated attack.

This derivation is a **KTU high-yield question** — examiners frequently ask students to compute the slashing amount for a given $(B, C, V)$ tuple.

### 3.2 Derivation: NPoS Phragmén Equalization (Polkadot)

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Let nominators be indexed by $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ with stake $b_i > 0$. Each nominator chooses a set $N_i \subseteq V$ of validators to back. Let $x_{i,v} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicate whether nominator $i$ backs validator $v$.

**Step 2 — Stake Constraint:**
Each nominator's entire stake must be distributed across their chosen validators. We can equivalently model a nominator as splitting their stake:

$$\sum_{v \in N_i} w_{i,v} = b_i, \quad w_{i,v} \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Validator Backer Set:**
Let $\sigma_v$ be the set of nominators backing $v$. The total backer stake of $v$ is:

$$S_v = \sum_{i \in \sigma_v} w_{i,v}$$

**Step 4 — Risk-Averse Objective (Phragmén's):**
The utility of the system is the sum of square roots of validator backings (a strictly concave function encouraging equal distribution):

$$\max \Phi(w) = \sum_{v \in V_{\text{elected}}} \sqrt{S_v}$$

subject to $\sum_{v \in N_i} w_{i,v} = b_i$.

**Step 5 — Lagrangian Derivation:**
Form the Lagrangian:

$$L = \sum_{v} \sqrt{S_v} - \sum_{i} \lambda_i \left(\sum_{v \in N_i} w_{i,v} - b_i\right)$$

**Step 6 — First-Order Conditions (KKT):**
For each $w_{i,v} > 0$:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_{i,v}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{S_v}} - \lambda_i = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda_i = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{S_v}}$$

This means **the square root of every validator's backer stake is equalized across all validators that nominator $i$ supports**. In other words, Phragmén's allocation ensures a nominator's stake is spread to **equalize the marginal backing** of all validators they back.

**Step 7 — Active Validator Cap:**
Phragmén's algorithm computes the optimal weight assignment and then elects the top $|V_{\text{max}}|$ validators (e.g., 297 in Polkadot).

**Step 8 — Result:**
NPoS achieves **decentralization-optimal** stake distribution without central authority — a key Polkadot innovation.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of PoS Variants

| Variant | Stake Model | Slashing | Min Stake | Throughput (TPS) | Key Risk | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Original PoS | Locked | None | None | ~30 | Nothing-at-Stake | Peercoin |
| DPoS | Delegated | None (jail) | None (vote) | 1,000–4,000 | Cartel | EOS, Tron, Lisk |
| LPoS (Leased) | Leased | None | 0 (full) / Variable (leaser) | ~100 | Node failure | Waves |
| BPoS (Casper) | Bonded | **Yes (3%–100%)** | 32 ETH | ~64 (eth2) | Correlation slashing | Ethereum 2.0 |
| Liquid PoS | Liquid delegation | Forfeit deposit | 0 | ~40 | Voter apathy | Tezos, Cosmos |
| NPoS | Bonded + Nominated | **Yes (joint)** | ~1 DOT | ~1,000 | Nominator slashing | Polkadot |
| PoI | Vested + Active | None | 10,000 XEM | ~4,000 | Sybil farms | NEM |
| Casper CBC | Bonded | Yes | Variable | Theoretical | Complexity | Research |
| Ouroboros | Locked | None | Variable | ~250 | Slot leader bias | Cardano |
| PoA | Identity-based | Reputation | None | ~1,000 | Collusion | VeChain |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: DPoS Block Production Cycle (EOS)

**Step 1 — Token holders cast votes** weighted by their stake: a wallet with 1000 EOS = 1,000 votes, distributed among BPs.

**Step 2 — Top 21 BPs** by total received votes form the **active producer set**.

**Step 3 — Round-robin scheduling** assigns each BP a 6-block window every 21 × 6 = 126 blocks (~63 seconds).

**Step 4 — Producer signs a block** in their assigned slot; 15 of 21 BPs co-sign the irreversible block.

**Step 5 — Producer reward** (newly minted EOS tokens) is shared: **25% to the BP, 75% to the reward pool** distributed pro-rata to all voters.

**Step 6 — Voters can re-delegate** at any time, ensuring **continuous competition**.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example: LPoS Reward Distribution

**Problem:** A Waves node has 5,000 WAVES of its own balance and receives 3 leases: $l_1 = 2{,}000$, $l_2 = 3{,}000$, $l_3 = 5{,}000$. Node fee = 5%. If the node earns 1,000 WAVES in block rewards this month, compute each leaser's reward.

**Step 1 — Effective Stake:**

$$S_{\text{eff}} = 5{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 15{,}000 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 2 — Pool After Node Fee:**

$$R_{\text{pool}} = 1{,}000 \times (1 - 0.05) = 950 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 3 — Per-Token Reward Rate:**

$$r = \frac{950}{15{,}000} = 0.06333 \text{ WAVES per WAVES staked}$$

**Step 4 — Lease 1 Reward:**

$$R_1 = 2{,}000 \times 0.06333 = 126.67 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 5 — Lease 2 Reward:**

$$R_2 = 3{,}000 \times 0.06333 = 190.00 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 6 — Lease 3 Reward:**

$$R_3 = 5{,}000 \times 0.06333 = 316.67 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 7 — Node Own Reward:**

$$R_{\text{node}} = (1{,}000 \times 0.05) + 5{,}000 \times 0.06333 = 50 + 316.67 = 366.67 \text{ WAVES}$$

**Step 8 — Verification:**

$$R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{\text{node}} = 126.67 + 190 + 316.67 + 366.67 \approx 1{,}000 \text{ WAVES} \; \checkmark$$

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS). How does it differ from original Proof of Stake in terms of validator selection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) is a consensus mechanism where token holders do **not** produce blocks themselves. Instead, they vote to elect a **fixed, limited set of block producers** (e.g., 21 in EOS, 27 in Tron) who take turns producing blocks in a round-robin fashion.

**Comparison with Original PoS (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Original PoS | DPoS |
|---|---|---|
| Validator count | Open (any staker) | Fixed small set (e.g., 21) |
| Selection | Probability proportional to stake | Election by stake-weighted vote |
| Block producer | Random rotation among all stakers | Round-robin among elected BPs |
| Throughput | ~15–30 TPS | 1,000–4,000 TPS |
| Finality | Probabilistic / economic | Near-instant with $\frac{2}{3}+1$ signatures |
| Examples | Peercoin, Nxt | EOS, BitShares, Lisk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Mentioning the **round-robin scheduling** + **supermajority finality** is enough to secure full marks. No need to over-explain EOS specifics unless asked.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*List and briefly explain the limitations of original Proof of Stake that motivated newer PoS variants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Nothing-at-Stake Problem (1 mark):** Since block validation costs negligible resources, validators have an incentive to vote on **all competing forks** during a chain split, destroying the security model.

2. **Wealth Concentration (1 mark):** A few "whale" addresses with massive stakes dominate the validator set, leading to centralization and the rich-get-richer problem.

3. **Low Participation / Apathy (1 mark):** Token holders often do not actively participate in validation; consensus becomes a low-engagement process, reducing true decentralization.

Other valid points (for extra credit): *Long-range attacks*, *initial stake distribution unfairness*, *weak identity of validators*, *bribing attacks*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: BPoS / Casper FFG**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bonded Proof of Stake (BPoS)** consensus mechanism used in **Ethereum 2.0**. Describe the **Casper FFG finality gadget**, the **slashing conditions**, and derive the **slashing penalty formula**. Show with a numerical example how a coordinated attack by $\frac{1}{3}$ of validators affects each attacker's stake. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **BPoS (Ethereum 2.0)** with **NPoS (Polkadot)** with respect to: (i) stake model, (ii) slashing mechanism, (iii) validator selection algorithm, (iv) economic finality, and (v) decentralization. Use a clear comparative table and highlight the role of **Phragmén's algorithm** in NPoS. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — BPoS / Casper FFG (7 marks)

**1. Introduction to BPoS (1 mark):**
BPoS is a PoS variant where validators must lock a **security deposit (bond)** — minimum **32 ETH** in Ethereum 2.0 — that is **forfeit (slashed)** if the validator misbehaves. Unlike original PoS, slashing introduces **real economic cost** to misbehavior, resolving the Nothing-at-Stake problem.

**2. Casper FFG Finality Gadget (2 marks):**
Casper FFG is a **finality overlay** on top of Ethereum's LMD-GHOST fork-choice rule. It provides **economic finality** through a two-step voting protocol:
- **Justify** — a block becomes *justified* when $\geq \frac{2}{3}$ of validators (by stake) vote for it.
- **Finalize** — a block is *finalized* when it has a justified child block. A finalized block is **irrevocable** unless attackers are willing to burn **at least 1/3 of total staked ETH**.

**3. Slashing Conditions (1 mark):**
A validator is slashed under two conditions:
- **Double-proposal** — signing two distinct blocks for the same slot.
- **Surround-voting** — casting two attestations $A_1, A_2$ such that $A_1$ surrounds $A_2$ (violating Casper's slashing condition).

**4. Slashing Penalty Derivation (2 marks):**
The total slashing amount is given by:

$$P = B \cdot \min\left(1,\; \frac{1}{32} + \frac{3C}{2V}\right)$$

where $B$ = bond, $C$ = number of validators slashed in the same period, $V$ = total active validators. The first term is the **initial constant penalty** ($\frac{B}{32}$), the second is the **correlation penalty** that scales with coordinated attacks.

**5. Numerical Example (1 mark):**
Suppose $V = 300{,}000$ validators, $C = \frac{V}{3} = 100{,}000$ (one-third coordinated attack), $B = 32$ ETH.

$$P = 32 \cdot \min\left(1,\; \frac{1}{32} + \frac{3 \cdot 100{,}000}{2 \cdot 300{,}000}\right) = 32 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{32} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 32 \cdot 0.53125 = 17 \text{ ETH}$$

**Each attacker loses ~17 ETH (~53% of their bond)** in a one-third coordinated attack.

---

#### Part (b) — BPoS vs. NPoS Comparison (7 marks)

**Comparative Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | BPoS (Ethereum 2.0) | NPoS (Polkadot) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Stake Model** | Validator posts 32 ETH personal bond | Validators + Nominators both stake DOT; nominators back validators |
| **(ii) Slashing** | Validator's bond slashed. Correlation penalty via $\frac{3C}{2V}$ | **Joint slashing** — if validator is slashed, all nominators backing it are also slashed |
| **(iii) Selection Algorithm** | RANDAO + VDF committee assignment; **random per slot** | **Phragmén's algorithm** — equalizes validator backing using $\max \sum \sqrt{w_v}$ |
| **(iv) Finality** | Economic finality after 2 justified epochs (~12.8 min) | GRANDPA finality gadget; near-instant after $\frac{2}{3}$ validators agree |
| **(v) Decentralization** | Liquid staking pools (Lido, Rocket Pool) may concentrate stake | Phragmén equalization **enforces** stake-spreading — a decentralization primitive |

**Phragmén's Algorithm Role (2 marks):**
NPoS uses **Phragmén's method** to compute the **stake-weighted optimal distribution** of nominator stake across validators. The objective is to maximize $\sum_{v} \sqrt{S_v}$ (sum of square roots of validator backings). Since $\sqrt{x}$ is **strictly concave**, this objective naturally **penalizes concentration** and **rewards equal distribution** — a Nash equilibrium that aligns validators' and nominators' incentives toward decentralization. Polkadot's 297-validator active set is thus **algorithmically** protected against plutocratic dominance, unlike BPoS where large staking pools (Lido holds ~30% of all staked ETH) can centralize control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake in Part (b):** Conflating the *joint slashing* of NPoS (where nominators are penalized too) with the *validator-only slashing* of BPoS. Examiners specifically test whether you understand that **NPoS is "two-tier slashing"** while **BPoS is "single-tier"**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: DPoS, LPoS, PoI**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** consensus mechanism. Using **EOS** as a case study, explain the role of the **21 Block Producers**, the **round-robin block production** process, and the mechanism by which finality is achieved. What are the chief vulnerabilities of DPoS? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS)** as implemented by the **Waves Platform**. Derive the formula for a leaser's reward share and solve a numerical problem where 3 leasers back a full node. Compare LPoS with **Liquid Proof of Stake (LPoS)** as in **Tezos**, highlighting the difference in custody and slashing. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — DPoS and EOS (7 marks)

**1. DPoS Definition (1 mark):**
