Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) is a democratic-vote-based consensus where token holders **elect a fixed set of delegates/block producers (BPs)** who take turns validating transactions in a round-robin order. Token voting power is proportional to stake, but **stake is not locked** for voting.

**2. The 21 Block Producers in EOS (2 marks):**
EOS has exactly **21 active Block Producers**, selected every 63-second round by stake-weighted voting. Each BP produces **6 consecutive blocks** before passing the lead to the next. If a BP fails to produce a block in its 0.5-second window, it is **skipped** and the next BP takes over. Block rewards: **25% to the BP**, **75% to the reward pool** distributed pro-rata to all voters (per their vote weight).

**3. Finality in DPoS (2 marks):**
A block becomes **irreversible (final)** when **at least 15 of the 21 BPs (i.e., $\frac{2}{3} + 1$)** have co-signed it. This is called **Last Irreversible Block (LIB)**. Because the 21 BPs are known and the schedule is deterministic, a single round achieves near-instant finality (~0.5 s per block, ~63 s for a full round).

**4. Chief Vulnerabilities (2 marks):**
- **Cartel formation** — top 21 BPs may collude to fix prices or censor transactions.
- **Voter apathy** — many token holders do not vote, leaving stake effectively under-utilized.
- **Vote buying** — BPs can offer side-payments (kickbacks) to large voters in exchange for votes.
- **Single-point-of-failure producers** — if multiple BPs go offline, block production slows dramatically.

---

#### Part (b) — LPoS (Waves) + LPoS (Tezos) (7 marks)

**1. LPoS (Waves Platform) Concept (2 marks):**
In Leased Proof of Stake, **small token holders can lease** their WAVES to a **full node (validator)** for a fixed term. The full node's **effective stake** for block-production probability is **its own balance plus all leased tokens**. Ownership of the leased tokens **never leaves** the leaser's wallet — it is a **non-custodial lock**. The full node then has a higher chance of being selected, and rewards are **shared** pro-rata with leasers (minus the node's commission fee).

**2. Leaser Reward Formula Derivation (1 mark):**
Let $S_{\text{eff}} = s_{\text{own}} + \sum_{k} l_k$ be the effective stake. Total reward $R$ earned by the node, after deducting a node fee $f$:

$$R_{\text{pool}} = R \cdot (1 - f), \qquad r = \frac{R_{\text{pool}}}{S_{\text{eff}}}$$

A leaser $k$ receives:

$$R_k = l_k \cdot r = l_k \cdot \frac{R \cdot (1 - f)}{s_{\text{own}} + \sum l}$$

**3. Numerical Example (2 marks):**
*Already worked out in Section 3.5 above.* A node with 5,000 WAVES and three leases of 2,000 / 3,000 / 5,000 WAVES, node fee 5%, total reward 1,000 WAVES:
- Effective stake = 15,000 WAVES
- Pool = 950 WAVES
- Leaser 1: 126.67 WAVES
- Leaser 2: 190.00 WAVES
- Leaser 3: 316.67 WAVES
- Node owner: 366.67 WAVES

**4. LPoS (Waves) vs. LPoS (Tezos) Comparison (2 marks):**

| Aspect | LPoS (Waves) | LPoS (Tezos) |
|---|---|---|
| **Custody** | Non-custodial lease (lease-period lock) | **Full custody retained** by delegator (rolling delegation) |
| **Slashing** | None | Forfeit of security deposit for double-baking/endorsing |
| **Flexibility** | Lease has fixed term | **Mid-cycle** re-delegation allowed |
| **Reward Source** | Block rewards + fees | Block rewards + fees + adaptive inflation |
| **Voting** | Implicit (lease to a node) | Explicit delegation to a baker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note — Name Collision:** Both Waves and Tezos are colloquially called "LPoS" but they are **fundamentally different**. Waves LPoS = **leasing-based**; Tezos LPoS = **liquid delegation-based**. Examiners test this distinction directly. Use the proper names: *LPoS* (Waves) and *Liquid PoS* (Tezos).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**
>
> 1. **Confusing LPoS and DPoS:** Students frequently equate them. DPoS = vote for fixed set of **delegates** (e.g., 21 BPs). LPoS = **lease** your stake to a full node — no fixed set, the network is open.
> 2. **Forgetting slashing in NPoS:** A common error is to state "NPoS validators get slashed but nominators are safe." The truth: **both** validator and nominator are slashed jointly. NPoS has **two-tier slashing**.
> 3. **Misidentifying the finality threshold:** In Casper FFG, students say "$\frac{2}{3}$ must be honest." The correct statement is "**$\geq \frac{1}{3}$ must be Byzantine to break safety**, and $> \frac{1}{2}$ Byzantine breaks liveness."
> 4. **Mixing Casper FFG with Casper CBC:** FFG = Friendly Finality Gadget (operational, used in Ethereum 2.0). CBC = Correct-by-Construction (theoretical research framework). Examiners expect precise terminology.
> 5. **Ignoring Nothing-at-Stake:** Original PoS discussion is incomplete without addressing the **Nothing-at-Stake** problem. BPoS resolves it via slashing; DPoS sidesteps it via delegated authority.
> 6. **Numerical errors in slashing:** A common mistake is using the wrong sign in $\min()$ — the cap is at the **bond size**, not unbounded.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Original PoS** suffers from **Nothing-at-Stake** — validators can vote on every fork at zero cost.
- [x] **DPoS** uses a **fixed, small set of block producers** (e.g., 21 in EOS, 27 in Tron) elected by stake-weighted voting.
- [x] **DPoS finality** requires **$\frac{2}{3} + 1$** of BPs to sign → near-instant.
- [x] **LPoS (Waves)** allows non-custodial **leasing** of tokens to a full node; leasers share rewards pro-rata.
- [x] **Liquid PoS (Tezos)** = **non-custodial delegation** with rolling re-delegation; **no transfer** of tokens.
- [x] **BPoS (Ethereum 2.0)** requires a **32 ETH bond**; slashing via $P = B \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{1}{32} + \frac{3C}{2V}\right)$.
- [x] **Casper FFG** = economic finality gadget using **Justify → Finalize** two-step voting.
- [x] **NPoS (Polkadot)** has **two roles** — validators and nominators — with **joint slashing** under misbehavior.
- [x] **Phragmén's algorithm** equalizes validator backing using $\max \sum \sqrt{w_v}$; decentralization-optimal.
- [x] **PoI (NEM)** weights **vested stake × activity score × $e^{-\lambda T}$** — addresses wealth concentration.
- [x] **Selection probability** in original PoS: $P_i = \frac{s_i}{\sum_j s_j}$.
- [x] **Safety threshold** in BFT PoS ≥ $\frac{1}{3}$ Byzantine; **Liveness threshold** > $\frac{1}{2}$ Byzantine.
- [x] **Casper FFG finality** = 2 consecutive justified epochs (~12.8 minutes in Ethereum 2.0).
- [x] **DPoS** achieves **1,000–4,000 TPS** (EOS: 4,000 TPS); **BPoS Ethereum 2.0** ~64 TPS pre-sharding.
- [x] **Tezos LPoS** uses **Emmy+ protocol**; **no slashing**, only deposit forfeiture on double-baking.
- [x] **Ouroboros (Cardano)** uses **VRF** for slot leadership — provably secure PoS.
- [x] **Nothing-at-Stake attack** is mitigated by **slashing** (BPoS, NPoS) — not by DPoS or original PoS.
- [x] **Liquid staking** (Lido, Rocket Pool) on BPoS is a **centralization risk** for Ethereum 2.0.
- [x] **Long-range attacks** are a unique risk in PoS where attackers with old private keys rewrite history.
- [x] **Coin age** was a concept in **Peercoin** (original PoS) — stake weighted by time held.
- [x] **All PoS variants must ensure** the cost of attack > cost of acquiring majority stake (root security invariant).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Contracts - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Contracts - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Contract (Formal Definition):**
> A *smart contract* is a self-executing, deterministic computer program stored on a distributed, tamper-resistant blockchain ledger that automatically enforces, executes, and records the terms of a digital agreement when predefined conditions are satisfied, without the need for trusted third-party intermediaries.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a smart contract is treated as an **on-chain deterministic state-transition function** that lives at a specific blockchain address, holds its own cryptographic balance, and runs inside a sandboxed virtual machine (typically the **Ethereum Virtual Machine — EVM**).

### 1.2 Historical Origin — Nick Szabo (1994)

> [!NOTE]
> The term *smart contract* was coined by cryptographer and computer scientist **Nick Szabo in 1994**, long before Bitcoin (2008) or Ethereum (2015). Szabo originally framed smart contracts as *“a set of promises, specified in digital form, including protocols within which the parties perform on these promises.”*

Szabo's insight: contractual clauses (conditions, obligations, penalties) can be embedded directly into **hardware and software**, eliminating ambiguity and the need for human enforcement.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Vending Machine

The cleanest mental model (also Szabo's own):

| Real-World Object | Smart Contract Equivalent | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Physical vending machine | Smart contract deployed on blockchain | Both are deterministic, automated, tamper-resistant |
| Insert coin + select item | `msg.value` + function call (`msg.data`) | Input condition is satisfied |
| Machine dispenses product automatically | Contract executes `transfer()` of asset/state | No cashier, no middleman |
| Cannot be opened and rewired mid-transaction | Once deployed, contract **bytecode is immutable** | State is cryptographically sealed |

> **Bottom line:** A vending machine is a *mechanical* smart contract. A blockchain smart contract is the *digital* version — programmable, globally verifiable, and unstoppable.

### 1.4 Why "Smart"? — The KTU Board-Examiner's Two-Test Definition

For the **ESE (End Semester Examination)**, examiners expect students to justify the word *smart*. A smart contract is "smart" because it satisfies **two engineering properties**:

1. **Automation** — the contract triggers itself when conditions are met.
2. **Determinism** — given the same input state, every node in the network produces the *exact* same output state. This is the foundational guarantee that lets thousands of untrusted nodes agree on execution.

### 1.5 Standard Boundary Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **Gas Limit** — the maximum computational effort a smart contract transaction is allowed to consume (denominated in **gas units**, not ether).
- **Gas Price** — the fee per unit of gas paid in **gwei** (1 gwei = $10^{-9}$ ETH).
- **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** — the runtime environment, technically a **quasi-Turing-complete** stack machine that executes smart contract bytecode. Quasi-Turing-complete because execution is bounded by gas.
- **Contract Address** — a **160-bit** (20-byte) hex string derived from the deployer's address and a transaction counter (`nonce`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Smart Contract

A smart contract is built from three logically distinct layers. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *list and explain* these.

1. **State Layer (Persistent Storage)**
   - Every contract owns a 256-bit-key → 256-bit-value mapping known as **contract storage**.
   - Storage slots are written to the blockchain's **Merkle Patricia Trie**, making them globally readable but expensive to modify.

2. **Logic Layer (Function Code)**
   - Written in a high-level language (**Solidity**, **Vyper**, or **Yul**), then compiled into **EVM bytecode**.
   - Bytecode is what is actually deployed and executed on every validating node.

3. **Interface Layer (ABI — Application Binary Interface)**
   - A JSON descriptor of a contract's functions and events.
   - Allows external accounts and other contracts to call functions in a structured, type-safe way.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Logic Steps

The execution of a smart contract is best understood as a **state transition function**:

- **Step 1 — Deployment:** A user (Externally Owned Account) sends a transaction whose `to` field is empty, and whose `data` field contains compiled contract bytecode. A new contract address is generated and the contract becomes live at that address.
- **Step 2 — Invocation:** A user or another contract sends a transaction (or message call) to the contract address, specifying a function and optional parameters.
- **Step 3 — Validation & Execution:** Every full node on the network independently runs the bytecode inside the EVM, consuming gas for each opcode.
- **Step 4 — Consensus:** All nodes compare their resulting state. They reach consensus (e.g., **Nakamoto consensus on PoW chains**, **Gasper consensus on Ethereum post-Merge**) only if every node produced the *same* state root.
- **Step 5 — Finalization:** The new state is committed to the blockchain; the user pays gas fees; the execution result is final and irreversible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Immutability Pitfall:** Once deployed, the *bytecode* cannot be changed. "Upgradeable" contracts use a proxy pattern (delegatecall) — the underlying logic can be swapped, but the storage address and primary contract identity remain.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $S_{n+1}$ | New global state after transaction | 256-bit word (Merkle root) |
| $S_n$ | Previous global state | 256-bit word (Merkle root) |
| $T$ | Transaction (signed message) | bytes (RLP-encoded) |
| $Y(S_n, T)$ | State-transition function (Ethereum Yellow Paper) | $Y: \mathbb{S} \times \mathbb{T} \to \mathbb{S}$ |
| $\mathbb{S}$ | Set of all valid blockchain states | Finite set |
| $\mathbb{T}$ | Set of all valid transactions | Finite set |
| $\vert g \vert$ | Gas consumed by a single opcode | gas units (e.g., ADD = 3, SSTORE = 20000) |
| $F_{tx}$ | Total transaction fee | wei $= 10^{-18}$ ETH |
| $F_{tx} = \vert g \vert \times p$ | Fee = gas used × gas price | wei |
| $A_c$ | Contract address | $A_c = \text{Keccak256}(RLP(\text{sender}, \text{nonce}))\left[12..32\right]$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** The state-transition function $Y$ is the mathematical heart of every blockchain that supports smart contracts (Ethereum, Polygon, BNB Chain, Avalanche C-Chain). The $F_{tx} = \vert g \vert \times p$ formula is the **single most-tested numerical** in KTU board exams for this module.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Smart contracts underpin the entire **Web3 / decentralized finance (DeFi)** stack:

- **Tokenization (ERC-20, ERC-721)** — programmable digital assets.
- **Decentralized Exchanges (Uniswap, Curve)** — automated market makers.
- **Supply-Chain Provenance (VeChain, Morpheus.Network)** — tamper-proof logistics records.
- **Decentralized Identity (DID)** — self-sovereign credentials.
- **Insurance Parametrics (Etherisc)** — flight-delay insurance that pays out automatically based on oracle data.
- **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations)** — on-chain governance with no executives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Contract Address Computation (Board-Favorite Numerical)

The **Contract Address** is generated deterministically. This derivation appears frequently in KTU ESE Part A (3-mark) questions.

$$
A_c = \text{Keccak256}\big(\,\text{RLP}\big(\,\text{sender\_address},\ \text{sender\_nonce}\,\big)\,\big)\big[12..32\big]
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Let the deploying account's address be $\text{sender} = \mathbf{0x6AC7...B33A}$.
- Let its current nonce be $n$.
- Recursive-Length Prefix (RLP) encode the pair: $\text{RLP}(\text{sender}, n)$.
- Apply the Keccak-256 hash function to obtain a 32-byte digest.
- Take the **last 20 bytes** (the rightmost, indices 12 through 32 exclusive) — this becomes the contract address.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transaction Fee Calculation (Classic KTU Numerical)

**Problem (typical 3-mark style):** A smart contract function call consumes 21,000 gas for a base transaction, plus 32,000 gas for an SSTORE operation, plus 6,800 gas for a transfer event log. The user sets a gas price of 25 gwei. Compute the total transaction fee in **wei**, **gwei**, and **ETH**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert g \vert_{\text{total}} &= \vert g \vert_{\text{base}} + \vert g \vert_{\text{SSTORE}} + \vert g \vert_{\text{LOG}} \\
&= 21{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 6{,}800 \\
&= 59{,}800 \ \text{gas units}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tx} &= \vert g \vert_{\text{total}} \times p \\
&= 59{,}800 \times 25 \ \text{gwei} \\
&= 1{,}495{,}000 \ \text{gwei}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tx} \text{ in ETH} &= 1{,}495{,}000 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 1.495 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tx} \text{ in wei} &= 1.495 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{18} \\
&= 1.495 \times 10^{15} \ \text{wei}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer key for the student:** $1{,}495{,}000$ gwei $= 1.495 \times 10^{-3}$ ETH $= 1.495 \times 10^{15}$ wei.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Determinism Constraint (Theoretical Reasoning)

For a network of $N$ full nodes to agree on a new state, the following must hold for every transaction $T_i$:

$$
Y(S_i, T_i) = Y'(S_i', T_i) = Y''(S_i'', T_i) = \dots = Y^{(N)}(S_i^{(N)}, T_i)
$$

where each $Y^{(k)}$ is the $k$-th node's local execution result. **Determinism guarantees** that all superscripts collapse to the same final state root.

- If any node includes **non-deterministic sources** (e.g., `block.timestamp`, `blockhash`, network calls, `now`), divergence occurs and consensus fails.
- This is why EVM prohibits native floating-point numbers, external I/O, and unbounded loops at the language level.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Smart Contract vs. Traditional Contract

| Property | Traditional Legal Contract | Smart Contract |
|---|---|---|
| **Enforcement** | Court system, lawyers | Code execution on blockchain |
| **Storage** | Paper / PDF / digital server | Decentralized ledger (immutable) |
| **Cost** | Lawyer fees, arbitration | Gas fee (computational cost) |
| **Trust Model** | Relies on trusted third party | Trustless (cryptographic) |
| **Speed** | Days to months | Seconds to minutes (block time) |
| **Mutability** | Amendable by parties | Immutable post-deployment (or proxy-upgradeable) |
| **Verifiability** | Requires legal expertise | Publicly auditable on-chain |
| **Geography** | Bound by jurisdiction | Borderless |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Smart Contract Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Externally Owned Account] -->|signs and sends TX| txpoolA[Network Transaction Pool]
    txpoolA -->|validator picks TX| evmA[EVM Sandbox on Validator Node]
    evmA -->|reads bytecode| storageA[Contract Storage Merkle Patricia Trie]
    evmA -->|executes opcodes| stateA[New State Root]
    stateA -->|broadcast block| consensusA[Consensus Layer Proof of Stake]
    consensusA -->|finalized block| chainA[Append to Blockchain]
    chainA -->|event logs emitted| oracleA[Off Chain Dapps and Oracles]
    oracleA -->|read state via RPC| userB[Front End Dapp UI]
    userB -->|triggers new TX| userA
```

### 4.2 Lifecycle State Machine of a Smart Contract

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SourceCode: Solidity file written
    SourceCode --> CompiledBytecode: solc compile
    CompiledBytecode --> DeployedContract: Send deployment TX
    DeployedContract --> Active: Block confirmation
    Active --> Active: Function call and state update
    Active --> SelfDestruct: Trigger SUICIDE opcode
    Active --> Frozen: Out of gas revert
    SelfDestruct --> [*]
    Frozen --> Active: Rebroadcast TX with higher gas
```

### 4.3 Mapping a Smart Contract Call to the State-Transition Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Previous State Sn] -->|combined with| inputB[Transaction T]
    inputB -->|fed into| functionA[Y Sn T]
    functionA -->|produces| outputA[New State Sn plus 1]
    outputA -->|validated by| nodeA[Every Full Node Independently]
    nodeA -->|consensus check| finalA[Single Agreed Final State]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define a smart contract. List any two properties.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Model Answer:**

A smart contract is a self-executing computer program that runs on a blockchain and automatically enforces the terms of a digital agreement when predefined conditions are satisfied, without the need for trusted intermediaries. *(2 marks for definition)*

**Two properties:** *(1 mark)*
- **Determinism:** Every node executing the contract produces the same output for the same input, ensuring consensus.
- **Immutability:** Once deployed, the contract's bytecode cannot be altered, guaranteeing tamper-proof execution.

#### **Question 2: State the role of the Ethereum Virtual Machine in smart contract execution.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Model Answer:**

The **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** is a sandboxed, quasi-Turing-complete runtime environment deployed on every Ethereum full node. Its role is to:
- Fetch the contract bytecode associated with the called address. *(1 mark)*
- Execute each opcode in a stack-based architecture, charging gas for every computational step. *(1 mark)*
- Produce a deterministic new state root, ensuring every node in the network reaches consensus on the resulting state. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Note:** ESE Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. Both alternatives below are module-internal (i.e., no choice across modules).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Smart Contracts — Definition, Characteristics, and Working Model**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define a smart contract. Explain any **five** key characteristics that distinguish a smart contract from a traditional legal contract.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step working model of a smart contract — from deployment to final state — with the help of the state-transition function $Y(S, T)$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A smart contract is a self-executing, deterministic program stored on a blockchain that automatically enforces, executes, and records contractual terms when predefined conditions are met, eliminating the need for trusted third parties.

**Five Key Characteristics (5 × 1 mark = 5 marks):**
- **Autonomy / Self-Execution:** Triggers automatically without human intervention once conditions are met.
- **Trustlessness:** Parties do not need to trust each other or any intermediary; they trust only the code and consensus mechanism.
- **Immutability:** Once deployed, the contract bytecode cannot be changed (or modified only via carefully designed upgradeability patterns).
- **Transparency:** All transactions and contract code are publicly auditable on the blockchain.
- **Determinism & Verifiability:** Given the same input state, every node produces the same output state, allowing cryptographic verification.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The working of a smart contract can be formally modeled using the Ethereum state-transition function:

$$Y: \mathbb{S} \times \mathbb{T} \to \mathbb{S}$$

**Step-by-step description (7 × 1 mark):**
- **Step 1:** A user crafts a signed transaction $T$ specifying the contract address, function selector, and arguments.
- **Step 2:** The transaction is broadcast to the network and enters the mempool.
- **Step 3:** A validator selects the transaction and loads the contract bytecode from the global state $S_n$.
- **Step 4:** The EVM executes the bytecode opcode-by-opcode in a sandboxed environment, consuming gas per instruction.
- **Step 5:** The EVM updates the contract's storage slots and emits any events.
- **Step 6:** The new state $S_{n+1} = Y(S_n, T)$ is computed locally by every full node.
- **Step 7:** Validators reach consensus on $S_{n+1}$, the block is appended to the chain, and the execution becomes irreversible.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Architecture, Components, and Lifecycle of Smart Contracts**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-layer architecture** of a smart contract: state layer, logic layer, and interface layer (ABI).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **lifecycle** of a smart contract from source code to self-destruction, including the role of the EVM and gas in the deployment phase.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Three-Layer Architecture (7 marks):**

- **State Layer — Contract Storage (2 marks):** Every smart contract owns a persistent key-value store (256-bit → 256-bit) implemented as part of the blockchain's **Merkle Patricia Trie**. Slots remain readable by anyone on-chain but writing to them is the most expensive EVM operation (~20,000 gas for a fresh slot).
- **Logic Layer — Bytecode (2 marks):** Source code (e.g., Solidity) is compiled into **EVM bytecode** — a sequence of low-level opcodes. This bytecode is what is actually deployed and executed by every validating node.
- **Interface Layer — ABI (3 marks):** The **Application Binary Interface (ABI)** is a JSON descriptor that maps function names and parameter types to their 4-byte selectors. DApps and other contracts use the ABI to encode calls and decode return data in a type-safe way.

> **Diagram (drawn by student):** A simple three-tier block diagram: *Source Code → Compiler → Bytecode*, side-by-side with *ABI* feeding into *External Callers* which interact with *Contract Storage*.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Lifecycle of a Smart Contract (7 marks):**

- **Phase 1 — Authoring (1 mark):** Developer writes a high-level contract in Solidity/Vyper/Yul.
- **Phase 2 — Compilation (1 mark):** `solc` or equivalent compiler produces bytecode + ABI.
- **Phase 3 — Deployment (2 marks):** A signed deployment transaction is sent with empty `to` field and bytecode in `data`. A new contract address $A_c$ is derived; the deployment cost is paid in gas.
- **Phase 4 — Active Operation (2 marks):** Users and other contracts invoke functions. The EVM executes opcodes; gas is deducted per opcode; state is updated.
- **Phase 5 — Termination (1 mark):** The contract may run indefinitely (the default) or be terminated via the `SELFDESTRUCT` opcode (EIP-6780 nuances apply post-Dencun). Once destroyed, the bytecode becomes inactive, though historical state remains on-chain forever.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "determinism" justification.** Defining a smart contract as merely "a contract on a blockchain" loses 1–2 marks. Always include *determinism* and *self-execution* explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "immutability of bytecode" with "immutability of state."** Examiners accept either, but stating *both* shows depth.
> 3. **Writing the formula $F_{tx} = \vert g \vert \times p$ with absolute-value pipes inside a markdown table.** This will break the table rendering. Write $\vert g \vert$ using the `\vert` LaTeX macro to keep the table intact.
> 4. **Skipping the gas calculation units.** Always state the final answer in **all three units** — wei, gwei, ETH — to claim full 3 marks on numerics.
> 5. **Conflating "quasi-Turing-complete" with "Turing-complete."** The EVM is quasi-Turing-complete because gas acts as a hard computational bound. Writing just "Turing-complete" is technically incorrect and costs a mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **smart contract** is a *self-executing, deterministic program* stored on a blockchain that enforces digital agreements without intermediaries.
- Coined by **Nick Szabo in 1994**, originally framed as *“a set of promises, specified in digital form.”*
- The word *smart* implies **automation + determinism** — both are mandatory for KTU definitions.
- Runs inside the **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** — a sandboxed, *quasi*-Turing-complete stack machine.
- Three architectural layers: **State (Storage)** + **Logic (Bytecode)** + **Interface (ABI)**.
- Formally modeled by the state-transition function: $\;Y: \mathbb{S} \times \mathbb{T} \to \mathbb{S}\;$ where $S_{n+1} = Y(S_n, T)$.
- Transaction fee formula: $\;F_{tx} = \vert g \vert \times p\;$ — measured in **wei** (smallest), gwei, and **ETH**.
- Contract address derivation: $\;A_c = \text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(\text{sender}, \text{nonce}))\big[12..32\big]$.
- Lifecycle stages: **Author → Compile → Deploy → Operate → Terminate (optional)**.
- Once deployed, **bytecode is immutable**; upgradeability requires proxy-pattern design.
- Key properties to memorize: **Deterministic, Immutable, Transparent, Trustless, Autonomous, Verifiable**.
- Real-world uses: ERC-20/721 tokens, DeFi (Uniswap), DAOs, supply-chain, parametric insurance, decentralized identity.
- The **vending machine** is the canonical real-world analogy — use it in 1-mark definition questions.
- **Memorize the units conversion:** 1 ETH = $10^9$ gwei = $10^{18}$ wei.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Contract Templates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Contract Templates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Smart Contract Templates** are pre-audited, reusable, standardized code structures written in high-level contract-oriented languages (typically **Solidity**, **Vyper**, or **Rust** for Solana) that encapsulate common business logic, token standards, and access-control patterns. They serve as the architectural blueprint for deploying production-grade decentralized applications (DApps) on platforms such as **Ethereum**, **Polygon**, and **BNB Chain**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, smart contract templates are positioned as the engineering counterpart to traditional software design patterns (like the *Gang of Four* patterns in OOP). They are modular skeletons that allow developers to inherit standardized behavior, thereby minimizing attack surfaces, reducing development cost, and enforcing interoperability across the Web3 ecosystem.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Legal Will Template"**
> Imagine you need to draft a legal Will. Instead of hiring a lawyer from scratch to write every clause, you walk into a stationery shop and pick up a **pre-printed Will template**. The template contains standard clauses (asset declaration, executor appointment, witness signatures) — you just fill in the blanks (names, amounts, dates). Once signed and notarized, it becomes legally binding.
> 
> Similarly, a **Smart Contract Template** is a pre-written, community-audited code skeleton (e.g., ERC-20 token standard). A developer "fills in the blanks" — specifying the token name, supply, decimals — and deploys it. Once deployed on the blockchain, it becomes **immutably binding** and self-executing.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Smart Contract Templates

| Characteristic | Engineering Significance |
|----------------|--------------------------|
| **Reusability** | Write once, deploy many times across different DApps |
| **Auditability** | Pre-verified by security firms (e.g., OpenZeppelin, Trail of Bits) |
| **Composability** | Templates can be combined (like LEGO blocks) to build complex DeFi protocols |
| **Gas Efficiency** | Optimized bytecode reduces transaction fees on the EVM |
| **Standardization** | Compliance with ERC (Ethereum Request for Comments) standards |

### 1.4 Categories of Smart Contract Templates

> [!NOTE]
> According to the KTU 2024 PCCGT601 syllabus (Module 3), smart contract templates are broadly classified into the following four categories:
> 1. **Token Standards**: ERC-20 (Fungible), ERC-721 (NFT), ERC-1155 (Multi-token)
> 2. **Access Control**: Ownable, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Multi-Sig Wallets
> 3. **Safety & Security**: Reentrancy Guard, Pausable, Upgradeable Proxies
> 4. **Utility Templates**: Crowdsale (ICO/IDO), Staking, Vault, Escrow, Multi-Signature Wallet

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of a Smart Contract Template

Every production-grade Solidity smart contract template follows a **seven-layer architectural anatomy**. Understanding this structure is critical for both KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) and laboratory viva questions.

**Layer 1 — Pragma Directive & Compiler Version Pinning**

$$\texttt{pragma solidity}^{\wedge} 0.8.x$$

This directive specifies the compiler version. The caret (`^`) operator allows compatibility with versions `0.8.x` where $x \geq 0$.

**Layer 2 — License Identifier (SPDX)**

$$\texttt{// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT}$$

Mandatory for compiler `0.6.8+` to avoid deployment warnings on the EVM.

**Layer 3 — Import Statements & Library Dependencies**

Templates typically import from the **OpenZeppelin Contracts** library — a gold-standard, formally verified smart contract repository.

**Layer 4 — Contract Declaration & Inheritance**

A template can inherit from multiple parent contracts using the `is` keyword (similar to Java's `extends` or C++'s `:`).

**Layer 5 — State Variables & Custom Errors**

State variables are permanently stored in contract storage slots.

**Layer 6 — Function Modifiers (Access Control Primitives)**

Modifiers wrap function logic with pre/post-conditions (e.g., `onlyOwner`, `whenNotPaused`).

**Layer 7 — Events for Off-Chain Indexing**

Events emit logs that are indexed by front-end applications (dApps) using the **EVM LOG opcode**.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all key formulas, gas metrics, and mathematical relationships used in smart contract template analysis. These are **board-exam favorites** and frequently appear in 14-mark derivations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility | Unit / Standard |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Gas Cost Calculation** | $\text{Total Fee} = \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$ | Determines transaction cost in Gwei | Gwei (giga-wei) |
| 2 | **Wei to Ether Conversion** | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$ | Base unit conversion for token values | Wei |
| 3 | **EVM Storage Slot** | 1 slot = 32 bytes (256 bits) | Determines gas cost of `SSTORE` | Bytes |
| 4 | **ERC-20 Total Supply Cap** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Balance}(a_i) = \text{Total Supply}$ | Conservation invariant for fungible tokens | Tokens |
| 5 | **Block Gas Limit (Ethereum)** | $\text{Block Gas Limit} \approx 30 \times 10^{6}$ | Maximum computation per block | Gas units |
| 6 | **Gas Refund (SELFDESTRUCT)** | Up to $\frac{1}{2}$ of contract balance (capped) | Incentivizes storage cleanup | Gas units |
| 7 | **Reentrancy Condition** | $\text{State Update} \text{ AFTER } \text{External Call}$ is vulnerable | Security invariant | Boolean |
| 8 | **ERC-721 Unique Token ID** | $\text{uint256}$ unique per NFT | Non-fungibility guarantee | Integer |
| 9 | **Timestamp Block Dependency** | $\text{block.timestamp} \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Time-locked contracts | Unix epoch seconds |
| 10 | **Keccak-256 Hash Output** | $\{0, 1\}^{256}$ | Cryptographic fingerprinting | 256-bit string |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: In the table above, the absolute value and set notation use `\vert` and `\mathbb{Z}` to prevent markdown table breaking. The vertical pipe `|` is reserved strictly as a column separator.

### 2.3 The Token Standard Family (ERC Hierarchy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists three ERC standards under smart contract templates. Below is the deep theoretical analysis of each:

#### 2.3.1 ERC-20 — The Fungible Token Standard

Formalized in **EIP-20 (Ethereum Improvement Proposal)**, ERC-20 defines a common interface for interchangeable tokens (e.g., USDC, DAI, UNI). The mandatory function signature set is:

$$\text{Interface}_{\text{ERC-20}} = \{ \text{totalSupply, balanceOf, transfer, transferFrom, approve, allowance} \}$$

The **transfer event signature** is computed via Keccak-256:

$$H_{\text{event}} = \text{Keccak-256}(\text{"Transfer(address,address,uint256)"})$$

This hash is the unique topic[0] emitted in the EVM LOG, allowing off-chain indexers (e.g., The Graph) to track every token movement.

#### 2.3.2 ERC-721 — The Non-Fungible Token Standard

ERC-721 enables unique digital assets (NFTs) where each token has a distinct `uint256` ID. The supply function is:

$$\vert \text{Total Supply}_{\text{ERC-721}} \vert = \vert \{ \text{ID} \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0} \mid \text{ID is minted} \} \vert$$

#### 2.3.3 ERC-1155 — The Multi-Token Standard

Proposed in **EIP-1155**, this standard unifies fungible and non-fungible tokens within a single contract using a 2D balance mapping:

$$\text{Balance}_{\text{ERC-1155}}: (\text{address}, \text{uint256}) \rightarrow \text{uint256}$$

This reduces deployment costs by up to **90%** compared to deploying separate ERC-20 and ERC-721 contracts.

### 2.4 Why Smart Contract Templates Are Engineering Best Practice

1. **Reduced Attack Surface**: Pre-audited libraries (OpenZeppelin) have undergone formal verification and bug bounties.
2. **Composability**: DeFi protocols (Aave, Compound, Uniswap) are built by *stacking* templates like Lego blocks.
3. **Regulatory Compliance**: Templates enforce standard behavior (e.g., ERC-1404 for security tokens).
4. **Developer Velocity**: Templates reduce time-to-market from weeks to hours.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gas Cost of a Template Deployment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board examiners frequently test the relationship between contract bytecode size and deployment gas cost. The following is a step-by-step derivation.

**Problem Statement**: Calculate the approximate deployment gas cost for a minimal ERC-20 template with a compiled bytecode size of $S$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the gas components**
A contract deployment involves two EVM operations: `CREATE` and `CODECOPY`.

$$\text{Gas}_{\text{deploy}} = \text{Gas}_{\text{CREATE}} + \text{Gas}_{\text{CODECOPY}} + \text{Gas}_{\text{init}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the EVM opcode costs**

The EVM Yellow Paper (Appendix G) defines:
- `CREATE` base cost: **53000 gas**
- `CODECOPY` per byte: **200 gas**
- Constructor execution: variable, call it $G_{\text{init}}$

**Step 3 — Formulate the final equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gas}_{\text{deploy}} &= 53000 + (200 \times S) + G_{\text{init}} \\
&= 53000 + 200S + G_{\text{init}} \quad \text{(in gas units)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ETH cost**
If the gas price is $p$ Gwei, then the deployment cost in ETH is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{ETH}} &= \frac{\text{Gas}_{\text{deploy}} \times p}{10^{9}} \quad \text{(since 1 ETH = 10}^{9}\text{ Gwei)} \\
&= \frac{(53000 + 200S + G_{\text{init}}) \times p}{10^{9}} \text{ ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**
For a typical ERC-20 template with $S = 5000$ bytes, $G_{\text{init}} = 100000$ gas, and $p = 30$ Gwei:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gas}_{\text{deploy}} &= 53000 + (200 \times 5000) + 100000 \\
&= 53000 + 1000000 + 100000 \\
&= 1153000 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{\text{ETH}} &= \frac{1153000 \times 30}{10^{9}} \\
&= 0.03459 \text{ ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: ERC-20 Balance Conservation Invariant

> [!NOTE]
> A correctly implemented ERC-20 template **must** satisfy the conservation law at all times.

**Invariant Statement**:

$$\sum_{a \in \text{Addresses}} \text{Balance}(a) = \text{Total Supply}$$

**Proof Sketch**:

Step 1 — At deployment, the deployer receives the full supply $S_0$:

$$
\text{Balance}(\text{deployer}) = S_0, \quad \sum_{a} \text{Balance}(a) = S_0
$$

Step 2 — A `transfer(from, to, amount)` decreases `from`'s balance and increases `to`'s balance by exactly `amount`:

$$
\text{Balance}(\text{from}) \leftarrow \text{Balance}(\text{from}) - \text{amount}
$$

$$
\text{Balance}(\text{to}) \leftarrow \text{Balance}(\text{to}) + \text{amount}
$$

Step 3 — The sum is unchanged because the changes cancel out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{a} \text{Balance}'(a) &= [\text{Balance}(\text{from}) - \text{amount}] + [\text{Balance}(\text{to}) + \text{amount}] + \sum_{a \neq \text{from, to}} \text{Balance}(a) \\
&= \sum_{a} \text{Balance}(a) - \text{amount} + \text{amount} \\
&= \sum_{a} \text{Balance}(a)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The invariant holds for any number of sequential transfers, as long as no function mints or burns tokens outside the defined supply mechanism.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Template Standards

| Property | ERC-20 | ERC-721 | ERC-1155 |
|----------|--------|---------|----------|
| **Token Type** | Fungible | Non-Fungible | Multi-Token (FT + NFT) |
| **Balance Mapping** | $\text{uint256}$ per address | $\text{uint256}$ per `(address, tokenID)` | $\text{uint256}$ per `(address, tokenID)` |
| **Total Supply** | Single global | Per tokenID | Per tokenID |
| **Approval Granularity** | Global allowance | Per tokenID | Per tokenID |
| **Batch Transfer** | Not native | Not native | **Native (`safeBatchTransferFrom`)** |
| **Gas Efficiency (Batch)** | Low | Low | **High** (single transaction) |
| **Use Case** | Currencies, utility tokens | Art, collectibles | Gaming, hybrid assets |
| **Inherits from** | `ERC20` (OZ) | `ERC721` (OZ) | `ERC1155` (OZ) |
| **EIP Number** | EIP-20 | EIP-721 | EIP-1155 |

### 3.4 The Solidity Template Skeleton — A Step-by-Step Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **canonical structure** of a smart contract template. Each block serves a specific engineering purpose. KTU students must memorize this seven-block structure for the ESE.

**Block 1 — License & Pragma**

```solidity
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.20;
```

**Block 2 — Imports from OpenZeppelin**

```solidity
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol";
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/access/Ownable.sol";
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/security/Pausable.sol";
```

**Block 3 — Contract Declaration with Multiple Inheritance**

```solidity
contract KTUStudentToken is ERC20, Ownable, Pausable {
```

**Block 4 — Constructor**

```solidity
constructor(address initialOwner) ERC20("KTU Student Token", "KTUST") Ownable(initialOwner) {
    _mint(msg.sender, 1000000 * 10 ** decimals());
}
```

**Block 5 — Custom Function with Modifier**

```solidity
function pause() public onlyOwner {
    _pause();
}

function mint(address to, uint256 amount) public onlyOwner {
    _mint(to, amount);
}
```

**Block 6 — Overridden Transfer Hook**

```solidity
function _update(address from, address to, uint256 value) internal override whenNotPaused {
    super._update(from, to, value);
}
```

**Block 7 — Events (Optional)**

```solidity
event TokensMinted(address indexed to, uint256 amount);
```

This skeleton, when compiled and deployed, is a **production-ready, KTU-aligned, audit-compliant ERC-20 token template**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML.

### 4.2 Diagram 1 — Smart Contract Template Architecture (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[LAYER 1 PRAGMA AND LICENSE] --> L2[LAYER 2 IMPORTS]
    L2 --> L3[LAYER 3 INTERFACE DECLARATION]
    L3 --> L4[LAYER 4 CONTRACT INHERITANCE]
    L4 --> L5[LAYER 5 STATE VARIABLES]
    L5 --> L6[LAYER 6 MODIFIERS AND FUNCTIONS]
    L6 --> L7[LAYER 7 EVENTS AND ERRORS]
    L7 --> DEP[COMPILED BYTECODE DEPLOYED TO EVM]
    L1 -.->|requires| OZ[OPENZEPPELIN LIBRARY]
    L2 -.->|requires| OZ
    L3 -.->|requires| OZ
    L4 -.->|requires| OZ
    L5 -.->|requires| OZ
    L6 -.->|requires| OZ
    L7 -.->|requires| OZ
```

### 4.3 Diagram 2 — Token Standard Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[SMART CONTRACT TEMPLATES] --> TOK[TOKEN STANDARDS]
    ROOT --> ACC[ACCESS CONTROL]
    ROOT --> SEC[SECURITY PRIMITIVES]
    ROOT --> UTI[UTILITY CONTRACTS]
    TOK --> ERC20[ERC20 FUNGIBLE TOKEN]
    TOK --> ERC721[ERC721 NON FUNGIBLE TOKEN]
    TOK --> ERC1155[ERC1155 MULTI TOKEN]
    ACC --> OWN[OWNABLE SINGLE ADMIN]
    ACC --> RBAC[ROLE BASED ACCESS CONTROL]
    ACC --> MULTISIG[MULTI SIGNATURE WALLET]
    SEC --> RENTRY[REENTRANCY GUARD]
    SEC --> PAUSE[PAUSABLE CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    SEC --> PROXY[UPGRADEABLE PROXY]
    UTI --> STAKING[STAKING POOL]
    UTI --> VAULT[YIELD VAULT]
    UTI --> ICO[ICO CROWDSALE]
    UTI --> ESCROW[ESCROW CONTRACT]
    ERC20 --> USE1[STABLECOINS GOVERNANCE TOKENS]
    ERC721 --> USE2[DIGITAL ART COLLECTIBLES]
    ERC1155 --> USE3[GAMING ASSETS HYBRID NFTS]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 3 — Smart Contract Deployment Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DEVELOPER WRITES TEMPLATE] --> B[SOLIDITY COMPILER SOLC]
    B --> C[BYTECODE AND ABI]
    C --> D[WALLET METAMASK]
    D --> E[SIGN TRANSACTION]
    E --> F[EVM CREATE OPCODE]
    F --> G[CONTRACT DEPLOYED ON CHAIN]
    G --> H[CONTRACT ADDRESS RETURNED]
    H --> I[DAPP FRONTEND INTERACTION]
    I --> J[STATE CHANGES VIA FUNCTION CALLS]
    J --> K[EVENTS EMITTED TO LOG]
    K --> L[OFF CHAIN INDEXER THE GRAPH]
    L --> M[USER VISIBLE UI UPDATE]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 4 — Inheritance Graph of a Sample Template

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[ERC20 BASE CONTRACT] --> EXT[ERC20Burnable EXTENSION]
    EXT --> CAPPED[ERC20Capped SUPPLY LIMIT]
    CAPPED --> PAUSABLE[Pausable CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    PAUSABLE --> OWNABLE[Ownable ADMIN CONTROL]
    OWNABLE --> FINAL[KTU STUDENT TOKEN FINAL CONTRACT]
    BASE -.->|provides| P1[TOTAL SUPPLY BALANCE OF TRANSFER]
    EXT -.->|provides| P2[BURN FUNCTION]
    CAPPED -.->|provides| P3[SUPPLY CEILING ENFORCEMENT]
    PAUSABLE -.->|provides| P4[EMERGENCY STOP]
    OWNABLE -.->|provides| P5[OWNER ONLY MODIFIER]
```

### 4.6 Diagram 5 — Reentrancy Attack Pattern (The Classic Vulnerability)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER CALLS WITHDRAW] --> B[CONTRACT CHECKS BALANCE]
    B --> C[BALANCE GREATER THAN ZERO]
    C --> D[CONTRACT SENDS ETHER VIA CALL]
    D --> E[RECEIVER FALLBACK TRIGGERS]
    E --> F[REENTERS WITHDRAW FUNCTION]
    F --> G[BALANCE STILL NOT UPDATED]
    G --> D
    D -.->|DRAINED| H[ALL ETHER DRAINED]
    FIX[CHECKS EFFECTS INTERACTIONS PATTERN] --> SOL[STATE UPDATE BEFORE EXTERNAL CALL]
    SOL --> SAFE[REENTRANCY GUARD MODIFIER]
    SAFE --> PROTECTED[CONTRACT PROTECTED]
```

### 4.7 Diagram 6 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (End-to-End DApp Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER[USER LAYER]
        UI1[METAMASK WALLET]
        UI2[DAPP BROWSER INTERFACE]
    end
    subgraph FRONT[FRONTEND LAYER]
        FE1[REACT OR NEXT JS]
        FE2[WEB3 JS OR ETHERS JS]
    end
    subgraph BLOCK[BLOCKCHAIN LAYER]
        BC1[SMART CONTRACT TEMPLATE DEPLOYED]
        BC2[OPENZEPPELIN LIBRARY INHERITED]
        BC3[EVM EXECUTION ENVIRONMENT]
    end
    subgraph OFF[OFF CHAIN LAYER]
        OC1[THE GRAPH INDEXER]
        OC2[IPFS STORAGE]
        OC3[ORACLE CHAINLINK]
    end
    UI1 --> FE2
    UI2 --> FE1
    FE1 --> FE2
    FE2 --> BC1
    BC1 --> BC2
    BC1 --> BC3
    BC3 --> OC1
    BC3 --> OC2
    BC3 --> OC3
    OC1 --> FE1
    OC2 --> FE1
    OC3 --> FE1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define a smart contract template. List any four popular categories of smart contract templates used in Ethereum development.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A **smart contract template** is a pre-written, reusable, and often pre-audited Solidity (or Vyper/Rust) code structure that encapsulates standard business logic and can be inherited or deployed directly to a blockchain.

**Four popular categories:**
1. **Token Standards** (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155)
2. **Access Control** (Ownable, RBAC, Multi-Sig)
3. **Security Primitives** (ReentrancyGuard, Pausable, Upgradeable)
4. **Utility Contracts** (Staking, Crowdsale, Escrow, Vault)

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for categories with examples = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*Differentiate between ERC-20 and ERC-721 token templates with two key differences.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ERC-20 | ERC-721 |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Fungibility** | Fungible (interchangeable) | Non-Fungible (unique) |
| **Token ID** | No token ID concept | Each token has unique `uint256` ID |
| **Balance Model** | Single balance per address | Per-token-ID balance |
| **Use Case** | Currency, utility tokens | NFTs, digital art, collectibles |

*(1 mark per valid difference with example = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. The student must attempt **one** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part is worth 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks]**
*Explain the seven-layer architectural anatomy of a smart contract template written in Solidity. Illustrate with a small annotated example.*

**Model Solution:**

The seven-layer anatomy ensures modularity, security, and maintainability.

**Layer 1 — Pragma Directive** pins the compiler version:

```solidity
pragma solidity ^0.8.20;
```

**Layer 2 — SPDX License Identifier** avoids deployment warnings on EVM:

```solidity
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
```

**Layer 3 — Import Statements** bring in OpenZeppelin contracts:

```solidity
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol";
```

**Layer 4 — Contract Declaration with Inheritance** uses the `is` keyword:

```solidity
contract MyToken is ERC20 {
```

**Layer 5 — State Variables** persist in EVM storage (each slot = 32 bytes).

**Layer 6 — Function Modifiers** enforce pre-conditions (e.g., `onlyOwner`).

**Layer 7 — Events** emit logs via the `LOG` opcode for off-chain indexers.

*(1 mark per layer with code snippet = 7 marks)*

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]**
*With a suitable example, explain the OpenZeppelin library and its significance in smart contract template development.*

**Model Solution:**

**OpenZeppelin** is an open-source, formally verified library of reusable smart contract templates for Ethereum and EVM-compatible chains. It provides battle-tested implementations of ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, access control, and security primitives.

**Example — Inheriting ERC-20:**

```solidity
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol";

contract KTU is ERC20 {
    constructor() ERC20("KTU Token", "KTUT") {
        _mint(msg.sender, 1000000);
    }
}
```

**Significance:**
1. **Security**: OpenZeppelin contracts are audited by top firms; using them reduces the risk of reentrancy, overflow, and access control bugs.
2. **Time-saving**: A token that takes weeks to code from scratch can be deployed in minutes.
3. **Composability**: DeFi protocols (Aave, Compound) compose OpenZeppelin contracts.
4. **Community Trust**: Adopted industry-wide; grants instant credibility.

*(2 marks for definition + 3 marks for example + 2 marks for significance = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks]**
*Describe the ERC-20 token standard template. List all six mandatory functions and explain any three with their signatures.*

**Model Solution:**

ERC-20, formalized in **EIP-20**, is the most widely adopted smart contract template for **fungible tokens**. It defines a common interface ensuring all tokens interoperate with wallets, exchanges, and dApps.

**The Six Mandatory Functions:**

1. `totalSupply() → uint256`
2. `balanceOf(address account) → uint256`
3. `transfer(address to, uint256 amount) → bool`
4. `transferFrom(address from, address to, uint256 amount) → bool`
5. `approve(address spender, uint256 amount) → bool`
6. `allowance(address owner, address spender) → uint256`

**Explanation of Three:**

**i. `totalSupply()`**: Returns the total number of tokens ever minted. Used by UI dashboards and analytics.

**ii. `transfer(address to, uint256 amount)`**: Moves `amount` tokens from `msg.sender` to `to`. Must emit a `Transfer` event.

**iii. `approve(address spender, uint256 amount)`**: Authorizes `spender` to withdraw up to `amount` tokens on behalf of `msg.sender`. Enables the two-step transfer pattern used by exchanges.

*(1.5 marks per function explained with signature = 4.5 marks, plus 2.5 marks for listing the full set = 7 marks)*

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]**
*What is a reentrancy attack? How does the ReentrancyGuard template protect against it? Show the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern as a remedy.*

**Model Solution:**

A **reentrancy attack** is a vulnerability where a malicious contract re-enters the victim contract's function *before* the state has been updated, draining funds repeatedly.

**Famous Example**: The **DAO Hack (2016)** — $60M drained from TheDAO contract on Ethereum.

**Vulnerable Code Pattern:**

```solidity
function withdraw(uint amount) public {
    require(balances[msg.sender] >= amount);
    (bool success,) = msg.sender.call{value: amount}("");
    balances[msg.sender] -= amount;  // STATE UPDATE AFTER EXTERNAL CALL
}
```

The attacker's `fallback()` function calls `withdraw()` again before `balances[msg.sender] -= amount` executes.

**Remedy 1 — Checks-Effects-Interactions Pattern:**

```solidity
function withdraw(uint amount) public {
    require(balances[msg.sender] >= amount);  // CHECKS
    balances[msg.sender] -= amount;          // EFFECTS (state update FIRST)
    (bool success,) = msg.sender.call{value: amount}("");  // INTERACTIONS LAST
}
```

**Remedy 2 — ReentrancyGuard Template (OpenZeppelin):**

```solidity
import "@openzeppelin/contracts/security/ReentrancyGuard.sol";

contract Vault is ReentrancyGuard {
    function withdraw(uint amount) public nonReentrant {
        require(balances[msg.sender] >= amount);
        balances[msg.sender] -= amount;
        payable(msg.sender).transfer(amount);
    }
}
```

The `nonReentrant` modifier uses a `locked` boolean state variable that is set to `true` at function entry and `false` at exit, blocking nested calls.

*(2 marks for definition + 2 marks for vulnerable code + 3 marks for remedy = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE**
> 
> 1. **Confusing ERC-20 with ERC-721**: Students frequently write "ERC-20 is for NFTs." This is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**. Always state ERC-20 = fungible, ERC-721 = non-fungible.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting SPDX License**: Omitting `// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT` causes a compiler warning and is a **0.5-mark penalty** in code-based questions.
> 
> 3. **Missing the `^` in Pragma**: Writing `pragma solidity 0.8.20;` (no caret) is **incorrect** for the 2024 scheme — it locks the compiler too strictly. Use `pragma solidity ^0.8.20;`.
> 
> 4. **Not Drawing Diagrams**: For 14-mark questions, examiners allocate **1-2 marks** explicitly for diagrams. Always include a Mermaid-style architecture or inheritance tree.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Gas Units**: `Gas Used` and `Gas Price` are different quantities. `Gas Used` is in **gas units**, `Gas Price` is in **Gwei**. Mixing them up is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the "Why"**: Examiners reward *reasoning*. Don't just list the seven layers — explain *why* each layer exists (e.g., "Pragma ensures compiler compatibility and prevents silent semantic differences across versions").

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Smart Contract Templates**

- **Definition**: Pre-written, reusable, pre-audited smart contract code structures (Solidity/Vyper/Rust).
- **Pragma Directive**: `pragma solidity ^0.8.20;` — pins compiler version.
- **SPDX License**: Mandatory identifier (e.g., MIT) to avoid deployment warnings.
- **OpenZeppelin**: Gold-standard, formally verified smart contract library.
- **ERC-20**: Fungible token standard (6 mandatory functions, 2 events).
- **ERC-721**: Non-fungible token standard (unique `uint256` tokenId).
- **ERC-1155**: Multi-token standard (batched transfers, gas-efficient).
- **Inheritance**: `contract Child is Parent1, Parent2 { }` — Solidity supports multiple inheritance.
- **Modifier**: `modifier onlyOwner() { require(msg.sender == owner); _; }` — the `_;` represents function body.
- **Pausable**: Circuit breaker for emergencies using `whenNotPaused` modifier.
- **Ownable**: Single-admin access control via `Ownable` from OpenZeppelin.
- **ReentrancyGuard**: Uses a `locked` boolean to prevent nested reentrant calls.
- **Gas Formula**: $\text{Total Fee} = \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$ (in Gwei, then divide by $10^9$ for ETH).
- **Storage**: 1 slot = 32 bytes (256 bits); `SSTORE` is expensive.
- **Wei Conversion**: $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18}$ Wei $= 10^9$ Gwei.
- **Events**: Emitted via `LOG` opcode; `event Transfer(address indexed from, address indexed to, uint256 value)`.
- **Conservation Invariant**: $\sum_{a} \text{Balance}(a) = \text{TotalSupply}$ (must hold for ERC-20).
- **Checks-Effects-Interactions**: State update must come **before** external call.
- **Templates are Compositional**: DeFi = OpenZeppelin + Custom Logic + Oracles.
- **Common Pitfalls**: Reentrancy, Integer Overflow (fixed in 0.8.0+), Access Control bypass, Front-running.
- **Diagrams are Mandatory**: Always include an inheritance or flow diagram in 14-mark answers.
- **Block Gas Limit (Ethereum Mainnet)**: $\approx 30 \times 10^6$ gas per block.
- **Upgradeable Proxies**: Use `UUPS` or `Transparent` pattern to avoid redeployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oracles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Oracles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oracle (Blockchain Context):** A blockchain oracle is a **trusted third-party service** that acts as a **bidirectional bridge** between a deterministic, on-chain smart contract and an **off-chain data source**, enabling the smart contract to read real-world, external information and/or push the results of on-chain computations to external systems. Oracles are the middleware layer that solves the **Oracle Problem** — the fundamental inability of blockchains to natively access off-chain data without compromising consensus.

Formally, an oracle can be defined as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{O} = \langle D, S, A, P, C, T_{upd} \rangle$$

Where:
- $D$ = the **Data Source** (e.g., a weather API, IoT sensor, stock feed)
- $S$ = the **Security Model** (centralized, federated, or decentralized)
- $A$ = the **Aggregation Logic** (mean, median, weighted vote)
- $P$ = the **Payment / Incentive Policy**
- $C$ = the **Cryptographic Commitment** (e.g., TLSNotary, Town Crier, ZK-proof)
- $T_{upd}$ = the **Update Frequency / Latency Tolerance**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a smart contract as a judge who lives inside a sealed courtroom.**
> The judge can ONLY rule based on evidence that is physically brought into the courtroom. The judge cannot look out the window, call the weather bureau, or check a stock ticker on the internet. An **Oracle is the trusted courier / attorney** who walks into the courtroom, brings the outside world's information as a signed legal document, and presents it as admissible evidence so the judge (smart contract) can make a correct ruling.

In simple words: **Blockchains are deaf, dumb, and blind to the outside world. Oracles give them senses.**

## 1.3 Why Oracles are Necessary

Smart contracts, by design, are **deterministic Turing machines** running on every node of a distributed ledger. If nodes were allowed to fetch external data freely, each node would get slightly different answers (e.g., different API response timestamps, different prices), and **consensus would break instantly**. Hence, the blockchain itself is intentionally sandboxed — and the oracle is the controlled, auditable escape hatch.

## 1.4 Key Metrics (Standard Industry Terms)

- **Latency:** Time taken from external event to on-chain availability (measured in **seconds**, typically **0–60 s** for DeFi price feeds).
- **Deviation Threshold:** Percentage price change that triggers an update (commonly **0.5% – 2%** for stable pairs, **5%+** for volatile pairs).
- **Heartbeat:** Periodic refresh interval even without deviation (e.g., **3600 s**).
- **Confidence Interval:** Statistical bound around the aggregated value, e.g., **±0.1%**.
- **Gas Cost per Update:** On-chain transaction fee consumed per oracle submission (Ethereum: **~50,000 – 200,000 gas**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Oracle Problem — Step-by-Step Logic

The Oracle Problem is a **trilemma** analogous to the CAP theorem:

1. **Connectivity** — The smart contract needs real-world data.
2. **Determinism** — Every validating node must get the *same* answer; otherwise, consensus fails.
3. **Trust Minimization** — Relying on a single source re-introduces centralization, defeating the purpose of blockchain.

A naive design would simply call `API.get("BTC/USD")` from inside a smart contract — this is **impossible** because:
- Solidity/EVM cannot perform HTTP requests.
- Different nodes get different responses at different times.
- APIs can lie, go offline, or be manipulated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution Architecture:** Push the data-fetching logic *outside* the consensus boundary, run it on a separate network of oracle nodes that **aggregate & sign** the result cryptographically, and submit a *single deterministic transaction* back on-chain. The smart contract then trusts the *signature*, not the source.

## 2.2 Classification of Oracles (Hierarchical Taxonomy)

| # | Type | Direction | Source | Trust Model | Real-World Example |
|---|------|-----------|--------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Software Oracle** | Inbound | Web APIs, databases | Centralized / Federated | Currency exchange rate feed |
| 2 | **Hardware Oracle** | Inbound | IoT sensors, RFID | Physical tamper-resistant | Supply-chain temperature logger |
| 3 | **Inbound Oracle** | Off-chain → On-chain | External world | Any | Price feeds for DeFi |
| 4 | **Outbound Oracle** | On-chain → Off-chain | Smart contract | Any | Smart lock unlocking after payment |
| 5 | **Centralized Oracle** | Either | Single provider | Single point of failure | A firm's private API |
| 6 | **Decentralized Oracle Network (DON)** | Either | Multiple nodes | Trust-minimized via aggregation | **Chainlink**, **Band Protocol** |
| 7 | **Contract-Specific Oracle** | Inbound | Designed for one dApp | Custom | Augur's reporter system |
| 8 | **Human Oracle** | Inbound | Experts / voters | Reputation-based | Kleros dispute resolution |
| 9 | **Compute-Enabled Oracle** | Both | Off-chain heavy compute | Verifiable (ZK/TEE) | Chainlink Functions, Chainlink VRF |
| 10 | **Cross-Chain Oracle** | Inbound | Another blockchain | Bridge + Oracle hybrid | Chainlink CCIP |

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU Exam Reference)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Notation | Engineering Meaning | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Aggregated Price | $P_{agg} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$ | Arithmetic mean of $N$ oracle reports | USD per unit |
| 2 | Weighted Median (robust) | $P_{med} = \text{median}(w_i \cdot P_i)$ | Resistant to outliers | USD per unit |
| 3 | Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI = \bar{P} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Statistical certainty on aggregation | ±% |
| 4 | Deviation Threshold | $\Delta P \geq \tau \cdot P_{prev}$ | Trigger condition for update | 0.5%–2% |
| 5 | Heartbeat Update | $t_{now} - t_{last} \geq T_{hb}$ | Time-based refresh trigger | 3600 s |
| 6 | Gas Cost per Update | $G = 21{,}000 + 16{,}000 \cdot n_{args}$ | On-chain storage cost (EIP-150 base) | ~200k gas |
| 7 | Trust Score | $TS = \prod_{i} (1 - p_i)$ | Joint probability of $N$ honest nodes | 0 → 1 |
| 8 | Latency SLA | $L_{SLA} = t_{onchain} - t_{event}$ | Time-to-feeds guarantee | seconds |
| 9 | Reputation Weight | $w_i = \frac{S_i}{\sum_j S_j}$ | Stake-weighted aggregation weight | 0 → 1 |
| 10 | Aggregation Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_i - \bar{P})^2$ | Spread among oracle reports | (USD)² |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use standard statistical definitions. The Confidence Interval uses the **z-score of 1.96** for a two-tailed **95% confidence level** under a normal distribution assumption. The $G$ formula uses the standard EVM transaction cost model where each non-zero byte of calldata costs **16 gas** and each zero byte costs **4 gas** (post-Istanbul hardfork).

## 2.4 Architectural Components of an Oracle System

A production-grade oracle (e.g., Chainlink) consists of **three on-chain contracts** and **off-chain infrastructure**:

1. **Consumer Contract** — The dApp that requests data.
2. **Oracle Contract (Service Layer)** — The on-chain entry point that emits events for off-chain nodes.
3. **Aggregator Contract** — The on-chain accumulator that stores the latest validated answer.
4. **Oracle Node** — The off-chain worker that listens for events, fetches data, signs, and submits.
5. **External Data Source** — The third-party API, sensor, or system.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DeFi (Decentralized Finance):** Lending protocols like **Aave** and **Compound** require real-time asset prices to trigger liquidations. A stale or wrong price can drain millions.
- **Insurance:** **Etherisc** and **Nexus Mutual** use weather/oracle feeds to auto-trigger crop or flight-delay payouts.
- **Supply Chain:** **IBM Food Trust / TradeLens** use hardware oracles (RFID + GPS) to track provenance.
- **Gaming & NFTs:** **Chainlink VRF** provides cryptographically verifiable randomness for on-chain games.
- **Enterprise:** **SWIFT + Chainlink CCIP** enables cross-chain interbank settlements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 End-to-End Oracle Transaction Walk-Through (Theoretical)

Below is the **exhaustive state-transition trace** of a single oracle price-feed update. Each step is shown with the protocol-level interaction.

### Setup

Let:
- $SC$ = Consumer contract (e.g., a lending protocol)
- $OC$ = Oracle contract on-chain
- $AG$ = Aggregator contract on-chain
- $N_1, N_2, \dots, N_k$ = $k$ independent oracle nodes
- $S_i$ = Stake of node $i$ in ETH
- $API$ = External price source

### Step 1: Off-Chain Event Occurs

The external world experiences a price change:

$$P_{BTC}^{real} \rightarrow P_{BTC}^{real} + \Delta P, \quad \Delta P \geq \tau \cdot P_{BTC}^{real}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The oracle node's monitoring engine polls the API at frequency $f_{poll}$ and compares the new value to the last on-chain value. The condition $\Delta P \geq \tau \cdot P_{prev}$ is a *Boolean predicate*; if TRUE, an update is initiated.

### Step 2: Each Node $N_i$ Fetches Data Independently

Each node queries the API and obtains its own reading:

$$P_i = API.getPrice(\text{BTC/USD}, t_{fetch,i})$$

**Conversion Logic:** Because networks are asynchronous, $t_{fetch,i}$ differs slightly per node. The system enforces a *staleness window* $\Delta t_{max}$ (typically 60 s) so that $P_i$ values are temporally aligned.

### Step 3: Off-Chain Cryptographic Signing

Each node $N_i$ produces a digital signature over a deterministic message:

$$\sigma_i = \text{ECDSA}_{priv_i}\Big(\; H\big( P_i \;\Vert\; \text{assetID} \;\Vert\; t_{fetch,i} \big) \;\Big)$$

**Conversion Logic:** Here $H(\cdot)$ is the Keccak-256 hash function. The signature $\sigma_i$ is bound to the price, asset identifier, and timestamp — preventing replay attacks.

### Step 4: Aggregation Off-Chain (Optional Pre-Aggregation)

The leader node (or a threshold crypto scheme) computes a robust aggregate:

$$P_{agg} = \text{WeightedMedian}\big( \{ (P_i, w_i) \}_{i=1}^{k} \big), \quad w_i = \frac{S_i}{\sum_j S_j}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Weighted median is preferred over mean because it is **Bressert robust** — a single malicious node can shift the mean by an unbounded amount, but the median is bounded by the inter-quartile distance.

### Step 5: On-Chain Submission Transaction

A single aggregated transaction is broadcast:

$$\text{Tx} = \big( P_{agg}, \; \text{assetID}, \; \text{epoch}, \; \text{aggregatorAddr}, \; \sigma_{agg} \big)$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is *one* transaction regardless of $k$ — the blockchain only sees the result, not the $k$ individual reports. This keeps the **consensus footprint constant** and preserves determinism.

### Step 6: Aggregator Contract Validates and Stores

The aggregator contract on-chain executes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{require}\big( \; \text{verifySig}(\sigma_{agg}, P_{agg}, \text{assetID}, \text{epoch}) \; \big) \\
\text{require}\big( \; t_{now} - P_{agg}.t_{fetch} \leq \Delta t_{max} \; \big) \\
\text{storage}[\text{assetID}].latestAnswer \leftarrow P_{agg} \\
\text{storage}[\text{assetID}].updatedAt \leftarrow t_{now}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first `require` checks that the submitter holds a valid ECDSA key for the registered oracle service. The second enforces **freshness**. Only after both checks pass is the new price written to storage.

### Step 7: Consumer Contract Reads the Price

The lending protocol calls the aggregator in the same transaction (or via a price-aware view function):

$$P_{onchain} = \text{AG.getLatestPrice}(\text{BTC})$$

**Conversion Logic:** This call is a `STATICCALL` — a read-only EVM opcode that does not modify state and therefore costs zero gas when called off-transaction.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Aggregation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Problem (commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions):**
> Five oracle nodes report the BTC/USD price as follows: $P_1 = 60{,}000$, $P_2 = 60{,}050$, $P_3 = 59{,}980$, $P_4 = 61{,}500$, $P_5 = 60{,}020$. The stakes are $S_1 = 100$, $S_2 = 200$, $S_3 = 50$, $S_4 = 10$, $S_5 = 150$ ETH. Compute the **arithmetic mean** and the **stake-weighted mean**, and decide whether node 4 should be flagged as an outlier (assume 2$\sigma$ rule).

### Solution

**Step A — Total Stake:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{tot} &= S_1 + S_2 + S_3 + S_4 + S_5 \\
&= 100 + 200 + 50 + 10 + 150 \\
&= 510 \; \text{ETH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Arithmetic Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{P} &= \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} P_i \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(60{,}000 + 60{,}050 + 59{,}980 + 61{,}500 + 60{,}020) \\
&= \frac{301{,}550}{5} \\
&= 60{,}310 \; \text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Stake-Weighted Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_w &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_i \cdot P_i}{S_{tot}} \\
&= \frac{(100)(60{,}000) + (200)(60{,}050) + (50)(59{,}980) + (10)(61{,}500) + (150)(60{,}020)}{510} \\
&= \frac{6{,}000{,}000 + 12{,}010{,}000 + 2{,}999{,}000 + 615{,}000 + 9{,}003{,}000}{510} \\
&= \frac{30{,}627{,}000}{510} \\
&\approx 60{,}052.94 \; \text{USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Variance and Outlier Detection:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_i - \bar{P})^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{4}\Big[ (60{,}000 - 60{,}310)^2 + (60{,}050 - 60{,}310)^2 \\
&\quad + (59{,}980 - 60{,}310)^2 + (61{,}500 - 60{,}310)^2 + (60{,}020 - 60{,}310)^2 \Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{4}\Big[ 96{,}100 + 67{,}600 + 108{,}900 + 1{,}416{,}100 + 84{,}100 \Big] \\
&= \frac{1{,}772{,}800}{4} \\
&= 443{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{443{,}200} \approx 665.73 \; \text{USD}
$$

The 2$\sigma$ bounds are:

$$
\bar{P} \pm 2\sigma = 60{,}310 \pm 1{,}331.46 = [58{,}978.54, \; 61{,}641.46]
$$

Since $P_4 = 61{,}500$ lies **inside** $[58{,}978.54, \; 61{,}641.46]$, node 4 is **not flagged** as a 2$\sigma$ outlier — though it is suspiciously close. A production system would either drop it (3$\sigma$ rule) or apply a heavier **stake-weighted penalty**.

**Conversion Logic of the answer:** A common KTU 14-mark question asks you to justify the choice of mean vs. median. The **arithmetic mean** is sensitive to a single malicious node (as $P_4$ shifted $\bar{P}$ upward). The **stake-weighted mean** is more robust because $S_4$ is small. The **stake-weighted median** would be the safest aggregation rule in production.

## 3.3 Latency-Bandwidth Trade-off Derivation

Let $T_{poll}$ be the polling period, $C_{onchain}$ be the gas cost per update, and $C_{gas}$ be the gas price. The **annual cost of an oracle feed** is:

$$
C_{year} = \frac{31{,}536{,}000}{T_{poll}} \cdot C_{onchain} \cdot C_{gas}
$$

For $T_{poll} = 60$ s, $C_{onchain} = 200{,}000$ gas, $C_{gas} = 30$ Gwei:

$$
C_{year} = \frac{31{,}536{,}000}{60} \cdot 200{,}000 \cdot 30 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{ETH}
$$

$$
C_{year} = 525{,}600 \cdot 6 \times 10^{-3} = 3{,}153.6 \; \text{ETH per year per feed}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **operational reality** behind DeFi oracle pricing — high-frequency oracles are expensive, which is why **deviation thresholds** exist: you only update when the data actually changes by $\tau$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Oracle Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    extWorld[External Data Source] -->|HTTP / IoT / API| nodeA[Oracle Node 1]
    extWorld -->|HTTP / IoT / API| nodeB[Oracle Node 2]
    extWorld -->|HTTP / IoT / API| nodeC[Oracle Node N]
    nodeA -->|Signed Report| aggOff[Off-chain Aggregator]
    nodeB -->|Signed Report| aggOff
    nodeC -->|Signed Report| aggOff
    aggOff -->|Single Signed Aggregate Tx| scOn[On-chain Oracle Contract]
    scOn -->|Validate + Store| agCon[Aggregator Contract]
    agCon -->|Read Call| dapp[Consumer Smart Contract]
    dapp -->|Trigger| action[On-chain Action e.g. Liquidation]
    style extWorld fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style aggOff fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style scOn fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style agCon fill:#577590,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style dapp fill:#f3722c,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style action fill:#d90429,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Oracle Taxonomy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    oracle[Blockchain Oracles]
    oracle --> dir[Direction Based]
    dir --> inb[Inbound]
    dir --> outb[Outbound]
    oracle --> src[Source Based]
    src --> sw[Software Oracle]
    src --> hw[Hardware Oracle]
    src --> hum[Human Oracle]
    oracle --> trust[Trust Model Based]
    trust --> cent[Centralized]
    trust --> fed[Federated]
    trust --> don[Decentralized Oracle Network]
    oracle --> use[Use-case Based]
    use --> defi[DeFi Price Feeds]
    use --> rnd[Randomness VRF]
    use --> cc[Cross Chain CCIP]
    use --> fn[Compute Functions]
    style oracle fill:#577590,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style dir fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style src fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Oracle Request-Response Sequence (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant SC as Consumer Contract
    participant OR as Oracle Contract
    participant ON as Oracle Node Off chain
    participant API as External API
    participant AG as Aggregator Contract
    U->>SC: Call function needing price
    SC->>OR: Emit OracleRequest event
    OR-->>ON: Off-chain listener wakes
    ON->>API: HTTP GET /price
    API-->>ON: Returns JSON price
    ON->>ON: Sign result with ECDSA
```
