| **Transparency** | Limited | Full public ledger |
| **Recount** | Manual & slow | Instant via smart contract |
| **Trust Model** | Trust EVM manufacturer | Trust mathematics |

**2. Cryptographic Primitives (3 Marks):**
- **Ring Signatures (1 Mark):** Voter signs anonymously within a group.
- **Homomorphic Encryption (1 Mark):** Tally votes without decrypting individual ballots.
- **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (1 Mark):** Prove ballot validity without revealing choice.

**3. Real-World Example (1 Mark):**
Estonia has piloted blockchain-based e-voting since 2008; West Virginia (USA) used blockchain for overseas military voting in 2018.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "blockchain" with "cryptocurrency"** — Government use cases are NOT about Bitcoin/Ether; they are about **DLT infrastructure**.
> 2. **Ignoring the Privacy–Transparency trade-off** — Always mention **Zero-Knowledge Proofs** when discussing citizen data.
> 3. **Writing about "Public Blockchain only"** — Examiners expect **Consortium Blockchain** as the government-preferred model.
> 4. **No mention of Consensus** — For 7-mark architecture questions, **PBFT or PoA** must be specified.
> 5. **Skipping Challenges** — A balanced answer must list scalability, regulation, and digital-divide challenges.
> 6. **No real-world example** — At least one verifiable deployment (Estonia, Dubai, Sweden) earns the extra mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Government Blockchain Use Cases**

- **Definition:** DLT applied to public-sector services for tamper-evident, transparent record-keeping.
- **Three Pillars:** **Immutability**, **Transparency**, **Decentralization**.
- **Preferred Model:** **Consortium Blockchain** (permissioned, governed by trusted agencies).
- **Consensus:** **PBFT** or **PoA** (not PoW) for government networks.
- **Key Use Cases:** Digital ID, Land Registry, GST/Tax, E-Voting, DBT/Welfare, Certificates, Judicial Records, Inter-Agency Data.
- **Land Registry Workflow:** Citizen → DID → Smart Contract → 4-Node Validation → PBFT Consensus → Immutable Title.
- **PBFT Quorum Formula:** $\lceil (2N+1)/3 \rceil$ — for $N=4$, quorum = 3.
- **Privacy Tool:** **Zero-Knowledge Proofs (ZKPs)** for verification without data exposure.
- **Welfare Innovation:** **Smart contract-based conditional DBT** eliminates ghost beneficiaries.
- **Cryptographic Primitives for E-Voting:** **Ring Signatures + Homomorphic Encryption + ZKPs**.
- **Real-World References:** **Estonia KSI**, **Dubai Smart Govt**, **Sweden Lantmäteriet**, **Singapore OpenCerts**, **Georgia (Country) NAPR**.
- **Critical Challenges:** Scalability, regulation, legacy integration, digital divide, quantum vulnerability.
- **Mathematical Anchor:** SHA-256 hash → **2^256** collision resistance (computationally unbreakable).
- **Citizen Empowerment:** Shift from **trust-by-authority** to **trust-by-mathematics**.
- **Auditability:** Every government action is permanently traceable, enabling **real-time RTI compliance**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health Care
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Health Care

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blockchain in Healthcare** refers to the application of distributed ledger technology (DLT) to securely store, share, and manage health-related data (EHRs, claims, prescriptions, clinical trial results, and drug supply chain records) across multiple stakeholders (patients, hospitals, insurers, pharmacies, and regulators) in a tamper-proof, auditable, and patient-centric manner.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus framing, healthcare is treated as a **high-impact application domain** of blockchain where the central problems are *data silos*, *lack of interoperability*, *vulnerability to tampering*, and *absence of patient ownership* over personal health information.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Domains under "Health Care" in Blockchain

| Sub-Domain | Core Problem Solved |
|------------|---------------------|
| Electronic Health Records (EHR) | Patient-controlled, interoperable medical history |
| Drug Supply Chain | Counterfeit drug elimination, end-to-end traceability |
| Clinical Trials | Tamper-proof trial data, transparent result reporting |
| Health Insurance Claims | Automated claim adjudication, fraud reduction |
| Medical Credentialing | Verification of doctor/hospital authenticity |
| Genomics & Precision Medicine | Secure monetization and sharing of genetic data |
| Public Health Surveillance | Anonymous, real-time disease outbreak tracking |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Medical Passport":**
> Imagine every human being carries a *digital medical passport* from birth to death. Every doctor visit, every prescription, every lab test, every vaccination is stamped into this passport by the consulting entity. The passport is **not stored at any single hospital** — instead, thousands of hospitals around the world hold a synchronized copy. If a malicious insider in Hospital A tries to alter your prescription record, the mismatch with the copies in Hospitals B, C, and D is immediately detected, just like a forged signature in a notarized document.
> 
> In this analogy, **the patient holds the key** to allow or deny any doctor from reading or writing into the passport. This is exactly what blockchain-based healthcare systems aim to deliver.

### 1.4 Critical Healthcare Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **HIPAA** (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, USA, 1996) — the standard for medical data privacy.
- **HL7 FHIR** (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) — the standard for healthcare data exchange.
- **ICD-10 / ICD-11** — international standard for disease classification.
- **GDPR** (EU) — governs patient consent and right-to-be-forgotten.
- **Average data breach cost in healthcare: ~USD 10.93 million** (highest among all industries, IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report).
- **~30% of all data breaches globally occur in healthcare** (Industry standard metric).

### 1.5 Why Blockchain and Not Just a Database?

| Feature | Traditional Centralized DB | Blockchain-Based System |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Data ownership | Hospital-owned | Patient-owned |
| Mutability | Admin can alter | Cryptographically immutable |
| Transparency | Closed (one organization) | Shared among permissioned nodes |
| Single point of failure | Yes | No |
| Auditability | Internal logs only | Cryptographic + consensus-based |
| Interoperability | Custom APIs, fragile | Standardized DLT protocols |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Layers of a Blockchain-Healthcare System

A typical blockchain-based healthcare architecture operates in **five logical layers**:

1. **Data Layer (Health Data Sources)**
   - EHR systems, IoT wearables, lab reports, genomic sequencers, pharmacy records.
   - Data is **hashed** (not stored raw on-chain due to size and privacy).

2. **Network / Storage Layer (Off-Chain Storage)**
   - Raw health data stored in **off-chain distributed storage** (IPFS, Filecoin, secure cloud).
   - Only the **cryptographic hash** and metadata is written on-chain.

3. **Consensus Layer**
   - Permissioned consensus mechanisms like **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)**, **Raft**, or **PoA (Proof of Authority)** are used because healthcare consortia are not fully anonymous.
   - Public chains like Ethereum may use **PoS (Proof of Stake)**.

4. **Smart Contract Layer**
   - Business rules: who can access what data, under what conditions, for how long.
   - Automates **insurance claim adjudication**, **consent management**, and **drug release at supply chain checkpoints**.

5. **Application Layer (User-Facing)**
   - Patient dashboards, doctor portals, insurer UIs, regulator dashboards, mobile apps.

### 2.2 Core Operational Flow — "Write-Once, Read-Authorized"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
> 1. A clinician generates a new medical event (e.g., a diagnosis, lab result, prescription).
> 2. The event is **digitally signed** by the clinician using their private key.
> 3. The raw data is stored **off-chain**; the SHA-256 hash and metadata are written on-chain.
> 4. The smart contract enforces that only parties listed in the patient's **consent registry** can read this hash pointer.
> 5. A new block is formed and propagated; consensus is reached.
> 6. The patient's mobile app updates its view of the EHR in real time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the mathematical backbone of any blockchain-healthcare system. Although healthcare is an *application* domain, the underlying cryptographic and consensus math is what makes the system trustworthy.

| Concept | Equation | Description | Standard |
|---------|----------|-------------|----------|
| Cryptographic Hash | $H(m) = h$ | One-way hash of message $m$ | SHA-256, Keccak-256 |
| Merkle Root | $H_{root} = H(H_{AB} \parallel H_{CD})$ where $H_{AB} = H(H_A \parallel H_B)$ | Root hash of the Merkle tree over all transactions in a block | SHA-256 |
| Block Hash | $B_n = H(B_{n-1} \parallel t \parallel n \parallel \text{Nonce})$ | Hash of block $n$ linking to previous block | SHA-256 |
| Digital Signature | $\sigma = S_K(m)$, verify with $V_K(\sigma, m) = \text{true}$ | Signing and verification using asymmetric key pair | ECDSA (secp256k1) |
| PoW Difficulty | $\text{Target} < \frac{2^{256}}{D}$ | Difficulty $D$ controls the hash space | Bitcoin/Ethereum |
| PBFT Fault Tolerance | $f < \frac{n}{3}$ | Tolerates up to $f$ faulty nodes in $n$-node network | Castro-Liskov 1999 |
| Interoperability Index | $I_{chain} = \frac{\text{Successful Cross-Chain TXs}}{\text{Total Cross-Chain Attempts}}$ | Performance metric for multi-chain health data | Custom |
| Smart Contract Gas | $\text{Cost}_{tx} = \sum_{i} G_{op_i} \cdot P_{gas}$ | Total transaction cost | Ethereum Yellow Paper |

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Healthcare

- The **Merkle Root** enables a hospital to prove to an insurer that a specific prescription exists in a block without revealing the entire block — critical for **selective disclosure**.
- The **PBFT fault tolerance formula** $f < n/3$ justifies why a 21-hospital consortium can tolerate up to 6 malicious nodes — directly applicable to **federated hospital networks**.
- The **Digital Signature equation** underpins non-repudiation: a doctor who signed a prescription cannot later deny issuing it.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Hospital Networks:** Mayo Clinic, Mount Sinai, and MIT piloted MedRec — a blockchain-based EHR system.
- **Pharma Giants:** Pfizer, IBM, and Walmart use blockchain to track pharmaceuticals and reduce counterfeit drug incidents.
- **Governments:** Estonia uses a KSI (Keyless Signature Infrastructure)-based blockchain to secure 100% of its citizens' health records since 2008.
- **Startups:** Medicalchain, Patientory, BurstIQ, Solve.Care, Nebula Genomics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Verifying a Drug's Provenance via Merkle Proof

A pharmaceutical regulator wants to prove that a specific drug batch $D$ exists in the supply chain log of the blockchain. The full block is too large to transmit. A **Merkle proof** is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Suppose a block contains **4 drug-tracking transactions** ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$). The Merkle tree is built as follows.

**Step 1:** Compute individual transaction hashes.

$$
H_1 = H(T_1), \quad H_2 = H(T_2), \quad H_3 = H(T_3), \quad H_4 = H(T_4)
$$

Each $H_i$ is a 256-bit SHA-256 output, often written as a 64-character hexadecimal string.

**Step 2:** Compute the second level of the tree by concatenating pairs and hashing.

$$
H_{12} = H(H_1 \parallel H_2), \quad H_{34} = H(H_3 \parallel H_4)
$$

Here, $\parallel$ denotes bitwise string concatenation. If $H_1$ is 256 bits and $H_2$ is 256 bits, then $H_1 \parallel H_2$ is 512 bits, which is then fed into SHA-256 to produce a 256-bit output $H_{12}$.

**Step 3:** Compute the Merkle Root.

$$
H_{root} = H(H_{12} \parallel H_{34})
$$

This single 256-bit value $H_{root}$ is what gets stored in the block header. It is a unique cryptographic fingerprint of all 4 transactions.

**Step 4:** Constructing the Merkle proof for transaction $T_1$.

To prove $T_1$ is in the block, the verifier does not need all 4 transactions. They only need:
- $H_1$ (computed locally from $T_1$)
- $H_2$ (provided by the prover)
- $H_{34}$ (provided by the prover)

**Step 5:** Verification computation by the regulator.

$$
H_{12}' = H(H_1 \parallel H_2)
$$

$$
H_{root}' = H(H_{12}' \parallel H_{34})
$$

The regulator compares $H_{root}'$ with the on-chain stored $H_{root}$.

$$
\text{Valid} \iff H_{root}' = H_{root}
$$

If equal, the proof is cryptographically valid. This is a **logarithmic verification** — for $N$ transactions, only $\log_2 N$ hashes are needed to verify, regardless of block size.

### 3.2 Derivation: PBFT Fault Tolerance in a Hospital Consortium

Consider a consortium of $n$ hospitals running PBFT for consensus over patient records.

**Step 1:** Define the variables.

Let $n$ be the total number of nodes. Let $f$ be the maximum number of faulty (Byzantine) nodes. PBFT requires that all honest nodes reach the same decision despite $f$ faulty nodes.

**Step 2:** Apply the PBFT safety condition.

PBFT requires a quorum of $2f + 1$ honest nodes to make progress. With $f$ faulty nodes, the total nodes must satisfy:

$$
n = 3f + 1 \quad \text{(minimum case)}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $f$ in terms of $n$.

$$
f = \frac{n - 1}{3}
$$

Rearranging to the canonical inequality form:

$$
f < \frac{n}{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply to a real consortium.

For a 21-hospital PBFT network:

$$
f < \frac{21}{3} = 7
$$

So the system can tolerate up to **6 fully Byzantine hospitals** (must be strictly less than 7). This is the analytical justification for why a 21-node network is the common choice in healthcare consortia.

### 3.3 Derivation: Consent Smart Contract Execution Cost

Suppose a patient grants access to a doctor for a 30-day window. The smart contract has the following operations:

| Operation | Gas Used (units) | Gas Price (Gwei) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Store consent record (SSTORE) | 20,000 | 50 |
| Emit access event (LOG) | 375 | 50 |
| Validate timestamp (TIMESTAMP) | 2 | 50 |
| Read patient ID (CALL) | 700 | 50 |

**Step 1:** Sum the gas units.

$$
G_{total} = 20{,}000 + 375 + 2 + 700 = 21{,}077 \text{ gas units}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by gas price to get the ETH cost.

$$
\text{Cost}_{ETH} = 21{,}077 \times 50 \text{ Gwei} = 1{,}053{,}850 \text{ Gwei}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert Gwei to ETH (1 ETH = $10^9$ Gwei).

$$
\text{Cost}_{ETH} = \frac{1{,}053{,}850}{10^9} = 0.00105385 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Step 4:** At an assumed price of 1 ETH = USD 3,000, the consent transaction costs:

$$
\text{Cost}_{USD} = 0.00105385 \times 3000 \approx \text{USD } 3.16
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the basis of cost-analysis questions often asked in KTU 14-mark questions. Mastering the *gas calculation pattern* is essential.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Healthcare Blockchain Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Mobile App] --> B[Smart Contract Layer]
    C[Doctor Portal] --> B
    D[Hospital EHR System] --> B
    E[Pharmacy System] --> B
    F[Insurance Provider] --> B
    G[Regulator Dashboard] --> B
    B --> H[Consensus Layer PBFT or PoA]
    H --> I[Off Chain Storage IPFS or Secure Cloud]
    I --> J[Raw Medical Data]
    B --> K[On Chain Hashes and Metadata]
    H --> L[Distributed Ledger across Hospitals]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**. The diagram represents a permissioned healthcare consortium where each entity interacts with the smart contract layer. The smart contract enforces consent, the consensus layer validates block creation, the off-chain storage handles bulky medical files, and the on-chain ledger stores only cryptographically verifiable pointers.

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Pharmaceutical Drug Supply Chain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drug Manufacturer] --> B[Smart Contract Mint and Sign Batch]
    B --> C[Wholesaler 1]
    C --> D[Wholesaler 2]
    D --> E[Pharmacy]
    E --> F[Patient]
    F --> G[Verification of Authenticity via QR Code]
    G --> H{Valid Block Hash?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Drug Confirmed Authentic]
    H -->|No| J[Counterfeit Alert Issued]
    B --> K[Blockchain Ledger Hash per Custodian]
    C --> K
    D --> K
    E --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Workflow Logic:**
> 1. The manufacturer signs a drug batch with their private key and writes a smart contract event.
> 2. Each custodian (wholesaler, pharmacy) appends a transfer event, creating an unbroken chain of custody.
> 3. The patient scans a QR code on the drug packaging; the verification app reads the blockchain to confirm the chain of custody is intact.
> 4. If any link in the chain is broken or invalid, a counterfeit alert is raised.

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Patient-Controlled EHR Access Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Patient
    participant SC as Smart Contract
    participant D as Doctor
    participant BC as Blockchain Ledger
    P->>SC: Submit Consent for Doctor D
    SC->>BC: Write Consent Record with Expiry
    D->>SC: Request Access to Patient EHR
    SC->>BC: Verify Consent and Expiry
    BC-->>SC: Consent Valid
    SC->>D: Grant Temporary Read Access
    D->>SC: Submit Diagnosis and Prescription
    SC->>BC: Write Diagnosis Hash and Prescription Hash
    BC-->>P: Notify Patient of New Record
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence Logic:**
> This sequence diagram demonstrates a **patient-centric access model**. The patient initiates consent, the doctor requests access, the smart contract enforces policy, and any new medical record is committed to the blockchain. The patient receives a real-time notification, ensuring transparency and patient agency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
**Define Electronic Health Records (EHR) and explain how blockchain technology can improve the management of EHRs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An Electronic Health Record (EHR) is a digital, real-time, patient-centered record that makes information available instantly and securely to authorized users across a healthcare ecosystem.

**Blockchain Improvement (2 Marks):** Blockchain improves EHR management by providing:
- **Immutability**: Once a record is added, it cannot be altered without detection.
- **Interoperability**: Different hospital systems can read/write to a shared ledger.
- **Patient Ownership**: Patients control access via private keys and smart contracts.
- **Auditability**: Every access is logged cryptographically, ensuring accountability.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Pattern]**
**List and briefly explain any three challenges of using blockchain in the healthcare sector.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Scalability (1 Mark):** Public blockchains like Ethereum handle only 15–30 transactions per second, while a hospital network may generate thousands of records daily.
2. **Regulatory Compliance (1 Mark):** GDPR's "right to be forgotten" conflicts with blockchain's immutability, since records cannot be deleted once written.
3. **Data Privacy on Public Chains (1 Mark):** Even hashed data on public chains can leak sensitive patterns; permissioned chains are often required.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **NOT** write generic blockchain advantages without linking them to a healthcare-specific use case. The examiner expects terms like *EHR*, *consent*, *HIPAA*, *counterfeit drug*, *clinical trial*.
> 2. When drawing diagrams, use **rectangles for entities and arrows for data flow**. Do not mix flowchart shapes.
> 3. For 14-mark questions, a **diagram is mandatory** for at least 4 marks.
> 4. Always mention **which type of blockchain** (public, private, consortium) is suitable for the use case.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of a blockchain-based healthcare system**. Include all five layers with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **five major use cases** of blockchain in healthcare, with one real-world example for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain how blockchain ensures **drug traceability and anti-counterfeiting** in the pharmaceutical supply chain. Include the role of smart contracts and a flow diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **technical, regulatory, and adoption challenges** of implementing blockchain in healthcare, with at least three examples in each category. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

#### Question A — Part (a) Model Solution

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A blockchain-based healthcare system is a permissioned distributed ledger designed to manage sensitive medical data across hospitals, insurers, regulators, and patients with cryptographic security.

**Layer 1: Data Source Layer (1 Mark):**
- EHR systems, lab equipment, wearable IoT devices, genomic sequencers, and pharmacy systems generate raw health data.
- Example: A glucometer reading from a diabetic patient.

**Layer 2: Off-Chain Storage Layer (1 Mark):**
- Raw data is too large (MRI scans, genomic sequences) to store on-chain.
- Stored in IPFS, Filecoin, or HIPAA-compliant cloud storage.
- Only the cryptographic hash and metadata are placed on-chain.

**Layer 3: Consensus Layer (1 Mark):**
- Permissioned networks use PBFT, PoA, or Raft because healthcare consortia are pre-approved.
- Example: A 21-hospital consortium using Hyperledger Fabric with PBFT, tolerating up to 6 faulty nodes per the formula $f < n/3$.

**Layer 4: Smart Contract Layer (1.5 Marks):**
- Encodes access control, consent management, insurance claim rules.
- Example: A smart contract that automatically pays an insurer once a doctor's signature on a treatment is verified.

**Layer 5: Application Layer (1.5 Marks):**
- Patient apps, doctor dashboards, regulator analytics tools.
- Example: Estonia's nationwide e-Health portal backed by KSI blockchain, serving 1.3 million citizens since 2008.

**Conclusion (Mandatory for full marks):**
A well-designed healthcare blockchain system must balance *immutability* with *patient privacy*, often using hybrid on-chain/off-chain storage and permissioned consensus.

#### Question A — Part (b) Model Solution

**Use Case 1: EHR Interoperability (1.4 Marks):**
Different hospitals using different software (e.g., Epic vs. Cerner) can read/write to a shared blockchain. Example: **MIT's MedRec** prototype demonstrated this with Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center.

**Use Case 2: Pharmaceutical Supply Chain (1.4 Marks):**
Each drug batch is registered on the blockchain at the manufacturer. The FDA's Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA) pilot uses blockchain. Example: **Walmart-IBM Hyperledger Fabric** reduced drug traceability time from 7 days to 2.2 seconds.

**Use Case 3: Clinical Trials (1.4 Marks):**
Trial protocols, patient consent forms, and outcome data are immutably logged. Example: **Pfizer and IBM** use blockchain to track clinical trial consent and reduce data tampering.

**Use Case 4: Health Insurance Claims (1.4 Marks):**
Smart contracts auto-execute claim payouts when pre-defined conditions are met. Example: **Solve.Care** platform processes cross-border insurance claims using Cardano blockchain.

**Use Case 5: Public Health Surveillance (1.4 Marks):**
Anonymous, real-time disease data is aggregated from multiple hospitals. Example: During **COVID-19**, the WHO explored blockchain for tracking vaccination certificates (e.g., **IBM Digital Health Pass**).

---

#### Question B — Part (a) Model Solution

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The pharmaceutical supply chain is vulnerable to counterfeit drugs, which the WHO estimates constitute ~10% of global drug supply. Blockchain provides end-to-end traceability.

**Step 1: Drug Registration (1.5 Marks):**
The manufacturer creates a unique digital identity (NFT or token) for each drug batch. Metadata includes batch number, manufacture date, expiration, and composition. A smart contract mints this identity and writes it on-chain.

**Step 2: Custodian Transfers (1.5 Marks):**
As the drug moves from manufacturer → wholesaler → distributor → pharmacy → patient, each custodian's private key signs a transfer event on-chain. The Merkle root of the batch is updated at each step.

**Step 3: Patient Verification (1.5 Marks):**
The patient scans a QR code on the packaging. The verification app queries the blockchain and displays the chain of custody. The patient can verify:
- The manufacturer is authentic.
- The drug has not been tampered with.
- The chain of custody is unbroken.

**Step 4: Counterfeit Detection (1.5 Marks):**
If a QR scan returns an invalid hash, mismatched manufacturer, or broken chain link, an alert is raised. Regulatory bodies can also run real-time analytics to detect anomalies.

**Diagram (Mandatory, embedded in solution):**
A flow diagram from Manufacturer → Wholesaler → Pharmacy → Patient with blockchain ledger attached at each stage (refer to Diagram 2 in Section 4 of these notes).

---

#### Question B — Part (b) Model Solution

**Technical Challenges (2.5 Marks):**
1. **Scalability**: Public chains process 15–30 TPS; healthcare requires thousands. Solution: Layer-2 solutions, sharding, permissioned chains.
2. **Storage**: Genomic data can be 100+ GB per patient; on-chain storage is infeasible. Solution: Off-chain IPFS with on-chain pointers.
3. **Latency**: PBFT round-trips introduce delays unsuitable for emergency care. Solution: Hybrid models with on-chain finality and off-chain caching.

**Regulatory Challenges (2.5 Marks):**
1. **GDPR Right to be Forgotten**: Conflicts with blockchain immutability. Solution: Off-chain storage with revocable pointers; hashed identifiers instead of personal data.
2. **HIPAA Compliance**: Blockchain must ensure that protected health information (PHI) is not exposed. Solution: Zero-knowledge proofs, encryption, permissioned access.
3. **Cross-border Data Laws**: Different countries have different rules. Solution: Multi-chain architectures with jurisdiction-specific ledgers.

**Adoption Challenges (2 Marks):**
1. **Resistance from Hospitals**: Legacy systems like Epic and Cerner are deeply entrenched. Solution: Middleware APIs.
2. **Lack of Standards**: No universal DLT standard for healthcare. Solution: Adoption of HL7 FHIR + blockchain profiles.
3. **High Initial Cost**: Consortium setup costs can be prohibitive for small hospitals. Solution: Cloud-based Blockchain-as-a-Service (BaaS) from IBM, Oracle, AWS.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU):**

- **Definition**: Blockchain in healthcare = distributed ledger for medical data with patient-centric access control.
- **Five Layers**: Data, Off-chain Storage, Consensus, Smart Contract, Application.
- **Three Core Use Cases to Always Mention**: EHR interoperability, drug supply chain, clinical trials.
- **Two Real-World Examples to Always Cite**: MIT MedRec (EHR) and Walmart-IBM Hyperledger Fabric (drug supply).
- **Key Blockchain Type**: **Permissioned consortium** (not public) for healthcare.
- **Consensus for Healthcare**: PBFT, PoA, Raft (not PoW due to energy + latency).
- **PBFT Formula**: $f < n/3$ — tolerate up to 6 faulty nodes in a 21-node network.
- **Merkle Proof Equation**: $H_{root} = H(H(H_1 \parallel H_2) \parallel H(H_3 \parallel H_4))$ — used for drug verification.
- **Three Top Challenges**: Scalability, GDPR conflict, regulatory compliance.
- **GDPR Solution**: Off-chain data with on-chain pointers; never store PHI on-chain.
- **HIPAA**: Governs US; blockchain must use encryption and access control to comply.
- **Key Terminology to Use in Answers**: "Permissioned", "PBFT", "Merkle Root", "Off-chain storage", "Smart contract consent", "Patient-centric", "Chain of custody".
- **KSI (Keyless Signature Infrastructure)**: Estonia's blockchain used for 100% of health records — must know for theory questions.
- **DSCSA (Drug Supply Chain Security Act)**: US law mandating drug traceability — used as a use-case trigger.
- **Gas Cost Calculation**: Know how to compute $\text{Cost}_{tx} = \sum G_{op_i} \cdot P_{gas}$ — frequently asked in 14-mark questions.
- **Right to Be Forgotten vs. Immutability**: Always present both sides and the technical compromise (off-chain revocation).
- **Don't Confuse**: Permissioned ≠ Private ≠ Public. Healthcare uses **permissioned consortium**, which is partially decentralized among known entities.
- **Examiner Loves**: Diagrams with rectangular blocks, labeled arrows, and a clear legend. Always include one in 14-mark answers.
- **Avoid**: Generic answers about "blockchain is secure and transparent" without tying back to a healthcare scenario.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Finance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Blockchain in Finance** refers to the application of distributed ledger technology (DLT) to financial services, enabling peer-to-peer (P2P) value transfer, decentralized financial instruments, programmable money, and trustless settlement systems without the need for traditional intermediaries such as banks, brokers, or clearinghouses.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCGT601)**, blockchain-based finance is a confluence of three core pillars:

1. **Cryptocurrencies** — digital assets secured by cryptographic primitives.
2. **Smart Contracts** — self-executing programs that automate financial logic.
3. **Decentralized Finance (DeFi)** — an open, permissionless financial ecosystem built on public blockchains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Public Notary" Ledger**
> Imagine a giant, transparent glass notebook that every villager owns an identical copy of. Whenever someone wants to transfer money to another, they don't go to the bank manager — they simply announce the transaction to everyone. Each villager verifies it, and once validated, the entry is permanently etched into the notebook. **No single person can erase or forge a line, and the book is visible to all.** This is precisely how blockchain-based finance operates — the *village* is the peer-to-peer network, the *notebook* is the distributed ledger, and the *validation process* is the consensus mechanism.

### 1.3 Key Terminology in Blockchain Finance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cryptocurrency** | A digital or virtual currency secured by cryptography, operating on a blockchain. |
| **Wallet** | A software/hardware tool that stores private/public key pairs for transacting. |
| **Smart Contract** | Code deployed on a blockchain that runs deterministically when predefined conditions are met. |
| **DeFi** | Decentralized Finance — financial services built without centralized intermediaries. |
| **Tokenization** | The process of representing real-world assets (RWA) as digital tokens on a blockchain. |
| **Stablecoin** | A cryptocurrency pegged to a stable reserve like USD, gold, or algorithmically stabilized. |
| **DEX** | Decentralized Exchange — a P2P marketplace for trading crypto assets. |
| **NFT** | Non-Fungible Token — a unique cryptographic token representing ownership of a specific asset. |
| **Consensus** | The mechanism by which distributed nodes agree on the validity of transactions. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Finance module specifically bridges the gap between *cryptographic primitives* (Modules 1 & 2) and *real-world business applications* (Module 5). Understanding finance is essential to evaluate blockchain's value proposition over traditional systems.

### 1.4 Traditional Finance vs. Blockchain Finance

| Dimension | Traditional Finance (TradFi) | Blockchain Finance (DeFi/CeFi) |
|---|---|---|
| **Intermediary** | Banks, brokers, clearinghouses | Smart contracts, protocols |
| **Settlement Time** | T+1 to T+3 days | Near-instant (seconds to minutes) |
| **Transparency** | Opaque (institution-controlled) | Fully transparent (public ledger) |
| **Accessibility** | KYC-gated, geography-restricted | Permissionless, global (24/7) |
| **Operational Hours** | Business hours | 24/7/365 |
| **Custody of Assets** | Third-party custodians | Self-custody via private keys |
| **Auditability** | Manual, expensive | Native, cryptographic |
| **Cost Structure** | High overhead, fees | Low protocol-level gas fees |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Financial Stack on Blockchain

Blockchain-based financial systems are best understood as a layered stack:

1. **Layer 0 — Infrastructure**: Internet, hardware, P2P networking.
2. **Layer 1 — Base Protocol**: The blockchain itself (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana).
3. **Layer 2 — Scaling Solutions**: Rollups, state channels, sidechains.
4. **Layer 3 — Application Protocols**: DeFi protocols, DEXs, lending platforms.
5. **Layer 4 — User Interface**: Wallets, dApps, dashboards.

### 2.2 Cryptographic Foundations of Financial Transactions

Every financial transaction on a blockchain relies on three core cryptographic operations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cryptography in Finance?**
> In a trustless network, cryptography replaces institutional trust with mathematical certainty. It guarantees *integrity* (data isn't tampered), *authenticity* (sender is verified), and *non-repudiation* (sender cannot deny the transaction).

#### 2.2.1 Hashing for Transaction Integrity
A cryptographic hash function maps arbitrary input to a fixed-size output. For finance, this ensures that transaction details cannot be altered post-broadcast.

$$H(M) = h \quad \text{where} \quad h \in \{0,1\}^{n}$$

**Key Properties Required:**
- **Pre-image resistance:** Given $h$, finding $M$ such that $H(M) = h$ is computationally infeasible.
- **Collision resistance:** Finding $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is infeasible.
- **Avalanche effect:** A 1-bit change in $M$ produces a drastically different $h$.

#### 2.2.2 Digital Signatures for Authorization
A digital signature scheme is a triple of algorithms $(KeyGen, Sign, Verify)$ that allows a user to prove ownership of funds without revealing their private key.

$$\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(M))$$

$$\text{Verify}(pk, H(M), \sigma) \in \{true, false\}$$

In Bitcoin/Ethereum, the **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)** with curve **secp256k1** is standard. The signature equation is:

$$\sigma = (r, s) \quad \text{where} \quad r = (k \cdot G)_x \mod n$$

- $G$ = generator point on the elliptic curve
- $k$ = randomly chosen per-signature nonce
- $n$ = order of the curve

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Concept (KTU):** The private key $sk$ controls the funds. Lose the key → lose the money. This is the principle of *self-sovereign money*.

#### 2.2.3 Merkle Trees for Efficient Verification
Transactions in a block are organized in a **Merkle Tree** (binary hash tree), enabling lightweight verification (SPV — Simplified Payment Verification).

$$\text{Merkle Root} = H(H(T_1 \vert\vert T_2) \vert\vert H(T_3 \vert\vert T_4))$$

This allows a light client to verify a transaction's inclusion in a block using only $O(\log n)$ hashes instead of $O(n)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form the core of any KTU numerical/analytical question on Finance.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hash Function** | $H(M) = h$ maps message to fixed digest | $h \in \{0,1\}^{256}$ for SHA-256 |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, H(M))$ | ECDSA on secp256k1 |
| 3 | **Signature Verification** | $\text{Verify}(pk, M, \sigma) \to \{0,1\}$ | Returns true/false |
| 4 | **Public Key Derivation** | $pk = sk \cdot G$ on elliptic curve | $G$ = base point |
| 5 | **Bitcoin Address** | $A = \text{BASE58}(H_{160}(pk))$ | RIPEMD-160 + SHA-256 |
| 6 | **Block Reward** | $R_t = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor t / 210000 \rfloor}$ BTC | $t$ = block height |
| 7 | **Total BTC Supply Cap** | $S_{max} = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC | Hard-coded protocol rule |
| 8 | **Mining Difficulty** | $D_{new} = \frac{D_{old} \cdot T_{actual}}{T_{target}}$ | Retarget every 2016 blocks |
| 9 | **Transaction Fee** | $fee = \sum inputs - \sum outputs$ | Native to UTXO model |
| 10 | **Gas Cost (Ethereum)** | $fee_{tx} = gasUsed \cdot gasPrice$ | $gasUsed$ in units, $gasPrice$ in Gwei |
| 11 | **Merkle Root (2 nodes)** | $root = H(H(a) \vert\vert H(b))$ | SHA-256 concatenation |
| 12 | **Halving Interval** | Every **210,000** blocks (≈4 years) | Bitcoin protocol |
| 13 | **Block Time (Bitcoin)** | Target = **10 minutes** | Adjusted via difficulty |
| 14 | **Block Time (Ethereum)** | Target = **12-15 seconds** | Post-Merge (PoS) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The financial blockchain stack powers:

- **Retail Banking**: Ripple (XRP) and Stellar enable cross-border remittances in **3-5 seconds** at fractions of a cent.
- **Trade Finance**: We.trade, Marco Polo — blockchain-based Letter of Credit (LoC) systems.
- **Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs)**: China's **Digital Yuan (e-CNY)**, India's **Digital Rupee (e₹)**, Bahamas' **Sand Dollar**.
- **Capital Markets**: Tokenized securities on platforms like **Polymath**, **Securitize**.
- **Insurance**: Smart contract-based parametric insurance (e.g., **Etherisc** for flight delay payouts).
- **Supply Chain Finance**: IBM's **TradeLens** for invoice financing and provenance.

### 2.5 Smart Contracts as Financial Primitives

> [!NOTE]
> A **smart contract** is *programmable money*. It transforms static ledger entries into dynamic financial instruments.

**Key financial smart contract operations:**

1. **Escrow**: Funds locked until conditions $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ are satisfied.
   $$state_{new} = \begin{cases} \text{release to seller} & \text{if} \ \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i \\ \text{refund to buyer} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

2. **Multi-Signature Wallets**: Require $m$-of-$n$ signatures for fund release.
   $$\text{release} \iff \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i \geq m, \quad s_i \in \{0,1\}$$

3. **Token Standards**:
   - **ERC-20**: Fungible tokens (currencies, stablecoins).
   - **ERC-721**: Non-fungible tokens (unique assets).
   - **ERC-1155**: Multi-token standard (hybrid).

|---|---|---|
| **DEX (AMM)** | Automated token swaps | Uniswap, SushiSwap |
| **Lending Protocol** | Collateralized borrowing/lending | Aave, Compound |

### 2.7 Tokenization of Real-World Assets (RWA)

Tokenization converts rights to a physical/financial asset into a digital token on a blockchain.

$$\text{Asset}_{real} \xrightarrow{\text{oracle + legal wrapper}} \text{Token}_{digital}$$

**Categories:**
- **Security Tokens**: Represent equity, debt, or ownership rights (regulated).
- **Utility Tokens**: Grant access to a service.
- **Asset-Backed Tokens**: Backed by reserves (e.g., gold, real estate).

**Benefits:**
- **Fractional Ownership**: $1M property → 1,000,000 tokens @ $1 each.
- **24/7 Liquidity**: Tokens trade on global, always-open markets.
- **Programmable Compliance**: Transfer rules enforced by smart contracts (e.g., accredited investor checks).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Numerical questions in the Finance module often test (a) block reward halving calculations, (b) transaction fee computation, (c) Merkle root derivation, and (d) digital signature verification. Master these step-by-step.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bitcoin Block Reward Over Time

**Problem:** Calculate the block reward in Bitcoin at block height $t = 630,000$ (approximately the 3rd halving era). Also calculate the total BTC in circulation assuming all blocks were mined on schedule.

**Step 1: Recall the halving formula.**

The Bitcoin block reward halves every 210,000 blocks:

$$R_t = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor t / 210000 \rfloor} \quad \text{(BTC)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the halving epoch.**

$$\lfloor t / 210000 \rfloor = \lfloor 630000 / 210000 \rfloor = \lfloor 3 \rfloor = 3$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula.**

$$R_{630000} = 50 \cdot 2^{-3} = 50 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 6.25 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

$$\text{At block height 630,000, the miner receives 6.25 BTC per block, in addition to transaction fees.}$$

**Step 5: Calculate total BTC mined up to block 630,000.**

Total BTC mined = (sum of rewards across 3 full epochs) + (current epoch partial)

$$\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= 210000 \cdot 50 = 10{,}500{,}000 \text{ BTC} \\
S_1 &= 210000 \cdot 25 = 5{,}250{,}000 \text{ BTC} \\
S_2 &= 210000 \cdot 12.5 = 2{,}625{,}000 \text{ BTC} \\
S_3 &= 630000 \cdot 6.25 = 3{,}937{,}500 \text{ BTC} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= S_0 + S_1 + S_2 + S_3 \\
&= 10{,}500{,}000 + 5{,}250{,}000 + 2{,}625{,}000 + 3{,}937{,}500 \\
&= 22{,}312{,}500 \text{ BTC}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Actual circulating supply is slightly different due to lost coins, mining delays, and the unmined coins. Theoretical cap: $S_{max} = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Transaction Fee Calculation (Bitcoin UTXO Model)

**Problem:** A transaction has:
- **Inputs:** 2 UTXOs of 0.5 BTC and 0.3 BTC
- **Outputs:** 1 payment of 0.7 BTC to recipient, 1 change return to sender

What is the implicit transaction fee, assuming the sender authorizes the full input sum?

**Step 1: State the fee formula.**

In Bitcoin's UTXO model:

$$fee = \sum_{i=1}^{n} inputs_i - \sum_{j=1}^{m} outputs_j$$

**Step 2: Sum the inputs.**

$$\sum inputs = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 3: Sum the outputs (excluding change for now, assume change = 0.1 BTC to sender).**

$$\sum outputs = 0.7 + 0.1 = 0.8 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the fee.**

$$fee = 0.8 - 0.8 = 0.0 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 5: Re-interpret with a real-world scenario.**

If the sender wants to pay a fee of 0.0001 BTC, the change must be reduced:

$$change = 0.8 - 0.7 - 0.0001 = 0.0999 \text{ BTC}$$

$$fee = 0.5 + 0.3 - 0.7 - 0.0999 = 0.0001 \text{ BTC}$$

**Verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.5 + 0.3 &= 0.8 \text{ BTC (in)} \\
0.7 + 0.0999 + 0.0001 &= 0.8 \text{ BTC (out)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text{Ledger balanced: } in = out$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Ethereum Gas Fee Computation

**Problem:** A smart contract execution consumes 21,000 gas (a standard ETH transfer). The gas price is 50 Gwei. The current ETH price is ₹2,00,000. Calculate the transaction fee in INR.

**Step 1: Recall the gas fee formula.**

$$fee_{tx} = gasUsed \times gasPrice \quad \text{(in ETH)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$fee_{tx} = 21000 \times 50 \text{ Gwei} = 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

**Step 3: Convert Gwei to ETH.**

$$1 \text{ ETH} = 10^9 \text{ Gwei}$$

$$fee_{tx} = \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{10^9} = 0.00105 \text{ ETH}$$

**Step 4: Convert ETH to INR.**

$$fee_{INR} = 0.00105 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹210$$

**Interpretation:** A standard ETH transfer at this gas price costs ₹210.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Merkle Root Construction

**Problem:** A block contains 4 transactions $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ with hashes:
- $H(T_1) = A$
- $H(T_2) = B$
- $H(T_3) = C$
- $H(T_4) = D$

Compute the Merkle Root $R$ using SHA-256 double hashing $H_{256}(x \vert\vert y)$.

**Step 1: Compute Level 1 (leaf-to-internal) hashes.**

$$H_{12} = H_{256}(A \vert\vert B)$$

$$H_{34} = H_{256}(C \vert\vert D)$$

**Step 2: Compute the Merkle Root.**

$$R = H_{256}(H_{12} \vert\vert H_{34})$$

**Step 3: Substitute the full expansion.**

$$R = H_{256}\big( H_{256}(A \vert\vert B) \ \vert\vert \ H_{256}(C \vert\vert D) \big)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double hashing?** Bitcoin uses $H_{256}(H_{256}(x))$ to defend against length-extension attacks on SHA-256.

### 3.5 Worked Example 5: Digital Signature Verification Logic

**Problem:** Trace the verification of a Bitcoin transaction with the following parameters:
- Message hash: $z = H(M)$
- Signature: $\sigma = (r, s)$
- Signer's public key: $P = pk \cdot G$

**Step 1: Verify that $r$ and $s$ are in the valid range.**

$$1 \leq r < n \quad \text{and} \quad 1 \leq s < n$$

If false, the signature is **invalid**.

**Step 2: Compute the modular inverse of $s$.**

$$s^{-1} \mod n = w$$

**Step 3: Compute two intermediate values.**

$$u_1 = z \cdot s^{-1} \mod n$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \mod n$$

**Step 4: Compute the curve point.**

$$(x_1, y_1) = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot P$$

**Step 5: The signature is valid if and only if:**

$$r \equiv x_1 \pmod{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The signer proves knowledge of $sk$ (the discrete log of $P$) by constructing a signature that only the holder of $sk$ could produce. Verification requires *no knowledge* of $sk$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Smart Contract Use Cases in Finance

| **Escrow** | `if (delivered && signed) release()` | Lawyer/escrow agent | Removes intermediary, 24/7 |
| **Lending** | `if (collateral ≥ 1.5× loan) issueLoan()` | Bank underwriting | Algorithmic, instant |
| **Insurance** | `if (flightDelayed > 2h) payout()` | Claims adjuster | Parametric, automated |
| **Royalty** | `if (resale) send 10% to creator` | Legal contract, manual | Native, enforced by code |
| **Voting** | `if (quorum && majority) execute()` | Proxy/board meeting | Transparent, immutable |
| **Tokenized Stocks** | `transfer(buyer, seller, qty, price)` | Broker + clearinghouse | T+0 settlement |
