However, **no one can ever spend the funds** at that address, because the ECDSA signature generation requires $k_{\text{priv}}$ which is gone. The coins are effectively **burned forever**, permanently removed from circulation. This is why an estimated **3.7 million BTC** (worth over $200 billion at peak) are stranded in inaccessible addresses — they are the "Satoshi-era" and "lost wallet" coins.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Public Key with Address (Cost: 1-2 marks):** Many students write that the "public key IS the address." This is **wrong**. The address is a *hash* of the public key, not the public key itself. The public key is 33 or 65 bytes; the address is 20 bytes (before encoding).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the `p` in Modular Arithmetic (Cost: 1 mark):** When writing the curve equation $y^2 = x^3 + 7$, students often omit "$\pmod{p}$." This makes the equation mathematically incorrect for blockchain purposes, as it describes a real-number curve, not the finite field curve used in cryptography.
> 
> 3. **Stating "Encryption" Instead of "Signing" (Cost: 2 marks):** Bitcoin and Ethereum do **not** primarily use public keys for *encrypting* messages. They use them for *signing* transactions. Conflating these is a fundamental conceptual error.
> 
> 4. **Missing the Nonce in ECDSA (Cost: 1-2 marks):** In the signature generation formula, students often write $s = e + r \cdot k_{\text{priv}}$ without the $k_{\text{nonce}}^{-1}$ modular inverse multiplier. This is a critical omission that makes the signature non-recoverable.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring Compression (Cost: 1 mark):** When explaining public key formats, students must mention the $0x02/0x03$ prefix bytes and the 33-byte compressed format. Forgetting this loses marks on storage efficiency questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4: Public Keys):**

- **Public Key = $K = k \times G$** (elliptic curve point multiplication).
- **Curve used**: `secp256k1` ($y^2 = x^3 + 7 \pmod{p}$).
- **Private Key Size**: 256 bits.
- **Public Key Size**: 33 bytes (compressed) or 65 bytes (uncompressed).
- **Address Size**: 20 bytes (160-bit RIPEMD-160 hash of SHA-256 hash of public key).
- **Bitcoin Address Prefix**: Starts with `1` (P2PKH) or `3` (P2SH) for Mainnet.
- **Address Encoding**: Base58Check (not Base64; omits `0`, `O`, `I`, `l`).
- **Security Basis**: Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP).
- **Signature Scheme**: ECDSA, with signature pair $(r, s)$ of 32 bytes each.
- **Verification Uses Only Public Information**: $e, r, s, G, K$ — no private key needed for verification.
- **Nonce is Sacred**: Never reuse $k_{\text{nonce}}$ — use RFC 6979 for deterministic nonces.
- **Asymmetric Property**: One-way function; cannot reverse $K$ to find $k$.
- **Quantum Threat**: Both RSA and ECC are breakable by Shor's Algorithm on a sufficiently large quantum computer (estimated ~4,000 logical qubits for secp256k1).
- **Key Loss = Total Loss**: Losing the private key means losing access forever; no central authority can recover it.
- **Public Key ≠ Address**: Always remember the address is a HASH of the public key, with checksum and encoding added.
- **Hashing Pipeline**: SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 → Version Byte → Double SHA-256 Checksum → Base58Check.
- **Use Case**: Signing transactions, deriving addresses, DID (Decentralized Identity), Smart Contract `msg.sender` resolution.
- **Real-world Cryptos using secp256k1**: Bitcoin, Ethereum (and most EVM chains), Litecoin, Dogecoin.
- **Real-world Cryptos using Ed25519**: Solana, Stellar, Cardano, OpenSSH (non-blockchain).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptocurrency Address:** A blockchain address is a short, alphanumeric string derived from a public key (and indirectly from a private key) through one-way cryptographic hash functions. It serves as the **public, shareable identifier** of a network participant — analogous to an email address or an account number — to which digital assets can be sent on a distributed ledger.

An address is **not** the public key itself. Rather, it is a transformed, compressed, and encoded representation of the public key, optimized for **human readability, error-detection, and compact transmission** across the peer-to-peer network.

In the context of KTU Module 4, addresses are studied under three major cryptocurrencies:

| Network | Address Prefix Example | Typical Length |
|---|---|---|
| **Bitcoin (Legacy P2PKH)** | Starts with `1` | 26–35 characters |
| **Bitcoin (SegWit Bech32)** | Starts with `bc1` | 42 characters |
| **Ethereum** | Starts with `0x` | 40 hex characters (42 with prefix) |
| **Litecoin** | Starts with `L` or `M` | 26–35 characters |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation — The "Digital Postbox" Model**
> Imagine a transparent, tamper-proof postbox mounted on a busy street corner. Anyone in the world can walk up, read the postbox number painted on it, and drop a letter inside. However, **only the person holding the physical key** can open the postbox and retrieve the contents.
>
> In this analogy:
> - The **postbox number** = the **blockchain address** (public, shareable)
> - The **transparent postbox** = the **public ledger** (visible to all)
> - The **physical key** = the **private key** (secret, used to spend)
> - **Dropping a letter** = **sending a transaction** to that address
> - The postbox number is **derived from the lock design** (public key), but you cannot reverse-engineer the key shape just by looking at the number.

This is the foundational principle: **addresses are public-facing identifiers; private keys are the secret spending authority.** Losing the private key is equivalent to losing the physical key — the postbox contents become permanently inaccessible.

## 1.3 Key Properties of Blockchain Addresses

> [!TIP]
> **Five Properties Every KTU Examiner Expects You to Know:**
> 1. **Deterministic** — The same private key always produces the same address.
> 2. **One-Way** — It is computationally infeasible to reverse an address back to its public or private key.
> 3. **Pseudonymous** — Addresses do not inherently reveal real-world identity.
> 4. **Unique** — The probability of collision is astronomically small (typically **1 in 2¹⁶⁰** for 160-bit hashes).
> 5. **Verifiable** — Built-in checksums allow detection of typos before funds are sent.

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use the Public Key?

A raw **secp256k1 public key** is 64 bytes (512 bits) of uncompressed data, or 33 bytes compressed. Transmitting and verifying such long strings is:
- **Error-prone** (one flipped bit changes the destination)
- **Inefficient** for QR code generation and storage
- **Visually cumbersome** for users

Hashing and encoding (covered in Section 2) compress, secure, and humanize these identifiers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Address Generation Pipeline — Conceptual Breakdown

The address is the final output of a **multi-stage cryptographic pipeline**. Let us break it down step-by-step for the canonical **Bitcoin Legacy (P2PKH)** address — the most heavily tested variant in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Private Key Generation
A 256-bit integer is sampled uniformly at random from the range **[1, n − 1]**, where **n ≈ 2²⁵⁶** is the order of the secp256k1 elliptic curve group.

### Step 2: Public Key Derivation (Elliptic Curve Multiplication)
The private key $k$ is multiplied with a fixed generator point $G$ on the secp256k1 curve:

$$P = k \cdot G$$

This produces a point $P = (x, y)$ on the curve. Because **elliptic curve discrete logarithm problem (ECDLP)** is hard, knowing $P$ does not reveal $k$.

### Step 3: Hash the Public Key (Two Sequential Hashes)
First, apply **SHA-256** to the (compressed or uncompressed) public key bytes, then apply **RIPEMD-160** to the SHA-256 output:

$$H_{160} = \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(P_{bytes}))$$

This 160-bit hash is the **Public Key Hash (PKH)**.

### Step 4: Prepend the Version Byte
For Bitcoin mainnet P2PKH, prepend the version byte **0x00**:

$$V = \text{0x00} \, \Vert \, H_{160}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Checksum (Double SHA-256)
Apply SHA-256 twice to $V$ and take the **first 4 bytes** of the result:

$$C = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(V))[:4]$$

### Step 6: Concatenate and Base58Check Encode
Append the checksum to the versioned payload, then encode the 25-byte string in **Base58Check** (a binary-to-text encoding that omits visually ambiguous characters like `0`, `O`, `I`, `l`).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every formula, hash boundary, and encoding constant you must memorize for the KTU University Examination.**

| Stage | Operation | Input Size | Output Size | Cryptographic Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Random Sampling | — | **256 bits** | CSPRNG (e.g., `/dev/urandom`) |
| 2 | EC Scalar Multiplication | 256 bits | 256 bits (point) | **secp256k1** ECDSA |
| 3a | First Hash | 64 bytes (uncompressed pubkey) | **256 bits** (32 bytes) | **SHA-256** |
| 3b | Second Hash | 32 bytes | **160 bits** (20 bytes) | **RIPEMD-160** |
| 4 | Version Prefix | 20 bytes | **21 bytes** | Append `0x00` (mainnet) |
| 5a | First Checksum Hash | 21 bytes | 32 bytes | **SHA-256** |
| 5b | Second Checksum Hash | 32 bytes | 32 bytes | **SHA-256** (again) |
| 5c | Checksum Truncation | 32 bytes | **4 bytes** | Take first 4 bytes |
| 6 | Binary-to-Text Encoding | 25 bytes | 26–35 chars | **Base58Check** |
| Alt-1 | Ethereum Hashing | 64 bytes | 32 bytes | **Keccak-256 (SHA3-256)** |
| Alt-2 | Ethereum Address | 32 bytes | **20 bytes** | Take last 20 bytes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):** SHA-256 outputs the full 32-byte digest. RIPEMD-160 takes the 32-byte SHA-256 output and produces a 20-byte digest. **The order is fixed: SHA-256 first, then RIPEMD-160. Reversing the order gives an invalid address.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Payment Processors:** E-commerce systems (e.g., BitPay, Coinbase Commerce) generate a **unique address per invoice** to simplify payment reconciliation and tracking.
- **Hardware Wallets:** Devices like Ledger and Trezor embed this pipeline in a **secure element chip**, ensuring the private key never leaves the device.
- **Smart Contract Wallets:** Ethereum addresses can also represent **deployed smart contracts**, where the 20-byte address is deterministically derived from the deployer's address and transaction nonce.
- **Cross-Chain Bridges:** Each blockchain network has its own address standard, requiring bridges to map user identities across heterogeneous ledgers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Bitcoin P2PKH Address from a Private Key

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** Let the private key be the well-known test vector $k = \text{0x0C28FCA386C7A227600B2FE50B7CAE11EC86D3BF1FBE471BE89827E19D72AA1D}$ (this is a publicly documented Bitcoin test private key, used here purely for pedagogical demonstration).

### Step 1 — Private Key Acquisition

$$k = \text{0x0C28FCA386C7A227600B2FE50B7CAE11EC86D3BF1FBE471BE89827E19D72AA1D}$$

This is a 32-byte (256-bit) integer lying in the valid range $[1, n-1]$, where **$n$ is the order of secp256k1**.

### Step 2 — Public Key Computation via Elliptic Curve Point Multiplication

Apply scalar multiplication on the secp256k1 curve:

$$P = k \cdot G$$

where $G$ is the standard generator point. The output is a coordinate pair $(x_P, y_P)$:

$$x_P = \text{0xA1B2C3D4E5F6...}$$

$$y_P = \text{0xF1E2D3C4B5A6...}$$

The **uncompressed public key** is the 65-byte serialization `04 ‖ x_P ‖ y_P`. The **compressed** form is `02 ‖ x_P` or `03 ‖ x_P` depending on the parity of $y_P$, giving 33 bytes.

### Step 3 — Double Hashing (SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160)

First, compute the SHA-256 of the public key:

$$\text{SHA256}(P_{uncompressed}) = H_1 \quad (32 \text{ bytes})$$

Then, hash the result with RIPEMD-160:

$$H_{160} = \text{RIPEMD160}(H_1)$$

The output is a **20-byte** (160-bit) digest. This is the *Public Key Hash (PKH)*. The 160-bit length is deliberate: it provides $\approx 2^{80}$ collision resistance under the **birthday bound**, which is considered computationally infeasible with current technology.

### Step 4 — Version Byte Prepending

For Bitcoin mainnet P2PKH, prepend the byte `0x00`:

$$V = \text{0x00} \, \Vert \, H_{160}$$

This produces a 21-byte payload. The `0x00` byte signals to the network that the address should be interpreted as a **Pay-to-PubKey-Hash** output script. (For testnet, the equivalent version byte is `0x6F`, producing addresses starting with `m` or `n`.)

### Step 5 — Checksum Computation (Double SHA-256)

Apply SHA-256 to the 21-byte payload:

$$H_{\text{check1}} = \text{SHA256}(V)$$

Apply SHA-256 *again* to this 32-byte hash:

$$H_{\text{check2}} = \text{SHA256}(H_{\text{check1}})$$

Retain only the **first 4 bytes** of $H_{\text{check2}}$ as the checksum:

$$C = H_{\text{check2}}[0:4]$$

The double-hash is a defense against **length-extension attacks** and ensures the checksum inherits full SHA-256 strength.

### Step 6 — Final Concatenation and Base58Check Encoding

Concatenate the versioned payload with the checksum:

$$F = V \, \Vert \, C \quad (25 \text{ bytes total})$$

Encode $F$ using the **Base58Check alphabet** $\Sigma_{58}$, which excludes the four characters `0`, `O`, `I`, `l` to eliminate visual ambiguity. The encoding is performed by treating $F$ as a large big-endian integer and repeatedly dividing by 58, collecting the remainders.

The final string is the **Bitcoin P2PKH address**:

$$A = \text{Base58Check}(F) \quad (\text{e.g., } \texttt{1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa})$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Ethereum Address

> [!NOTE]
> Ethereum uses a simpler pipeline since it relies on its native Keccak-256 hash (a SHA-3 variant) rather than the SHA-256/RIPEMD-160 chain.

### Step 1 — Public Key Acquisition

Same as Bitcoin: $P = k \cdot G$ on secp256k1, producing 64 bytes (concatenation of $x$ and $y$, omitting the `04` prefix).

### Step 2 — Keccak-256 Hashing

$$H_{\text{eth}} = \text{Keccak-256}(x_P \, \Vert \, y_P)$$

This produces a 32-byte digest. Keccak-256 is the algorithm that won the NIST SHA-3 competition; it is structurally distinct from the SHA-256 used by Bitcoin.

### Step 3 — Address Extraction

Discard the first 12 bytes; retain the last 20 bytes:

$$A_{20} = H_{\text{eth}}[12:32]$$

### Step 4 — EIP-55 Checksum Capitalization

To enable case-sensitive error detection without bloating the address, Ethereum applies the **EIP-55 mixed-case checksum**:

1. Compute Keccak-256 of the lowercase hex address string.
2. For each hex character in the address, **capitalize it** if the corresponding nibble of the hash is $\geq 8$.

This produces a checksum that is **visually embedded** in the address capitalization, such as:

$$\texttt{0x5aAeb6053F3E94C9b9A09f33669435E7Ef1BeAed}$$

Wallets that fail to verify capitalization risk sending funds to an unintended address if a single character is mistyped.

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Comparison Table (Frequently Asked in KTU Part A 3-Mark Questions):**

| Property | Bitcoin (P2PKH) | Ethereum | Bitcoin (Bech32) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hash Function** | SHA-256 + RIPEMD-160 | Keccak-256 | SHA-256 + RIPEMD-160 |
| **Address Length** | 25 bytes (encoded: ~34 chars) | 20 bytes (encoded: 42 chars) | 32 bytes (encoded: 42 chars) |
| **Encoding** | Base58Check | Hex (with EIP-55) | **Bech32** |
| **Error Detection** | 4-byte SHA-256 checksum | Mixed-case EIP-55 | **BCH code** (error-correcting) |
| **Prefix** | `1` (mainnet) | `0x` | `bc1` (mainnet) |
| **Case Sensitivity** | Yes | Yes (EIP-55) | **No** (lowercase only) |
| **SegWit Compatible** | No | N/A | **Yes** |
| **QR Code Efficiency** | Moderate | Moderate | **High** (shorter string) |

## 3.4 Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) Address Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern wallets (per **BIP-32 / BIP-44**) derive an **entire tree of addresses** from a single **seed phrase** (12 or 24 words, per BIP-39). The pipeline is:

$$\text{Seed Phrase} \xrightarrow{\text{PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512}} \text{Seed (512 bits)} \xrightarrow{\text{HMAC-SHA512}} (\text{Chain Code}, \text{Private Key})$$

$$\text{Private Key} \rightarrow \text{Public Key} \rightarrow \text{Address} \quad \text{(as per Sections 3.1 / 3.2)}$$

The **Chain Code** (256 bits) plus the **Parent Key** form the basis for deriving **child keys** at any depth in the hierarchy, enabling `m/44'/0'/0'/0/0`-style paths. This allows users to **generate a fresh address for every transaction**, dramatically improving privacy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bitcoin P2PKH Address Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Random 256-bit Integer]:::input --> B[Private Key k]
    B --> C[EC Scalar Multiplication: P = k * G on secp256k1]
    C --> D[Public Key Point: 64 bytes]
    D --> E[SHA-256 Hash]
    E --> F[RIPEMD-160 Hash]
    F --> G[20-byte Public Key Hash]
    G --> H[Prepend Version Byte 0x00]
    H --> I[21-byte Versioned Payload]
    I --> J[SHA-256 First Pass]
    J --> K[SHA-256 Second Pass]
    K --> L[Take First 4 bytes = Checksum]
    L --> M[Concatenate: 25 bytes total]
    M --> N[Base58Check Encoding]
    N --> O[Final Bitcoin Address: starts with 1]

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ethereum Address Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Private Key k]:::input --> B[EC Multiply: P = k * G]
    B --> C[64-byte Public Key: x || y]
    C --> D[Keccak-256 Hashing]
    D --> E[32-byte Digest]
    E --> F[Discard First 12 bytes]
    F --> G[Retain Last 20 bytes]
    G --> H[Add 0x Prefix]
    H --> I[Apply EIP-55 Mixed-Case Checksum]
    I --> J[Ethereum Address: 0x...]:::output

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — HD Wallet Address Tree (BIP-32 / BIP-44)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[12/24-word Seed Phrase BIP-39]:::input --> SD[PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 Expansion]
    SD --> SEED[512-bit Binary Seed]
    SEED --> HMAC[HMAC-SHA512 Derivation]
    HMAC --> CC[Chain Code 256 bits]
    HMAC --> PK[Master Private Key]
    PK --> ADDR1[Address m/44/0/0/0/0]
    PK --> ADDR2[Address m/44/0/0/0/1]
    PK --> ADDR3[Address m/44/0/0/0/2]
    PK --> ADDRN[... infinite child addresses]
    ADDR1 --> USE[Each Address = Fresh Receive ID]
    ADDR2 --> USE
    ADDR3 --> USE
    ADDRN --> USE

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Transaction Flow Using Addresses

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice
    participant Network
    participant Bob
    Alice->>Alice: Generate fresh address from HD path
    Alice->>Bob: Share address publicly (e.g., QR code)
    Bob->>Bob: Validate address checksum
    Bob->>Network: Broadcast TX: input -> Alice address
    Network->>Network: Verify digital signature with public key
    Network->>Network: Update UTXO / World State
    Network-->>Alice: Balance visible at Alice address
    Alice->>Alice: Spend using private key (never revealed)
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison Chart — Encoding Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Binary 25 bytes]:::input --> B{Encoding Scheme}
    B -->|Base58Check| C[Bitcoin Legacy: 1xxx]
    B -->|Bech32| D[Bitcoin SegWit: bc1xxx]
    B -->|Hex + EIP-55| E[Ethereum: 0xxx]
    B -->|Base32 / Base64| F[Other chains]

    C --> C1[34 chars, mixed case, 4-byte SHA-256 checksum]
    D --> D1[42 chars, lowercase only, BCH error correction]
    E --> E1[42 chars, mixed case, EIP-55 visual checksum]
    F --> F1[Variable, application-specific]

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a blockchain address. List any four key properties it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A blockchain address is a short, alphanumeric string derived from a user's public key through one-way cryptographic hash functions, used as a public identifier to send and receive digital assets on a distributed ledger.

**Four key properties:**
1. **Deterministic** — The same private key always generates the same address.
2. **One-Way** — Computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the address.
3. **Pseudonymous** — Does not inherently reveal real-world identity.
4. **Error-Detectable** — Contains a checksum to catch transmission/typo errors.

*(Word count: ~80 words — well within the 3-mark answer expectation)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Why does Bitcoin apply SHA-256 first and then RIPEMD-160 when generating an address, instead of using a single hash?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Two sequential hashes serve complementary purposes:
> 1. **SHA-256 (256-bit output)** first provides broad cryptographic diffusion across the full 64-byte public key, ensuring avalanche effects on every bit.
> 2. **RIPEMD-160 (160-bit output)** then compresses the result to a **shorter, more efficient** 20-byte digest suitable for compact address encoding and reduced on-chain storage footprint.
>
> Additionally, using a hash *not derived from the SHA-2 family* (RIPEMD-160) provides **algorithm diversity**, so a future break of SHA-256 alone would not immediately compromise address security.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete process of generating a **Bitcoin Legacy (P2PKH) address** from a private key. Mention all hash functions and their output sizes at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Base58Check encoding** and **Bech32 encoding** used in Bitcoin addresses. Discuss how Bech32 provides superior error detection through **BCH codes**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]

The complete pipeline is:

1. **Private Key (256 bits):** Randomly sampled integer $k \in [1, n-1]$. **(1 mark)**
2. **Public Key (64 bytes):** Computed via $P = k \cdot G$ on the secp256k1 curve. **(1 mark)**
3. **SHA-256 Hash (32 bytes):** Applied to the public key bytes. **(1 mark)**
4. **RIPEMD-160 Hash (20 bytes):** Applied to the SHA-256 output, producing the **Public Key Hash (PKH)**. **(1 mark)**
5. **Version Byte Prepending (21 bytes):** Append `0x00` for mainnet. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Double SHA-256 Checksum (4 bytes):** First 4 bytes of `SHA256(SHA256(payload))`. **(1 mark)**
7. **Base58Check Encoding:** Produces the final 26–35 character address starting with `1`. **(1.5 marks)**

*(See Section 4.1 for the visual block diagram.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]

| Aspect | Base58Check | Bech32 |
|---|---|---|
| **Alphabet** | 58 characters (excludes 0, O, I, l) | 32 characters (lowercase + digits, excludes 1, b, i, o) |
| **Address Prefix** | `1` (mainnet) | `bc1` (mainnet) |
| **Error Detection** | 4-byte SHA-256 checksum | **BCH (Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem) code** |
| **Error Correction** | Detection only | **Detection AND correction** of up to 4 errors |
| **Case Sensitivity** | Yes | No (lowercase only) |
| **SegWit Compatible** | No | **Yes** |

**BCH Codes — Technical Detail (3 marks):** Bech32 employs a **polynomial code over GF(32)**. The 30 checksum bits at the end of a Bech32 string are computed as the BCH code of the human-readable part and the data part. This BCH construction guarantees that:
- Any **single error** is always detected and **can be corrected**.
- Any **burst of up to 4 errors** is detected with overwhelming probability.
- Wallets can thus **automatically correct minor QR code scanning errors**, a major UX improvement over Base58Check.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of generating an **Ethereum address** from a private key. Compare it with the Bitcoin address generation process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallet**? With a suitable diagram, explain how BIP-32 and BIP-39 standards together enable the derivation of an entire tree of addresses from a single seed phrase. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]

**Ethereum Address Generation Steps:** **(4 marks)**
1. **Private Key (256 bits):** Random 32-byte integer.
2. **Public Key (64 bytes):** $P = k \cdot G$ on secp256k1, serialized as $x \Vert y$ (no `04` prefix).
3. **Keccak-256 Hash (32 bytes):** Applied to the 64-byte public key.
4. **Address Extraction (20 bytes):** Take the **last 20 bytes** of the Keccak digest (discard first 12).
5. **EIP-55 Checksum:** Hex-encode the 20 bytes, compute Keccak-256 of the lowercase string, and capitalize each character whose corresponding hash nibble is $\geq 8$.

**Comparison with Bitcoin (3 marks):**

| Stage | Bitcoin | Ethereum |
|---|---|---|
| Hash Function | SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 | Keccak-256 |
| Hash Output Truncation | Full 20 bytes used | Last 20 of 32 bytes used |
| Version Byte | Yes (`0x00`) | No |
| Checksum | 4 bytes appended | Embedded in case (EIP-55) |
| Final Encoding | Base58Check | Hexadecimal with prefix `0x` |

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A **Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallet** is a wallet that derives an entire tree-like structure of key pairs (and therefore addresses) from a **single master seed**, allowing users to manage unlimited addresses with one backup.

**BIP-39 — Seed Phrase (2 marks):**
- A 12 or 24-word **mnemonic phrase** drawn from a standardized 2048-word English wordlist.
- The phrase is passed through **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** (with a salt = `"mnemonic" + passphrase`) over 2048 iterations to produce a **512-bit binary seed**.

**BIP-32 — Hierarchical Derivation (2.5 marks):**
- The 512-bit seed is split into a **256-bit master private key** and a **256-bit master chain code**.
- Child keys are derived by **HMAC-SHA512(parent_chain_code, parent_pubkey ‖ index)**, yielding a new 256-bit child key + 256-bit chain code.
- The path `m/44'/0'/0'/0/0` is the standard **BIP-44** convention for the first Bitcoin receive address.

**Diagrammatic Summary (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.3** for the full Mermaid HD wallet tree.

**Benefits (1 mark):**
- **Single backup** (the seed phrase) secures all addresses.
- **Privacy:** Fresh address per transaction avoids address reuse.
- **Auditing:** Hierarchical structure enables organized accounting (e.g., separate branches for business vs. personal use).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in 'Addresses' Questions:**
>
> 1. **Hash Order Confusion (–2 marks):** Writing RIPEMD-160 *before* SHA-256. The order is **strictly SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160** for Bitcoin.
> 2. **Wrong Version Byte (–1 mark):** Testnet uses `0x6F`, not `0x00`. Always clarify which network the question references.
> 3. **Confusing Keccak-256 with SHA-3 (–1 mark):** Ethereum uses the original **Keccak** submission, which has a slightly different padding rule than the final standardized **SHA3-256**. They produce *different* hashes for the same input.
> 4. **Forgetting the Checksum Stage (–2 marks):** The 4-byte checksum is *not optional* in Base58Check; omitting it makes the address invalid.
> 5. **Misstating EIP-55 Mechanism (–1 mark):** The EIP-55 checksum is based on the Keccak hash of the **lowercase hex string**, not the address bytes. Students often write the wrong input.
> 6. **BIP-39 vs. BIP-32 Mix-up (–1 mark):** BIP-39 is the *mnemonic phrase*; BIP-32 is the *hierarchical key derivation*. They are complementary, not interchangeable.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Addresses**
>
> - **Definition:** Address = hashed + encoded public key identifier; not the key itself.
> - **Bitcoin P2PKH Pipeline:** Private Key → EC Multiply → SHA-256 → RIPEMD-160 → `0x00` prefix → Double SHA-256 → 4-byte checksum → Base58Check.
> - **Bitcoin Bech32 Pipeline:** Same hashing, but uses **BCH error-correcting codes** and lowercase-only alphabet; prefix `bc1`.
> - **Ethereum Pipeline:** Private Key → EC Multiply → Keccak-256 → Last 20 bytes → `0x` prefix → EIP-55 mixed-case checksum.
> - **Encoding Alphabet Sizes:** Base58 = 58 chars, Bech32 = 32 chars, Hex = 16 chars.
> - **Hash Output Sizes:** SHA-256 = 256 bits, RIPEMD-160 = 160 bits, Keccak-256 = 256 bits.
> - **Address Lengths:** Bitcoin Legacy ≈ 25–35 chars, Bitcoin Bech32 = 42 chars, Ethereum = 42 chars.
> - **One-Way Property:** Backed by **pre-image resistance** of SHA-256/RIPEMD-160/Keccak-256 and **ECDLP** hardness.
> - **HD Wallets (BIP-32 + BIP-39):** Single seed phrase → unlimited tree of addresses; supports privacy via per-transaction fresh addresses.
> - **EIP-55:** Visual checksum by **capitalizing hex characters** based on Keccak hash nibbles $\geq 8$.
> - **BCH Codes (Bech32):** Detect and **correct** up to 4-character errors — a major UX advantage.
> - **Collision Probability:** $\approx 1/2^{80}$ under birthday-bound for 160-bit hash space.
> - **Common Mistake:** Never use the *same* address repeatedly if privacy matters; modern wallets auto-rotate.
> - **Key Pairing:** Address = public (share freely); Private Key = secret (never transmit, never store in plaintext).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transactions - Lifecycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transactions - Lifecycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of distributed ledger technology, a **Transaction** is a cryptographically signed data structure that records the transfer of digital assets (cryptocurrency, tokens, or arbitrary data) between participants on a blockchain network. The **Transaction Lifecycle** encompasses the complete sequence of states a transaction traverses from its initial creation by a sender, through network propagation, validation, mining/ordering, block inclusion, and ultimately achieving **finality** on the distributed ledger.

Formally, a transaction lifecycle can be represented as a finite state machine:

$$T_{lifecycle}: S_0 \xrightarrow{\text{construct}} S_1 \xrightarrow{\text{sign}} S_2 \xrightarrow{\text{broadcast}} S_3 \xrightarrow{\text{validate}} S_4 \xrightarrow{\text{include}} S_5 \xrightarrow{\text{confirm}} S_6$$

where $S_0$ = Unbuilt, $S_1$ = Raw, $S_2$ = Signed, $S_3$ = In Mempool, $S_4$ = Validated, $S_5$ = In Block, $S_6$ = Finalized (irreversible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO2):** The transaction lifecycle forms the foundational layer for understanding consensus, mining, and smart-contract execution. Mastery of this topic is essential to score in both Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you want to send a **registered post** parcel from Kochi to Delhi that contains a valuable cheque:

| Blockchain Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| Sender's Wallet | You (the cheque writer) |
| Transaction Creation | Writing the cheque with amount, date, payee |
| Digital Signature | Your unique signature on the cheque |
| Broadcast | Posting the cheque at the post office |
| Mempool | A sorting room where posts await dispatch |
| Miner/Validator | The postmaster who bundles many letters into a sealed bag |
| Block | The sealed bag of registered letters |
| Block Confirmation | The bag travelling through multiple post offices, each stamping it |
| Finality | The bag is archived in the central records office — now irrefutable |

> [!NOTE]
> The critical insight: in blockchain, every "post office" (node) keeps a copy of the entire chain. Reversing a confirmed transaction would require rewriting history at **all post offices simultaneously** — an economically and computationally infeasible task.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

- **UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output):** The fundamental unit of value in Bitcoin-like ledgers; the output of a previous transaction that has not yet been consumed.
- **Mempool (Memory Pool):** A node's in-memory queue of unconfirmed but validated transactions awaiting block inclusion.
- **Locktime / nSequence:** Transaction-level fields that delay or condition the spendability of outputs.
- **Finality:** The property that a transaction, once confirmed, cannot be reversed without economically prohibitive cost.
- **Probabilistic Finality:** Confirmation strength grows with the number of appended blocks (PoW).
- **Absolute Finality:** A transaction is finalized the moment a supermajority (e.g., 2/3) of validators attest to it (BFT-style PoS).
- **Double-Spend Attack:** A malicious attempt to spend the same UTXO in two competing transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorise:** In Bitcoin, a transaction is considered *practically irreversible* after **6 confirmations** (approximately **60 minutes** at 10-min block intervals). The **51% threshold** is the attack cost for full chain takeover.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Stage Transaction Lifecycle — Detailed Walkthrough

### Stage 1 — **Transaction Construction (Origination)**
The sender's wallet application:
1. Retrieves the sender's **UTXO set** (in UTXO-model chains like Bitcoin) or **nonce** (in account-model chains like Ethereum).
2. Selects sufficient inputs to cover the payment plus the **transaction fee** (gas).
3. Constructs the transaction object: `version`, `inputs[]`, `outputs[]`, `locktime`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bitcoin Transaction Structure (KTU Exam Favorite Diagram):**
> ```
> [Version 4B][Input Count][Inputs][Output Count][Outputs][Locktime 4B]
> ```
> Each input contains: `Previous TX Hash (32B)`, `Output Index (4B)`, `ScriptSig`, `Sequence (4B)`.
> Each output contains: `Value (8B in satoshis)`, `ScriptPubKey`.

### Stage 2 — **Cryptographic Signing (Authorization)**
The sender uses their **private key** $k_{priv}$ to produce a digital signature over the transaction hash. Using **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** on curve **secp256k1**:

$$\text{Signature } \sigma = (r, s) = \text{Sign}_{k_{priv}}(H(T))$$

where $H(T) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(T))$ is the **double-SHA-256** transaction hash (Bitcoin convention). The signature can be verified using the sender's public key $K_{pub}$ via:

$$\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(H(T), \sigma) \rightarrow \{\text{True}, \text{False}\}$$

### Stage 3 — **Network Propagation (Broadcast)**
The signed transaction is flooded across the peer-to-peer network using a **gossip protocol**:
- The node sends the transaction to **8–10 random peers**.
- Each receiving peer validates the transaction (Stage 4) and forwards it further.
- Average network propagation latency: **2–5 seconds** in Bitcoin, **~400 ms** in Ethereum.

### Stage 4 — **Node-Level Validation**
Each full node independently executes these checks:

| # | Validation Rule | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Correct digital signature | Prevents forgery |
| 2 | Sufficient UTXO value | Prevents inflation |
| 3 | UTXO not already spent | Prevents double-spend |
| 4 | Script execution returns `true` | Enforces spending conditions |
| 5 | `locktime` constraints satisfied | Enables time-locked transactions |
| 6 | Transaction size ≤ policy limit | Prevents DoS via huge TXs |

### Stage 5 — **Mempool Queuing (Ordering Pending)**
Validated transactions enter the **mempool**, where miners select transactions based on fee-per-byte (Bitcoin) or gas-price (Ethereum) priority. The mempool is **local to each node** — different nodes may hold slightly different pending sets.

### Stage 6 — **Block Inclusion (Mining / Validation)**
A miner (PoW) or validator (PoS) constructs a candidate block:
- Selects high-fee transactions from the mempool.
- Computes the **Merkle Root** of the transaction list.
- Mines the block by solving the PoW puzzle (Bitcoin) or is selected by the PoS protocol (Ethereum 2.0).

The PoW puzzle is:

$$\text{Find } \text{nonce } n \text{ such that } H(\text{Header} \,\vert\, n) < \text{Target}$$

### Stage 7 — **Confirmation & Finality**
Once the block is broadcast, every other node verifies and appends it. Each subsequent block added on top is a **confirmation**. After $k$ confirmations, the probability of a successful reversal (under honest-majority assumption) drops as:

$$P_{\text{reversal}}(k) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{\infty} \binom{\lambda}{i} \cdot p^i \cdot (1-p)^{\lambda-i}$$

where $p$ is the attacker's hash power and $\lambda$ is the number of confirmations required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Probabilistic vs. Absolute Finality Comparison:**
> - **Bitcoin (PoW):** Probabilistic. Finality probability increases asymptotically; never reaches 100%.
> - **Ethereum 2.0 (Casper FFG):** Absolute. Finalized after two epochs (~12.8 min) with 2/3+1 supermajority attestation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $H(T) = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(T))$ | Bitcoin transaction ID (TXID) | 256-bit hash |
| $\sigma = (r,s) = \text{Sign}_{k_{priv}}(H(T))$ | ECDSA signature | 512–512 bits |
| $H(\text{Header} \,\vert\, n) < T$ | PoW mining condition | Target $T$ in compact form |
| $\text{Difficulty} = \frac{D_1}{T}$ | Mining difficulty | Adjusted every **2016 blocks** |
| $P_{\text{reversal}}(k) \approx (q/p)^{k}$ | Reversal probability after $k$ confirmations | $q$ = attacker hash power |
| $\text{Block Time} = \frac{\text{Difficulty} \cdot 2^{32}}{\text{Hashrate}}$ | Expected time to find next block | Bitcoin: ~**10 min** |
| $\text{Block Reward} = \text{Base} + \sum \text{fees}$ | Miner incentive | Halved every **210,000 blocks** |
| $\text{Mempool Fee Rate} = \frac{\text{fees}}{\text{size (bytes)}}$ | Selection priority | measured in **sat/vB** |
| $\text{Gas Used} = \sum_{op} \text{gas}(op)$ | Ethereum transaction cost | **gwei** denomination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** The transaction lifecycle model is the blueprint for designing any distributed state-machine replication system — from banking ledgers to supply-chain tracking and IoT data integrity layers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — ECDSA Signature Verification for a Transaction

We will derive, step by step, how a full node mathematically verifies a transaction signature, since this is a **frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

### Given
- Curve: **secp256k1** with order $n$, generator $G$.
- Sender's private key: $d_A \in [1, n-1]$.
- Sender's public key: $Q_A = d_A \cdot G$.
- Transaction hash: $z = H(T)$ (a 256-bit integer, truncated from the actual hash if longer).
- Signature tuple: $\sigma = (r, s)$ produced by signer.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Verify that $r$ and $s$ lie in the valid range $[1, n-1]$.**

This prevents signature malleability attacks.

$$1 \le r \le n-1, \quad 1 \le s \le n-1$$

If either condition fails, the signature is rejected.

**Step 2 — Compute the modular inverse of $s$.**

Let $s^{-1}$ denote the modular multiplicative inverse of $s$ modulo $n$:

$$s \cdot s^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

**Step 3 — Recover two intermediate scalar values $u_1$ and $u_2$.**

These scalars combine the message hash and the signature component $r$ with the inverse of $s$:

$$u_1 = z \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{n}$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the elliptic curve point $(x_1, y_1)$.**

This is the cornerstone of ECDSA verification — the verification equation directly mirrors the signing process:

$$(x_1, y_1) = u_1 \cdot G \;+\; u_2 \cdot Q_A$$

**Step 5 — Reduce $x_1$ modulo $n$.**

$$R = x_1 \bmod n$$

**Step 6 — Final verification check.**

$$\text{Signature valid} \iff R = r$$

If the check passes, the node is cryptographically assured that the transaction was signed by the holder of the private key corresponding to $Q_A$ and that the transaction data $T$ has not been tampered with.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "explain ECDSA verification," present Steps 1–6 in order. Marks are awarded for explicitly writing the modular arithmetic and the elliptic curve point addition.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Merkle Root Construction (Block Confirmation)

A block can contain thousands of transactions. To commit them efficiently, the miner builds a **Merkle Tree**.

**Step 1 — Hash each transaction.**

Let the transactions in the block be $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$. Compute:

$$H_i = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(T_i))$$

**Step 2 — Pair and hash upward.**

For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, \lceil n/2 \rceil$:

$$H_{i}^{\text{upper}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(H_{2i-1} \,\vert\, H_{2i}))$$

**Step 3 — Recurse until a single root remains.**

The recursion terminates when only one hash remains — this is the **Merkle Root**, which is included in the 80-byte block header.

**Step 4 — Cost of inclusion proof (SPV verification).**

A Simplified Payment Verification (SPV) client needs only $\log_2(n)$ hashes to prove that $T_k$ is in the block, rather than all $n$ transactions.

$$\text{Proof Size} = \log_2(n) \cdot 32 \text{ bytes}$$

For a block with 2048 transactions, the proof size is $11 \times 32 = 352$ bytes.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical / Conceptual Patterns

| Pattern Type | Trigger Wording | Required Response |
|---|---|---|
| Define transaction | "What is a blockchain transaction?" | Define with UTXO/Account, signature, immutability |
| Stages question | "Explain the lifecycle" | List 7 stages with role of each actor |
| ECDSA derivation | "Derive signature verification" | Steps 1–6 above |
| Finality types | "Compare probabilistic vs absolute finality" | Table with PoW vs BFT-PoS |
| Double-spend | "How does Bitcoin prevent double-spend?" | UTXO + consensus + confirmations |
| Mempool | "Explain mempool operation" | Local queue, fee-based priority, DoS protection |
| Merkle root | "Why is Merkle root included in header?" | Efficient SPV, tamper detection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Full Transaction Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[S1 Sender Wallet Constructs TX] --> B[S2 Sign with Private Key ECDSA]
    B --> C[S3 Broadcast via Gossip Protocol]
    C --> D[S4 Each Node Validates Signature UTXO Scripts]
    D --> E{Valid?}
    E -- No --> F[Drop TX and Log Error]
    E -- Yes --> G[S5 Enter Local Mempool]
    G --> H[S6 Miner Validator Selects High Fee TXs]
    H --> I[Build Candidate Block with Merkle Root]
    I --> J[Solve PoW Puzzle OR Attest in PoS]
    J --> K[S7 Block Broadcast to Network]
    K --> L[All Nodes Append Block to Chain]
    L --> M[Each New Block = 1 Confirmation]
    M --> N[After k Confirmations Transaction Finalized]
    N --> O[State S6 Finality Reached]
    F --> P[Sender Can Resubmit Corrected TX]
```

```mermaid

    end

    end

    end

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Sender → Network → Miner → Chain

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as Sender Wallet
    participant Peer1 as Peer Node A
    participant Peer2 as Peer Node B

    Sender->>Sender: Construct TX, sign with k_priv
    Sender->>Peer1: Broadcast signed TX

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a blockchain transaction. List its essential components with reference to the Bitcoin transaction format.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A blockchain transaction is a cryptographically signed data structure used to transfer digital value or data on a distributed ledger. In Bitcoin, the essential components are:
1. **Version (4 bytes):** Specifies the transaction format version.
2. **Input Count & Inputs:** References to previous unspent transaction outputs (UTXOs), including the TXID, output index, unlocking script (`ScriptSig`), and sequence number.
3. **Output Count & Outputs:** Specifies the value (in satoshis) and the locking script (`ScriptPubKey`) for the recipient.
4. **Locktime (4 bytes):** The earliest time/block height at which the transaction may be included in a block.

> Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is a mempool? Why is it stored locally on each node rather than globally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **mempool (memory pool)** is the in-memory queue where a node stores valid but unconfirmed transactions awaiting inclusion in a future block.
It is kept **locally** rather than globally because:
1. **Latency:** A global synchronized pool would introduce unacceptable delay; gossip-based propagation is faster.
2. **DoS resistance:** Limiting each node's mempool size prevents memory exhaustion attacks.
3. **Diverse policies:** Each node operator may enforce different fee/relay policies (e.g., minimum relay fee).
4. **Network partition tolerance:** Local mempool allows nodes to keep operating even when temporarily disconnected.

> Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification (any 2 valid reasons).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the complete **lifecycle of a blockchain transaction** from construction to finality, highlighting the role of digital signatures, mempool, and consensus.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **ECDSA signature generation and verification process** used in Bitcoin transactions. Show the mathematical verification equation.

#### Model Solution

**(a) The Transaction Lifecycle (7 marks):**

The transaction lifecycle comprises seven well-defined stages, each enforced by specific actors in the network:

1. **Transaction Construction (1 mark):** The sender's wallet constructs the transaction by selecting UTXOs (in Bitcoin) or using a nonce (in Ethereum), specifying recipient address, amount, and fee. The raw transaction is built in the standard serialization format.

2. **Cryptographic Signing (1 mark):** The wallet computes the transaction hash $H(T)$ using double-SHA-256 and signs it with the sender's private key $k_{priv}$ using ECDSA on curve secp256k1, producing signature $\sigma = (r, s)$.

3. **Broadcast / Propagation (1 mark):** The signed transaction is gossiped to 8–10 peers, which validate it and forward it further. Propagation completes network-wide in 2–5 seconds.

4. **Node-Level Validation (1 mark):** Each full node independently checks the signature, UTXO validity, script execution, and locktime. Invalid transactions are dropped; valid ones enter the mempool.

5. **Mempool Queuing (1 mark):** Validated transactions are held in the local mempool, ordered by fee-per-byte. Miners select the highest-paying transactions for the next block.

6. **Block Inclusion (1 mark):** A miner constructs a block, computes the Merkle root, and either solves the PoW puzzle (Bitcoin) or is selected as a validator (PoS). The block is then broadcast.

7. **Confirmation & Finality (1 mark):** Each additional block on top of the block containing the transaction is a confirmation. After 6 confirmations (~1 hour in Bitcoin), the transaction is **practically irreversible**.

**(b) ECDSA Signature Generation and Verification (7 marks):**

**Key Generation:**
- Choose private key $d_A \in [1, n-1]$.
- Compute public key $Q_A = d_A \cdot G$ (elliptic curve scalar multiplication).

**Signature Generation:**

$$r = (k \cdot G).x \bmod n$$

$$s = k^{-1} \cdot (z + r \cdot d_A) \bmod n$$

where $k$ is a fresh random nonce, $z = H(T)$, and $\sigma = (r, s)$.

**Signature Verification:**

$$u_1 = z \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot s^{-1} \bmod n$$

$$R = (u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot Q_A).x \bmod n$$

$$\text{Signature valid} \iff R = r$$

**Diagram (3 marks):**
- Show the signing flow: $T \rightarrow H(T) \rightarrow (r,s) = \text{Sign}_{k_{priv}}(H(T))$.
- Show the verification flow: $H(T), (r,s), Q_A \rightarrow \text{Verify} \rightarrow \text{True/False}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often forget to **include the modulo-$n$ operation** in the verification equation. This loses 1–2 marks.
> - Forgetting to mention the **fresh random nonce $k$** requirement in signing loses a mark.
> - ECDSA verification must explicitly use the **public key $Q_A$**, not the private key — examiners deduct marks for this confusion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss **probabilistic finality** and **absolute finality** in blockchain systems. How does the number of confirmations affect the reversal probability in Bitcoin?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **UTXO model** and **Account model** of transactions. Compare them with respect to privacy, scalability, and smart-contract support.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Probabilistic vs. Absolute Finality (7 marks):**

**Probabilistic Finality (3 marks):** In Nakamoto PoW systems (Bitcoin), a confirmed block can theoretically be reversed by a chain reorganization. The probability of reversal drops exponentially as more blocks are mined on top of the block containing the transaction. The reversal probability after $k$ confirmations is:

$$P_{\text{reversal}}(k) \approx \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^{k}$$

where $p$ is the honest hash power and $q = 1-p$ is the attacker's hash power. For $q = 0.1$ and $k = 6$, $P_{\text{reversal}} \approx 10^{-6}$, considered negligible.

**Absolute Finality (3 marks):** In BFT-style PoS systems (Ethereum 2.0, Tendermint, HotStuff), once a supermajority (typically 2/3) of validators attest to a block, it is **deterministically final** — it cannot be reverted without at least 1/3 of validators being slashed (losing their staked capital). Finality is reached in two epochs (~12.8 minutes in Ethereum).

**Comparative Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Probabilistic (PoW) | Absolute (BFT-PoS) |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Theoretically possible | Economically impossible |
| Time to finality | ~60 min (6 conf.) | ~12.8 min (2 epochs) |
| Energy cost | High | Low |
| Throughput | Low | Higher |

**(b) UTXO vs. Account Model (7 marks):**

**UTXO Model (3.5 marks):**
- Used by Bitcoin, Litecoin, Cardano.
- The ledger state is the set of all unspent transaction outputs.
- Each transaction consumes existing UTXOs and creates new ones.
- Analogy: cash transactions — you spend exact bills and receive change.
- Advantages: Better privacy (new address per TX), parallel validation, simpler double-spend prevention.
- Disadvantages: Less intuitive for smart contracts, state management is harder.

**Account Model (3.5 marks):**
- Used by Ethereum, Solana, Binance Smart Chain.
- The ledger state is a list of accounts with balances and contract storage.
- Each transaction is a state transition: `Account[from].balance -= value; Account[to].balance += value`.
- Analogy: bank ledger — your account balance is debited.
- Advantages: Intuitive, efficient for smart contracts and dApps, simpler state queries.
- Disadvantages: Sequential transaction processing (nonce), less privacy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write "UTXO model is more secure" without justification. Use the **double-spend prevention by UTXO set lookup** as your justification.
> - Forgetting to mention **nonce** in the Account model costs 1 mark.
> - Smart-contract compatibility comparison is **mandatory** — the Account model is preferred for dApps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Transaction Lifecycle:**

- ☐ A **transaction** is a signed data record transferring value; not the same as a "block" or "message."
- ☐ Seven lifecycle stages: **Construct → Sign → Broadcast → Validate → Mempool → Mine/Include → Confirm/Finalize.**
- ☐ Bitcoin uses **secp256k1 + ECDSA**; transaction ID is **double-SHA-256**.
- ☐ The **mempool is local**, not global; it is fee-driven and DoS-protected.
- ☐ A block header includes the **Merkle root** of all transactions — enables SPV.
- ☐ **6 confirmations** in Bitcoin ≈ practical irreversibility (~60 minutes).
- ☐ PoW gives **probabilistic finality**; BFT-PoS gives **absolute finality** after 2/3+ attestation.
- ☐ **UTXO model** (Bitcoin): cash-like, parallel, privacy-friendly.
- ☐ **Account model** (Ethereum): bank-like, sequential via nonce, smart-contract friendly.
- ☐ **ECDSA verification equation:** $R = (z \cdot s^{-1} \cdot G + r \cdot s^{-1} \cdot Q_A).x \bmod n$; valid iff $R = r$.
- ☐ **Double-spend prevention** rests on (i) UTXO set uniqueness, (ii) consensus-driven ordering, and (iii) economic cost of reversal.
- ☐ **Common KTU keywords** to memorize: mempool, finality, double-spend, locktime, ScriptPubKey, ScriptSig, nonce, merkle root, SPV.
- ☐ For 14-mark answers, **always include a labeled diagram** (lifecycle flowchart or sequence diagram) — this typically earns 1–2 bonus marks.
- ☐ Examiner's "**loss-of-marks traps**": missing modulo-$n$, forgetting fresh nonce, confusing probabilistic/absolute finality, omitting the Merkle root role.

> **End of Module 4 — Transaction Lifecycle Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coinbase Transactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coinbase Transactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coinbase Transaction (Definition – KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Coinbase Transaction** is a special, protocol-defined transaction that is mandatorily placed as the **first transaction in every valid block** of a Bitcoin-like (UTXO-based) blockchain. Unlike regular transactions, it has **no real inputs** (it mints new cryptocurrency *ex nihilo*) and is uniquely used to **(a) reward the miner/validator** for producing the block, and **(b) collect the aggregate transaction fees** from all the regular transactions packed inside that block. Its `scriptSig` field is replaced with an arbitrary `coinbase` data field.

In the formal UTXO model, every transaction must reference unspent transaction outputs (UTXOs) as inputs. The coinbase transaction is the **sole exception** to this rule — it is the only transaction allowed to bring new value into the circulating supply.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **treasurer at a sealed-vote bank vault**:
- Every evening, the treasurer opens a new ledger (a *block*).
- The first line they write is **not a customer withdrawal**. Instead, it is the bank **printing new currency** and **pocketing all the service charges** collected that day from customer transactions.
- This first, privileged line is the **Coinbase Transaction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary**
> A coinbase transaction is the miner's "salary cheque" plus their "tips" (fees), authorized by the network protocol itself. It is the *only* way new coins legally enter circulation in a Bitcoin-style blockchain.

## 1.3 Core Structural Properties (Bullet Snapshot)

- **Position:** Always the **first transaction** (`tx_index = 0`) in the block's `tx` array.
- **Inputs:** Exactly **one input**, but it has **no real previous output reference** — instead of a `txid` and `vout`, it contains a 32-byte all-zeros hash and a `vout` of `0xFFFFFFFF`.
- **Coinbase Data Field:** Arbitrary byte string of **2 to 100 bytes** (post-BIP-34, must start with the block height).
- **Outputs:** One or more outputs, summing to **(Block Reward) + (Σ Transaction Fees of all other txs in the block)**.
- **Maturity Rule:** The newly minted outputs of a coinbase transaction **cannot be spent** until **100 confirmations** (i.e., 100 blocks have been mined on top of it) — this is the **Coinbase Maturity Rule**.
- **Total Supply Cap:** Sum of all coinbase rewards over time is geometrically bounded at **21,000,000 BTC** for Bitcoin.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact to Memorize**
> The coinbase transaction has **exactly one input**, but that input is "synthetic" — it does not spend an existing UTXO. This is why the **Total Input Value** of a coinbase transaction is conceptually **zero**, even though its output is non-zero.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Functions of a Coinbase Transaction

1. **Monetary Issuance (Inflation Schedule):**
   The protocol dictates the *only* legitimate source of new coins. The block reward follows a deterministic, mathematically pre-scheduled **halving curve**.

2. **Fee Aggregation:**
   The miner collects the cumulative fees of all non-coinbase transactions in the block:
   
   $$\text{Fee}_{\text{aggregated}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \text{Fee}(tx_i)$$

   where $n$ is the total number of transactions in the block and $tx_0$ is the coinbase.

3. **Optional Data Carrier:**
   The `coinbase` data field (formerly called `scriptSig` for coinbase) can carry **arbitrary bytes** — used historically for miner tags ("Mined by AntPool"), BIP-34 height encoding, and even the **SegWit/ BIP-141 commitment** (`OP_RETURN`-style witness commitment).

## 2.2 The Halving Schedule (The Core Economic Engine)

The block reward is halved every **210,000 blocks** (≈ every 4 years at 10-minute target block times).

### Derivation of the Block Reward Function

Let $h$ be the current block height, and $e$ be the **epoch number** (number of halvings that have occurred):

$$e = \left\lfloor \frac{h}{210{,}000} \right\rfloor$$

The block reward (in BTC for Bitcoin) is:

$$R(h) = R_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{e} = 50 \cdot 2^{-e} \text{ BTC}$$

where $R_0 = 50$ BTC is the **genesis block reward**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $R_0 = 50$ BTC?**
> The genesis block (Block #0, mined by Satoshi Nakamoto on 3 Jan 2009) embedded a reward of exactly 50 BTC, which became the canonical initial issuance of the protocol.

### Current Reward Status (Post-2024 Halving)

| Halving Event | Block Height | Approx. Date | Reward Before → After |
|---------------|--------------|--------------|------------------------|
| Genesis       | 0            | Jan 2009     | **50 BTC**             |
| 1st Halving  | 210,000      | Nov 2012     | 50 → 25 BTC            |
| 2nd Halving  | 420,000      | Jul 2016     | 25 → 12.5 BTC          |
| 3rd Halving  | 630,000      | May 2020     | 12.5 → 6.25 BTC        |
| 4th Halving  | 840,000      | Apr 2024     | 6.25 → **3.125 BTC**   |
| 5th Halving (est.) | 1,050,000 | ~2028     | 3.125 → 1.5625 BTC     |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Epoch (Halving Period) | $e = \left\lfloor h \,/\, 210{,}000 \right\rfloor$ | integer | $h$ = block height |
| 2 | Block Reward | $R(h) = 50 \cdot 2^{-e}$ | BTC | Drops to 0 when $e \geq 64$ |
| 3 | Total Block Subsidy | $\text{Coinbase Output} = R(h) + \sum \text{Fees}$ | BTC | Must equal sum of `vout` amounts |
| 4 | Coinbase Maturity | 100 blocks | confirmations | Coins unspendable before this |
| 5 | Total Supply Cap | $S_{\infty} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k}$ | BTC | Geometric series → 21,000,000 |
| 6 | Coinbase Data Field Size | $\geq 2$ bytes, $\leq 100$ bytes | bytes | BIP-34 mandates height prefix |
| 7 | Halving Interval | **210,000 blocks** | blocks | ≈ 4 years |
| 8 | Total Coins Per Epoch | $E_k = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k}$ | BTC | Epoch $k$ total issuance |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Monetary Policy Implementation:** The coinbase transaction *is* the cryptocurrency's central bank — a fully algorithmic, transparent, and auditable monetary policy written in code rather than decree.
- **Mining Economics:** It governs miner profitability, hash rate trends, and ultimately network security. As rewards diminish, **transaction fees must replace block subsidy** to sustain the security budget (a critical post-2144 concern).
- **Block Validation Heuristics:** Full nodes and block explorers use the **coinbase position** and the **maturity rule** as primary trust invariants when validating chains.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The 21 Million BTC Supply Cap

This is the single most important numerical question in KTU exams on this topic. We derive it **rigorously** using a geometric series.

### Step 1: Define the Issuance Per Epoch

In every epoch (between two consecutive halvings), the block reward is constant. The number of blocks per epoch is exactly:

$$N = 210{,}000 \text{ blocks}$$

### Step 2: Issuance in the $k$-th Epoch

During epoch $k$ (where $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$), the per-block reward is $50 \cdot 2^{-k}$ BTC. Therefore, the **total coins issued in epoch $k$** are:

$$E_k = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-k} \text{ BTC}$$

### Step 3: Total Supply = Infinite Geometric Sum

The total supply $S_{\infty}$ is the sum across all epochs:

$$S_{\infty} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-k}$$

$$S_{\infty} = 10{,}500{,}000 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Geometric Series Identity

For a geometric series with first term $a$ and common ratio $r$ (where $\vert r \vert < 1$):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^{k} = \frac{1}{1-r}$$

Here, $r = \tfrac{1}{2}$, so:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{\tfrac{1}{2}} = 2$$

### Step 5: Final Multiplication

$$S_{\infty} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (KTU Gold Standard Answer):** The maximum total supply of Bitcoin is exactly **21,000,000 BTC**, mathematically enforced by the halving schedule of the coinbase transaction reward. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation: When Will the Last BTC Be Mined?

The reward $R(h)$ becomes zero when:

$$50 \cdot 2^{-e} = 0 \implies e \to \infty \text{ in the limit}$$

However, in practice, reward drops below **1 satoshi** (smallest unit = $10^{-8}$ BTC) at $e = 64$ (since $50 \cdot 2^{-64} \approx 2.71 \times 10^{-18}$ BTC). After the 64th halving, the protocol rounds down to zero, and miners will rely **entirely on transaction fees**.

The block height at which the last whole bitcoin is mined is approximately:

$$h_{\text{last whole}} = 33 \cdot 210{,}000 = 6{,}930{,}000 \text{ (around year 2140)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Block #840,000 (The 2024 Halving Block)

**Question (KTU-style):** A miner successfully mines the 4th-halving boundary block. The block contains 2,500 regular transactions whose total fees equal 0.847 BTC. Compute the total coinbase output the miner receives.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Determine the epoch:**

$$e = \left\lfloor \frac{840{,}000}{210{,}000} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 4 \right\rfloor = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the block reward:**

$$R = 50 \cdot 2^{-4} = \frac{50}{16} = 3.125 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 3 — Add aggregate fees:**

$$\text{Coinbase Output} = 3.125 + 0.847 = 3.972 \text{ BTC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Rule:** The sum of all `vout` amounts in the coinbase transaction must equal **exactly 3.972 BTC** (within the smallest unit precision). The full node rejects the block otherwise.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Coinbase vs Regular Transaction

| Property | Regular Transaction | **Coinbase Transaction** |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| Position in block | Any index $\geq 1$ | **Always index 0** |
| Number of inputs | $\geq 1$ | Exactly **1 (synthetic)** |
| Previous `txid` reference | Real, 32-byte hash | **32 bytes of zeros** |
| Previous `vout` index | $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | **`0xFFFFFFFF`** |
| `scriptSig` content | Valid unlocking script | Arbitrary data (BIP-34 height + extra) |
| Spendable immediately | Yes (after confirmations) | **No — 100-block maturity** |
| Value source | Existing UTXOs | **Newly created + collected fees** |
| Can spend another coinbase? | No (maturity) | **No (maturity)** |
| Witness commitment present? | No | **Yes (SegWit / BIP-141)** |
| Affects monetary supply? | No (zero-sum transfer) | **Yes (inflationary)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coinbase Transaction Inside a Block — Structural Flow

The diagram below illustrates the **canonical block structure** with the coinbase transaction occupying the privileged first position, plus its internal data layout.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BLOCK N] --> B[Block Header]
    A --> C[Transaction Array]
    
    B --> B1[Previous Block Hash]
    B --> B2[Merkle Root]
    B --> B3[Timestamp]
    B --> B4[Difficulty Target]
    B --> B5[Nonce]
    
    C --> T0["tx_index = 0: COINBASE TX"]
    C --> T1["tx_index = 1: Regular TX"]
    C --> T2["tx_index = 2: Regular TX"]
    C --> T3["tx_index = k-1: Regular TX"]
    
    T0 --> CB1["vin: ONE synthetic input"]
    T0 --> CB2["vout: one or more outputs"]
    
    CB1 --> CB1a["prev_txid = 0x0000...0000 (32 zero bytes)"]
    CB1 --> CB1b["prev_vout = 0xFFFFFFFF"]
    CB1 --> CB1c["scriptSig = coinbase data (2 to 100 bytes)"]
    CB1 --> CB1d["sequence = 0xFFFFFFFF"]
    
    CB2 --> CB2a["value_1 = R + fees (to miner address)"]
    CB2 --> CB2b["value_2... value_m = optional OP_RETURN / splits"]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffd700,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2c5282,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2c5282,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style T0 fill:#b8860b,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    style T1 fill:#4a5568,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]
**Q: Define a coinbase transaction. Why is it considered unique compared to a regular Bitcoin transaction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **coinbase transaction** is the **first transaction** included in every block of a Bitcoin-like blockchain. It is unique because **(1)** it does not reference any previous transaction output (its `prev_txid` is a 32-byte all-zeros hash and `prev_vout` is `0xFFFFFFFF`); **(2)** it is the **only transaction allowed to create new coins** from the protocol's predetermined issuance schedule; and **(3)** it serves as the mechanism for the miner to collect the **block reward plus the aggregate transaction fees** of all other transactions in the block. **(1 mark each point = 3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]
**Q: What is the Coinbase Maturity Rule, and why is it enforced by the protocol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Coinbase Maturity Rule** states that the outputs of a coinbase transaction **cannot be spent** until **100 additional blocks** have been mined on top of the block containing that coinbase. **(2 marks)** It is enforced to protect the network against **chain reorganizations (reorgs)**: if a block is orphaned, all the newly minted coins it created must revert to non-existence, and the maturity window guarantees that no spent coins can be invalidated by a reorg. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ CHOICE A: Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the structure of a Bitcoin block with special emphasis on the **position, components, and uniqueness** of the coinbase transaction. Discuss how BIP-34 changed the coinbase data field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A miner successfully mines block number **1,260,000** during a period when the block contains regular transactions whose **total fees amount to 1.234 BTC**.
**(i)** Calculate the **epoch number** at this height.
**(ii)** Compute the **block subsidy** in BTC.
**(iii)** Determine the **total coinbase output** the miner is entitled to.
**(iv)** Briefly explain what happens to the block reward after the 64th halving. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Block Structure and Coinbase Uniqueness (7 marks):**

A Bitcoin block consists of a **block header** (80 bytes: previous block hash, merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, nonce) and a **transaction array**. The **first transaction (index 0)** of this array is always the **coinbase transaction**. **(1 mark)**

The coinbase transaction is structurally unique:
- It contains **exactly one input**, but this input does not spend any real UTXO. Its `prev_txid` is set to **32 zero bytes** and `prev_vout` to **`0xFFFFFFFF`**. **(1 mark)**
- Its `scriptSig` field (called the *coinbase data*) is **arbitrary**, with size constrained to **2–100 bytes**. **(1 mark)**
- The transaction output equals the **block subsidy (current reward) + total fees** of all other transactions in the block. **(1 mark)**
- The coinbase outputs are subject to a **100-block maturity rule** before they can be spent. **(1 mark)**

**BIP-34 Change:** Before BIP-34 (activated March 2013), the coinbase data was purely arbitrary. BIP-34 mandated that the **first part of the coinbase data must encode the block height** as a script number, ensuring each coinbase is uniquely identifiable by height. This prevents duplicate coinbase hashes across the chain and resolves an attack vector on unmerged mining. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Epoch Number (2 marks):**

$$e = \left\lfloor \frac{1{,}260{,}000}{210{,}000} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 6 \right\rfloor = 6$$

**(ii) Block Subsidy (2 marks):**

$$R = 50 \cdot 2^{-6} = \frac{50}{64} = 0.78125 \text{ BTC}$$

**(iii) Total Coinbase Output (2 marks):**

$$\text{Coinbase Output} = R + \text{Fees} = 0.78125 + 1.234 = 2.01525 \text{ BTC}$$

**(iv) Post-64th Halving Behaviour (1 mark):**

After the **64th halving** (around year **2140**), the block subsidy $R = 50 \cdot 2^{-64} \approx 2.71 \times 10^{-18}$ BTC drops **below 1 satoshi** (the smallest indivisible unit, $10^{-8}$ BTC). The protocol rounds the subsidy **down to zero**, and miners will earn income **exclusively from transaction fees**. This is the **post-subsidy / fee-only era** of Bitcoin.

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B: Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** Derive mathematically that the **maximum total supply** of Bitcoin is exactly **21,000,000 BTC**, by modelling the halving schedule as an infinite geometric series. State clearly any assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the following with respect to the coinbase transaction:
**(i)** Why does the block reward halve every 210,000 blocks instead of on a fixed calendar date?
**(ii)** What is the role of the coinbase data field in **merged mining** and **miner signaling**?
**(iii)** Compare the coinbase model of issuance with a **pre-mined** cryptocurrency (like XRP or pre-mined ERC-20s). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Derivation of 21M BTC Supply Cap (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:** Block time is 10 minutes on average; one halving occurs every 210,000 blocks; reward starts at 50 BTC and halves each epoch; halving continues indefinitely (or until the reward rounds to zero). **(1 mark)**

**Step 1 — Define epoch $k$:** $k = 0$ is the genesis era, $k = 1$ after the first halving, etc. The number of blocks per epoch is $N = 210{,}000$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Per-epoch issuance:** During epoch $k$, the per-block reward is $50 \cdot 2^{-k}$ BTC, so:

$$E_k = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-k} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-k} \text{ BTC} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 3 — Total supply as infinite sum:**

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-k} = 10{,}500{,}000 \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 4 — Geometric series identity:** Since $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} r^{k} = \tfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = 2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$S = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Hence, the **maximum total supply of Bitcoin is mathematically proven to be 21,000,000 BTC**. ∎

---

**(b) Discussion Questions (7 marks):**

**(i) Why halve every 210,000 blocks and not by date? (3 marks):**
Halving by block count (not by calendar date) makes the issuance schedule **independent of real-world time and hashrate fluctuations**, making the monetary policy **deterministic and time-invariant**. If the network finds blocks faster or slower than the 10-minute target, the **calendar year** of each halving shifts, but the **total coin issuance per epoch remains mathematically identical**. This guarantees that the 21M cap holds regardless of mining hardware evolution (ASICs, quantum advances, etc.) — a property called **issuance invariance**.

**(ii) Role of coinbase data in merged mining and signaling (2 marks):**
The arbitrary `coinbase` bytes field is used by miners to **embed extra data** — historically miner names ("Mined by F2Pool"), **AuxPoW commitments** (for merged mining of Namecoin, Dogecoin, etc., as standardized in **BIP-301**), and **BIP-9 version-bit signaling** for soft fork activation. The unique per-block height prefix from BIP-34 also enables **auxiliary proof-of-work chains** to reference the parent chain's block.

**(iii) Coinbase issuance vs. pre-mined crypto (2 marks):**
In the **coinbase model**, coins are distributed **fairly over time** through competitive mining — a transparent, ongoing, decentralized distribution. In a **pre-mined model** (XRP, pre-mined ERC-20 tokens, etc.), the entire supply is generated at genesis and held by insiders/teams, who then distribute it via sales, airdrops, or burns. Coinbase-based issuance is **algorithmically fair but energy-intensive**; pre-mining is **capital-efficient but centralization-prone**, with the risk of "insider dumping" harming market confidence.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Coinbase Transaction Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the fee component:** Many students compute only $R(h)$ and forget to add $\sum \text{Fees}$. The coinbase output is **$R(h) + \text{Total Fees}$**, not just the subsidy. *(Lose 1–2 marks easily.)*
> 2. **Using the wrong epoch formula:** A common error is using $\left\lceil h / 210{,}000 \right\rceil$ instead of $\left\lfloor h / 210{,}000 \right\rfloor$. Always remember: block **0** is in **epoch 0**, block **209,999** is still in **epoch 0**, and block **210,000** is in **epoch 1**.
> 3. **Ignoring the 21M derivation requirement:** KTU often asks for a **step-by-step geometric series derivation** (5-step answer). Skipping any step costs a mark.
> 4. **Confusing Coinbase (the company) with coinbase (the transaction):** The lowercase "coinbase" is the blockchain concept. The company **Coinbase Global Inc.** is unrelated — don't mix them up in your answer.
> 5. **Omitting BIP-34 details:** If asked about "uniqueness" or "structure," mentioning **BIP-34 block height encoding** is a 2-mark booster.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Ultra-Rapid Revision Checklist — Coinbase Transactions**

- ✅ A **coinbase transaction** is the **first transaction (index 0)** in every Bitcoin block.
- ✅ It has **exactly one synthetic input** with `prev_txid = 0x00…00` (32 zero bytes) and `prev_vout = 0xFFFFFFFF`.
- ✅ It is the **sole source of new coin issuance** in the protocol (monetary inflation).
- ✅ The **coinbase output = Block Reward (subsidy) + Σ Transaction Fees** of all other txs in the block.
- ✅ The block reward follows $R(h) = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210{,}000 \rfloor}$ BTC.
- ✅ **Halving** occurs every **210,000 blocks** (≈ 4 years), not by date.
- ✅ **Coinbase maturity** = **100 blocks** before newly minted outputs become spendable.
- ✅ The **coinbase data field** must be **2–100 bytes**, and post-BIP-34 must begin with the **block height** as a script-encoded integer.
- ✅ The **total supply cap** is **21,000,000 BTC**, derived from the geometric series $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2^{-k}$.
- ✅ The cap is reached mathematically around year **2140** (block ≈ 6,930,000), after which miners earn **fees only**.
- ✅ Coinbase transactions can also carry the **SegWit witness commitment** (BIP-141) and **merged-mining AuxPoW tags** (BIP-301).
- ✅ It is the **only transaction allowed to violate the UTXO spending rule** (no real previous output is consumed).

> **Memory Hook:** *"Coinbase is the king's first decree — it mints the money, collects the tax, and announces the height, all in one privileged line at the top of the block ledger."* 👑
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
