<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transaction Validation** is the deterministic, multi-stage cryptographic and structural verification process executed by every full node in a decentralized peer-to-peer network to confirm that a proposed transaction strictly conforms to the protocol's consensus rules *before* it is admitted into a candidate block, propagated across the network, or permanently appended to the distributed ledger.

In the context of **Bitcoin**, the **Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO)** model is used; in **Ethereum**, the **Account/Balance model** with smart contract execution is used. Validation ensures the **"Three Pillars"**:

1. **Authenticity** — The sender actually owns the private key (verified via **ECDSA**).
2. **Integrity** — The transaction data has not been tampered with (verified via **SHA-256** hashing).
3. **Conservation of Value** — No new currency is created out of thin air (verified via UTXO arithmetic).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Teller on a Photocopier"

Imagine a world where every person in a country is a photocopy machine, and each photocopy machine holds an *exact replica* of every single bank cheque ever written. When you want to spend **₹500**:

- You don't "deduct" from a balance; instead, you point to a **previous cheque worth ₹500** that was made out to you (a **UTXO**), and you **tear it up** by signing a new cheque that re-assigns that exact ₹500 to someone else.
- **Every single photocopy machine (node)** in the country simultaneously runs a checklist on your new cheque:
  1. Is your signature genuine?
  2. Does the cheque number actually exist and is it unspent?
  3. Are the inputs adding up to the outputs (no counterfeit money created)?
- If **all** machines agree, the cheque is valid. If even **one** machine disagrees, the cheque is rejected by the entire system.

> [!NOTE]
> This distributed, redundant checking is precisely what makes blockchain "trustless" — you don't trust a single authority; you trust the mathematical consensus of thousands of independent validators.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Bitcoin Block Size Limit** | **1,000,000 bytes** (~1 MB) | Limits transactions per block |
| **Bitcoin Block Weight Limit** | **4,000,000 weight units** (SegWit) | Effective limit post-2017 |
| **Bitcoin Block Time** | **~10 minutes** | Average target |
| **Ethereum Block Gas Limit** | **30,000,000 gas** | Dynamic per block |
| **Ethereum Block Time** | **~12 seconds** | Slot-based (post-Merge) |
| **ECDSA Curve (secp256k1)** | **256-bit** private key | Used in Bitcoin & Ethereum |
| **Hash Output Length** | **256 bits** (32 bytes) | SHA-256, double-hashed in Bitcoin |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Fire:** When asked "What does validation *not* do?", remember — validation does **NOT** include reaching consensus on the *order* of blocks. That is the job of the **consensus mechanism** (PoW/PoS). Validation is a **local, deterministic** check done by every node on every transaction and block.

---

## 1.4 Transaction Anatomy (Prerequisite)

A **raw Bitcoin transaction** contains:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `version` | Protocol version (4 bytes) |
| `input count` | Number of inputs (varint) |
| `inputs[]` | References to previous UTXOs + unlocking scripts (`scriptSig`) |
| `output count` | Number of outputs (varint) |
| `outputs[]` | New UTXOs + locking scripts (`scriptPubKey`) + value in **satoshis** |
| `locktime` | Earliest block/time the tx can be mined |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Satoshi** = 10⁻⁸ of a Bitcoin. **1 BTC = 100,000,000 satoshis**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Layer Validation Architecture

Validation in blockchain is best understood as a **two-layer funnel**:

### **Layer 1 — Network-Level Validation (Pre-Mempool)**
Performed by *any* full node receiving a transaction over the wire:

- **Syntactic correctness:** Is the raw byte structure well-formed?
- **Size constraints:** Is the transaction ≤ **100,000 bytes** (Bitcoin standard policy)?
- **Dust threshold:** Are outputs below the dust limit (typically **546 satoshis** for P2PKH at 3 sat/vB)?
- **Standard script templates:** Does it use only "standard" transaction types?
- **Mempool acceptance:** If all checks pass → pushed to the mempool.

### **Layer 2 — Consensus-Level Validation (Block Inclusion)**
Performed by miners/validators when assembling a block:

- **Full script execution:** Run `scriptSig` + `scriptPubKey` → must result in `TRUE`.
- **UTXO set lookup:** Each input must reference a real, existing, unspent output.
- **Double-spend check:** No input may already be spent by a tx in the same/parent block.
- **Conservation law:** `Σ(inputs) ≥ Σ(outputs)` (the difference is the implicit **fee**).
- **Coinbase maturity check:** Coinbase outputs require **100 confirmations** before spendable (Bitcoin).

## 2.2 The Five-Stage Validation Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most exam-relevant** framework for KTU. Memorize the order.

| Stage | Check | What is Verified | Failure Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Syntax Parse** | Raw byte structure | Valid `version`, varints, field count | Tx dropped, peer may be banned |
| **2. Input Existence** | UTXO database lookup | Each `prev_txid` exists in active UTXO set | Tx marked invalid (orphan) |
| **3. Unspent Status** | UTXO set flag check | Referenced output not already consumed | **Double-spend rejected** |
| **4. Script Verification** | Concatenated `scriptSig ∥ scriptPubKey` execution | Script returns `TRUE` on stack | Tx rejected |
| **5. Conservation & Fee** | Integer arithmetic | `Σ inputs − Σ outputs = fee ≥ 0` | Tx rejected if `fee < 0` (counterfeit) |

## 2.3 UTXO Model vs Account Model

| Property | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|
| State representation | Set of unspent outputs | Map of addresses → balances |
| Validation paradigm | **Stateless** (each tx independent) | **Stateful** (depends on global state) |
| Parallel processing | **High** (independent UTXOs) | Limited (sequential per account) |
| Double-spend check | UTXO already in set? | Account nonce matches expected? |
| Fee formula | `fee = Σinputs − Σoutputs` | `fee = gasUsed × gasPrice` |
| Smart contract | Limited (Bitcoin Script, non-Turing-complete) | **Turing-complete EVM** |
| Failure handling | Individual input can fail | All-or-nothing atomic execution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum uses a `nonce` per account** — every transaction from address `A` must have `nonce = A's previous nonce + 1`. This is how Ethereum prevents double-spends in the account model.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `fee_tx = Σ(v_in) − Σ(v_out)` | Transaction fee (UTXO model) | satoshis; **must be ≥ 0** |
| 2 | `H(T) = SHA256(SHA256(T))` | Bitcoin transaction ID (`txid`) | 256-bit output |
| 3 | `s·G = R + H(z)·P_K` | ECDSA signature verification | secp256k1 curve |
| 4 | `BIP-34 height` | Coinbase must include block height | 100-block maturity |
| 5 | `Δ_block ≤ 4,000,000` wu | Block weight (SegWit) | witness data discount |
| 6 | `gasUsed ≤ blockGasLimit` | Ethereum block resource cap | **30M** gas (target) |
| 7 | `effective_fee = priority_fee + max_fee − base_fee` | EIP-1559 fee formula | gwei |
| 8 | `Σ(sigOps) ≤ 20,000` per block | Bitcoin sigop limit | prevents DoS |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** Notice that vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown parser — this is why the formula sheet above uses colon-aligned rows instead of pipe-aligned cells when a vertical bar is needed.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transaction validation is the **gateway to every blockchain** — from cryptocurrency (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin) to enterprise DLTs (Hyperledger Fabric uses **endorsement policy validation** instead). It enables:

- **Cross-border payments** (Ripple, Stellar — strict account-sequence validation)
- **Supply chain provenance** (each "transaction" is a product-handover event with cryptographic signature)
- **DeFi protocols** (every smart contract invocation is a state-transitioning transaction validated by every EVM node)
- **CBDCs** (Central Bank Digital Currencies rely on validated, signed, double-spend-proofed transactions)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The UTXO Conservation Law

The most fundamental invariant in any UTXO-based blockchain. We prove that the conservation law `Σ(v_in) ≥ Σ(v_out)` is both **necessary** and **sufficient** to prevent currency inflation.

### Step 1: Define the Transaction as a Value-Transfer Function

Let a transaction `T` have:
- `n` inputs referencing UTXOs `U_1, U_2, …, U_n` with values `v_1, v_2, …, v_n`
- `m` outputs creating new UTXOs `U'_1, U'_2, …, U'_m` with values `v'_1, v'_2, …, v'_m`

The transaction destroys `U_1…U_n` and creates `U'_1…U'_m`.

### Step 2: State the Total Value in the System

Let `S_t` denote the total value locked in the UTXO set at time `t`. Initially, `S_0 = 0`. The only way new value enters is through the **coinbase transaction** (block reward + fees).

### Step 3: Write the Block-Level Conservation Equation

A block `B` contains a coinbase transaction `C` (creating `v_coinbase` new satoshis) and a set of regular transactions `{T_i}`. For each `T_i`:

$$v_{\text{in},i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} v_{j}$$

is the sum of input values, and

$$v_{\text{out},i} = \sum_{k=1}^{m_i} v'_{k}$$

is the sum of output values. The fee is

$$f_i = v_{\text{in},i} - v_{\text{out},i}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Conservation Law Per Transaction

For the transaction to be valid:

$$v_{\text{in},i} \geq v_{\text{out},i}$$

Equivalently, in shorthand using the `\vert` notation to avoid table-breaking pipes:

$$f_i \,=\, v_{\text{in},i} \;-\; v_{\text{out},i} \;\geq\; 0$$

This is the **conservation law**. The inequality is strict `≥` rather than `=` because a positive fee is permitted (it is collected by the miner, effectively destroying the UTXO and re-creating it via the coinbase).

### Step 5: Prove No Inflation Across the Whole Blockchain

Summing over all transactions in block `B`:

$$S_{t+1} = S_t + v_{\text{coinbase}} = S_t + (\text{block subsidy}) + \sum_i f_i$$

Since `v_coinbase` is **deterministic from the block height** (halving schedule), the total supply grows at a known, bounded rate. **No transaction can ever create value ex-nihilo.** This is the elegant mathematical guarantee of the UTXO model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If a student writes "validation checks `Σinputs = Σoutputs`" they are **wrong** — the correct check is `Σinputs ≥ Σoutputs` (the difference is the fee). This is a classic **1-mark trap** in KTU papers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ECDSA Signature Verification (The `scriptSig` Heart)

Every transaction input carries a signature proving ownership. Here is the full mathematical verification flow.

### Step 1: Inputs to the Verification Algorithm

Given a signature `(r, s)`, a message hash `z = H(m)` (the sighash), and the public key `P_K`:

### Step 2: Curve & Constants

The elliptic curve is **secp256k1** with generator point `G` and prime order `n`.

### Step 3: Modular Inverse

Compute the modular inverse of `s` modulo `n`:

$$w = s^{-1} \bmod n$$

This is computable via the Extended Euclidean Algorithm; `w` always exists because `s` is non-zero and `gcd(s, n) = 1`.

### Step 4: Compute Two Scalars

$$u_1 = z \cdot w \bmod n$$

$$u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod n$$

### Step 5: Compute the Verification Point

$$\text{Point} \; X \;=\; u_1 \cdot G \;+\; u_2 \cdot P_K$$

This requires one **point multiplication** and one **point addition** on the elliptic curve — both `O(log n)` operations.

### Step 6: Extract the x-coordinate

Let `X = (x_X, y_X)`. Define:

$$v = x_X \bmod n$$

### Step 7: Final Equality Check

The signature is **valid if and only if**:

$$v = r$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The equation `s·G = R + H(z)·P_K` is derived from the signing equation `s = k⁻¹(z + r·d_A) mod n`, where `k` is the per-signature nonce and `d_A` is the private key. Multiplying both sides by `k` and rearranging yields the verification equation. If the signature was generated by the holder of the private key corresponding to `P_K`, the equation holds; otherwise, the probability of a random `(r, s)` satisfying it is `2⁻²⁵⁶` — cryptographically negligible.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The `sighash` (What Exactly is Signed?)

A critical subtlety: the signature does NOT sign the entire raw transaction. It signs a **transformed version** depending on the `sighash flag` byte.

| Sighash Flag | Hex | What is Signed |
|---|---|---|
| `SIGHASH_ALL` | `0x01` | All inputs **and** all outputs |
| `SIGHASH_NONE` | `0x02` | All inputs, **no** outputs (anyone can redirect funds) |
| `SIGHASH_SINGLE` | `0x03` | All inputs, **only** the output with same index |
| `SIGHASH_ANYONECANPAY` | `0x80` | Modifier — others can add inputs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Gold:** `SIGHASH_ALL` is the default and most common. The `ANYONECANPAY` modifier enables **crowdfunding transactions** — multiple parties add their own inputs to a transaction whose outputs are pre-determined.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Transaction Validation Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Transaction Bytes Received] --> B{Syntax Valid?}
    B -- No --> Z1[Drop Tx, Log Peer]
    B -- Yes --> C{Size Within Policy?}
    C -- No --> Z1
    C -- Yes --> D{Standard Script Type?}
    D -- No --> Z2[Non-Standard Pool]
    D -- Yes --> E[Push to Mempool]
    E --> F[Block Assembly by Miner]
    F --> G{All Inputs Exist in UTXO Set?}
    G -- No --> Z3[Mark as Invalid - Orphan]
    G -- Yes --> H{All Inputs Unspent?}
    H -- No --> Z4[Reject - DOUBLE SPEND]
    H -- Yes --> I[Execute scriptSig + scriptPubKey]
    I --> J{Script Returns TRUE?}
    J -- No --> Z5[Reject - Invalid Signature]
    J -- Yes --> K{Sum Inputs greater or equal Sum Outputs?}
    K -- No --> Z6[Reject - Counterfeit Value]
    K -- Yes --> L[Valid Transaction - Add to Block]
    L --> M[Update UTXO Set - Remove Spent, Add New]
    M --> N[Transaction Confirmed]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style Z1 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style Z2 fill:#78350f,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style Z3 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style Z4 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style Z5 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style Z6 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 UTXO Set Evolution Across a Transaction (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["Before Tx T1 (UTXO Set)"]
        U1["UTXO A: 5 BTC to Alice"]
        U2["UTXO B: 3 BTC to Alice"]
    end

    subgraph TX["Transaction T1"]
        T1["Inputs: U1 (5 BTC) + U2 (3 BTC) = 8 BTC"]
        T2["Output 1: 6 BTC to Bob"]
        T3["Output 2: 1.9 BTC to Alice (change)"]
        T4["Fee: 0.1 BTC to Miner"]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["After T1 (New UTXO Set)"]
        N1["UTXO C: 6 BTC to Bob"]
        N2["UTXO D: 1.9 BTC to Alice"]
    end

    U1 --> T1
    U2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> N1
    T1 --> T3
    T3 --> N2
    T1 --> T4

    style U1 fill:#312e81,stroke:#818cf8,color:#ffffff
    style U2 fill:#312e81,stroke:#818cf8,color:#ffffff
    style N1 fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff
    style N2 fill:#064e3b,stroke:#34d399,color:#ffffff
    style T1 fill:#1e293b,stroke:#94a3b8,color:#ffffff
    style T4 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 ECDSA Verification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Signature r,s] --> B[Compute w = s^-1 mod n]
    C[Message Hash z] --> D
    PK[Public Key Pk] --> E
    B --> D[Compute u1 = z*w mod n]
    B --> F[Compute u2 = r*w mod n]
    D --> G[Compute u1*G]
    F --> E[Compute u2*Pk]
    G --> H[Add points X = u1*G + u2*Pk]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Extract x-coordinate x_X]
    I --> J[Compute v = x_X mod n]
    J --> K{v equals r?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Signature VALID]
    K -- No --> M[Signature REJECTED]

    style L fill:#065f46,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    style PK fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Validation Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Node as Full Node
    participant Mem as Mempool
    participant UTXO as UTXO Database
    participant SCR as Script Engine
    participant Con as Consensus Rules

    Node->>Mem: Receive raw tx
    Mem->>Mem: Check syntax, size, standardness
    Mem-->>Node: Accept to mempool
    Note over Node,Con: Mining/Validation Phase
    Node->>UTXO: Lookup each prev_txid
    UTXO-->>Node: UTXO data + scriptPubKey
    Node->>SCR: Execute scriptSig || scriptPubKey
    SCR-->>Node: TRUE / FALSE
    Node->>Con: Check sum(inputs) >= sum(outputs)
    Con-->>Node: Fee = difference
    Node->>UTXO: Update set (remove spent, add new)
    Node-->>Node: Block accepted
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define transaction validation in a blockchain network. List the four primary checks performed during validation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Transaction validation is the deterministic verification process executed by every full node in a blockchain network to confirm that a proposed transaction conforms to the protocol's consensus rules before being included in a block.

**Four primary checks (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Syntactic correctness** — Transaction bytes conform to the protocol's data structure.
2. **Input/UTXO validity** — Each input references an existing, unspent transaction output.
3. **Script/signature verification** — The unlocking script (e.g., ECDSA signature) satisfies the locking script.
4. **Conservation of value** — `Σ(inputs) ≥ Σ(outputs)`, ensuring no counterfeiting.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the UTXO model and the Account model of transaction validation (any 3 points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per point):**

| # | UTXO Model (Bitcoin) | Account Model (Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | State is a **set of unspent outputs** | State is a **balance map** of accounts |
| 2 | **Stateless** — each tx is independently verified | **Stateful** — depends on global state & nonce |
| 3 | Double-spend prevented by **UTXO existence check** | Double-spend prevented by **sequential account nonce** |
| 4 | Fee = `Σin − Σout` | Fee = `gasUsed × gasPrice` |
| 5 | Limited scripting (non-Turing-complete) | Turing-complete EVM execution |

*(Any 3 points = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When answering transaction validation questions, students frequently **forget to mention the `locktime` and `nSequence` field checks** in the validation pipeline, and they often confuse the **mempool-level policy** checks (non-consensus) with **consensus-level** checks. A 14-mark question that asks for the "validation pipeline" requires you to distinguish these two layers clearly — failing to do so can cost **2–3 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **five-stage transaction validation pipeline** used in a UTXO-based blockchain. Include the specific data structures checked at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A user constructs a Bitcoin transaction with the following: Input 1 = `3.0 BTC`, Input 2 = `2.5 BTC`, Output 1 = `4.0 BTC` to merchant, Output 2 = `1.4 BTC` to self (change). Compute the miner's fee. Explain what happens if Output 1 is modified to `4.2 BTC`.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Five-Stage Validation Pipeline [7 marks: 1 per stage + 2 for synthesis]:**

1. **Stage 1 — Syntax & Structural Validation [1 mark]:** The raw transaction bytes are parsed to verify the protocol `version` field, the `input count` and `output count` varints are well-formed, and all field lengths match their declared sizes. Malformed transactions are immediately dropped and the peer may be banned.

2. **Stage 2 — Input Existence Check [1 mark]:** Each input's `prev_txid` is looked up in the **UTXO database** (a LevelDB/IndexedDB-backed cache on every full node). If a referenced transaction ID does not exist in the chain, the transaction is marked an **orphan** and held until the parent appears.

3. **Stage 3 — Unspent Status Check [1 mark]:** Each referenced output's "spent" flag is inspected. If the output is already consumed (i.e., appears as an input of any transaction in the current main chain or in the candidate block), the new transaction is rejected as a **double-spend**.

4. **Stage 4 — Script Verification [2 marks]:** For each input, the node concatenates the `scriptSig` (unlocking script from new tx) with the `scriptPubKey` (locking script from the referenced UTXO) and executes them on the **Bitcoin Script engine**. Execution must terminate with `TRUE` on the stack top. This verifies the **ECDSA signature** against the provided public key and message hash.

5. **Stage 5 — Conservation & Fee Check [1 mark]:** The arithmetic check `Σ(input values) ≥ Σ(output values)` is enforced. The difference is the implicit transaction fee awarded to the miner. Outputs that exceed inputs trigger a "counterfeit value" rejection.

6. **Synthesis [1 mark]:** Only after **all five stages** pass sequentially is the transaction deemed valid, added to the candidate block, the UTXO set updated (spent outputs removed, new outputs added), and propagated.

---

**Part (b) — Fee Calculation [7 marks: 3 for calculation + 4 for analysis]:**

**Step 1: List given values [1 mark]**
- Input 1: `v_in1 = 3.0 BTC`
- Input 2: `v_in2 = 2.5 BTC`
- Output 1: `v_out1 = 4.0 BTC` (to merchant)
- Output 2: `v_out2 = 1.4 BTC` (change)

**Step 2: Apply the conservation formula [2 marks]**

$$f = \sum v_{\text{in}} - \sum v_{\text{out}} = (3.0 + 2.5) - (4.0 + 1.4) = 5.5 - 5.4$$

$$\boxed{f = 0.1 \; \text{BTC}}$$

**Step 3: Analysis of the modified scenario [2 marks]**

If Output 1 is changed to `4.2 BTC`, then:

$$f' = 5.5 - (4.2 + 1.4) = 5.5 - 5.6 = -0.1 \; \text{BTC}$$

**Step 4: Consequence [2 marks]**

A negative fee of `−0.1 BTC` is **impossible** because it would mean the transaction is *creating* `0.1 BTC` from nothing — a violation of the conservation law. The validation pipeline will **reject the transaction at Stage 5** with the error "counterfeit value" or "transaction fee is negative." No miner will include it in any block, and the network will discard it.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Distribution for 14-mark Q:** Expect roughly 7 marks for the descriptive part and 7 for the numerical/analytical part, with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying equal weight.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **ECDSA signature verification algorithm** used in validating transaction signatures. State the curve parameters used in Bitcoin.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **mempool**? Explain its role in transaction propagation. Differentiate between **policy-level** and **consensus-level** validation with examples.

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — ECDSA Signature Verification [7 marks: 5 for steps + 2 for diagram/parameters]**

**Step 1: Algorithm Inputs [1 mark]**
- Signature pair `(r, s)` — both 256-bit integers
- Message hash `z = H(m)` (a 256-bit SHA-256 output)
- Public key `P_K` (a point on the secp256k1 curve)

**Step 2: Modular Inverse [1 mark]**

$$w = s^{-1} \bmod n$$

where `n` is the order of the generator point `G`.

**Step 3: Scalar Computation [1 mark]**

$$u_1 = z \cdot w \bmod n, \quad u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod n$$

**Step 4: Elliptic Curve Point Operation [1 mark]**

$$X = u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot P_K$$

**Step 5: Final Check [1 mark]**

If the x-coordinate of `X` (modulo `n`) equals `r`, the signature is valid.

$$v = x_X \bmod n \stackrel{?}{=} r$$

**Curve parameters (Bitcoin) [1 mark]:** Curve is `secp256k1`: defined by `y² = x³ + 7` over the prime field `𝔽_p` where `p = 2²⁵⁶ − 2³² − 977`. The generator order is `n ≈ 2²⁵⁶`. Public key compression uses the `0x02/0x03` prefix.

**Block diagram (textual, 1 mark):** [Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3]

---

**Part (b) — Mempool & Validation Layers [7 marks]**

**Mempool Definition [1 mark]:** The mempool (memory pool) is the **in-memory holding area** of a full node where valid-but-unconfirmed transactions are stored while awaiting inclusion in a block. It is a **policy-level** construct, not part of consensus.

**Role in propagation [2 marks]:**
- Acts as a **buffer** between transaction broadcast and block confirmation.
- Enables **transaction fee market** — miners prioritize high-fee transactions from their mempool.
- Facilitates **gossip protocol** propagation — nodes relay valid tx to peers (typically 8–12 connections).

**Policy-level vs Consensus-level [4 marks: 2 each]:**

| Aspect | Policy-Level Validation | Consensus-Level Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **What** | Node-specific rules | Network-wide mandatory rules |
| **Standard tx types** | Only "standard" scripts accepted (P2PKH, P2SH, P2WPKH, P2TR) | Any valid script is accepted |
| **Dust limit** | Reject outputs below dust threshold (e.g., **546 sats**) | No dust limit — any output size valid |
| **Minimum relay fee** | e.g., **1 sat/vB** in Bitcoin Core | No minimum — a 0-fee tx is valid consensus-wise |
| **Mempool size** | Node-configurable (default **300 MB**) | Not applicable |
| **Consequence of violation** | Tx held in "non-standard pool" or dropped | Tx **invalid**; rejected by every node |
| **Example** | A non-standard `OP_RETURN` of 1000 bytes is dropped by policy | A transaction double-spending an input is rejected by consensus |

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Transaction Validation**

- ✅ **Validation = local, deterministic check** by every full node. **Consensus = network-wide agreement** on block order. Do not confuse the two.
- ✅ The **five-stage pipeline**: Syntax → Input Existence → Unspent Status → Script Verification → Conservation & Fee.
- ✅ **Conservation Law:** `Σ(inputs) ≥ Σ(outputs)`. The difference is the **fee**. Strict `=` is **WRONG**.
- ✅ **Bitcoin uses secp256k1**; **ECDSA** is the signature scheme; **SHA-256(SHA-256(x))** is the double-hash for `txid`.
- ✅ **UTXO model** is stateless; **Account model** uses a `nonce` per account.
- ✅ **Coinbase maturity:** 100 blocks in Bitcoin before a mined reward can be spent.
- ✅ **Mempool** is policy-level; only standard transactions are relayed.
- ✅ **Sighash flags** determine what parts of a tx are signed: `ALL (0x01)`, `NONE (0x02)`, `SINGLE (0x03)`, plus the `ANYONECANPAY (0x80)` modifier.
- ✅ **Bitcoin block size:** 1 MB legacy / **4,000,000 weight units** SegWit. **Ethereum block gas limit:** ~**30,000,000** gas.
- ✅ **Double-spend prevention:** UTXO already in set (UTXO model) OR nonce mismatch (Account model).
- ✅ **ECDSA verification equation to memorize:** `s·G = R + H(z)·P_K` and the check `v = x_X mod n ?= r`.
- ✅ **EIP-1559 fee formula:** `effective_fee = priority_fee + min(max_fee − base_fee, priority_fee)`.
- ✅ Always mention **both layers** (policy + consensus) in 14-mark answers for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bitcoin Mining - Purpose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin Mining - Purpose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bitcoin Mining** is the decentralized, computationally intensive process by which network participants (called **miners**) validate pending transactions, bundle them into candidate blocks, solve a cryptographic puzzle (Proof-of-Work), and append new blocks to the Bitcoin blockchain. In doing so, miners collectively maintain the **distributed consensus** of the network, prevent **double-spending**, secure the ledger through economic cost, and are rewarded with newly minted bitcoins and transaction fees.

In formal cryptographic terms, mining is the repeated application of the **SHA-256** hash function on a block's header (with varying nonces) until a hash value is found that lies numerically below a dynamically adjusted **target threshold** — a probabilistic search governed by the **difficulty parameter**.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Locked In

| Term | Meaning (Concise) |
|---|---|
| **Miner** | A node that competes to solve the PoW puzzle |
| **Nonce** | A 32-bit field varied to produce different hash outputs |
| **Target** | A 256-bit number; valid hash must be below it |
| **Difficulty** | Inverse measure of target — higher difficulty = smaller target |
| **Block Reward** | New BTC issued to the miner who finds a valid block |
| **Coinbase Tx** | The special first transaction that creates new BTC |
| **Halving** | Scheduled 50% reduction of block reward every 210,000 blocks |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Digital Gold Rush

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Global Lottery Safe-Cracker"**
>
> Imagine thousands of people simultaneously trying to crack a giant safe. Each guess requires real electricity and effort. The first person to crack it gets a fixed gold prize, the cracked safe reveals a list of legitimate transactions, and everyone immediately agrees on the contents because cracking the safe was so hard that no one could fake it. The next day, the safe manufacturer makes the lock slightly harder to reflect how fast the crowd is cracking them. That is **Bitcoin mining in plain English**.

## 1.4 The Fourfold Purpose of Bitcoin Mining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board expects you to enumerate exactly these FOUR purposes:**
>
> 1. **Issuance of new bitcoins (Monetary Policy)** — controlled supply via block reward
> 2. **Transaction Validation** — verifies legitimacy and prevents double-spending
> 3. **Decentralized Consensus** — agrees on a single global ledger without a central authority
> 4. **Network Security** — makes attacking the chain economically infeasible

Every purpose maps to an engineering function: issuance implements **monetary policy**, validation implements **state transition**, consensus implements the **Nakamoto protocol**, and security implements **Sybil resistance via Proof-of-Work**.

## 1.5 Where Bitcoin Mining Fits in the Protocol Stack

Mining is the **engine** of the Bitcoin protocol. It bridges three layers:

- **Application Layer** → User transactions
- **Consensus Layer** → Mining + PoW (where this topic lives)
- **Network Layer** → Block propagation (Gossip protocol)

The **average block time** is targeted at **10 minutes**, and the **total BTC cap** is hard-capped at **21 million coins** — both enforced solely by mining mathematics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mining Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The full mining operation can be decomposed into the following deterministic sequence:

1. **Transaction Pool Aggregation** — The miner collects unconfirmed transactions from its mempool.
2. **Coinbase Transaction Construction** — A special transaction is prepended that mints new BTC into the miner's wallet (this is the only source of new supply).
3. **Merkle Root Computation** — All transactions are pairwise hashed into a Merkle tree; the root summarizes the entire transaction set.
4. **Block Header Assembly** — The header binds: `version`, `previous block hash`, `Merkle root`, `timestamp`, `target (bits)`, and `nonce`.
5. **Proof-of-Work Search** — The miner iterates over `nonce` (and extra-nonce) until `SHA256(SHA256(header)) < target`.
6. **Block Broadcast** — Once found, the block is gossiped to peers; the network accepts it if valid.
7. **Reward Credit** — After 100 confirmations (≈ ~16.6 hours), the coinbase output becomes spendable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why double SHA-256?** Bitcoin uses `H(x) = SHA256(SHA256(x))` — called **HASH256** — to mitigate potential length-extension attacks on raw SHA-256 and to align with the historical design of Satoshi's reference client.

## 2.2 The Cryptographic Heart — Hash Function Properties Required

For mining to be secure, the underlying hash function **H** must satisfy:

- **Pre-image resistance** — Given `h`, finding `x` such that `H(x)=h` is infeasible.
- **Second pre-image resistance** — Given `x1`, finding `x2` so `H(x1)=H(x2)` is infeasible.
- **Collision resistance** — Finding any `x1, x2` with `H(x1)=H(x2)` is infeasible.
- **Puzzle friendliness** — No shortcut to finding a hash below the target other than brute force.

Bitcoin uses **SHA-256**, standardized by **NIST FIPS 180-4**, with a **256-bit digest length** and a deterministic output space of size $2^{256}$.

## 2.3 Target, Difficulty, and Probability

The mining search is a **Bernoulli trial**: each hash attempt is an independent trial with success probability $p$ given by:

$$p = \frac{\text{target}}{2^{256}}$$

If the global hash rate (total attempts per second across all miners) is $H$ hashes/sec, then the expected time to find a block is:

$$E[T] = \frac{1}{H \cdot p} = \frac{2^{256}}{H \cdot \text{target}}$$

Bitcoin retargets the difficulty every **2016 blocks** (≈ **2 weeks** at 10-min blocks) to maintain an average block interval of **600 seconds**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 ESE — memorize it.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Target from Difficulty | $\text{target} = \dfrac{\text{target}_{\max}}{\text{difficulty}}$ | $\text{target}_{\max} = 2^{224}$ (initial genesis difficulty = 1) |
| Hash success probability | $p = \dfrac{\text{target}}{2^{256}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Expected time to find block | $E[T] = \dfrac{2^{256}}{H \cdot \text{target}}$ | Seconds |
| Actual time for 2016 blocks | $t_{\text{actual}} = 2016 \cdot t_{\text{actual\_per\_block}}$ | Seconds |
| New difficulty | $\text{difficulty}_{\text{new}} = \text{difficulty}_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{2016 \cdot 600}$ | Unitless |
| Block reward (era $n$) | $R_n = 50 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | BTC; $h$ = block height |
| Total supply cap | $S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 21{,}000{,}000$ | BTC |
| Miner's break-even revenue | $\text{Revenue} = R_n + \sum \text{fees}_i$ | BTC per block |
| Network hash rate from difficulty | $H \approx \text{difficulty} \cdot 2^{32} / 600$ | Hashes/sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality check for KTU:** The expected total bitcoin supply converges to **21,000,000 BTC** — never exceeding it — because the geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$ converges to exactly that figure. The last bitcoin is expected to be mined around the year **2140**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bitcoin mining, beyond its monetary function, has driven innovation in:

- **Specialized hardware design** (ASICs — Application-Specific Integrated Circuits)
- **Energy markets and grid balancing** (curtailed renewable energy usage)
- **Cryptographic security research** (incentivized auditing of hash functions)
- **Decentralized consensus algorithms** (PoW, PoS, PoH — all descend from Nakamoto's mining insight)
- **Digital forensics & timestamping** (proof-of-existence services)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Bitcoin mining is the first deployed system that turns **electricity into unforgeable digital scarcity** — a foundational concept for modern distributed systems engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Bitcoin Supply Cap = 21,000,000 BTC

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Relevance:** This is a classic 7-mark derivation question in KTU board exams. The steps below are precisely what the examiner expects.

**Step 1 — Identify the reward schedule.**

Every 210,000 blocks, the block reward is halved. The block reward at era $n$ is:

$$R_n = 50 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n \quad \text{(BTC per block)}$$

**Step 2 — Count blocks per era.**

Each era contains exactly **210,000 blocks** (since 210,000 × 10 min ≈ 4 years).

**Step 3 — Sum total BTC emitted across all eras.**

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

**Step 4 — Factor the constants out of the sum.**

$$S_{\text{total}} = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the infinite geometric series.**

For a geometric series with first term $a=1$ and common ratio $r = 1/2$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 1/2} = 2$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back.**

$$S_{\text{total}} = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2$$

$$S_{\text{total}} = 210000 \cdot 100$$

$$\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Bitcoin protocol enforces a hard cap of 21 million coins. After the final halving era, miners will rely solely on **transaction fees** for revenue.

## 3.2 Derivation: Difficulty Retargeting Formula

**Step 1 — Define actual elapsed time for the last 2016 blocks.**

Let $t_i$ be the actual time (in seconds) taken to mine the $i$-th block in the previous epoch of 2016 blocks.

$$T_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{2016} t_i$$

**Step 2 — Define the ideal elapsed time.**

The protocol target is 600 seconds per block:

$$T_{\text{ideal}} = 2016 \cdot 600 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ seconds} \quad (\text{exactly } 2 \text{ weeks})$$

**Step 3 — Relate old and new target.**

The new target must scale inversely with the time ratio. If blocks came faster than ideal, the new target must be smaller (harder).

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to difficulty.**

Since $\text{difficulty} = T_{\max} / \text{target}$:

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting values.**

$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{1{,}209{,}600}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 4× clamp rule.**

> [!NOTE]
> Bitcoin Core enforces a safety clamp: the difficulty cannot change by more than a factor of **4** (up or down) in a single retargeting period, to prevent instability from sudden hash-rate swings.

$$D_{\text{new}} = \min\!\left(4 \cdot D_{\text{old}},\ \max\!\left(\tfrac{1}{4} D_{\text{old}},\ D_{\text{old}} \cdot \tfrac{1{,}209{,}600}{T_{\text{actual}}}\right)\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Expected Time to Find a Block

**Step 1 — Each hash attempt is a Bernoulli trial.**

Probability of success per attempt:

$$p = \frac{\text{target}}{2^{256}}$$

**Step 2 — Probability of no success after $n$ attempts.**

$$P(\text{no success in } n) = (1-p)^n$$

**Step 3 — Probability of success on attempt $n$.**

$$P(\text{success on attempt } n) = (1-p)^{n-1} \cdot p$$

**Step 4 — Expected value of the success attempt number.**

For a geometric distribution:

$$E[N] = \frac{1}{p}$$

**Step 5 — Convert attempts to time using hash rate $H$ (hashes/sec).**

$$E[T] = \frac{E[N]}{H} = \frac{1}{H \cdot p} = \frac{2^{256}}{H \cdot \text{target}}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**

At network hash rate $H \approx 6 \times 10^{19}$ H/s (≈ 600 EH/s) and difficulty $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{13}$:

$$E[T] \approx \frac{2^{256}}{6 \times 10^{19} \cdot \frac{2^{224}}{10^{13}}} \approx 600 \text{ seconds} \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 4 numerical patterns. They recur in KTU ESE and internals.**

**Pattern 1 — Reward Halving Schedule**
Compute block reward at height $h$:
- Halvings occur every 210,000 blocks.
- Era number: $n = \lfloor h / 210000 \rfloor$.
- Reward: $R_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$ BTC (until 0).

**Pattern 2 — Probability of Mining a Block (Solo Miner)**
$$P(\text{find block in time } \tau) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \tau}, \quad \lambda = H_{\text{miner}} \cdot p$$

**Pattern 3 — Mining Pool Expected Share**
If a pool has hash rate $H_p$ and the network has $H_{\text{net}}$:
$$P(\text{pool finds next block}) = \frac{H_p}{H_{\text{net}}}$$

**Pattern 4 — Energy Cost vs. Reward Breakeven**
$$\text{Profit} = (R_n + \text{Fees}) \cdot P_{\text{BTC}} - (H_{\text{miner}} \cdot \text{J/hash} \cdot t) \cdot P_{\text{kWh}}$$

where $J$ is joules per hash, $P_{\text{kWh}}$ is electricity price, $P_{\text{BTC}}$ is BTC price.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that the coinbase transaction has a **100-confirmation maturity period**. Spending immature coinbase outputs is invalid and will be rejected by consensus — this costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bitcoin Mining Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mempool: Unconfirmed Transactions] --> B[Transaction Aggregator]
    B --> C[Coinbase Tx Constructor]
    C --> D[Merkle Tree Builder]
    D --> E[Block Header Assembler]
    E --> F[Proof of Work Search Loop]
    F --> G{Hash less than Target?}
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H[Valid Block Found]
    H --> I[Gossip Propagation to Peers]
    I --> J[Network Validation]
    J --> K[Block Appended to Chain]
    K --> L[Block Reward Credited]
    L --> M[100 Confirmation Maturity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The miner continuously loops over `nonce` in the *Proof of Work Search Loop*. On success, the block is propagated, validated by every full node, and added to each node's copy of the blockchain. The reward matures after 100 confirmations.

## 4.2 Block Header Structure (80 Bytes)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Block Header - 80 bytes] --> A1[Version 4 bytes]
    H --> A2[Previous Block Hash 32 bytes]
    H --> A3[Merkle Root 32 bytes]
    H --> A4[Timestamp 4 bytes]
    H --> A5[Difficulty Target Bits 4 bytes]
    H --> A6[Nonce 4 bytes]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Only the **header** (80 bytes) is hashed in PoW. The miner's search varies the **nonce** and an **extra-nonce** inside the coinbase transaction (which alters the Merkle root) to keep searching after the 4-byte nonce space is exhausted.

## 4.3 The Bitcoin Issuance & Halving Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0[Era 0: 2009 - 2012 / Reward 50 BTC] --> E1[Era 1: 2012 - 2016 / Reward 25 BTC]
    E1 --> E2[Era 2: 2016 - 2020 / Reward 12.5 BTC]
    E2 --> E3[Era 3: 2020 - 2024 / Reward 6.25 BTC]
    E3 --> E4[Era 4: 2024 - 2028 / Reward 3.125 BTC]
    E4 --> E5[Era 5: 2028 onwards / Reward 1.5625 BTC]
    E5 --> EN[... converges to 21M cap by 2140]
```

## 4.4 The Four Purposes of Mining — Functional View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Bitcoin Mining] --> P1[Purpose 1: New BTC Issuance]
    P --> P2[Purpose 2: Transaction Validation]
    P --> P3[Purpose 3: Decentralized Consensus]
    P --> P4[Purpose 4: Network Security]
    P1 --> R1[Coinbase Tx + Block Reward]
    P2 --> R2[Mempool Verification + UTXO Check]
    P3 --> R3[Nakamoto Consensus + Longest Chain Rule]
    P4 --> R4[Sybil Resistance via Proof of Work]
```

## 4.5 Mining Ecosystem Stakeholders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Users] -->|Submit Tx| M[Miners]
    M -->|Include in Block| N[Full Nodes]
    N -->|Validate| BC[Blockchain Ledger]
    BC -->|Confirm| U
    M -->|Pay Fees| M
    M -->|Earn Reward| M
    H[Hardware Vendors] -->|ASICs| M
    P[Pool Operators] -->|Coordinate| M
    M -->|Share Rewards| P
    P -->|Distribute| M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering view:** Mining is a *closed-loop economic system* balancing four forces — **issuance, fees, hardware cost, and electricity cost**. Equilibrium is reached when marginal miner revenue equals marginal mining cost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`**
*List and briefly explain the four main purposes of Bitcoin mining.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four main purposes of Bitcoin mining are:
1. **Issuance of new bitcoins** — mining is the only mechanism that creates new BTC, enforcing the protocol's fixed monetary policy.
2. **Transaction validation** — miners verify each transaction's signature, UTXO status, and double-spend safety before inclusion in a block.
3. **Decentralized consensus** — through Proof-of-Work, miners collectively agree on a single global ordering of transactions without a trusted central authority.
4. **Network security** — the high computational cost of mining makes Sybil attacks and double-spend attacks economically infeasible. *(3 marks — 0.75 per point)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`**
*What is the role of the nonce in Bitcoin mining? Why is SHA-256 applied twice?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **nonce** is a 32-bit field in the block header that miners vary to produce different hash outputs while searching for a value below the target. Since SHA-256 is deterministic, varying the nonce is the only way to explore the hash space for a solution. **Double SHA-256** (HASH256) is used to mitigate potential length-extension attacks on raw SHA-256, providing an additional safety layer, and to preserve backward compatibility with Satoshi's original reference implementation. *(3 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 split)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with sub-parts typically `(a) 7 marks` and `(b) 7 marks`. Internal choice is mandatory — students must attempt **only one** option per question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four main purposes of Bitcoin mining. How does each purpose contribute to the overall functioning of the Bitcoin network? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Bitcoin block is mined with a difficulty of $D = 8 \times 10^{12}$. The network hash rate is $H = 4 \times 10^{19}$ H/s. Compute the expected time to find the next block. Also calculate the block reward if the block height is $h = 700{,}000$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The four purposes are:
- **Purpose 1: New Bitcoin Issuance** — Each valid block contains a coinbase transaction that creates new BTC. This is the only source of new supply and implements a predictable, disinflationary monetary policy.
- **Purpose 2: Transaction Validation** — Miners verify cryptographic signatures, confirm UTXO availability, and reject double-spends. Validated transactions are bundled into blocks, achieving a confirmed state transition.
- **Purpose 3: Decentralized Consensus (Nakamoto Consensus)** — By accepting the chain with the most cumulative Proof-of-Work, all honest nodes converge on a single transaction history without coordination.
- **Purpose 4: Network Security** — PoW imposes a real-world economic cost on block production, making it prohibitively expensive for an attacker to rewrite history (51% attack cost scales with global hash rate).

*Each purpose explained in 1.5 marks × 4 = 6 marks, with 1 mark for synthesizing contribution to network function.*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute target from difficulty.**

$$\text{target} = \frac{\text{target}_{\max}}{\text{difficulty}} = \frac{2^{224}}{8 \times 10^{12}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the expected time formula.**

$$E[T] = \frac{2^{256}}{H \cdot \text{target}} = \frac{2^{256}}{4 \times 10^{19}} \cdot \frac{8 \times 10^{12}}{2^{224}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$E[T] = \frac{2^{32} \cdot 8 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^{19}} = \frac{4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \cdot 8 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^{19}}$$

$$E[T] = \frac{3.436 \times 10^{22}}{4 \times 10^{19}} \approx 858 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the block reward at height 700,000.**

Era number: $n = \lfloor 700000 / 210000 \rfloor = \lfloor 3.33 \rfloor = 3$

$$R_3 = 50 \cdot 2^{-3} = \frac{50}{8} = 6.25 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

The expected block time is approximately **858 seconds (~14.3 minutes)**, and the block reward at height 700,000 is **6.25 BTC**. *(1 mark for units, 1 mark for conclusion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question, 2024 Scheme Aligned]`**

**(a)** Describe the Bitcoin mining process in detail. Include in your answer: (i) block header construction, (ii) Proof-of-Work mechanism, and (iii) the role of the Merkle root. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the total maximum supply of Bitcoin, showing that it converges to 21,000,000 BTC. What happens to miner revenue after the final halving? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Block Header Construction (2.5 marks):** The 80-byte block header contains six fields: version (4B), previous block hash (32B), Merkle root (32B), timestamp (4B), difficulty bits (4B), and nonce (4B). These are concatenated and double-hashed in PoW. The previous hash chains the block to its parent, while the Merkle root commits to all transactions.

**(ii) Proof-of-Work Mechanism (2.5 marks):** Miners compute `SHA256(SHA256(header))` repeatedly, varying the nonce (and the extra-nonce inside the coinbase) until the resulting digest is numerically less than the **target**. This is a brute-force probabilistic search with success probability `p = target / 2^256`. The first miner to find a valid hash broadcasts the block and receives the reward.

**(iii) Role of the Merkle Root (2 marks):** The Merkle root is a single 32-byte hash that uniquely commits to the entire set of transactions in the block. It enables **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — light clients can prove a transaction's inclusion via a Merkle path without downloading all transactions. Changing any transaction alters the root, breaking PoW.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric structure of the halving schedule.**

$$S = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

**Step 2 — Factor constants.**

$$S = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the infinite geometric series.**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 1/2} = 2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and compute.**

$$S = 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 5 — Discussion of post-final-halving revenue (2 marks).**

After the final halving (around year 2140), the coinbase reward becomes zero. Miners will then earn revenue **exclusively from transaction fees**. This shifts the security model from *subsidy-based* to *fee-based* — a known long-term concern studied in the literature as the **"security budget problem"**. For the network to remain secure, transaction volume must be sufficient to compensate miners via fees alone.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing halving era and block height** — students often use the wrong era index $n$. Always compute $n = \lfloor h/210000 \rfloor$ first.
> 2. **Forgetting the `2^256` in expected time derivation** — writing $E[T] = 1/(H \cdot p)$ is incomplete without substituting $p = \text{target}/2^{256}$.
> 3. **Writing "miners get new BTC for solving math"** — this loses 2 marks immediately. The proper phrasing is: *miners get new BTC for performing computationally verifiable work that secures the network*.
> 4. **Mixing up `target` and `difficulty`** — they are inverses. Difficulty 1 corresponds to maximum target $2^{224}$.
> 5. **Omitting the maturity period (100 confirmations)** for coinbase outputs in any revenue calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Bitcoin mining** is the process of validating transactions, securing the network, issuing new BTC, and achieving consensus via Proof-of-Work.
- The **four purposes** are: *(i) issuance, (ii) validation, (iii) consensus, (iv) security* — enumerate them in this exact order.
- The **block header** is 80 bytes: `version, prev_hash, merkle_root, timestamp, bits, nonce`.
- PoW uses **double SHA-256** (`HASH256`) on the header until the result is below the **target**.
- **Difficulty retargets** every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) with a **4× clamp** per period.
- Average block time: **10 minutes** (600 seconds).
- **Block reward halves** every **210,000 blocks**; starting reward was **50 BTC**.
- **Total supply cap = 21,000,000 BTC** — derived via the geometric series $\sum 50 \cdot 2^{-n} \cdot 210000$.
- The **coinbase transaction** is the only source of new BTC; it matures after **100 confirmations**.
- **Success probability per hash**: $p = \text{target} / 2^{256}$.
- **Expected time to find block**: $E[T] = 2^{256} / (H \cdot \text{target})$.
- **After 2140**, miners will earn only **transaction fees** — the "security budget problem."
- Mining is a **Bernoulli process / geometric distribution**; each hash is independent.
- **SHA-256** properties required: pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance, puzzle friendliness.
- The **Genesis block** had difficulty = 1 and reward = 50 BTC.
- **Double spending prevention** is achieved by requiring economic cost (PoW) to produce blocks.
- **Nakamoto Consensus** = longest-chain rule + cumulative Proof-of-Work + honest-majority assumption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consensus Algorithm (KTU Standard Definition):**
> A *consensus algorithm* is a fault-tolerant distributed-computing protocol that enables a network of mutually untrusted, geographically dispersed nodes to collectively agree on a single, canonical state of the distributed ledger (blockchain) and on the validity and ordering of transactions, even in the presence of malicious or faulty participants.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCCGT601** syllabus, Module 4 treats the topic *"Algorithm"* as the family of **Distributed Consensus Protocols** that govern how trustless, peer-to-peer blockchain networks reach agreement. These algorithms solve two fundamentally hard problems simultaneously:

1. **Byzantine Generals Problem** — tolerating arbitrary, malicious, or irrational behaviour from network participants.
2. **Distributed State Synchronization** — ensuring every honest node converges to the same ledger state despite asynchronous network delays, message loss, and node churn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology You Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**
> - **Consensus** — collective agreement on ledger state.
> - **Byzantine Fault** — any arbitrary, malicious, or arbitrary failure of a node.
> - **Crash Fault** — node simply stops responding (subset of Byzantine).
> - **Sybil Attack** — adversary spawns many fake identities.
> - **51% Attack** — adversary controls majority of network resources.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **village council of 100 people** trying to agree on a single shared ledger book of transactions. The challenge?

- 📮 Everyone communicates only by passing handwritten letters (no central authority).
- 🦹 Some villagers are **thieves** (Byzantine actors) who will actively lie, forge, or double-spend.
- ✉️ Some letters get delayed, lost, or duplicated.

How does the village agree, in a fair, tamper-proof manner, that *"Alice sent 10 coins to Bob"* is the only valid entry in the ledger at 10:00 AM today?

| Real World Analogy | Blockchain Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A village lottery to pick the recorder | **Proof of Work (PoW) — mining** |
| A village where wealthy elders vote on records | **Proof of Stake (PoS)** |
| A pre-selected trusted jury of 21 elders | **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** |
| Military generals needing to agree despite traitors | **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** |
| Only a few known, verified notaries sign records | **Proof of Authority (PoA)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** *"Consensus algorithm = the rules of the game that decide who gets to write the next page of the blockchain's history book, and how the rest of the players verify it is truthful."*

---

## 1.3 Why Consensus Algorithms Are Mandatory in Blockchain

Traditional centralized systems (banks, databases) avoid the consensus problem because they have a **single trusted server** as the source of truth. Blockchain **removes** this central server, so consensus algorithms are the *replacement* for institutional trust. They provide:

- **Decentralization** — no single point of control.
- **Trustlessness** — strangers can transact without knowing each other.
- **Immutability** — once agreed upon, records cannot be retroactively altered.
- **Censorship resistance** — no authority can block a valid transaction.
- **Double-spend prevention** — same coin cannot be spent twice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies the Course Outcome *CO4 — Analyze consensus protocols for distributed ledger technologies* under Module 4 of PCCGT601.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Consensus Algorithms (Foundation for the Rest of the Notes)

Consensus algorithms fall into two broad engineering families:

### A. Proof-Based / Probabilistic Consensus
- **Proof of Work (PoW)** — Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin, Ethereum (legacy).
- **Proof of Stake (PoS)** — Ethereum 2.0, Cardano (Ouroboros), Polkadot (NPoS).
- **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** — EOS, Tron, BitShares.
- **Proof of Burn (PoB)** — Slimcoin.
- **Proof of Capacity / Space** — Burstcoin, Chia (uses plots).
- **Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET)** — Hyperledger Sawtooth (Intel SGX).

### B. Voting-Based / Deterministic (BFT Family)
- **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** — Hyperledger Fabric.
- **Tendermint BFT (BFT-DPoS)** — Cosmos.
- **HotStuff (BFT)** — Facebook Libra/Diem (now Aptos, Sui).
- **Raft / Paxos** — permissioned ledgers (crash-fault tolerant only).
- **Federated BFT (FBFT)** — Stellar.
- **Avalanche consensus** — Avalanche (C-Chain).

> [!NOTE]
> **Permissionless vs Permissioned is the BIG dividing line:**
> - **Permissionless (public)** → must use Sybil-resistant mechanisms (PoW/PoS) because anyone can join.
> - **Permissioned (private/consortium)** → identity is known → can use faster BFT voting (PBFT/Raft).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Proof of Work (PoW) — Operational Decomposition

PoW was first formalized by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor (1993) and adapted by Satoshi Nakamoto for Bitcoin (2008). It is **probabilistic-finality**: a block is *probably* final once buried under enough subsequent blocks.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Transaction Pool (Mempool):** Each full node receives broadcasted, unconfirmed transactions and stores them in its local *mempool*.
2. **Block Construction:** A miner selects a set of valid transactions, structures them into a Merkle tree, and builds a candidate block header.
3. **Hash Puzzle:** The miner repeatedly varies a **nonce** (Number used ONCE) until the SHA-256(SHA-256(header)) produces a hash value numerically *less than* a target threshold **T** set by the network difficulty.
4. **Broadcast:** The first miner to find a valid nonce broadcasts the block to peers.
5. **Verification:** Other nodes trivially verify the solution with a single hash computation — asymmetric cost (hard to find, easy to verify).
6. **Chain Extension:** Valid blocks are appended; the canonical chain is the one with the **most cumulative work** (Longest Chain Rule / Nakamoto Consensus).
7. **Reward:** Successful miner receives the **block subsidy** (new BTC) + **transaction fees**.

### Why Does This Work? The Economic & Cryptographic Backbone

- **Computational cost** imposes a real-world price (electricity, hardware) on block production → Sybil resistance (one identity can't dominate cheaply).
- **Hash functions** (SHA-256) are **pre-image resistant** and **puzzle-friendly** → no shortcut exists to find a low hash.
- **Longest chain rule** → economic incentive: rational miners extend the honest chain to capture rewards rather than waste resources on a doomed fork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden KTU point:** The difficulty **D** is dynamically adjusted every **2016 blocks** in Bitcoin to maintain a target block time of **~10 minutes**, regardless of total network hash power.

---

## 2.2 Proof of Stake (PoS) — Operational Decomposition

PoS replaces computational work with **economic stake** as the Sybil-resistance mechanism. Validators lock (stake) the network's native cryptocurrency as collateral.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Staking Phase:** Validators deposit a minimum stake (e.g., **32 ETH** for Ethereum 2.0) into a staking contract.
2. **Validator Selection:** A pseudo-random algorithm selects a proposer (weighted by stake size, often combined with randomization for fairness).
3. **Block Proposal & Attestation:** Selected validator proposes a block; others attest (vote) on its validity.
4. **Slashing Condition:** If a validator double-signs or violates protocol rules, their staked coins are partially or fully **slashed** (confiscated) as a penalty.
5. **Reward Distribution:** Honest validators earn staking rewards proportional to stake (annualized yield, e.g., **~3–5% APR** on Ethereum).
6. **Finality Gadget (Casper FFG in Ethereum):** After 2/3 supermajority attestation, a block is **justified**, and after a second round, **finalized** (irreversible without ≥1/3 of total stake being slashed).

### Why Does This Work?

- **Skin in the game:** validators stand to lose their stake if they misbehave.
- **Nothing-at-stake problem** is mitigated by **slashing** (in Ethereum's Casper).
- **Energy efficiency:** no mining, no massive electricity consumption.

---

## 2.3 Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)

Designed by **Miguel Castro and Barbara Liskov (1999)**, PBFT is the workhorse of permissioned blockchains like **Hyperledger Fabric**.

### Operational Logic

1. **Pre-Prepare:** Primary (leader) node broadcasts a pre-prepare message with the proposed block.
2. **Prepare Phase:** Replica nodes broadcast prepare messages to all others.
3. **Commit Phase:** Once a node receives **2f+1** matching prepare messages, it broadcasts a commit message.
4. **Reply Phase:** After receiving **2f+1** commit messages, the node executes the request and replies to the client.

### The Core Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PBFT Safety Theorem (CRITICAL KTU formula):**
> PBFT achieves safety (all honest nodes agree on the same sequence of operations) as long as the total number of nodes **n ≥ 3f + 1**, where **f** is the maximum number of Byzantine (malicious) faulty nodes.

| Total Nodes (n) | Max Byzantine (f) | Tolerated Fault Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1 | 25% |
| 7 | 2 | ~28.6% |
| 10 | 3 | 30% |
| 22 | 7 | ~31.8% |
| 100 | 33 | 33% |

> [!WARNING]
> PBFT is **NOT Sybil-resistant** — it requires known identities. That is why it is restricted to permissioned/consortium ledgers.

---

## 2.4 Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)

DPoS elects a **small, fixed set of delegates (witnesses/block producers)** through coin-holder voting. Used by **EOS, Tron, BitShares**.

- Token holders vote using stake weight → top N (e.g., 21 in EOS) become block producers.
- Producers take turns producing blocks in round-robin.
- Misbehaving producers are voted out.

**Trade-off:** Highest throughput, but more centralized (small validator set).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize every row below — these are KTU ESE favourites.**

| Symbol | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ | $T = \dfrac{T_{\text{max}} \cdot \text{time\ of\ 2016\ blocks}}{2016 \cdot 600\text{s}}$ | Bitcoin difficulty retargeting every **2016** blocks (target **10 min** each) |
| $H$ | $H(\text{header}) < T$ | Mining success condition: hash must be less than target |
| $D$ | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{max}}}{T}$ | Difficulty metric — higher D → smaller target → harder puzzle |
| $E_{\text{block}}$ | $E_{\text{block}} = P_{\text{network}} \cdot t_{\text{block}}$ | Energy per block in PoW (Watt × seconds) |
| $P_{\text{reward}}$ | $P_{\text{reward}} = P_{\text{subsidy}} + \sum_{i \in \text{block}} \text{fee}_i$ | Total miner reward per block |
| $n_{\text{req}}$ | $n_{\text{req}} \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum PBFT nodes for **f** Byzantine faults |
| $Q_{\text{BFT}}$ | $Q_{\text{BFT}} = \lfloor \tfrac{n - 1}{3} \rfloor$ | Quorum size in BFT (number of faulty nodes tolerated) |
| $P_{\text{select}}$ | $P_{\text{select}}(i) = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} s_j}$ | PoS selection probability proportional to stake $s_i$ |
| $R_{\text{APR}}$ | $R_{\text{APR}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{total\ per\ year}}}{S_{\text{total\ staked}}}$ | Annualized staking yield (e.g., 4–6% in Ethereum 2.0) |
| $t_{\text{conf}}$ | $t_{\text{conf}} \approx 6 \cdot t_{\text{block}}$ | Confirmation time for ~99.99% finality in Bitcoin (6 blocks × 10 min) |
| $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{H_r}{D \cdot 2^{32}}$ | Expected number of hashes per share/solution in PoW mining |
| $t_{\text{final}}$ | $t_{\text{final}} = 2 \cdot t_{\text{epoch}}$ | Ethereum 2.0 finality in **2 epochs** (each **6.4 min**, so ~**12.8 min**) |
| $\text{TPS}_{\max}$ | $\text{TPS}_{\max} = \dfrac{\text{Block\ size}}{\text{Block\ time} \cdot \text{Avg\ tx\ size}}$ | Theoretical max transactions per second |

> **Constants to remember verbatim:**
> - **Bitcoin block time:** **10 minutes**
> - **Ethereum block time:** **~12 seconds**
> - **Bitcoin difficulty adjustment:** every **2016 blocks**
> - **Bitcoin halving:** every **210,000 blocks** (approx every **4 years**)
> - **Bitcoin max supply:** **21,000,000 BTC**
> - **PBFT fault tolerance:** exactly **1/3** (asymptotic, i.e., $\lfloor(n-1)/3\rfloor$)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bitcoin Mining Difficulty Retargeting (DAA)

### Problem Statement
The Bitcoin protocol must retarget the mining difficulty every 2016 blocks so that the average block interval remains **600 seconds (10 minutes)**, regardless of how much total hash power joins or leaves the network. Derive the new target.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $t_{\text{actual}}$ = total time taken to mine the previous **2016** blocks (seconds).
- $t_{\text{expected}}$ = expected time = $2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ seconds (2 weeks).
- $T_{\text{old}}$ = previous block target (a 256-bit integer).
- $T_{\text{new}}$ = new block target to be calculated.

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{t_{\text{expected}}} = \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}
$$

To prevent abrupt, destabilizing swings, the protocol clamps this ratio to a maximum 4× change per period:

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \min\!\Bigl(\max\!\bigl(\tfrac{t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600},\ \tfrac{1}{4}\bigr),\ 4\Bigr)
$$

The new difficulty $D$ is then defined as inversely proportional to the target:

$$
D = \frac{T_{\text{max}}}{T_{\text{new}}}
$$

**Numerical Example for KTU Board Exam:**

Suppose the previous 2016 blocks took $t_{\text{actual}} = 1{,}814{,}400$ seconds (1.5× longer than expected — meaning miners joined the network and blocks came faster than 10 min, so difficulty must INCREASE and target must DECREASE).

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{1{,}814{,}400}{1{,}209{,}600} = T_{\text{old}} \times 1.5
$$

Wait — that *increases* the target (makes it easier), which is **wrong**. Let us carefully re-examine:

- If blocks came *faster* than expected ($t_{\text{actual}} < t_{\text{expected}}$) → too easy → decrease target → increase difficulty.
- If blocks came *slower* than expected ($t_{\text{actual}} > t_{\text{expected}}$) → too hard → increase target → decrease difficulty.

The correct formula is therefore:

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{t_{\text{expected}}}{t_{\text{actual}}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{1{,}209{,}600}{1{,}814{,}400} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.667 \cdot T_{\text{old}}
$$

So the target is reduced to 2/3 of its previous value, meaning the difficulty **increased by 50%**, restoring the 10-minute block interval. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Many students write the ratio inverted. Always remember: **faster blocks → smaller target → higher difficulty**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Time to Find a Block (PoW)

A miner with hash power $h$ (in H/s) is mining on a network with total hash power $H_{\text{net}}$. The probability that *this specific* miner finds the next block on any hash attempt is:

$$
p = \frac{h}{H_{\text{net}}}
$$

Each hash attempt is a Bernoulli trial. The number of hashes $X$ needed follows a **Geometric distribution**:

$$
P(X = k) = (1 - p)^{k-1} \cdot p, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

The expected number of hashes:

$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{H_{\text{net}}}{h}
$$

The expected time to find a block:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{block}}] = \frac{\mathbb{E}[X]}{\text{hash\ rate}} = \frac{H_{\text{net}} / h}{h} \cdot (\text{time\ per\ hash})
$$

For the *entire network* ($h = H_{\text{net}}$), the expected time per block is the protocol's target — for Bitcoin:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{network}}] = 600 \text{ seconds}
$$

For an *individual* miner controlling a fraction $\alpha = h / H_{\text{net}}$ of network hash power, the expected block-finding time is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{miner}}] = \frac{600}{\alpha} \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** If a miner controls 0.1% ($\alpha = 0.001$) of Bitcoin's network hash power, expected time per block is:
> $$\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{miner}}] = \frac{600}{0.001} = 600{,}000 \text{ seconds} \approx 6.94 \text{ days}$$
> This is why **mining pools** exist — to reduce variance for small miners.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PBFT Byzantine Fault Tolerance Threshold

### Problem
Show that PBFT requires $n \geq 3f + 1$ nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine failures while maintaining **safety** (all honest nodes agree on the same value) and **liveness** (the system keeps making progress).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Setup:**
- Total nodes: $n$
- Byzantine (faulty/malicious): $f$
- Honest (correct): $n - f$

**For Safety — Any two honest nodes must reach the same decision:**

Consider a message broadcast in the Prepare phase. For a node to be certain a value is *locked*, it needs responses from enough nodes that at least one group of honest nodes has confirmed it. The two largest possible groups of *non-overlapping* honest nodes are:

- Group A: $f$ honest + $f$ Byzantine = $2f$ nodes
- Group B: $f$ honest + $f$ Byzantine = $2f$ nodes
- Remaining honest: $n - 3f$ nodes (which must exist for safety)

For these two groups to *not* both be able to give conflicting decisions, the protocol needs a **2f + 1** quorum. The intersection of two such quorums must contain at least one **honest** node (to prevent the Byzantine nodes from splitting the decision).

Mathematically, the intersection of any two quorums of size $2f + 1$ is:

$$
|Q_1 \cap Q_2| = |Q_1| + |Q_2| - |Q_1 \cup Q_2| \geq (2f + 1) + (2f + 1) - n = 4f + 2 - n
$$

For this intersection to contain at least one **honest** node, we need:

$$
4f + 2 - n \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \leq 4f + 1
$$

Wait — that's the wrong direction. Let me reason again from first principles:

**Correct Reasoning (Liveness + Safety):**

- To make progress, a quorum must form of size $\geq 2f + 1$ (this quorum must contain at least $f + 1$ honest nodes since at most $f$ are Byzantine).
- For *safety*, any two quorums (one from an honest primary, one from a Byzantine primary attempting equivocation) must overlap in at least one **honest** node.
- The worst case: Byzantine nodes try to make a quorum look "satisfied" while honest nodes think a different quorum is satisfied. To prevent this, the intersection must contain a non-Byzantine node.
- If two quorums of size $2f + 1$ each exist, their worst-case intersection is:
  - $2f + 1$ + $2f + 1$ - $n$ = $4f + 2 - n$.
  - This must be $\geq 1$ to guarantee a shared node exists.
  - But that *shared* node might be Byzantine. To guarantee at least **one honest** node in the intersection, we need:
  - $4f + 2 - n > f$ (so the intersection has more than $f$ nodes, exceeding all possible Byzantine counts in it).
  - This gives: $n < 3f + 2$, i.e., $n \leq 3f + 1$.

$$
\boxed{n \geq 3f + 1}
$$

**Numerical Verification Table:**

| n | f_max = (n-1)/3 floor | Why this works |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | 1 | 3 honest outvote 1 Byzantine |
| 7 | 2 | 5 honest outvote 2 Byzantine |
| 10 | 3 | 7 honest outvote 3 Byzantine |
| 13 | 4 | 9 honest outvote 4 Byzantine |
| 100 | 33 | 67 honest outvote 33 Byzantine |

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just 2f + 1?**
> Because in the worst case, all $f$ Byzantine nodes could be in *both* quorums. To make sure at least 1 honest node is shared, you need the quorums large enough that $f$ byzantines cannot dominate the intersection. That forces the $3f + 1$ bound.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability of 51% Attack in PoS

In a Proof of Stake system, an attacker must control more than half of the total staked currency. Let:
- $S_{\text{total}}$ = total staked supply
- $S_{\text{attacker}}$ = stake held by attacker
- $p = S_{\text{attacker}} / S_{\text{total}}$ = attacker's fraction of stake

The probability of the attacker being selected as a block proposer in any given slot is simply $p$. The probability of a successful double-spend via a private fork of length $k$ blocks is:

$$
P_{\text{success}} = \left( p \right)^k \quad \text{(if attacker produces all k blocks alone)}
$$

But realistically, the honest network produces blocks in parallel. The attacker's fork wins only if the attacker's chain outpaces the honest chain. With attacker hash power / stake fraction $p$ and honest fraction $q = 1 - p$:

$$
P_{\text{attacker\ wins}} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } p > 0.5 \text{ (always eventually)} \\ \left( \dfrac{p}{q} \right)^{k_{\text{honest}}}, & \text{otherwise, exponential decay} \end{cases}
$$

For Ethereum's Casper FFG finality, slashing makes the attack catastrophically expensive: an attacker who violates the finality rule loses at least **1/3 of total stake** (~$10 billion+ as of 2024). The expected cost of a successful attack:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{attack}} = S_{\text{stake\ slashed}} + \text{opportunity\ cost} + \text{reputation\ loss}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 takeaway:** PoS does not eliminate the 51% attack — it raises its **economic cost** astronomically. This is called the **"crypto-economic security"** model.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Block Reward Halving in Bitcoin

Bitcoin's protocol halves the block subsidy every 210,000 blocks (~4 years). Let $R_n$ be the block reward after $n$ halvings:

$$
R_n = R_0 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n
$$

where $R_0 = 50$ BTC (initial reward at genesis, January 2009).

| Halving Event (n) | Block Height | Year (approx) | Reward $R_n$ (BTC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (Genesis) | 0 | 2009 | 50.00000000 |
| 1 | 210,000 | 2012 | 25.00000000 |
| 2 | 420,000 | 2016 | 12.50000000 |
| 3 | 630,000 | 2020 | 6.25000000 |
| 4 | 840,000 | 2024 | 3.12500000 |
| 5 | 1,050,000 | 2028 | 1.56250000 |

Total BTC ever mined (geometric series):

$$
\text{Total BTC} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} R_0 \times 210{,}000 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = R_0 \times 210{,}000 \times 2 = 50 \times 210{,}000 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}
$$

The cap of **21 million BTC** is therefore a mathematical inevitability of the halving schedule. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Block reward halves every 4 years → geometric series converges to 21 million. Sum = 50 × 210,000 × 2 = 21M."*

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Define consensus algorithm. List any four popular consensus algorithms used in blockchain.**

**Model Answer:**

A consensus algorithm is a fault-tolerant protocol that allows a distributed network of untrusted nodes to agree on a single, canonical state of the shared ledger, despite the presence of malicious or faulty participants.

Four popular consensus algorithms:
1. **Proof of Work (PoW)** — used in Bitcoin; miners solve computational puzzles.
2. **Proof of Stake (PoS)** — used in Ethereum 2.0; validators stake coins.
3. **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** — used in Hyperledger Fabric; voting-based.
4. **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** — used in EOS; coin holders vote for delegates.

---

|---|---|---|
| Resource | Computational power (electricity) | Staked cryptocurrency |
| Validator Selection | First to solve puzzle wins | Weighted random by stake size |
| Energy Use | Very high | Very low (~99.95% less) |
| Attack Penalty | Wasted energy | Slashing of staked coins |

> - ✏️ In comparison questions, ALWAYS use a **table** — KTU examiners give easy marks for tabular answers.
