Compute double-spend success probability. **Solution**: $$P_{\text{success}} = \left(\frac{0.30}{0.70}\right)^6$$ Calculate the ratio: $$\frac{0.30}{0.70} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$ Now raise to the 6th power: $$0.4286^6 = (0.4286^2)^3 = (0.1837)^3 \approx 0.00620$$ **Result**: $P_{\text{success}} \approx 0.62\%$. This is why **6 confirmations** is considered secure against attackers with up to $\sim 30\%$ hashrate. ### Step 6: Inverting for Required Confirmations For a target success probability $\epsilon$, solve for $z$: $$z = \frac{\ln \epsilon}{\ln(q/p)} = \frac{\ln \epsilon}{\ln(q) - \ln(1-q)}$$ **Example**: To achieve $P \leq 0.001$ (0.1%) with $q = 0.10$: $$z = \frac{\ln(0.001)}{\ln(0.10/0.90)} = \frac{-6.908}{\ln(0.1111)} = \frac{-6.908}{-2.197} \approx 3.14$$ Therefore $z = 4$ confirmations suffice. ## 3.2 Derivation: Merkle Tree Membership Proof Complexity ### Setup A Merkle tree with $N$ transactions. We want to prove transaction $T_i$ is included in the root $R$. ### Step 1: Structure For $N = 2^k$ transactions, the tree has $k$ levels. Each non-leaf node is: $$\text{node}_{parent} = H(\text{node}_{left} \vert\vert \text{node}_{right})$$ ### Step 2: Proof Size To prove inclusion, the prover sends the **Merkle path**: at each level, the sibling hash. Total hashes required: $k = \log_2 N$. ### Step 3: Verification Cost Verifier recomputes the path from $T_i$ up to $R$ using the $k$ sibling hashes. Each step is one hash: $$\text{Verification cost} = k = \log_2 N \text{ hashes}$$ ### Step 4: Numerical Example For $N = 4096 = 2^{12}$ transactions: $$\text{Proof size} = 12 \text{ hashes} \times 32 \text{ bytes} = 384 \text{ bytes}$$ Compared to sending all $4096 \times 250 \approx 1$ MB of transactions, this is a **2600× compression** — a key reason SPV (Simplified Payment Verification) clients can run on mobile devices. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Centralization Risks Across Consensus Types | Attack Type | PoW Vulnerability | PoS Vulnerability | Mitigation | |---|---|---|---| | **51% Attack** | High if hashrate cheap to rent | High if stake is liquid | Slashing, finality gadgets | | **Sybil Attack** | Resisted by hash cost | Resisted by stake cost | Identity binding (weak) | | **Long-Range Attack** | Immune (cost of rewriting) | **Highly vulnerable** | Weak subjectivity checkpoints | | **Nothing-at-Stake** | Immune | **Vulnerable** without slashing | Slashing conditions | | **Selfish Mining** | Theoretical at > 25% hashrate | Less applicable | Uniform random selection | | **Censorship** | Mining pool collusion | Validator collusion | Proposer-builder separation (PBS) | > **KTU Examiner's Insight**: For 14-mark questions, students who present a **comparative table like the above** and discuss **at least three attack types** in depth typically score **12–14/14**. One-sided answers (e.g., only discussing 51% attack) max out at **8–9/14**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Centralized vs. Decentralized vs. Distributed Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph centralized["CENTRALIZED LEDGER"] nodeA[Client A] --> nodeHUB[Central Authority] nodeB[Client B] --> nodeHUB nodeC[Client C] --> nodeHUB nodeD[Client D] --> nodeHUB nodeHUB --> nodeDB[(Central Database)] end subgraph decentralized["DECENTRALIZED PEER TO PEER"] peerA[Node A] --- peerB[Node B] peerB --- peerC[Node C] peerC --- peerD[Node D] peerD --- peerA peerA --- peerC peerB --- peerD end classDef centralStyle fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff classDef decentStyle fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff class nodeHUB centralStyle class peerA,peerB,peerC,peerD decentStyle ``` **Reading the diagram**: - **Top (red)**: Every client depends on one central server — single point of failure. - **Bottom (green)**: Every node connects to multiple peers — no single point of failure, but the **density of connections** is what gives Bitcoin its security. ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Security Layer Stack in Blockchain ```mermaid flowchart TB layer5["LAYER 5: INCENTIVE LAYER
Block rewards, transaction fees, slashing penalties"] layer4["LAYER 4: CONSENSUS LAYER
Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, PBFT"] layer3["LAYER 3: PROTOCOL LAYER
Block validation, fork choice rule, mempool rules"] layer2["LAYER 2: DATA STRUCTURE LAYER
Merkle trees, hash chains, block headers"] layer1["LAYER 1: CRYPTOGRAPHIC LAYER
SHA-256, ECDSA, secp256k1, RIPEMD-160"] layer1 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer5 classDef cryptoStyle fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff classDef dataStyle fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1864ab,color:#fff classDef protoStyle fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1864ab,color:#000 classDef consStyle fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#fab005,color:#000 classDef incentStyle fill:#ff922b,stroke:#d9480f,color:#fff class layer1 cryptoStyle class layer2 dataStyle class layer3 protoStyle class layer4 consStyle class layer5 incentStyle ``` **Interpretation**: Each layer **depends on the layer below**. Compromising SHA-256 (Layer 1) breaks everything above. Compromising the incentive layer (Layer 5) can lead to economic attacks even if cryptography is intact. ## 4.3 Diagram 3: 51% Attack Workflow (Double-Spend) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Att as Attacker participant Merch as Merchant participant Net as Honest Network participant Victim as Victim Note over Att,Victim: Initial state: Attacker has 30 percent hashrate Att->>Merch: Send 1000 BTC for product Merch->>Net: Broadcast transaction Net->>Net: Add to Block 100, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105 (6 conf) Merch->>Att: Ship product (waits 6 conf) loop Secret mining Att->>Att: Mine private chain in parallel end Att->>Net: Release longer private chain (Blocks 100-108) Net->>Net: Fork choice rule accepts attacker's chain Net->>Net: Original transaction REVERTED Victim->>Victim: Lost 1000 BTC, attacker has both product AND coins Note over Net: Result: Double-spend successful ``` **Critical insight**: The attacker's chain must be **strictly longer** (more cumulative work) to be accepted by the **Nakamoto consensus fork choice rule** (longest chain = most work). ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Centralization Vector Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph crypto["CRYPTOGRAPHIC LAYER"] cryptoC["Algorithm choice
concentrated in
NIST standards"] end subgraph node["NODE LAYER"] nodeC["Few thousand
full nodes
geographically
concentrated"] end subgraph mine["MINING LAYER"] mineC["Top 4 pools
exceed 50 percent
of hashrate"] end subgraph stake["STAKING LAYER"] stakeC["Lido plus
Coinbase plus
Binance stake
exceed 33 percent"] end subgraph exchange["EXCHANGE LAYER"] exchangeC["Top 3 CEXs
control 60 percent
of spot volume"] end subgraph gov["GOVERNANCE LAYER"] govC["Core dev teams
and foundations
set protocol rules"] end cryptoC --> nodeC nodeC --> mineC mineC --> stakeC stakeC --> exchangeC exchangeC --> govC classDef riskStyle fill:#ff8787,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff class cryptoC,nodeC,mineC,stakeC,exchangeC,govC riskStyle ``` **Interpretation**: Centralization compounds **upward through layers**. Even if the cryptographic layer is fully decentralized, centralization at the exchange or governance layer can still compromise the system's security properties. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** > **"With a suitable example, explain the term '51% attack' in the context of blockchain security."** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: A 51% attack occurs when a single entity or colluding group controls **more than 50%** of a blockchain network's total mining hashrate (in PoW) or stake (in PoS). This majority control allows the attacker to **(1 mark)**: 1. **Reverse their own transactions** (enabling double-spending) **(1 mark)** 2. **Prevent new transactions** from gaining confirmations (censorship) **(0.5 marks)** 3. **Prevent other miners** from finding blocks (monopolistic mining) **(0.5 marks)** **Example**: In **Ethereum Classic (January 2019)**, the attacker reorganized over **100 blocks** and double-spent approximately **$1.1 million** in ETC. The attack was possible because ETC's hashrate was so low that renting 51% was affordable. ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** > **"Distinguish between 'centralization' and 'decentralization' in blockchain systems. Why does excessive centralization pose a security risk?"** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: | Parameter | Centralization | Decentralization | |---|---|---| | **Control** | One entity decides | Distributed among peers | | **Failure mode** | Single point of failure | Byzantine fault tolerant | | **Trust** | Trust the authority | Trustless / cryptographic | | **Example** | Hyperledger Fabric | Bitcoin, Ethereum | **Security risk of centralization (1 mark)**: When control concentrates, an attacker only needs to compromise **one entity** (regulator hack, insider attack, server seizure) to compromise the entire network. This **collapses the security model** to the security of the weakest centralized point. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Module 4 Part B offers internal choice. You must answer **one** of the two questions below. Each question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern Model]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the layered security architecture of a blockchain system. Discuss the role of cryptographic hashing, digital signatures, and Merkle trees in ensuring data integrity and authenticity. > > **(b) [7 marks]** An attacker controls **20%** of the total hashrate in a PoW blockchain. A merchant waits for **5 confirmations** before releasing goods. Calculate the probability that the attacker can successfully double-spend. Comment on the security implications. #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1: Layered architecture (3 marks)** — Draw the diagram from Section 4.2 above. Identify the 5 layers: Cryptographic → Data Structure → Protocol → Consensus → Incentive. **Step 2: Cryptographic hashing role (1.5 marks)**: SHA-256 provides **pre-image** and **collision resistance**, making it computationally infeasible to forge block hashes. Each block header contains `prev_hash`, linking blocks immutably. Any modification to a past block changes its hash, cascading invalidation to all subsequent blocks — this is the source of immutability. **Step 3: Digital signatures role (1.5 marks)**: ECDSA signatures authenticate transaction senders. Only the holder of the private key can sign, and the signature is bound to the transaction hash. Non-repudiation is mathematically enforced. **Step 4: Merkle tree role (1 mark)**: Merkle trees enable **logarithmic-sized proofs** of transaction inclusion (SPV). The Merkle root in the block header commits to all transactions — altering any transaction breaks the root, which breaks the block hash, which breaks the chain. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: State the formula (2 marks)**: $$P_{\text{success}} = \left(\frac{q}{1-q}\right)^z$$ **Step 2: Substitute values (1 mark)**: - Attacker hashrate: $q = 0.20$ - Honest hashrate: $p = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$ - Confirmations: $z = 5$ **Step 3: Compute ratio (1 mark)**: $$\frac{q}{p} = \frac{0.20}{0.80} = 0.25$$ **Step 4: Compute the power (2 marks)**: $$P_{\text{success}} = (0.25)^5 = (0.25)^2 \times (0.25)^2 \times 0.25$$ $$= 0.0625 \times 0.0625 \times 0.25 = 0.00390625 \times 0.25$$ $$\approx 0.000977$$ **Step 5: Security comment (1 mark)**: $P_{\text{success}} \approx 0.098\%$, which is **less than 0.1%**. This is generally considered **acceptable** for low-to-medium value transactions. However, for transactions worth more than approximately **$10,000**, additional confirmations (e.g., $z = 10$ or higher) are recommended. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern Model]** > > **(a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the various **centralization vectors** that emerge in blockchain systems. How does the **Nakamoto Coefficient** quantify decentralization? Provide at least three real-world examples of centralization-induced vulnerabilities. > > **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Blockchain Trilemma** as formulated by Vitalik Buterin. With a comparative table, discuss how **Bitcoin**, **Ethereum (post-Merge)**, and **Solana** each attempt to balance the three properties. #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Step 1: Define centralization vectors (2 marks)**: Centralization vectors are the layers of a blockchain ecosystem where control concentrates. The **six major vectors** are: 1. **Mining/Staking concentration** (hashrate or stake pools) 2. **Full node distribution** (geographic and ISP concentration) 3. **Development/governance** (core teams, foundations) 4. **Exchange custody** (CEX dominance) 5. **Stablecoin issuer control** (USDC/USDT blacklist power) 6. **Layer-2 sequencer centralization** (Arbitrum, Optimism) **Step 2: Nakamoto Coefficient definition (2 marks)**: The **Nakamoto Coefficient** is the **minimum number $k$ of independent entities** required to collude and exceed 51% control of a given subsystem. A higher coefficient = more decentralized. For example: - **Bitcoin mining pools** (2024): top 2 pools = ~52% → Nakamoto Coefficient = **2** (very centralized) - **Ethereum validators** (2024): Lido + Coinbase + Binance ≈ 55% → Nakamoto Coefficient = **3** (centralized) **Step 3: Real-world vulnerabilities (3 marks)**: 1. **Ethereum Classic 51% Attack (2019)**: Low hashrate + rental markets (NiceHash) enabled attacker to rewrite 100+ blocks → **$1.1M** double-spent. 2. **Ronin Bridge Hack (2022)**: Validator set was effectively controlled by **5 of 9 nodes** (Nakamoto Coefficient = 5, all operated by Sky Mavis). 4 compromised = 173,600 ETH stolen (~$600M). 3. **Celsius/Voyager Bankruptcies (2022)**: Centralized custody of customer assets on exchanges led to **billions in user losses** when exchanges collapsed — a non-blockchain but adjacent centralization failure. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **Step 1: Define the trilemma (2 marks)**: The **Blockchain Trilemma** (Vitalik Buterin, ~2017) states that a blockchain can **at most strongly satisfy two** of the following three properties: - **Decentralization**: Low barrier to participate (run a node, become a validator) - **Security**: Resistance to attacks (51%, Sybil, censorship) - **Scalability**: High transaction throughput (TPS) **Step 2: Comparative analysis (4 marks)**: | Property | Bitcoin | Ethereum (PoS) | Solana | |---|---|---|---| | **Decentralization** | **High** (~17,000 nodes, low staking) | **Medium** (~1M validators, Lido stake concentration) | **Low** (~2,000 nodes, hardware requirements) | | **Security** | **Very High** ($40B+ hashrate equivalent) | **High** (slashing, finality gadgets) | **Medium** (multiple outages, halting issues) | | **Scalability** | **Low** (~7 TPS) | **Medium** (~30 TPS L1, ~3000 TPS L2) | **Very High** (~65,000 TPS theoretical) | | **Trilemma position** | Decent + Sec | Decent + Sec (slight) | Sec + Scale | **Step 3: Trade-off commentary (1 mark)**: - **Bitcoin** sacrifices scalability for maximum decentralization and security. - **Ethereum** pursues all three through **Layer-2 rollups** (Optimism, Arbitrum, zkSync) — moving execution off-chain while keeping L1 decentralized and secure. - **Solana** sacrifices decentralization (high hardware requirements: 256GB RAM, 1TB SSD) to achieve speed. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in "Security and Centralization" Questions** > > 1. **Confusing decentralization with distribution** (1 mark loss each occurrence). A system can be *distributed* (multiple servers) but still *centralized* (one owner). Always state *who controls* the nodes. > > 2. **Omitting the formula derivation steps** (3–4 marks loss). For 51% attack questions, you must show: (a) random walk setup, (b) ratio $q/p$, (c) exponentiation, (d) numerical substitution. Skipping the ratio step is a 2-mark deduction. > > 3. **Saying "Bitcoin is fully decentralized"** (1–2 marks loss). This is factually wrong in 2024. Use the term "**partially decentralized**" or "**permissionless**" instead. > > 4. **Ignoring economic incentives** (2 marks loss). The Nakamoto consensus is not *just* a hash-power rule — it is a **game-theoretic** mechanism. Always mention miner rewards and the cost of attack. > > 5. **Not drawing diagrams** (1–2 marks loss). KTU evaluators explicitly reward diagrams in 7-mark sub-questions. A 51% attack without a flow diagram loses at least 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 4 — Security and Centralization** ### Core Concepts (Must-Know) - ☐ **51% Attack**: Majority hashrate/stake enables double-spend and censorship. Success probability $P = (q/p)^z$. - ☐ **Double-Spend**: Spending the same UTXO twice; the fundamental attack blockchain prevents. - ☐ **Sybil Attack**: Creating many fake identities. Resisted by **PoW** (hash cost) and **PoS** (stake cost). - ☐ **Long-Range Attack** (PoS-specific): Replaying old validator keys to rewrite history. Mitigated by **weak subjectivity**. - ☐ **Nothing-at-Stake** (PoS-specific): Validators voting on multiple forks because it costs nothing. Mitigated by **slashing**. - ☐ **Selfish Mining** (PoW): Withholding blocks to gain disproportionate revenue. Possible above ~25% hashrate. - ☐ **Eclipse Attack**: Isolating a node from the honest network by surrounding it with malicious peers. - ☐ **Timejacking Attack**: Manipulating a node's time perception via false block timestamps. ### Centralization Metrics (Must-Know) - ☐ **Nakamoto Coefficient**: Smallest $k$ such that $k$ entities hold > 51%. Higher = more decentralized. - ☐ **Gini Coefficient** of hashrate/stake distribution: 0 = perfect equality, 1 = total monopoly. - ☐ **Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)**: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2$ where $s_i$ is the market share. HHI > 2500 = highly concentrated. ### Key Numbers (Memorize) - ☐ **SHA-256** → 256-bit output, $2^{128}$ collision resistance. - ☐ **ECDSA secp256k1** → 256-bit keys, ~128-bit security. - ☐ **6 confirmations** → Bitcoin standard for high-value tx. - ☐ **12 confirmations** → Recommended for amounts > $1M. - ☐ **BFT limit** → $f < n/3$ malicious nodes. - ☐ **Nakamoto consensus** → $q < 0.5$ for security. ### The Trilemma (Memorize All Three Corners) - ☐ **Decentralization** ↔ **Security** ↔ **Scalability** — pick two. - ☐ **Bitcoin**: Decent + Sec, no Scale. - ☐ **Ethereum**: All three (with L2s). - ☐ **Solana**: Sec + Scale, no Decent. - ☐ **Hyperledger**: Scale + (controlled) Decent, no public Sec. ### Quick-Recall Formulas - ☐ $P_{\text{double-spend}} = (q/p)^z$ - ☐ $z_{\text{required}} = \ln(\epsilon) / \ln(q/p)$ - ☐ Merkle proof size $= \log_2(N) \times 32$ bytes - ☐ BFT fault tolerance: $n \geq 3f + 1$ - ☐ Mining puzzle: Find nonce such that $H(\text{block header}) < \text{target}$ - ☐ Difficulty adjustment: Bitcoin retargets every **2016 blocks** ($\approx$ 2 weeks) ### Last-Minute Conceptual Q&A Prep - ☐ Why is **SHA-256** used and not MD5? → MD5 has known collisions; SHA-256 is collision-resistant. - ☐ Why **ECDSA** and not RSA? → Smaller keys, faster signature, better for blockchain blocks. - ☐ Why **6 confirmations**? → Empirically chosen so that $P_{\text{revert}} < 0.001$ for $q \leq 0.10$. - ☐ What is **weak subjectivity** in PoS? → New nodes must trust recent checkpoints from a trusted source to avoid long-range attacks. - ☐ How does **slashing** work? → Validators that double-sign lose a portion of their staked ETH (typically 1 ETH minimum, up to 100%). > **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Security and Centralization**

topics.title: Ethereum - The Ethereum Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethereum - The Ethereum Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum** is an open-source, **Turing-complete**, distributed computing platform based on a **decentralized blockchain ledger** featuring a built-in programming language (Solidity) and a runtime execution engine — the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** — that enables the deployment and execution of **smart contracts** and **decentralized applications (DApps)** without downtime, censorship, or third-party interference.

It was proposed by **Vitalik Buterin** in late **2013**, and the network went live on **July 30, 2015**. Ethereum extends the blockchain paradigm pioneered by Bitcoin from being merely a distributed ledger for value transfer (currency) to becoming a **general-purpose, programmable settlement layer** for any kind of digital state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Turing Completeness** means the language can, in principle, compute any computable function given sufficient resources (subject to the **gas limit** constraint in Ethereum).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "World Computer" Analogy

Imagine a giant, transparent, unstoppable **vending machine** that lives in the sky, replicated on thousands of computers worldwide. Anyone can drop a coin (Ether/ETH) into it, select a snack (call a smart contract function), and the machine guarantees the snack is dispensed correctly — no shopkeeper, no manager, no banks.

- **Bitcoin** = A specialized calculator that only does one thing: securely transfer value.
- **Ethereum** = A general-purpose laptop (the "world computer") that anyone can rent time on by paying a small fee (gas) to run any program (smart contract) that they upload.

| Component | Real-World Analogy |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Ethereum Network** | A global, shared supercomputer |
| **Smart Contract** | A vending machine with self-executing rules |
| **Ether (ETH)** | The fuel/money to rent the computer |
| **Gas** | The precise "electricity units" measured to prevent waste |
| **EVM** | The CPU of this supercomputer |

## 1.3 Why Ethereum Matters — The Programmable Blockchain

Ethereum introduced three fundamental innovations over Bitcoin:

1. **State Transitions with Arbitrary Computation** — Bitcoin's Script language is intentionally limited (not Turing-complete). Ethereum's EVM can execute any logic a developer can write.
2. **Account-Based Model** — Unlike Bitcoin's UTXO model, Ethereum uses an **account-based** model (similar to a bank account balance), which simplifies smart contract state management.
3. **Smart Contracts as First-Class Citizens** — Code is deployed *on-chain* and has its own address, balance, and persistent storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Wei** = 10⁻¹⁸ ETH (smallest unit)
> - **Gwei** = 10⁻⁹ ETH (commonly used for gas pricing)
> - **Block Time** ≈ **12 seconds** (vs Bitcoin's 10 minutes)
> - **Ether Supply** — No hard cap; targeted ~**0.5%–2%** annual issuance post-Merge
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ethereum Network — Core Components Breakdown

The Ethereum network is composed of several interdependent layers. Let us break down each component logically.

### A. Accounts (The "Actors" of Ethereum)

There are precisely **two** types of accounts, and this is a high-frequency KTU exam question.

**1. Externally Owned Accounts (EOA)**
- Controlled by humans via **private keys**.
- Has an **ether balance** and a **nonce**.
- Can initiate transactions.
- **No associated code** (empty by default).

**2. Contract Accounts (CA)**
- Controlled by their **contract code** (smart contract).
- Has an ether balance, nonce, code, and storage.
- **Cannot** initiate a transaction on its own; only reacts to received transactions or messages.
- Owns its own code hash and storage root.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both account types share the same address space (20-byte addresses derived from the Keccak-256 hash of the public key).

### B. Transactions and Messages

- **Transaction** = A signed data package that triggers a state change. Signed using the **ECDSA** algorithm.
- **Message** = A data packet passed between contracts (not stored on-chain, never visible to the outside world, cheaper than transactions).

### C. The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)

The EVM is a **quasi-Turing-complete** **256-bit stack-based** virtual machine. "Quasi" because computation is bounded by an externally imposed **gas limit**.

It is:
- **Sandboxed** — Code has no access to the network, filesystem, or other processes.
- **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output.
- **Isolated** — Runs independently on every full node.

### D. Gas — The Anti-Abuse Mechanism

**Gas** is a unit measuring the computational effort required to execute operations on Ethereum. Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost (e.g., `ADD` = 3 gas, `SSTORE` = 20,000 gas for a new slot).

> [!NOTE]
> **Gas solves the Halting Problem** in a decentralized setting — since nodes cannot predict whether a program will run forever, gas forces the sender to pre-pay a finite amount of "fuel" that is consumed as the EVM executes.

### E. The State — World's Global Computer Memory

Ethereum's state is a massive data structure called the **Merkle Patricia Trie**, mapping:
$$\text{State} = \{ \text{Address} \rightarrow (\text{Nonce}, \text{Balance}, \text{StorageRoot}, \text{CodeHash}) \}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transaction Fee** | $\text{Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$ | **Wei** (or Gwei) | Paid by the transaction sender |
| **Gas Limit** | Maximum gas the sender is willing to spend | **Gas** | Unused gas is refunded |
| **Block Gas Limit** | Total gas allowed in a single block | **~30,000,000 Gas** (post-London) | Prevents blocks from being too large |
| **Ether Denominations** | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 10^9 \text{ Gwei}$ | ETH, Gwei, Wei | Memorize: **Wei** = $10^{-18}$ |
| **Block Reward** | $\text{Reward} = \text{Static Block Reward} + \text{Tx Fees} + \text{Uncle Reward}$ | ETH | Pre-Merge: ~2 ETH; Post-Merge (PoS): variable |
| **Account Address** | $\text{Addr} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{PubKey})[12:32]$ | 20 bytes | Last 20 bytes of the public key hash |
| **EVM Word Size** | 256 bits (32 bytes) | bits | Optimized for Keccak-256 and elliptic curves |
| **Block Time** | Target ≈ 12 seconds | seconds | Used for difficulty adjustment |
| **State Transition** | $\boldsymbol{\sigma_{t+1}} \equiv \Pi(\boldsymbol{\sigma_t}, T)$ | — | $\Pi$ = state transition function, $T$ = transaction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION FOR EXAMS:**
> - $\sigma$ (sigma) = World State
> - $\Pi$ (capital Pi) = State Transition Function
> - $T$ = Transaction
> - $\text{KEccak256}$ = Ethereum's hashing algorithm (NOT standard SHA3 — note the difference!)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Smart Contracts** → Automate escrow, supply-chain tracking, and insurance claims.
- **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations)** → Corporate governance without central authority.
- **DeFi (Decentralized Finance)** → Lending, borrowing, trading without banks.
- **NFTs** → Digital asset ownership & provenance.
- **Web3** → Identity, data ownership, and censorship-resistant social media.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ethereum State Transition Function (Yellow Paper Formalism)

The Ethereum Yellow Paper formally defines the network as a transaction-based state machine. Let us derive the complete state transition function step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the Initial State

The world state $\sigma$ is a mapping between **160-bit addresses** and **account state objects**. We start with the genesis state:

$$
\sigma_0 = \{ \text{Pre-funded addresses and contract code from the genesis block} \}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The state is initialized at block 0 (genesis) with predetermined accounts holding initial ETH and any pre-deployed smart contracts (e.g., the famous `0x000...001` contract).

### Step 2 — Apply the State Transition Function $\Pi$

For any given state $\sigma$ and a transaction $T$, the new state is computed as:

$$
\sigma' = \Pi(\sigma, T)
$$

**Textual Logic:** The function $\Pi$ takes the current world state and a valid transaction, and outputs the updated state after the transaction is executed.

### Step 3 — Decompose $\Pi$ into Sub-Functions

The transition function $\Pi$ is composed of three atomic operations applied in sequence:

$$
\Pi(\sigma, T) \equiv \begin{cases} \text{Pre-Validation} \rightarrow \text{Gas Payment} \rightarrow \text{Execution} \end{cases}
$$

Formally:

$$
\Pi(\sigma, T) = \Lambda(\sigma, T) \quad \text{(pre-validation and gas deduction)}
$$

followed by the in-contract execution using the EVM.

### Step 4 — Block-Level State Transition

Since transactions are grouped into blocks, the block-level transition is the sequential application of all transactions in the block:

$$
\sigma_{n+1} = \Pi_{\text{block}}(\sigma_n, B) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Pi(\sigma_{i-1}, T_i) \quad \text{where } \sigma_N = \sigma_{n+1}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The state evolves from block $n$ to block $n+1$ by executing all $N$ transactions in block $B$ one by one, with the state updated after each transaction.

### Step 5 — Transaction Execution Internals

For a single transaction $T$, the EVM executes:

$$
(O, \sigma') = \bigotimes(\sigma, S, G, T)
$$

Where:
- $\bigotimes$ = EVM execution function
- $O$ = Sub-state (logs, suicide-refunds, touched accounts)
- $S$ = Sender account state
- $G$ = Available gas
- $T$ = Transaction object

### Step 6 — Total Gas Cost Calculation

The total transaction fee is derived as:

$$
\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times G_{\text{price}}
$$

Where:
- $G_{\text{used}}$ = Total gas consumed by the EVM (intrinsic + execution)
- $G_{\text{price}}$ = Gas price in Wei per gas unit (set by sender, post-EIP-1559 includes base fee + priority tip)

**Textual Logic:** Every opcode, every storage read/write, and every byte of transaction data costs gas. The product of gas consumed and gas price yields the final fee paid in Wei.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Gas Fee Calculation

**Problem:** A user sends a simple ETH transfer. The intrinsic gas cost is **21,000 gas**, and the user sets a gas price of **50 Gwei**. Calculate the total transaction fee in ETH.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Values

$$
G_{\text{used}} = 21{,}000 \text{ gas}, \quad G_{\text{price}} = 50 \text{ Gwei}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Fee Formula

$$
\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times G_{\text{price}}
$$

$$
\text{TxFee} = 21{,}000 \times 50 \text{ Gwei}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Product

$$
\text{TxFee} = 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ Gwei}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Gwei to ETH

Since $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^{-9} \text{ ETH}$:

$$
\text{TxFee} = 1{,}050{,}000 \times 10^{-9} \text{ ETH}
$$

$$
\text{TxFee} = 1.05 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-9} \text{ ETH} = 1.05 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{TxFee} = 0.00105 \text{ ETH}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — EOA vs Contract Account

| Parameter | Externally Owned Account (EOA) | Contract Account (CA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initiates Transaction?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (reacts only) |
| **Controlled By** | Private Key | Contract Code (bytecode) |
| **Code Hash** | Keccak-256 of empty string | Keccak-256 of deployed bytecode |
| **Storage Root** | Empty (default 0) | Merkle Patricia Trie root of storage |
| **Has Private Key?** | Yes | No |
| **Created By** | Generating a key pair | Deploying a transaction from EOA |
| **Use Case** | User wallets | Smart contracts, tokens, DAOs |
| **Gas Cost to Create** | 0 (free) | ~32,000+ gas (deployment cost) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Ethereum Network Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the layered architecture of the Ethereum network, mapping how transactions flow from the user through the EVM to update the global state.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1["USER LAYER"]
        u1[End User with EOA Wallet]
        u2[DApp Browser e.g. MetaMask]
    end

    subgraph layer2["NETWORK LAYER"]
        n1[Ethereum Node Client e.g. Geth Besu]
        n2[P2P DevP2P Protocol]
        n3[Transaction Mempool TxPool]
    end

    subgraph layer3["CONSENSUS LAYER"]
        c1[Beacon Chain PoS]
        c2[Validator Network]
    end

    subgraph layer4["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        e1[Ethereum Virtual Machine EVM]
        e2[State Database Merkle Patricia Trie]
        e3[Gas Accounting Engine]
    end

    subgraph layer5["STATE LAYER"]
        s1[World State sigma]
        s2[Account States Nonce Balance Storage Code]
    end

    u1 --> n1
    u2 --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> e1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> e1
    e1 --> e3
    e1 --> e2
    e2 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> n1
```

## 4.2 Transaction Lifecycle Flow Diagram

This diagram shows the precise flow of a transaction from the moment it is signed until it is permanently recorded on the blockchain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Creates Transaction in Wallet] --> B[Sign with Private Key ECDSA]
    B --> C[Serialize RLP Encode]
    C --> D[Broadcast to Network P2P]
    D --> E[Nodes Validate Basic Checks]
    E --> F[Add to Mempool]
    F --> G[Validator Picks Transaction]
    G --> H[Execute in EVM]
    H --> I{Valid State Transition?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Include in Block]
    I -- No --> K[Reject and Forfeit Gas]
    J --> L[Block Propagated to Network]
    L --> M[Finality via PoS Casper FFG]
    M --> N[State Updated Globally]
    N --> O[Transaction Receipt Generated]
```

## 4.3 Account Model Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AEOA["EXTERNALLY OWNED ACCOUNT EOA"]
        e1[160 bit Address]
        e2[Nonce: Transaction Count]
        e3[Balance: Wei]
        e4[CodeHash: EMPTY]
        e5[StorageRoot: EMPTY]
        e6[Controlled By: Private Key]
    end

    subgraph ACA["CONTRACT ACCOUNT CA"]
        c1[160 bit Address]
        c2[Nonce: Contract Calls Count]
        c3[Balance: Wei]
        c4[CodeHash: Keccak of Bytecode]
        c5[StorageRoot: Merkle Patricia Root]
        c6[Controlled By: Code Logic]
    end

    AEOA -->|Initiates Tx To| ACA
    ACA -->|Executes Code and Returns| AEOA
```

## 4.4 EVM Execution Stack Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["EVM STACK MACHINE 256 bit Word Size"]
        push[PUSH1 PUSH32]
        add[ADD]
        mul[MUL]
        sstore[SSTORE: 20000 gas]
        sload[SLOAD: 2100 gas]
        pop[POP]
    end

    subgraph MEM["EVM MEMORY Volatile"]
        mload[MLOAD]
        mstore[MSTORE]
    end

    subgraph STR["EVM STORAGE Persistent Key Value"]
        slot1[Slot 0]
        slot2[Slot 1]
        slot3[Slot N]
    end

    push --> add
    add --> mul
    mul --> sstore
    sstore --> STR
    sload --> STR
    push --> MEM
    MEM --> push
    STR -->|Refund| sstore
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:** The execution layer (EVM) is the heart of Ethereum. Stack operations are cheap (~3 gas), memory is moderately priced (~3 gas per word), and **storage** is the most expensive (~20,000 gas) because it permanently alters the global state trie.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

### Question 1: What is the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)? List its key characteristics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is the **decentralized, sandboxed, stack-based runtime execution engine** of the Ethereum network. Every full node runs an instance of the EVM to execute smart contract bytecode in a **deterministic** and **isolated** environment.

**Key Characteristics (3 of these for full marks):**
1. **Quasi-Turing Completeness** — Can run any logic bounded by the gas limit.
2. **256-bit Word Size** — Optimized for cryptographic operations (Keccak-256, elliptic curves).
3. **Deterministic** — Same input always produces the same output across all nodes.
4. **Sandboxed / Isolated** — No access to network, filesystem, or other processes.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Question 2: Differentiate between EOA and Contract Account in Ethereum.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | EOA | Contract Account |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Control** | Private Key | Smart Contract Code |
| **Initiation** | Can start transactions | Can only react to received transactions |
| **Code Hash** | Empty | Keccak-256 of deployed bytecode |
| **Storage** | None | Has persistent storage (Merkle Patricia Trie) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU ESE (End Semester Exam) follows internal choice for all 14-mark module questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Module 4 Pattern]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the architecture of the Ethereum network in detail with a neat diagram. List the different types of accounts in Ethereum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The Ethereum network is a **distributed state machine** consisting of the following key architectural components:

1. **User Layer** — Users interact via wallets (MetaMask, Ledger) or DApps.
2. **Application Layer** — Smart contracts deployed on-chain (written in Solidity, Vyper).
3. **Execution Layer** — The EVM runs the bytecode and manages gas accounting.
4. **Consensus Layer** — Post-Merge, this is the **Beacon Chain** running **Proof-of-Stake (Casper FFG + LMD GHOST)**.
5. **Network Layer** — P2P protocol (DevP2P) for transaction and block propagation.
6. **Data Layer** — The blockchain itself, storing blocks containing transactions and state roots.

**Account Types:**
- **Externally Owned Account (EOA):** Controlled by a private key; can initiate transactions; no code.
- **Contract Account (CA):** Controlled by code; cannot initiate transactions; has persistent storage and a code hash.

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**Q.A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an Ethereum account. What are the four fields stored in every account state? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

An Ethereum account is a **four-field data structure** stored in the world state Merkle Patricia Trie.

The four fields are:

| Field | Purpose | Size |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nonce** | Transaction count for EOAs / contract call count for CAs | 64 bits |
| **Balance** | Wei held by the account | 256 bits |
| **Storage Root** | Root hash of the account's storage trie (empty for EOA) | 256 bits |
| **Code Hash** | Keccak-256 of the contract bytecode (empty string hash for EOA) | 256 bits |

**Account Address Derivation:**
- For EOAs: $\text{Address} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{PublicKey})[12:32]$ (last 20 bytes).
- For Contracts: $\text{Address} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(\text{Sender}, \text{Nonce}))[12:32]$.

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 4 Pattern]**

**Q.B (a)** What is Gas in Ethereum? Why is it necessary? Explain the formula for calculating transaction fees. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:** Gas is a unit that measures the **computational effort** required to execute operations on the Ethereum network. Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost defined in the Ethereum Yellow Paper.

**Why Gas is Necessary (3 reasons for 3 marks):**
1. **Halting Problem Solution** — Prevents infinite loops from blocking the network.
2. **Spam Prevention** — Makes DoS attacks economically infeasible.
3. **Resource Pricing** — Allocates limited block space fairly via a market mechanism.

**Transaction Fee Formula:**
$$
\text{TxFee} = G_{\text{used}} \times G_{\text{price}}
$$

- $G_{\text{used}}$ = Gas consumed during execution.
- $G_{\text{price}}$ = Price per gas unit (in Wei/Gwei).
- Post **EIP-1559 (London Hard Fork, August 2021)**, the formula is: $G_{\text{price}} = \text{BaseFee} + \text{PriorityTip}$.
- The $\text{BaseFee}$ is **burned** (destroyed), and the $\text{PriorityTip}$ goes to the validator.

**Worked Example:** A transaction uses 21,000 gas at 50 Gwei.
$\text{TxFee} = 21{,}000 \times 50 = 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 0.00105 \text{ ETH}$.

---

**Q.B (b)** Explain the Ethereum state transition function. How is a block's final state computed from a sequence of transactions? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The Ethereum state transition function is defined formally in the Yellow Paper as:

$$
\sigma' = \Pi(\sigma, T)
$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Current world state
- $T$ = Transaction
- $\Pi$ = State transition function
- $\sigma'$ = New world state

**Block-Level State Transition:**
For a block $B$ containing $N$ transactions, the final state is computed as:

$$
\sigma_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Pi(\sigma_{i-1}, T_i)
$$

**Step-by-Step Process (3 steps for 3 marks):**
1. **Pre-Validation** — Check signature, nonce, and sender balance.
2. **Gas Deduction** — Subtract intrinsic gas cost × gas price from sender's balance.
3. **EVM Execution** — Run the transaction; update balances, storage, and create logs.

**Why It's Important:** This deterministic, atomic state transition is what allows thousands of nodes to **independently verify** and reach consensus on the same global state.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Wei vs Gwei vs ETH** — Wrong conversion = up to **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing "SHA-3" instead of "Keccak-256"** — Ethereum's hash is a **pre-standardized variant** of Keccak, not NIST SHA-3. This is a favorite trick question!
> 3. **Forgetting the gas limit** — Gas is mandatory, not optional, in the fee formula.
> 4. **Calling EOA "User Account"** — Always use the formal term "Externally Owned Account".
> 5. **Mixing up account-based vs UTXO model** — Ethereum is **account-based**; Bitcoin is **UTXO-based**. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Not mentioning EIP-1559 base fee burn mechanism** — This is a frequent **2-mark short question**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Quick Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**

- [ ] **Ethereum** = Programmable blockchain + EVM + Smart Contracts.
- [ ] **Two account types:** EOA (key-controlled) and Contract Account (code-controlled).
- [ ] **EOA** can initiate transactions; **Contract** cannot — it only reacts.
- [ ] **EVM** = 256-bit stack machine, sandboxed, deterministic, quasi-Turing complete.
- [ ] **Gas** = Anti-abuse mechanism that solves the Halting Problem.
- [ ] **Tx Fee Formula:** $\text{Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$.
- [ ] **EIP-1559 (London Fork):** $\text{GasPrice} = \text{BaseFee (burned)} + \text{PriorityTip (to validator)}$.
- [ ] **State Transition:** $\sigma' = \Pi(\sigma, T)$.
- [ ] **World State** = Merkle Patricia Trie of all accounts.
- [ ] **Keccak-256** is Ethereum's hash (NOT standard SHA-3).
- [ ] **Address Derivation:** Last 20 bytes of Keccak-256 of the public key.
- [ ] **Block Time ≈ 12 seconds** (vs Bitcoin's 10 minutes).
- [ ] **Post-Merge Consensus:** Proof-of-Stake via Beacon Chain (Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST).
- [ ] **Block Gas Limit ≈ 30 million gas** (post-London fork).
- [ ] **Wei** = $10^{-18}$ ETH, **Gwei** = $10^{-9}$ ETH — **MEMORIZE THIS**.
- [ ] **Ethereum is account-based, NOT UTXO-based** — the most common confusant.
- [ ] **Block Reward** = Static reward + Tx Fees (no uncle rewards post-Merge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for the Exam:**
> *"Ethereum is a **world computer** running an **EVM**, where **EOAs** pay **gas** to trigger **state transitions** recorded in a **Merkle Patricia Trie**, secured by **PoS validators** on the **Beacon Chain**."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of the Ethereum Ecosystem - Keys and Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components of the Ethereum Ecosystem - Keys and Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Keys and Addresses in Ethereum?

In the Ethereum ecosystem, every externally owned account (EOA) is mathematically governed by a pair of cryptographic keys: a **Private Key** and a **Public Key**, from which a unique **Ethereum Address** is derived. Together, these three artifacts form the identity layer of Ethereum — they are the user's *signature*, *identity*, and *receiving endpoint* rolled into a single deterministic chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An Ethereum **account** is a 20-byte identifier (the *address*) paired with cryptographic state consisting of a 256-bit **private key** $k$ and its corresponding **public key** $K = k \cdot G$, where $G$ is the base point of the **secp256k1** elliptic curve. The address is the lower 160 bits of the Keccak-256 hash of the public key.

### 1.2 The Three Building Blocks at a Glance

| Artifact | Length | Format | Visibility | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Private Key $k$ | **256 bits** (32 bytes) | 64 hex characters | **SECRET** | Signs transactions; never shared |
| Public Key $K$ | 512 bits (64 bytes) | 128 hex chars (uncompressed) | Public | Verified against signatures |
| Ethereum Address | 160 bits (20 bytes) | 40 hex chars, prefixed `0x` | Public | Acts as account "account number" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Ethereum uses the **secp256k1** elliptic curve (same as Bitcoin) and the **Keccak-256** hash function (the original Keccak, **not** the final NIST SHA-3 standard). This distinction is a frequent KTU exam trap.

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy: Your Blockchain House

Imagine each Ethereum user owns a **transparent glass mailbox** sitting on a global street called the *Ethereum State Machine*:

- **The Address** (`0xAbC...1234`) is the **mailbox number** painted on the box. Everyone can see it, and anyone in the world can drop a letter (transaction) into it.
- **The Public Key** is the **mailbox's verification certificate** that proves the box belongs to the cryptographic identity behind the scene.
- **The Private Key** is the **physical key you hold in your pocket** — the only object in the universe that can open the mailbox and authorize outgoing mail. Lose it, and your mail is locked forever. Share it, and a thief can send mail pretending to be you.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The address is *one-way derived* from the public key, which is *one-way derived* from the private key via **elliptic curve scalar multiplication** (a trapdoor function). You cannot reverse-engineer the private key from the address — this is the bedrock of Ethereum's security model.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Ethereum

Unlike Bitcoin, Ethereum accounts are **not just balance ledgers**. They contain four fields:

$$ \text{Account State} = (n, \, \sigma, \, \text{code}, \, \text{storage}) $$

where $n$ is the **nonce**, $\sigma$ is the **ether balance**, `code` is the contract bytecode (empty for EOAs), and `storage` is the persistent key-value trie. The address is the **primary key** into the global state trie, and the private key is the **only authority** allowed to transition that state via signed transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes that **Keys and Addresses are the on-chain identity primitive** of Ethereum — without them, there is no signer, no EOA, and no transaction origin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cryptographic Stack Behind Ethereum Identities

Ethereum identity is built on three pillars of modern applied cryptography, layered as follows:

1. **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (secp256k1)** → derives public keys from private keys
2. **Keccak-256 Hashing** → compresses public keys into fixed-size digests
3. **Encoding & Checksum Schemes** → formats the digest into a usable, error-detectable address

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

#### Step A — Private Key Generation
- A private key $k$ is a randomly chosen integer in the range:
$$ 1 \le k < n $$
where $n$ is the **order of the secp256k1 group**:
$$ n = 2^{256} - 2^{128} - \text{0x14551231950B75FC4402DA1732FC9BEBF} $$
- Generated using a **CSPRNG** (Cryptographically Secure Pseudorandom Number Generator). Modern wallets use the OS entropy source (`/dev/urandom` on Linux, `BCryptGenRandom` on Windows).
- The search space is approximately $2^{256} \approx 10^{77}$ — comparable to the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 256 bits?** A 256-bit key provides **128 bits of security** under the best-known discrete-log attacks on secp256k1 (Pollard's rho), making brute force computationally infeasible even with nation-state adversaries.

#### Step B — Public Key Derivation
- Public key is obtained by **elliptic curve scalar multiplication**:
$$ K = k \cdot G $$
where $G$ is the standardized base point (generator) of secp256k1.
- Geometrically, this is "add $G$ to itself $k$ times" using the chord-and-tangent rule.
- The result $K$ is a point $(x, y)$ on the curve, encoded as an **uncompressed 65-byte** string: `0x04` prefix + 32-byte $x$ + 32-byte $y$.
- For Ethereum addresses, only the **64 bytes** of $(x \parallel y)$ are used (the 0x04 prefix is discarded).

#### Step C — Address Derivation (The Keccak-256 Truncation)
1. Drop the `0x04` prefix from the uncompressed public key → 64 bytes $(x \parallel y)$.
2. Compute the **Keccak-256** hash of these 64 bytes:
$$ h = \text{Keccak256}(x \parallel y) \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{32-byte digest} $$
3. Take the **rightmost (least-significant) 20 bytes** of $h$:
$$ \text{address\_bytes} = h[12..31] $$
4. Prefix with `0x` → the **raw lowercase Ethereum address** (40 hex chars).
$$ A = \text{0x} \;\Vert\; \text{hex}(h[12..31]) $$

#### Step D — EIP-55 Checksum Encoding (Capitalization Layer)
- The lowercase address is **ambiguous** — a single typo can silently send funds to a wrong address.
- **EIP-55** introduces a mixed-case checksum:
  1. Compute $\text{Keccak256}(\text{lowercase hex of address without "0x"})$.
  2. For each character in the hex string, if the corresponding hex digit of the hash is $\ge 8$, **uppercase** the character; otherwise leave it lowercase.
- The capitalization carries no semantic information for the EVM (it accepts all-lowercase), but wallets reject mismatched checksums to catch typos.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EIP-55 is OPTIONAL but strongly recommended.** EIP-1191 later extended this with chain-id-aware checksums for cross-chain replay protection.

#### Step E — Address Variants and Legacy Formats
| Format | Standard | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Raw 20-byte | — | Lowest-level form used by the EVM |
| Hex `0x` | — | Standard 40-char hex string |
| **EIP-55** | EIP-55 | Mixed-case checksummed |
| **ICAP** | EIP-50 | International Bank Account Number-style; rarely used |
| **ENS Name** | EIP-137 | Human-readable `vitalik.eth` resolved via ENS registry |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cryptographic Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every numerical constant, equation, and boundary value you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Master this and you master the question bank.

| Concept | Equation / Constant | Value / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| secp256k1 curve | $y^{2} \equiv x^{3} + 7 \pmod{p}$ | **No B coefficient**; deliberately simple |
| Field prime $p$ | $2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977$ | Defines the finite field $\mathbb{F}_{p}$ |
| Group order $n$ | $2^{256} - 2^{128} - 0\text{x}14551231950B75FC4402DA1732FC9BEBF$ | Order of the cyclic subgroup |
| Generator point $G$ | $(G_{x}, G_{y})$ | $G_{x} = 0\text{x}79BE667E...$ fixed by SECG |
| Private key range | $1 \le k < n$ | Never use $k = 0$ |
| Public key size | 64 bytes $(x \parallel y)$ | `0x04`-prefixed 65 bytes raw |
| Hash function | $\text{Keccak256}$ | **NOT** NIST SHA-3-256 (different padding) |
| Address size | 20 bytes | = rightmost 160 bits of Keccak-256 output |
| Checksum rule (EIP-55) | Uppercase iff hash hex digit $\ge 8$ | Per character, position-mapped |
| Total key space | $2^{256}$ | Effective security $\approx 2^{128}$ |
| Address entropy | $2^{160}$ | 48 bits short of hash output (truncation) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The key/address pipeline is not academic — it powers:

- **Hardware Wallets (Ledger, Trezor)**: Store the private key in a Secure Element; never expose it to the host computer.
- **MetaMask / Browser Wallets**: Generate keys in-browser via `crypto.getRandomValues`; encrypt them into a **keystore V3 JSON file** using a user passphrase (scrypt + AES-128-CTR).
- **Layer-2 Rollups (Optimism, Arbitrum)**: Use the same key material to sign L2 transactions; address format is *identical* on L2.
- **Multisig Wallets (Gnosis Safe)**: Each owner is a regular EOA; the Safe contract checks `m-of-n` signatures via `ecrecover`.
- **Account Abstraction (ERC-4337)**: Future direction where the "private key" may be replaced by biometric signatures, smart contract logic, or social recovery — but the address remains the 20-byte identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** Because Keccak-256 is a one-way function and secp256k1 scalar multiplication is a trapdoor, the entire security of an Ethereum account reduces to the secrecy of the 32-byte private key. **There is no customer support, no password reset, no recovery email.**

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — From Private Key to Ethereum Address

We will work through the complete pipeline with every algebraic and binary step explicitly stated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Canonical Demonstration**
> Private key chosen: $k = \text{0x}0\text{x}4C0883A69102937D6231471DA5D2B3F0A65B3F4A3B0E8A2C5F4D3B2A1E0F1234$ (a valid 256-bit test value).

### Step 1 — Validate the Private Key Range

$$ k = \text{0x}4C0883A69102937D6231471DA5D2B3F0A65B3F4A3B0E8A2C5F4D3B2A1E0F1234 $$

$$ n = 2^{256} - 2^{128} - 0\text{x}14551231950B75FC4402DA1732FC9BEBF $$

We require $1 \le k < n$. Since $k$ is a 256-bit value and $n$ is also a 256-bit value with $k$ well below the maximum, the constraint is satisfied. (The probability of a random 256-bit number exceeding $n$ is roughly $2^{-128}$, negligible in practice.)

### Step 2 — Compute the Public Key $K = k \cdot G$

The secp256k1 base point is the standardized generator:

$$ G = (G_{x}, \, G_{y}) $$

$$ G_{x} = \text{0x}79\text{BE}667\text{E}F9\text{DC}BBAC55A06295CE870B07029\text{BF}CDB2\text{DC}E28D959F2815B16F $$

$$ G_{y} = \text{0x}483\text{ADA}7726\text{A}3\text{C}4655\text{DA}4\text{FBFC}0\text{E}1108\text{A}8\text{FD}17B448A68554199\text{C}47\text{D}08\text{FFB}10\text{D}4B8 $$

Scalar multiplication is performed using the **double-and-add algorithm**:

$$ K = k \cdot G = \underbrace{G + G + \cdots + G}_{k \,\text{times}} $$

For pedagogical clarity, the algorithm decomposes $k$ into binary and iteratively applies point doubling ($2P$) and point addition ($P + Q$) on the curve:

$$ 2P = \left( \lambda^{2} - 2x_{P}, \; \lambda(3x_{P}^{2} + a) - y_{P} \cdot \lambda^{-1} \right) \quad \text{on } y^{2} = x^{3} + ax + b $$

The result is a curve point $K = (K_{x}, K_{y})$ — both are 256-bit integers mod $p$.

The uncompressed SEC1 encoding:

$$ K_{\text{raw}} = \text{0x}04 \;\Vert\; K_{x}\,\text{(32 bytes)} \;\Vert\; K_{y}\,\text{(32 bytes)} \quad \text{(65 bytes total)} $$

### Step 3 — Strip the 0x04 Prefix

$$ K_{\text{strip}} = K_{x} \;\Vert\; K_{y} \quad \text{(64 bytes)} $$

### Step 4 — Apply Keccak-256

$$ h = \text{Keccak256}(K_{\text{strip}}) $$

$$ h = \text{0x}5\text{be}1\text{cd}2\text{f}1\text{ec}6\text{ce}0\text{c}09\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e} $$

(This is an illustrative 32-byte output; the actual hash depends on $k$.)

### Step 5 — Take the Last 20 Bytes

$$ \text{address\_bytes} = h[12 \ldots 31] \quad \text{(the rightmost 20 bytes)} $$

$$ \text{address\_bytes} = \text{0x}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e} $$

### Step 6 — Prefix with 0x

$$ A_{\text{lower}} = \text{0x}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e} $$

This is the **lowercase raw Ethereum address**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — EIP-55 Checksum Capitalization

Given the lowercase address (without the `0x` prefix):

$$ \text{hex} = \text{8d4c1d4d5e8d4c1d4d5e8d4c1d4d5e8d4c1d4d5e} $$

(40 hex characters; each character is one of `0–9`, `a–f`.)

### Step 1 — Hash the Lowercase Hex

$$ H = \text{Keccak256}(\text{hex}_{\text{lower}}) $$

$$ H = \text{0x} \underbrace{3F2A\ldots}_{32 \text{ bytes, 64 hex chars}} $$

### Step 2 — Iterate Character by Character

For each of the 40 characters at position $i$ in `hex`, look at the corresponding hex digit at position $i$ in $H$. If that digit is $\ge 8$, uppercase the character at position $i$ in the address.

**Tabular mapping** (illustrative excerpt for the first 10 characters):

| Position $i$ | Address char $c_{i}$ | Hash digit $h_{i}$ | Is $h_{i} \ge 8$? | Final $c_{i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `8` | `3` | No | `8` |
| 1 | `d` | `F` | Yes | `D` |
| 2 | `4` | `2` | No | `4` |
| 3 | `c` | `A` | Yes | `C` |
| 4 | `1` | `5` | No | `1` |
| 5 | `d` | `6` | No | `d` |
| 6 | `4` | `9` | Yes | `4` (digits unchanged) |
| 7 | `d` | `0` | No | `d` |
| 8 | `5` | `B` | Yes | `5` (digits unchanged) |
| 9 | `e` | `8` | Yes | `E` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** The rule applies the **uppercase transformation only to letters a–f**. Decimal digits `0–9` are never recased, even if the corresponding hash digit is high.

### Step 3 — Reassemble the Checksummed Address

$$ A_{\text{checksum}} = \text{0x}8\text{D}4\text{C}1\text{d}4\text{D}5\text{E}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e}8\text{d}4\text{c}1\text{d}4\text{d}5\text{e} $$

This is the final **EIP-55 checksummed Ethereum address**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — The Address-to-State Lookup

When a transaction is sent to address $A$, the EVM resolves it via the **Merkle Patricia Trie** (state trie):

$$ \text{StateRoot} \xrightarrow{\text{lookup}(A)} \text{AccountState}(A) = (n, \, \sigma, \, \text{code}, \, \text{storage}) $$

**Binary path encoding of an address:**

Each byte of the 20-byte address is split into **two 4-bit nibbles**, and each nibble becomes a level in the trie:

$$ A = (a_{0}, a_{1}, \ldots, a_{19}) \quad \text{where each } a_{i} \in [0, 15] $$

$$ \text{trie\_path} = (\text{high}(a_{0}), \text{low}(a_{0}), \text{high}(a_{1}), \text{low}(a_{1}), \ldots, \text{high}(a_{19}), \text{low}(a_{19})) $$

This gives a 40-level-deep path through the hexary trie, uniquely identifying the account node in the global state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** This is why Ethereum uses hex-prefix (HP) encoding for trie nodes — it is collision-free and efficiently distinguishes between "extension" and "leaf" nodes at every level.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Ethereum Identity Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Safeguard:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ENTROPY SOURCE - CSPRNG] --> A1[PRIVATE KEY K - 256 BIT INTEGER]
    A1 --> A2{VALIDATE K RANGE - 1 LE K LESS THAN N}
    A2 -->|INVALID| A3[REJECT - REGENERATE]
    A2 -->|VALID| A4[PUBLIC KEY COMPUTATION - EC MULT BY G]
    A4 --> A5[UNCOMPRESSED PUBKEY - 65 BYTES 04 XY]
    A5 --> A6[STRIP 0x04 PREFIX - 64 BYTES]
    A6 --> A7[HASH WITH KECCAK256 - 32 BYTE DIGEST]
    A7 --> A8[TAKE LAST 20 BYTES - TRUNCATE]
    A8 --> A9[LOWERCASE RAW ADDRESS - 0X PREFIX]
    A9 --> A10[EIP55 CHECKSUM HASH - KECCAK256 OF LOWERCASE HEX]
    A10 --> A11[APPLY CAPITALIZATION RULE - DIGIT GE 8]
    A11 --> A12[FINAL CHECKSUMMED ADDRESS - 40 HEX CHARS]
    A12 --> A13[STATE TRIE LOOKUP - ACCOUNT STATE NODE]
    A1 --> A14[SIGN TRANSACTION - ECDSA SIGN OPERATION]
    A14 --> A15[TX R S V - SIGNATURE TRIPLET]
    A15 --> A16[ECRECOVER - VERIFY ON CHAIN]
    A16 --> A17[SIGNER ADDRESS DERIVED - MATCHES EOA]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture Map — Wallet Storage Layers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[USER PASSPHRASE] --> B1[SCRYPT KDF - N IS 262144 R 8 P 1]
    B1 --> B2[DERIVED ENCRYPTION KEY - 32 BYTES]
    B2 --> B3[AES128 CTR MODE - CIPHERTEXT]
    B3 --> B4[KEYSTORE V3 JSON - UTF8 FILE]
    B4 --> B5[USER STORAGE - DISK OR ICLOUD]
    B5 --> B6[DECRYPTION ON DEMAND - WALLET UNLOCK]
    B6 --> B1
    B6 --> A1[PRIVATE KEY K IN MEMORY]
    A1 --> A4[PUBLIC KEY COMPUTATION]
    A4 --> A12[FINAL CHECKSUMMED ADDRESS]
```

### 4.3 The Verification Loop — ECDSA Signature Round-Trip

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[TX HASH - 32 BYTES] --> C1[PRIVATE KEY K - SIGNS]
    C1 --> C2[ECDSA SIGN - PRODUCE R S]
    C2 --> C3[SIGNATURE TRIPLET - R S V]
    C3 --> C4[BROADCAST TX - WITH SIGNATURE]
    C4 --> C5[EVM RECEIVES TX]
    C5 --> C6[ECRECOVER PRECOMPILE - INPUT HASH R S V]
    C6 --> C7[RECOVERED PUBLIC KEY - 64 BYTES]
    C7 --> C8[ADDRESS DERIVATION - KECCAK256 TRUNCATE]
    C8 --> C9[RECOVERED ADDRESS - COMPARED TO TX FROM]
    C9 -->|MATCH| C10[VALID SIGNATURE - EXECUTE TX]
    C9 -->|MISMATCH| C11[INVALID SIGNATURE - REVERT]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The three diagrams together form a closed loop — keys feed addresses, addresses authorize signing, signatures verify back to the originating address. Any break in this chain (a leaked private key, a malformed signature, a mismatched recovery byte) causes the entire transaction to revert at the EVM level.

### 4.4 Why Mermaid Was Chosen Over Physical Drawings

For cryptographic pipelines, physical drawings (hand-drawn flow charts) are pedagogically inferior to functional block diagrams because:

- The **directionality** of the transformation (private → public → address) is one-way and irreversible — this must be visually unambiguous.
- The **checksum feedback loop** in EIP-55 is a second hash applied to the output of the first — a small but easy-to-miss step.
- The **signature verification loop** (sign locally, verify on-chain via `ecrecover`) closes the round-trip in a way that no static drawing conveys.

Hence we use **block-level functional architecture** to capture the **data flow and trust boundary** between the user's local device and the Ethereum Virtual Machine.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between an Ethereum Externally Owned Account (EOA) and a Contract Account. Mention the role of private keys in this context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | EOA | Contract Account |
|---|---|---|
| Controlled by | **Private key holder** | Contract code (no private key) |
| Has associated code | No | Yes (bytecode + storage) |
| Initiates transactions | Yes | Yes (via `CALL`/`DELEGATECALL`) |
| Creation | Free (key generation) | Costs gas |

**Role of private keys:** The private key is the *sole* cryptographic authority for an EOA. It is used to sign transactions via the **ECDSA** algorithm on the secp256k1 curve, producing a signature $(r, s, v)$ that the EVM verifies using the `ecrecover` precompiled contract (address `0x01`). The recovered address is matched against the `tx.origin` or `msg.sender` field to authorize the transaction. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the Keccak-256 hash function in deriving an Ethereum address from a public key.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Keccak-256 hash function plays a **two-step role** in Ethereum address derivation:

1. **Compression and one-way mapping:** Keccak-256 takes the 64-byte public key $(x \parallel y)$ and produces a fixed-size 32-byte digest. This is a one-way (trapdoor) function — it is computationally infeasible to recover the public key from the hash.
2. **Truncation for fixed-length identity:** The rightmost 20 bytes of the Keccak-256 digest are taken to form the 160-bit Ethereum address, which serves as the account's primary key in the global state trie.

**Crucial distinction:** Ethereum uses the original **Keccak-256** (the algorithm submitted to the NIST SHA-3 competition), **not** the finalized SHA-3-256 standard — the two differ in their padding rules, producing different outputs for the same input. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-end ESE questions carry 14 marks and present an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both options below are self-contained, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the complete pipeline of deriving an Ethereum address from a private key. Mention the cryptographic primitives used at each stage. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Ethereum address derivation pipeline consists of four distinct cryptographic stages:

**Stage 1 — Private Key Generation**
- A 256-bit random integer $k$ is sampled uniformly from $[1, n-1]$ using a CSPRNG.
- Constraint: $k \neq 0$ and $k < n$, where $n$ is the secp256k1 group order.
- The output is a 32-byte secret.

**Stage 2 — Public Key Derivation via Elliptic Curve Scalar Multiplication**
- Compute $K = k \cdot G$ on the secp256k1 curve $y^{2} = x^{3} + 7 \pmod{p}$.
- $G$ is the fixed generator point.
- Result is a curve point $K = (K_{x}, K_{y})$, each coordinate a 256-bit integer.
- Encoded uncompressed as `0x04 ‖ Kx ‖ Ky` (65 bytes).

**Stage 3 — Hash and Truncate**
- Discard the `0x04` prefix → 64 bytes.
- Compute $h = \text{Keccak256}(K_{x} \parallel K_{y})$ → 32-byte digest.
- Take the last 20 bytes: $\text{address\_bytes} = h[12..31]$.

**Stage 4 — Format and Checksum**
- Prefix with `0x` → lowercase raw address.
- Apply **EIP-55** mixed-case checksum for error detection.
- The final 40-hex-character string is the canonical Ethereum address.

**Pipeline diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D0[PRIVATE KEY K 32 BYTES] --> D1[EC MULT K TIMES G]
    D1 --> D2[PUBKEY K 64 BYTES NO PREFIX]
    D2 --> D3[KECCAK256 HASH 32 BYTES]
    D3 --> D4[TAKE LAST 20 BYTES]
    D4 --> D5[RAW LOWERCASE ADDRESS]
    D5 --> D6[EIP55 CHECKSUM APPLIED]
    D6 --> D7[FINAL ETHEREUM ADDRESS]
```

**Cryptographic primitives summary:** (i) **secp256k1 ECDSA** for key pair generation, (ii) **Keccak-256** sponge construction for hashing, (iii) **EIP-55** capitalization rule for checksum. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Discuss the security implications of the truncation step in address derivation. Why is the address only 20 bytes when the hash output is 32 bytes? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The 160-bit address space:**

The Keccak-256 hash produces 256 bits of output, but only the lower 160 bits are retained. This means the address space is $2^{160} \approx 1.46 \times 10^{48}$ possible addresses.

**Security implications:**

1. **Birthday attack on collision:** By the birthday paradox, collisions become probable after $\sqrt{2^{160}} = 2^{80}$ addresses. While $2^{80}$ is currently computationally infeasible (estimated cost exceeds the GDP of Earth for years of Bitcoin mining), it is *theoretically* weaker than the full $2^{256}$ hash output.
2. **Pre-image resistance is preserved:** It is still infeasible to find a public key whose Keccak-256 hash truncates to a target 160-bit address. The effective security is approximately $2^{160}$ operations — beyond any classical computer's reach.
3. **Quantum threat:** A sufficiently powerful quantum computer running Grover's algorithm could achieve $2^{80}$ operations against a 160-bit pre-image, which is *within* theoretical reach. This is one reason Ethereum's roadmap (EIP-4337, post-quantum signatures) considers future migration.
4. **Efficiency vs security trade-off:** 20 bytes (40 hex chars) is short enough to be human-readable and cheap to store in the state trie, while being long enough to make random-address collisions astronomically improbable for everyday use.

**Why 20 bytes specifically?**

- **Bitcoin influence:** Bitcoin uses HASH160 (RIPEMD-160 of SHA-256) for the same 160-bit address size. Ethereum inherited this choice for compatibility with existing infrastructure and to keep state trie paths short.
- **Sufficient entropy:** $2^{160}$ is far larger than the number of accounts any single blockchain will ever hold — even with mass adoption, the number of EOAs will not exceed $2^{40}$.
- **Cost:** Each additional byte in the address increases the storage cost of every account by 8 gas (for SSTORE), so shorter addresses save gas across the entire state.

**Conclusion:** The 20-byte truncation is a deliberate engineering compromise between **security, storage efficiency, and compatibility**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the EIP-55 checksum mechanism for Ethereum addresses. Provide the algorithm and discuss its importance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Background:**
Before EIP-55, Ethereum addresses were case-insensitive and had no built-in error detection. A single typo (e.g., `0xAbc...` instead of `0xaBc...`) could send funds to a different, unrecoverable address.

**EIP-55 Algorithm:**

1. Take the lowercase hex representation of the address (excluding the `0x` prefix) — a 40-character string.
2. Compute $\text{Keccak256}$ of this lowercase string → 32-byte hash $H$.
3. Convert $H$ to a 64-character hex string.
4. For each character $c_{i}$ at position $i$ (where $0 \le i < 40$) in the address:
   - If $c_{i}$ is a letter (a–f) AND the $i$-th hex digit of $H$ is $\ge 8$, **uppercase** $c_{i}$.
   - Otherwise, leave $c_{i}$ unchanged.
5. Prepend `0x` to obtain the checksummed address.

**Pseudocode (KTU expects conceptual clarity, not code execution):**

$$ A_{\text{checksum}} = \text{0x} \;\Vert\; \bigoplus_{i=0}^{39} \; f(c_{i}, h_{i}) $$

where:

$$ f(c_{i}, h_{i}) = \begin{cases} \text{upper}(c_{i}) & \text{if } c_{i} \in \{a..f\} \text{ and } h_{i} \ge 8 \\ c_{i} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Properties:**

- **Case-preserving for digits:** Digits 0–9 are never uppercased.
- **Length-preserving:** Always exactly 40 hex chars + `0x`.
- **EVM-compatible:** The EVM treats the address as case-insensitive, so the checksum is purely a *client-side* validation feature.

**Importance:**

1. **Error detection:** A typo that changes the capitalization of at least one letter invalidates the checksum on re-verification. Approximately **99.986%** of common typos (single-character insertions, deletions, substitutions) are detected.
2. **No on-chain cost:** Checksum verification is done by the wallet UI before broadcasting — no gas is consumed.
3. **Standardization:** All major wallets (MetaMask, MyEtherWallet, Ledger Live) implement EIP-55.

**Limitations:** EIP-55 is not cryptographically secure — it is a *probabilistic* error detection scheme, not a checksum in the CRC sense. A determined attacker can craft two addresses that differ in only their capitalization and have the same hash — but such addresses are computationally infeasible to construct (≈ $2^{160}$ work to find a collision). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Describe the structure of an Ethereum Keystore V3 file. How is the private key protected? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Keystore V3 File — Overview:**

A Keystore V3 file is a **JSON document** that stores an **encrypted version of the private key**, protected by a user-supplied passphrase. It is the standard format used by early Ethereum wallets (MyEtherWallet, Geth pre-HD era).

**Structure (Key Fields):**

| Field | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `address` | hex string | The Ethereum address (lowercase, with `0x`) |
| `crypto.cipher` | string | Symmetric cipher used — typically `"aes-128-ctr"` |
| `crypto.ciphertext` | hex string | The encrypted private key |
| `crypto.cipherparams.iv` | hex string | 16-byte initialization vector |
| `crypto.kdf` | string | Key derivation function — typically `"scrypt"` |
| `crypto.kdfparams` | object | Parameters: `dklen`, `salt`, `n`, `r`, `p` |
| `crypto.mac` | hex string | HMAC-SHA3 of the ciphertext, ensures integrity |
| `id` | UUID | Unique wallet identifier |
| `version` | number | `3` for V3 keystores |

**Encryption Pipeline:**

1. **Key derivation:** The user passphrase is fed into **scrypt** (a memory-hard KDF) with parameters:
$$ (dkLen, n, r, p, \text{salt}) = (32, 262144, 8, 1, \text{16-byte random salt}) $$
   The output is a 64-byte derived key. The first 32 bytes become the AES-128-CTR encryption key; the last 32 bytes become the HMAC-SHA3 verification key.
2. **Encryption:** The 32-byte private key is encrypted with **AES-128-CTR** using the derived encryption key and a random 16-byte IV. The result is the `ciphertext`.
3. **Integrity:** $\text{hmac} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{derived\_mac\_key} \parallel \text{ciphertext})$ — this is the `mac` field. Decryption first verifies the MAC, preventing padding-oracle and tampering attacks.

**Security Properties:**

- **Brute-force resistance:** The scrypt parameters ($n = 2^{18}$, $r = 8$, $p = 1$) make a single passphrase guess cost ~100 ms and ~64 MB of RAM — billions of dollars of hardware cannot parallelize this efficiently.
- **Tamper evidence:** Any modification to `ciphertext` invalidates the MAC.
- **Portability:** The JSON file can be stored on a USB drive, in cloud storage, or printed as a QR code — the encryption is self-contained.

**Why scrypt and not PBKDF2?** Scrypt is **memory-hard**, designed to resist GPU/ASIC brute-force attacks that devastated PBKDF2-secured wallets in the early 2010s. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Keccak-256 with SHA-3-256.** These are **NOT** identical. Ethereum uses the original Keccak with `0x01` padding, not the NIST-standardized SHA-3 with `0x06` padding. Hashes of the same input differ. **Examiner strictness: −1 mark** if you write "SHA-3" without qualification.
>
> 2. **Confusing Bitcoin's HASH160 with Ethereum's address derivation.** Bitcoin uses `RIPEMD160(SHA256(pubkey))`. Ethereum uses `Keccak256(pubkey)[12:]`. Examiner will deduct 2 marks for mixing these up.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 0x04 prefix strip.** Many students write `Keccak256(0x04 ‖ Kx ‖ Ky)` and lose 1 mark. The correct input is **only** the 64 bytes of $(K_{x} \parallel K_{y})$.
>
> 4. **Writing "EIP-55 is a security feature"** — it is an *error-detection* feature, not cryptographic security. Examiners will mark this as imprecise.
>
> 5. **Omitting the `0x` prefix in the final address.** The address is `0x` + 40 hex chars = 42 chars total. Writing just 40 chars loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the asymmetric nature of the EIP-55 rule.** Decimals (0–9) are never uppercased. Many students apply the rule to all characters blindly — examiner penalty applies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Your Wall**

- ✅ Ethereum identity = **Private Key (256-bit)** + **Public Key (512-bit uncompressed)** + **Address (160-bit)**.
- ✅ Private key range: $1 \le k < n$, where $n \approx 2^{256}$.
- ✅ Curve: **secp256k1**, defined by $y^{2} = x^{3} + 7 \pmod{p}$ with $p = 2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977$.
- ✅ Public key derivation: $K = k \cdot G$ via **elliptic curve scalar multiplication** (double-and-add).
- ✅ Hash function: **Keccak-256** (NOT SHA-3-256, NOT SHA-256).
- ✅ Address = last 20 bytes of Keccak-256(public key without 0x04 prefix).
- ✅ EIP-55 checksum: uppercase letter iff corresponding Keccak-256 hash hex digit $\ge 8$.
- ✅ EIP-55 is **case-insensitive for the EVM** but **error-detecting for wallets**.
- ✅ Keystore V3 uses **scrypt** (KDF) + **AES-128-CTR** (cipher) + **HMAC-SHA3** (integrity).
- ✅ ECDSA signature = $(r, s, v)$ triple; `ecrecover` precompile at `0x01` recovers signer.
- ✅ Address space: $2^{160}$; effective security $\approx 2^{160}$ against classical pre-image attacks.
- ✅ Quantum threat: Grover's algorithm reduces to $2^{80}$ — a long-term concern, not imminent.
- ✅ **No password reset, no recovery, no customer support** — security rests on private-key secrecy alone.
- ✅ Address variants: lowercase, EIP-55, ICAP (rare), ENS (resolver-based).
- ✅ EOA ≠ Contract Account — EOAs have private keys, contract accounts have code.
- ✅ Signing happens **off-chain**; verification happens **on-chain** via `ecrecover`.
- ✅ HD wallets (BIP-32/39/44) extend single seed to multiple key pairs — not in syllabus core but related.
- ✅ Remember: Address is the **primary key** of the global state trie; lookup uses nibble-wise path encoding.
- ✅ The 20-byte length is a **Bitcoin-era inheritance** for storage efficiency.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Keys and Addresses. Best of luck with your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accounts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accounts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Account in Blockchain?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> In the Ethereum protocol, an **Account** is a fundamental data structure that represents an entity on the blockchain. Every account possesses a **20-byte address** (160 bits) and a persistent state that is updated whenever a message call or transaction interacts with it. The entire state of the Ethereum network is fundamentally a mapping between account addresses and account states.

Mathematically, the global state can be expressed as:

$$
\Sigma : \mathbb{A} \rightarrow \mathbb{S}
$$

where:
- $\mathbb{A}$ = the set of all valid 160-bit account addresses
- $\mathbb{S}$ = the set of all possible account states
- $\Sigma$ = the global state transition function

There are precisely **two (2) types of accounts** in Ethereum:

| # | Account Type | Controlling Entity |
|---|--------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Externally Owned Account (EOA)** | Controlled by humans (private keys) |
| 2 | **Contract Account (CA)** | Controlled by code (smart contract logic) |
