## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Banking Analogy (Beginner-Friendly):**
> - An **EOA is like your personal bank locker** — YOU hold the physical key (private key) and only you can open it and move valuables (cryptocurrency/ETH).
> - A **Contract Account is like a vending machine** — A piece of code (the machine's wiring) decides what happens when you interact with it. You drop coins, the machine's logic automatically dispenses a product; no human or private key is required at that point.
> - Every locker (EOA) and every vending machine (CA) has a unique ID (the 20-byte address) that is publicly known.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** The *only* difference between an EOA and a Contract Account is the **codeHash** field. For an EOA, the codeHash is the hash of an empty string. For a CA, the codeHash contains the hash of the deployed EVM bytecode.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology & Metrics

- **Wei**: The smallest denomination of Ether. $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$
- **Gwei**: A common gas price unit. $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$
- **Nonce**: A monotonically increasing counter that prevents replay attacks.
- **Gas**: A unit measuring the computational effort required to execute operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Account State Structure (The Four Pillars)

Every Ethereum account, regardless of type, is defined by exactly four fields stored in the **State Trie** (a Modified Merkle Patricia Trie):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Account State = { nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash }**

Let us analyze each field with operational precision:

### 2.1.1 Nonce
- **For EOA:** Equals the number of transactions *sent* from this account. Starts at 0 and increments.
- **For Contract Account:** Equals the number of contracts *created* by this account.
- **Purpose:** Prevents replay attacks (a transaction with nonce $N$ cannot be executed twice).

### 2.1.2 Balance
- The number of **Wei** owned by this address.
- Minimum unit: **1 Wei**.
- Stored as a 256-bit unsigned integer.

### 2.1.3 Storage Root
- A 256-bit hash pointing to the **root node of the account's Merkle Patricia Trie**.
- For EOAs: This is the hash of an **empty trie**.
- For Contract Accounts: This contains the actual key-value storage of the smart contract.

### 2.1.4 Code Hash
- A 256-bit hash of the EVM bytecode.
- For EOAs: Hash of an **empty string** (`keccak256("")`).
- For Contract Accounts: Hash of the deployed smart contract logic.

## 2.2 Why Two Account Types? (Engineering Rationale)

| Feature | Externally Owned Account (EOA) | Contract Account (CA) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Has private key | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Initiates transactions | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only responds) |
| Code execution | ❌ No code | ✅ EVM bytecode |
| Gas cost to create | **0 gas** (off-chain) | **32,000+ gas** (on-chain) |
| Can call other contracts | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| State changes | Only nonce/balance | Full storage manipulation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are exam-critical. Memorize these constants exactly as shown.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Constant | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Total Transaction Fee | $C_{tx} = G_{used} \times P_{gas}$ | Wei |
| 2 | Wei ↔ ETH Conversion | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$ | — |
| 3 | Wei ↔ Gwei Conversion | $1 \text{ Gwei} = 10^9 \text{ Wei}$ | — |
| 4 | Base Transaction Cost | $G_{base} = 21{,}000$ | Gas |
| 5 | Contract Creation Base | $G_{create} = 32{,}000$ | Gas |
| 6 | Message Call Base | $G_{call} = 2{,}100$ | Gas |
| 7 | SSTORE (0 → non-zero) | $G_{sstore} = 20{,}000$ | Gas |
| 8 | SSTORE (modification) | $G_{sstore\_mod} = 5{,}000$ | Gas |
| 9 | SSTORE (non-zero → 0) | $G_{sstore\_clear} = 2{,}900$ | Gas (refund) |
| 10 | Calldata zero byte | $G_{zero} = 4$ | Gas/byte |
| 11 | Calldata non-zero byte | $G_{nonzero} = 16$ | Gas/byte |
| 12 | EVM Opcode execution | $G_{op} \in [1, 375]$ | Variable gas |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** When using absolute value or set notation in your exam answer sheet, **NEVER** use the `|` character inside markdown tables (it breaks the syntax). Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ as seen above.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

- **Wallet Development:** EOA mechanics drive MetaMask, Ledger, Trezor architectures.
- **Smart Contract Auditing:** Understanding `storageRoot` and `codeHash` is essential for proxy pattern security.
- **Gas Optimization:** Engineers use SSTORE cost formulas to minimize deployment costs.
- **Account Abstraction (EIP-4337):** Modern wallets use smart contract accounts (ERC-4337) — directly derived from understanding both account types.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Cost of a Transaction

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams. Let us derive the total cost of a standard ETH transfer (EOA → EOA) step by step.

### Problem Setup
A user Alice wants to send **0.5 ETH** to Bob, with a gas price of **50 Gwei** and the transaction contains **68 bytes of calldata** (all zero bytes).

### Step 1: Identify the Gas Components

The total gas used is the sum of three components:

$$
G_{used} = G_{base} + G_{data} + G_{execution}
$$

- $G_{base}$ = base transaction cost
- $G_{data}$ = calldata cost
- $G_{execution}$ = EVM execution cost

### Step 2: Calculate Base Cost

Since Alice is sending a simple ETH transfer (no contract interaction):

$$
G_{base} = 21{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

This is the **intrinsic gas** charged for any transaction.

### Step 3: Calculate Calldata Cost

Given 68 bytes of zero calldata, and zero byte cost = 4 gas:

$$
G_{data} = N_{zero} \times 4 + N_{nonzero} \times 16
$$

$$
G_{data} = 68 \times 4 + 0 \times 16
$$

$$
G_{data} = 272 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Execution Cost

For a simple ETH transfer, no EVM code is invoked:

$$
G_{execution} = 0 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 5: Sum the Gas Components

$$
G_{used} = 21{,}000 + 272 + 0
$$

$$
G_{used} = 21{,}272 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 6: Convert Gas Price to Wei

$$
P_{gas} = 50 \text{ Gwei} = 50 \times 10^9 \text{ Wei}
$$

$$
P_{gas} = 5 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wei}
$$

### Step 7: Apply the Master Formula

$$
C_{tx} = G_{used} \times P_{gas}
$$

$$
C_{tx} = 21{,}272 \times 5 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
C_{tx} = 1.0636 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}
$$

### Step 8: Convert Back to ETH

$$
C_{tx} = \frac{1.0636 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} \text{ ETH}
$$

$$
C_{tx} = 1.0636 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}
$$

### Final Answer

> **Total Transaction Fee = 0.0010636 ETH**

The amount debited from Alice's account = 0.5 + 0.0010636 = **0.5010636 ETH**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Contract Account Creation

### Problem Setup
A developer deploys a smart contract with **2,400 bytes** of compiled bytecode. Gas price = **20 Gwei**.

### Step 1: Identify Creation Cost Formula

$$
G_{create} = G_{base\_create} + G_{code} + G_{execution}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Code Storage Cost

For each non-zero byte: 16 gas; for zero byte: 4 gas.
Assume all 2,400 bytes are non-zero (worst case):

$$
G_{code} = 2400 \times 16 = 38{,}400 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Base Creation Cost

$$
G_{base\_create} = 32{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 4: Assume Constructor Execution Cost

Assume constructor consumes 50,000 gas:

$$
G_{execution} = 50{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 5: Sum the Components

$$
G_{create} = 32{,}000 + 38{,}400 + 50{,}000
$$

$$
G_{create} = 120{,}400 \text{ gas}
$$

### Step 6: Compute Final Cost

$$
C_{create} = 120{,}400 \times 20 \times 10^9
$$

$$
C_{create} = 2.408 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 0.002408 \text{ ETH}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EOA vs CA Creation

| Derivation Step | EOA | Contract Account |
|----------------|-----|------------------|
| Triggered by | Off-chain wallet | On-chain transaction |
| Base gas cost | 0 | 32,000 |
| Requires code | No | Yes |
| Storage cost | 0 | 16 gas/byte |
| Address generation | keccak256(pubkey)[12:32] | keccak256(rlp(sender, nonce))[12:32] |
| Visible in Mempool | Yes | Yes (during creation) |
| Failure rollbacks | N/A | All gas consumed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Account Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Blockchain Account] --> B[Type 1: EOA]
    A --> C[Type 2: Contract Account]
    
    B --> B1[nonce: 0,1,2...]
    B --> B2[balance: 256-bit Wei]
    B --> B3[storageRoot: empty trie hash]
    B --> B4[codeHash: keccak256 of empty string]
    B --> B5[Controlled by Private Key]
    
    C --> C1[nonce: contracts created]
    C --> C2[balance: 256-bit Wei]
    C --> C3[storageRoot: MPT root]
    C --> C4[codeHash: keccak256 of EVM bytecode]
    C --> C5[Controlled by Code Logic]
    
    B5 --> D1[Initiates Transactions]
    B5 --> D2[Signs with ECDSA]
    
    C5 --> E1[Cannot Initiate TX]
    C5 --> E2[Responds to Messages]
    C5 --> E3[Executes EVM Opcodes]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style B5 fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style C5 fill:#ef4444,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Transaction Lifecycle on an Account

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User Initiates Action]) --> P1{Account Type?}
    P1 -->|EOA| P2[Sign TX with Private Key]
    P1 -->|Contract| P3[Triggered by Message Call]
    
    P2 --> P4[Broadcast to Mempool]
    P3 --> P5[Execute EVM Bytecode]
    
    P4 --> P6[Validators Pick TX]
    P6 --> P7[Execute EVM]
    P7 --> P8{Deduct Gas}
    P8 --> P9[Update Nonce: n+1]
    P9 --> P10[Update Balance]
    P10 --> P11[Update Storage Root]
    P11 --> End([State Root Updated Globally])
    
    P5 --> P8
    
    style Start fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style End fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#fbbf24,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: State Trie Storage Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[State Root - Global] --> H1[Hash of Account 1]
    R --> H2[Hash of Account 2]
    
    H1 --> A1[Account A: EOA]
    H1 --> A2[Account B: EOA]
    H2 --> C1[Account C: Contract]
    
    A1 --> F1[nonce, balance, emptyRoot, emptyCodeHash]
    A2 --> F2[nonce, balance, emptyRoot, emptyCodeHash]
    C1 --> F3[nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash]
    
    F3 --> S1[Storage Trie: k1-v1, k2-v2...]
    
    style R fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style S1 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram: Account State Update

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Input_Phase [INPUT PHASE]
        I1[Previous State Sigma]
        I2[Transaction TX]
        I3[Block Info]
    end
    
    subgraph Transition [STATE TRANSITION FUNCTION]
        T1[Verify Signature]
        T2[Check Nonce Match]
        T3[Compute Gas Used]
        T4[Deduct Gas]
    end
    
    subgraph Output_Phase [OUTPUT PHASE]
        O1[New Nonce]
        O2[New Balance]
        O3[New Storage Root]
        O4[New Code Hash]
    end
    
    I1 --> T1
    I2 --> T1
    I3 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T4 --> O1
    T4 --> O2
    T4 --> O3
    T4 --> O4
    
    style Input_Phase fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style Transition fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style Output_Phase fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]
**Q: Differentiate between Externally Owned Accounts (EOA) and Contract Accounts (CA) in Ethereum. List any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EOA | Contract Account |
|-----------|-----|------------------|
| **Control** | Controlled by private key (human/user) | Controlled by code (smart contract) |
| **Code Field** | codeHash is the hash of empty string | codeHash contains hash of EVM bytecode |
| **Transaction Initiation** | Can initiate transactions | Cannot initiate transactions; only responds |
| **Creation Cost** | Free (off-chain) | Costs gas (32,000+ gas) |
| **Storage Root** | Hash of empty trie | Hash of populated storage trie |

*(Any three points = 3 marks; 1 mark each)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]
**Q: What is an account nonce? Explain its significance with respect to replay attack prevention.**

**Model Answer:**

A **nonce** is a scalar value equal to the number of transactions sent from an account (for EOAs) or the number of contracts created (for CAs).

**Significance (3 points):**
1. **Replay Attack Prevention:** Once a transaction with nonce $N$ is processed, any future attempt to re-broadcast the same transaction is rejected because the account's current nonce has already incremented to $N+1$.
2. **Ordering Guarantees:** Transactions from a single account are processed strictly in nonce order, preventing double-spending.
3. **State Consistency:** The nonce forms part of the account state's cryptographic commitment in the Merkle Patricia Trie.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### Question A: Full ESE-Style 14-Mark Question [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete account state structure in Ethereum with a neat diagram. Describe each of the four fields in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A user wants to send a transaction with the following parameters:
- Value transferred: 1.2 ETH
- Calldata: 100 bytes (all non-zero)
- Gas price: 30 Gwei
- Contract creation involved (constructor uses 80,000 gas)
- Base intrinsic gas: 21,000

Calculate the **total transaction cost in ETH**.

---

### Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Account State Structure:**

The Ethereum account state is a 4-tuple:

$$
\sigma[a] = (n, b, s, c)
$$

where:
- $a$ = 160-bit account address
- $n$ = **nonce** (transaction counter)
- $b$ = **balance** (in Wei)
- $s$ = **storageRoot** (256-bit hash of storage trie root)
- $c$ = **codeHash** (256-bit hash of contract code)

**Field-by-Field Explanation (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Nonce (n):** A scalar value tracking the number of transactions sent by an EOA or contracts created by a CA. Initialized to 0, incremented by 1 after each successful operation. Critical for preventing replay attacks.

2. **Balance (b):** A 256-bit unsigned integer representing the Wei (smallest ETH unit) owned by the account. Cannot be negative. Updated atomically with every value transfer.

3. **Storage Root (s):** A 256-bit cryptographic hash (Keccak-256) of the root node of the account's Merkle Patricia Trie. For EOAs, this is the hash of an empty trie. For CAs, it contains the persistent key-value storage of the contract.

4. **Code Hash (c):** A 256-bit hash of the EVM bytecode stored at this address. For EOAs, this is `keccak256("")`. For CAs, this points to the immutable deployed code.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the structural layout.

---

### Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $N_{nonzero} = 100$ bytes
- $N_{zero} = 0$ bytes
- $P_{gas} = 30 \text{ Gwei} = 3 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wei}$
- Contract creation base = 32,000 gas
- Constructor execution = 80,000 gas
- Base intrinsic = 21,000 gas

**Step 1: Compute Calldata Cost (1 mark)**

$$
G_{data} = N_{nonzero} \times 16 + N_{zero} \times 4
$$

$$
G_{data} = 100 \times 16 + 0 \times 4 = 1{,}600 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Base Cost (1 mark)**

Since contract creation is involved:

$$
G_{base\_create} = 32{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 3: Sum Total Gas (1 mark)**

$$
G_{used} = 32{,}000 + 1{,}600 + 80{,}000 = 113{,}600 \text{ gas}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Master Formula (1 mark)**

$$
C_{tx} = G_{used} \times P_{gas}
$$

$$
C_{tx} = 113{,}600 \times 3 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
C_{tx} = 3.408 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to ETH (1 mark)**

$$
C_{tx} = \frac{3.408 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} = 3.408 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Total Debit (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Total Debit} = \text{Value} + \text{Fee} = 1.2 + 0.003408
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Debit} = 1.203408 \text{ ETH}}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The sender's account balance will decrease by 1.203408 ETH, the recipient's balance increases by 1.2 ETH, and the validator (block producer) receives 0.003408 ETH as the gas fee.

---

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 3]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of the Ethereum state transition function $\Sigma$ when a transaction is applied. Clearly show how an account's state changes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the gas cost structure of:
- (i) An EOA-to-EOA simple ETH transfer (180 bytes of zero calldata, gas price = 25 Gwei)
- (ii) A contract creation transaction (2,000 bytes non-zero calldata, 60,000 gas execution, gas price = 25 Gwei)

Determine which transaction is costlier and by what amount.

---

### Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**State Transition Function:**

The Ethereum state transition function is denoted:

$$
\Sigma' = \Upsilon(\Sigma, B)
$$

where $\Sigma$ is the previous state, $B$ is the new block, and $\Sigma'$ is the resulting state.

**Block Processing Steps (1 mark each):**

1. **Check Intrinsic Validity:** Verify block format, parent hash, difficulty, gas limit.
2. **Transaction-by-Transaction Application:** For each transaction TX in block B:
   a. Verify RLP encoding and signature validity.
   b. Match nonce with account's current nonce.
   c. Compute intrinsic gas: $G_{intrinsic} = 21{,}000 + 32{,}000$ (if contract creation).
   d. Deduct gas cost from sender's balance.
   e. Increment sender's nonce.
   f. Execute EVM bytecode (if any).
   g. Update recipient's balance (if value > 0).
   h. Refund unused gas to sender.
3. **Apply Block-level Rewards:** Add block reward to beneficiary (miner/validator).
4. **Compute New State Root:** $\sigma' = \text{MPT\_update}(\sigma, \text{all updates})$

**Account State Update Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.4 — *Functional Block Diagram: Account State Update*.

**Key Equation (1 mark):**

$$
\sigma'[a] = \begin{cases}
(n+1, b - G_{used} \times P_{gas}, s, c) & \text{if } a = \text{sender} \\
(n_a, b_a + v, s_a, c_a) & \text{if } a = \text{recipient and } v > 0 \\
(n_a + 1, b_a, s'_a, c_a) & \text{if } a = \text{contract creator}
\end{cases}
$$

---

### Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Part (i): EOA-to-EOA Transfer**

Given: $N_{zero} = 180$, $P_{gas} = 25 \text{ Gwei} = 2.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wei}$

$$
G_{base} = 21{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
G_{data} = 180 \times 4 + 0 \times 16 = 720 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
G_{execution} = 0 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
G_{total\_i} = 21{,}000 + 720 = 21{,}720 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
C_i = 21{,}720 \times 2.5 \times 10^{10} = 5.43 \times 10^{14} \text{ Wei} = 0.000543 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Part (ii): Contract Creation**

Given: $N_{nonzero} = 2{,}000$, $G_{exec} = 60{,}000$ gas

$$
G_{base\_create} = 32{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
G_{data} = 2{,}000 \times 16 + 0 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
G_{total\_ii} = 32{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 124{,}000 \text{ gas}
$$

$$
C_{ii} = 124{,}000 \times 2.5 \times 10^{10} = 3.1 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 0.0031 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Comparison (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta C = C_{ii} - C_i = 0.0031 - 0.000543
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta C = 0.002557 \text{ ETH}}
$$

The contract creation transaction is **costlier by 0.002557 ETH** (approximately 5.7× more expensive than the simple EOA-to-EOA transfer).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often write the final cost in Gwei instead of Wei or ETH. Always explicitly state the unit at each conversion step.
> 
> 2. **Missing Calldata Cost:** Many students forget to add $G_{data}$ and only use the base 21,000 gas. This loses **1-2 marks** immediately.
> 
> 3. **EOA Creation Cost Mistake:** Writing that EOAs cost gas to create. They are **FREE** to create (off-chain key generation).
> 
> 4. **CodeHash Confusion:** Writing that EOAs have *no* codeHash. They DO have one — it is the hash of an empty string. The field exists; it is just empty.
> 
> 5. **Nonce Misdirection:** Students often say nonce "prevents double-spending." This is **partially correct**. The technically precise answer: nonce prevents **replay attacks** and ensures **transaction ordering**.
> 
> 6. **Missing the Storage Root for EOAs:** Forgetting that the storageRoot for EOAs is the hash of an *empty* Merkle Patricia Trie (not zero, not null — specifically the empty trie hash).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Accounts"**

- 🔑 **Two Account Types Only:** EOA (key-controlled) and CA (code-controlled). No other types exist in Ethereum.

- 🔑 **Four State Fields Always:** `nonce`, `balance`, `storageRoot`, `codeHash` — every account has all four.

- 🔑 **EOA codeHash = keccak256("")** (empty string hash). This is the **only distinguishing field** between EOA and CA at the data level.

- 🔑 **EOA Creation = FREE** (off-chain). **CA Creation = 32,000 gas base + 16 gas/byte**.

- 🔑 **Master Cost Formula:** $C_{tx} = G_{used} \times P_{gas}$. Remember this symbol-by-symbol.

- 🔑 **Base Gas Constants:** Simple TX = **21,000**; Contract Creation = **32,000**; Message Call = **2,100**.

- 🔑 **Calldata Costs:** Zero byte = **4 gas**; Non-zero byte = **16 gas** (non-zero is 4× more expensive).

- 🔑 **SSTORE Costs:** Setting from 0 to non-zero = **20,000** (most expensive storage op); Modifying existing = **5,000**; Clearing to 0 = **2,900** (refundable).

- 🔑 **Wei ↔ ETH:** Always divide by $10^{18}$ to convert Wei → ETH.

- 🔑 **Nonce Rules:** Each account's transactions must be processed in **strictly increasing nonce order**; replays of the same nonce are rejected.

- 🔑 **EOAs Initiate, CAs Respond:** Only EOAs can start a transaction. Contract accounts can only execute in response to a message call.

- 🔑 **Address Generation:** EOA = last 20 bytes of `keccak256(public_key)`; CA = last 20 bytes of `keccak256(RLP(sender, nonce))`.

- 🔑 **Account Abstraction (EIP-4337):** Modern best-practice wallets use smart contract accounts, blurring the EOA/CA distinction. Be ready to discuss this if asked in advanced questions.

- 🔑 **State Trie:** All account states are stored in a global **Modified Merkle Patricia Trie** rooted at the world state root — this is what makes light-client proofs possible.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transactions and Messages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transactions and Messages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Blockchain Transaction?

A **Transaction** in a blockchain network is a cryptographically signed data packet that is submitted by an externally owned account (EOA) to the network, instructing the state to be modified. In **Ethereum**, a transaction is the only mechanism through which the global state trie can be altered.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Ethereum-centric):**  
> A *transaction* is a signed message originated by an EOA, which is then serialized, broadcast across the peer-to-peer network, and ultimately included by validators in a block. It represents a **single atomic state transition attempt** that either fully succeeds (status = 1) or fully fails (status = 0), without any partial effects.

Formally, in the **Ethereum Yellow Paper**, a transaction $\mathbf{T}$ is a tuple of fields:
$$\mathbf{T} = (T_n, T_p, T_g, T_t, T_v, T_d, T_w, T_r, T_s)$$
where each field has a specific role (covered in Section 2).

## 1.2 What is a Message in Ethereum?

A **Message** is the internal counterpart of a transaction. It exists **only** when a smart contract calls another contract (or sends Ether to another contract) using the `CALL`, `DELEGATECALL`, `STATICCALL`, or `CALLCODE` opcodes. Messages are never serialized, never propagate across the network, and never need to be cryptographically signed.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction:**  
> - **Transaction** = *external*, *signed*, *network-visible*, *consumes gas directly from EOA*.  
> - **Message** = *internal*, *unsigned*, *EVM-internal*, *spends gas from the parent execution frame*.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Postal Service Model

Think of the Ethereum network as a **giant national postal system**:

| Real-World Element | Blockchain Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A sealed letter with your signature | A **signed transaction** (signed with private key) |
| The stamp you pay to the post office | The **gas fee** paid to the validator |
| Postman carries the letter between cities | **Peer-to-peer gossip protocol** propagates the tx |
| Letters stored in a post-office box | **Transaction pool (mempool / txpool)** |
| Letter enters a sorting bag with others | **Block assembly** by the validator/sequencer |
| Internal memo passed between two departments | **Message call** between smart contracts |
| Memo requires no external signature | **Message** has no `v, r, s` signature fields |

This analogy is the foundation for understanding the **transaction lifecycle** in Section 3.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Ethereum Constants to Memorize for KTU:**  
> - **Wei** = $10^0$ Wei (smallest unit)  
> - **Gwei** = $10^9$ Wei (gas is *typically* quoted in Gwei)  
> - **Ether (ETH)** = $10^{18}$ Wei  
> - **Block Gas Limit (post-Berlin)** ≈ **30,000,000 gas**  
> - **EIP-1559 Base Fee Adjustment Window** = parent block gas used / target = **15,000,000 gas**  
> - **Transaction Nonce** is a strictly monotonically increasing counter starting at **0** for each EOA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Ethereum Transaction (Legacy / Type-0)

A legacy transaction contains **nine (9) fields**. The KTU board examiner expects you to enumerate all of them with the correct byte-size:

| # | Field | Symbol | Bytes | Purpose | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nonce | $T_n$ | ≤ 32 | Sequence number of tx from sender | `uint256` |
| 2 | Gas Price (wei) | $T_p$ | ≤ 32 | Price per unit of gas (legacy only) | `uint256` |
| 3 | Gas Limit | $T_g$ | ≤ 32 | Max gas the sender is willing to pay | `uint256` |
| 4 | Recipient | $T_t$ | 20 | Destination address (or `∅` for contract creation) | `address` |
| 5 | Value (wei) | $T_v$ | ≤ 32 | Amount of ETH to transfer | `uint256` |
| 6 | Data | $T_d$ | 0 – ∞ | Payload (input to contract or init code) | `bytes` |
| 7 | v | $T_w$ | 1 | Recovery id of ECDSA signature (27 or 28) | `uint8` |
| 8 | r | $T_r$ | 32 | ECDSA signature component | `uint256` |
| 9 | s | $T_s$ | 32 | ECDSA signature component | `uint256` |

## 2.2 EIP-1559 Transaction (Type-2, London Hard Fork, August 2021)

After the London hard fork, legacy fields were extended:

| # | Field | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain ID | $T_c$ | Replay-protection across Ethereum / Polygon / BSC |
| 2 | Nonce | $T_n$ | Same as legacy |
| 3 | Max Priority Fee (tip) | $T_m$ | Tip to the validator |
| 4 | Max Fee Per Gas | $T_f$ | Absolute ceiling the sender is willing to pay |
| 5 | Gas Limit | $T_g$ | Same as legacy |
| 6 | To | $T_t$ | Destination |
| 7 | Value | $T_v$ | ETH to transfer |
| 8 | Data | $T_d$ | Payload |
| 9 | Access List | $T_a$ | Pre-declared storage slots (EIP-2930) |
| 10 | v, r, s | — | Signature fields |

## 2.3 Transaction Categories in Ethereum

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Three (3) Sub-Types of Transactions Recognized by the EVM:**
> 1. **Value Transfer (Message Call with Value)** — EOA → EOA transfer of ETH. The `data` field is empty.
> 2. **Contract Creation Transaction** — $T_t = \emptyset$ (null). The `data` field contains the **init code** of the new contract.
> 3. **Contract Call (Message Call)** — EOA or contract invokes a function on an existing contract. The `data` field contains the **function selector (4 bytes) + ABI-encoded arguments**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-Ready Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas below are **high-yield** — they appear every year in either Module Internal or End Semester evaluations.

$$
\text{Total Transaction Fee} \;=\; T_g^{\text{used}} \;\times\; T_p^{\text{effective}}
$$

$$
T_p^{\text{effective}} \;=\; \min\!\left(T_f,\; B_{\text{base}} + T_m\right) \quad \text{(EIP-1559)}
$$

$$
B_{\text{base}}^{\text{next}} \;=\; B_{\text{base}}^{\text{current}} \;\times\; \left(1 + \frac{G^{\text{used}}_{\text{parent}} - G^{\text{target}}}{8 \cdot G^{\text{target}}}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Transaction Hash} \;=\; \text{Keccak256}\bigl(\text{RLP-Encode}(\mathbf{T})\bigr)
$$

$$
\text{Contract Address} \;=\; \text{Keccak256}\bigl(\text{RLP}(T_s,\, T_n - 1)\bigr)[\,12:32\,]
$$

$$
\text{Signature Verification} \;=\; \text{ECRECOVER}(\text{Keccak256}(\text{raw}),\; T_w,\, T_r,\, T_s) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{sender}
$$

$$
\text{Nonce Rule: } \forall\, i \in \text{EOA: } T_{n,i+1} \;>\; T_{n,i}
$$

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Banking & FinTech:** Cross-border value-transfer transactions are the foundation of stablecoin settlement.
- **Supply Chain:** Contract-call transactions trigger business logic when goods cross IoT checkpoints.
- **DAOs:** Every on-chain vote is a *message call* to the Governor contract — making the message abstraction a *governance primitive*.
- **Layer-2 Rollups:** Optimistic and ZK rollups are essentially **batched message calls** rolled into a single `T_t$ = rollup inbox` transaction.

## 2.6 Messages vs. Transactions — Definitive Comparison

| Attribute | Transaction | Message |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Externally Owned Account (EOA) | Smart contract (EVM internal) |
| Signed | Yes (ECDSA via private key) | No (inherits parent's authority) |
| Serialized via RLP | Yes | No |
| Propagated via P2P network | Yes | No |
| Contributes to block gas limit | Yes (consumes from block) | Yes (charged to parent frame) |
| Can be included in txpool | Yes | No |
| Can revert independently | Yes (status = 0) | Only via `REVERT` opcode |
| Modifies nonce of sender | Yes | No (caller's nonce unaffected) |
| Has `v, r, s` | Yes | No |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Transaction Lifecycle — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

> [!NOTE]  
> The lifecycle below is the **canonical KTU answer** for any 7-mark or 14-mark question on transaction flow. Every step must be explained.

### Step 1 — Construction in the Wallet
The user (via MetaMask, Ledger, or Geth console) populates the nine (or ten, in EIP-1559) fields of $\mathbf{T}$. The wallet then encodes $\mathbf{T}$ using **Recursive Length Prefix (RLP) serialization** so it can be transmitted as raw bytes.

### Step 2 — ECDSA Signing
The wallet computes the hash:
$$H = \text{Keccak256}\bigl(\text{RLP-Encode}(T_n,\, T_p,\, T_g,\, T_t,\, T_v,\, T_d)\bigr)$$

The signature $(\sigma_r, \sigma_s, \sigma_v)$ is produced by signing $H$ with the sender's private key $k_{\text{priv}}$:
$$(\sigma_r,\, \sigma_s) = \text{ECDSA-Sign}(H,\, k_{\text{priv}})$$

The recovery id $\sigma_v \in \{27, 28\}$ identifies which of the two possible $y$-coordinates on the secp256k1 elliptic curve was used.

### Step 3 — Transaction Hash Generation
The **TxHash** (which is what users see on Etherscan) is:
$$\text{txHash} = \text{Keccak256}\bigl(\text{RLP-Encode}(T_n,\, T_p,\, T_g,\, T_t,\, T_v,\, T_d,\, T_w,\, T_r,\, T_s)\bigr)$$

This is a 32-byte identifier that uniquely tags the transaction.

### Step 4 — Local Validation
The node checks **four** pre-conditions before gossip:
1. Signature is valid (recovers a non-zero 20-byte address).
2. Nonce equals `account.nonce`.
3. Sender balance $\geq T_v + T_p \times T_g$ (legacy) or $\geq T_v + T_f \times T_g$ (EIP-1559).
4. Intrinsic gas is sufficient ($21{,}000$ for a simple transfer; $32{,}000$ plus $4$ gas per zero byte and $16$ gas per non-zero byte for the data field; $200$ gas per storage slot in access list).

### Step 5 — Propagation via Gossip
The validated transaction is broadcast to **up to 8 peers** (the *sqrt(n)* heuristic in the devp2p protocol). Each peer re-validates and forwards further. This is the **gossip protocol** or **tx propagation phase**.

### Step 6 — Mempool Insertion
The transaction enters the validator's **transaction pool (txpool)**. It is segregated into:
- **Pending Sub-Queue**: ordered by `effectiveGasPrice` descending, with nonce-tie-breaking.
- **Queued Sub-Queue**: txs that are *nonce-gapped* (waiting for a missing intermediate nonce).

### Step 7 — Block Assembly & Ordering
The validator's block builder runs a **state-transition selection algorithm** that fills the block up to the **30 M gas limit** with the highest-paying valid pending transactions. This determines the **block's transactions root**:
$$\text{TxRoot} = \text{MerklePatriciaTrie}(\text{indexed by txHash})$$

### Step 8 — State Transition Execution
For each transaction $\mathbf{T}_i$ in the block, the EVM executes the message call:

$$
\sigma_{i+1} \;=\; \Xi(\sigma_i,\, \mathbf{T}_i)
$$

where $\sigma_i$ is the world state before the $i$-th transaction and $\sigma_{i+1}$ is the new world state. $\Xi$ is the **state transition function** of Ethereum.

### Step 9 — Receipt Generation
A **Transaction Receipt** is emitted:
$$\mathbf{R} = (R_{\text{status}},\, R_{\text{cumGasUsed}},\, R_{\text{bloomLog}},\, R_{\text{logsRoot}})$$

The receipt is hashed via Keccak256 and accumulated into the block's **Receipts Root**.

### Step 10 — Finalization
After $\mathbf{k}$ confirmations (commonly $\mathbf{k} = 12$ for client confidence, or $\mathbf{k} = 64$ for exchanges), the transaction is considered *finalized*. In PoS, this happens at the **two-epoch boundary** (~12.8 minutes).

## 3.2 Worked Example: EIP-1559 Effective Gas Price Calculation

> [!TIP]  
> This is a *favourite* of the KTU board examiner — practice this!

**Given:**
- Base Fee $B_{\text{base}} = 30$ Gwei
- Max Priority Fee $T_m = 2$ Gwei
- Max Fee Per Gas $T_f = 50$ Gwei
- Gas Used $T_g^{\text{used}} = 21{,}000$ (simple ETH transfer)

**Step A — Compute Effective Gas Price**
$$
T_p^{\text{effective}} \;=\; \min\!\left(T_f,\; B_{\text{base}} + T_m\right)
$$

Substitute:
$$
T_p^{\text{effective}} \;=\; \min\!\left(50,\; 30 + 2\right) \;=\; \min(50,\, 32) \;=\; 32 \text{ Gwei}
$$

**Step B — Compute Total Fee**
$$
\text{Fee} \;=\; T_p^{\text{effective}} \;\times\; T_g^{\text{used}}
$$
$$
\text{Fee} \;=\; 32 \times 10^9 \;\times\; 21{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Fee} \;=\; 672 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wei}
$$
$$
\text{Fee} \;=\; 0.000000672 \text{ ETH}
$$

**Step C — Compute Refund (because $T_f > T_p^{\text{effective}}$)**
$$
\text{Refund} \;=\; (T_f - T_p^{\text{effective}}) \;\times\; T_g^{\text{used}}
$$
$$
\text{Refund} \;=\; (50 - 32) \;\times\; 10^9 \;\times\; 21{,}000
$$
$$
\text{Refund} \;=\; 378 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wei} \;=\; 0.000000378 \text{ ETH}
$$

The refund is automatically credited back to the sender — this is one of EIP-1559's primary UX improvements.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes of a Transaction

> [!WARNING]  
> Students often confuse these — the KTU examiner specifically watches for this in 14-mark answers.

| Failure Mode | Status Field | Gas Refund? | State Reverted? | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Out of Gas (OOG) | 0 (after **EIP-658**) | **No** (all gas consumed) | Yes | $T_g$ too low |
| Revert (REVERT opcode) | 0 | **Yes** (unused gas refunded) | Yes | Solidity `require()` / `assert()` failure |
| Invalid Signature | — (not in block) | N/A | N/A | Wrong chain or tampered fields |
| Insufficient Balance | 0 | **No** | Yes | Sender balance < $T_v + \text{fee}$ |
| Nonce too low | — (rejected) | N/A | N/A | Tx already mined |
| Nonce gap (too high) | Pending in queue | N/A | N/A | Missing intermediate nonce |

## 3.4 Base Fee Adjustment Derivation (EIP-1559)

The base fee adjusts to keep block utilization at **50%** of the gas target (i.e., **15,000,000 gas**). The exact formula from the Yellow Paper is:

$$
B_{\text{base}}^{\text{next}} \;=\; B_{\text{base}}^{\text{current}} \;\times\; \left(1 + \frac{G^{\text{used}}_{\text{parent}} - G^{\text{target}}}{8 \cdot G^{\text{target}}}\right)
$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $B_{\text{base}}^{\text{current}} = 30$ Gwei, $G^{\text{used}}_{\text{parent}} = 22{,}500{,}000$ (75% full), $G^{\text{target}} = 15{,}000{,}000$.

$$
\Delta B \;=\; 30 \times \left(1 + \frac{22.5\text{M} - 15\text{M}}{8 \times 15\text{M}}\right)
$$
$$
\Delta B \;=\; 30 \times \left(1 + \frac{7.5\text{M}}{120\text{M}}\right) \;=\; 30 \times 1.0625 \;=\; 31.875 \text{ Gwei}
$$

This shows a 6.25% increase, consistent with the **+12.5% / -12.5%** bound on per-block base fee change.

## 3.5 Contract Address Derivation

For a contract creation transaction with sender address $A$ and nonce $N$:

$$
\text{Address}_{\text{contract}} \;=\; \mathcal{K}\bigl(\text{RLP}(A,\, N)\bigr)[\,12:32\,]
$$

where $\mathcal{K}$ is Keccak256 and $[\,12:32\,]$ denotes slicing the **last 20 bytes** of the 32-byte digest. The first 12 bytes are discarded to match the **160-bit address space** of Ethereum.

> [!NOTE]  
> This is exactly why two contracts deployed by the same EOA at nonces 0 and 1 have **predictable, deterministic addresses** — a property heavily exploited by counterfactual deployment (EIP-1014 / `CREATE2`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction Lifecycle — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[User Wallet UI] --> A1[Construct Tx Fields]
    A1 --> A2[RLP Encode Payload]
    A2 --> A3[Sign with ECDSA Private Key]
    A3 --> A4[Compute TxHash via Keccak256]
    A4 --> A5[Local Node Validation]
    A5 -->|Valid| A6[Add to Local Mempool]
    A5 -->|Invalid| A5R[Drop and Penalize Peer]
    A6 --> A7[Gossip to 8 Neighbouring Peers]
    A7 --> A8[Peer to Peer Network Flood]
    A8 --> A9[Validator Block Builder]
    A9 --> A10[Sort by Effective Gas Price]
    A10 --> A11[Pack into Block up to 30M Gas]
    A11 --> A12[EVM State Transition Function]
    A12 --> A13[Generate Transaction Receipt]
    A13 --> A14[Update Receipts Root in Block Header]
    A14 --> A15[Block Finalization after 2 Epochs]
    A15 --> A16[TX Considered Final in World State]
```

## 4.2 Transaction Field Structure Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX[Transaction Object T] --> F1[Nonce - 32 bytes max]
    TX --> F2[Gas Price or Max Fee - 32 bytes max]
    TX --> F3[Gas Limit - 32 bytes max]
    TX --> F4[To Address - 20 bytes]
    TX --> F5[Value in Wei - 32 bytes max]
    TX --> F6[Data Payload - variable]
    TX --> F7[Signature v - 1 byte]
    TX --> F8[Signature r - 32 bytes]
    TX --> F9[Signature s - 32 bytes]
    F2 -.-> F2a[Legacy Gas Price]
    F2 -.-> F2b[Max Fee Per Gas EIP1559]
    F2 -.-> F2c[Max Priority Fee EIP1559]
    F4 -.-> F4a[Null indicates Contract Creation]
    F4 -.-> F4b[EOA Address indicates Value Transfer]
    F4 -.-> F4c[Contract Address indicates Message Call]
    F6 -.-> F6a[Empty for pure value transfer]
    F6 -.-> F6b[Init Code for contract creation]
    F6 -.-> F6c[Function Selector plus ABI for contract call]
```

## 4.3 Transaction vs. Message — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX[TRANSACTION DOMAIN - EXTERNAL]
        direction TB
        T1[EOA Signs with Private Key] --> T2[RLP Serialized and Broadcast]
        T2 --> T3[Enters Mempool with Nonce]
        T3 --> T4[Validator Selects in Block]
        T4 --> T5[Spawns Initial Message Frame]
    end
    subgraph MSG[MESSAGE DOMAIN - INTERNAL]
        direction TB
        M1[Parent Contract Executes CALL Opcode]
        M1 --> M2[No Signature Required]
        M2 --> M3[No Network Propagation]
        M3 --> M4[Gas Charged to Parent Frame]
        M4 --> M5[May Spawn Sub Messages Recursively]
    end
    T5 -.transitions to.-> M1
    M5 -.returns.-> T5
```

## 4.4 EIP-1559 Block-Construction Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Validator Opens New Block Slot] --> B1[Read Current Base Fee from Parent]
    B1 --> B2[Sort Pending Txs by Effective Tip]
    B2 --> B3{Block Gas Used plus Current Gas less than 30M?}
    B3 -->|Yes| B4[Include Tx and Deduct Gas]
    B4 --> B3
    B3 -->|No| B5[Stop Filling Block]
    B5 --> B6[Compute New Base Fee using 50 percent target]
    B6 --> B7[Sign Block with Consensus Keys]
    B7 --> B8[Broadcast to Beacon Chain Attesters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Transaction and a Message in the Ethereum blockchain. List any three distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *Transaction* is a signed, externally-originated instruction that is propagated across the P2P network to alter the global state. A *Message* is an internal, EVM-level call generated when a contract executes `CALL`, `DELEGATECALL`, or similar opcodes.

Three distinguishing features:

1. **Signature**: Transactions are signed with the EOA's private key (yielding `v, r, s`); Messages have no signature.
2. **Propagation**: Transactions are broadcast via the devp2p gossip protocol; Messages exist only within the EVM execution frame.
3. **Nonce Impact**: A transaction increments the sender's account nonce; A message does not affect the caller's nonce.

*(1 mark per difference, 3 marks total — examiner's standard rubric)*

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List all nine fields of a legacy Ethereum transaction and state the size (in bytes) of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{T} = (T_n, T_p, T_g, T_t, T_v, T_d, T_w, T_r, T_s)
$$

| Field | Bytes |
|---|---|
| Nonce $T_n$ | ≤ 32 |
| Gas Price $T_p$ | ≤ 32 |
| Gas Limit $T_g$ | ≤ 32 |
| To $T_t$ | 20 |
| Value $T_v$ | ≤ 32 |
| Data $T_d$ | 0 – variable |
| v $T_w$ | 1 |
| r $T_r$ | 32 |
| s $T_s$ | 32 |

*(1 mark for the list, 1 mark for sizes, 1 mark for the formula/format — allocate to your best fit.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) Describe the complete lifecycle of an Ethereum transaction from its construction in a wallet to its finalization on the blockchain. Include all major steps and the role of the mempool. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Construction (1 mark):** The wallet collects user input (recipient, value, gas, data) and populates the 9 (or 10 with EIP-1559) fields of the transaction tuple $\mathbf{T}$.

2. **Signing (1 mark):** The wallet RLP-encodes the unsigned payload, hashes it via Keccak256, and signs the digest with the user's private key using **ECDSA over secp256k1**, producing $(\sigma_r, \sigma_s, \sigma_v)$.

3. **Local Validation (0.5 mark):** The local node checks signature validity, nonce continuity, and sender balance $\geq T_v + \text{intrinsic gas cost} \times T_p$.

4. **Gossip & Mempool Insertion (1 mark):** The transaction is broadcast to up to 8 peers (the **devp2p sqrt(n)** heuristic). Each peer re-validates and forwards. The tx enters the validator's *pending sub-queue* if its nonce matches the account's current nonce, or the *queued sub-queue* if there is a nonce gap.

5. **Block Selection (1 mark):** The block builder greedily sorts pending transactions by `effectiveGasPrice` descending and packs them into a candidate block up to the **30 M gas** ceiling.

6. **EVM Execution (1 mark):** Each transaction triggers the state transition function $\sigma_{i+1} = \Xi(\sigma_i, \mathbf{T}_i)$ which deducts the fee, increments the nonce, and either transfers value or executes contract code.

7. **Receipt & Finalization (1 mark):** A transaction receipt is generated (containing `status`, `cumGasUsed`, `logsBloom`). After 2 epochs (~12.8 min) in PoS, the block is finalized and the transaction is irreversibly part of the chain state.

**(b) Explain EIP-1559 in detail. Derive the formula for the effective gas price and compute the total transaction fee for a sample scenario. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Theory (4 marks):** EIP-1559 (London Hard Fork, August 2021) reformed Ethereum's fee market by introducing:
- A **base fee** $B_{\text{base}}$ that is algorithmically adjusted per block and **burned** (destroyed).
- A **max priority fee** $T_m$ (tip) paid to the validator.
- A **max fee per gas** $T_f$ that acts as the sender's absolute ceiling.

This replaced the inefficient blind first-price auction of the legacy gas market and made fees more predictable.

**Derivation (2 marks):** The effective gas price is the minimum of the user's max fee and the sum of the base fee and priority fee:
$$
T_p^{\text{effective}} = \min\!\left(T_f,\; B_{\text{base}} + T_m\right)
$$

**Numerical (1 mark):** Given $B_{\text{base}} = 40$ Gwei, $T_m = 3$ Gwei, $T_f = 60$ Gwei, $T_g^{\text{used}} = 21{,}000$:

$$
T_p^{\text{effective}} = \min(60,\, 40 + 3) = \min(60, 43) = 43 \text{ Gwei}
$$
$$
\text{Total Fee} = 43 \times 10^9 \times 21{,}000 = 903 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wei} = 0.000000903 \text{ ETH}
$$
$$
\text{Burnt Base Fee} = 40 \times 10^9 \times 21{,}000 = 840 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wei} = 0.00000084 \text{ ETH}
$$
$$
\text{Validator Tip} = 3 \times 10^9 \times 21{,}000 = 63 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wei} = 0.000000063 \text{ ETH}
$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) With neat diagram, explain the difference between Transaction and Message in Ethereum. Discuss the situations where each is used. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Transaction (3 marks):** A transaction is an externally-signed, RLP-encoded message that travels from an EOA through the P2P network into a block. It is *the only mechanism* by which the global state can be modified by an outside actor. Used for:
- ETH transfer between EOAs.
- Contract deployment (creation transaction).
- Initial function call into a contract from a user.

**Message (3 marks):** A message is a value-transfer / call initiated from within a contract to another contract or EOA. It is created via EVM opcodes — `CALL`, `DELEGATECALL`, `STATICCALL`, `CALLCODE`. Used for:
- Inter-contract communication (e.g., a DEX router calling a liquidity pool).
- Sending ETH from a contract back to a user (e.g., a withdraw function).
- Reentrancy-prone flows (which is why the `ReentrancyGuard` pattern exists).

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3 (Transaction vs. Message — Comparative Flow). The examiner expects a clear distinction between the two domains and a labelled arrow showing the transition from a transaction to its first message frame.

**(b) Describe the role of ECDSA in transaction signing. Explain the fields $v$, $r$, $s$ in the transaction tuple and derive the formula for the public key (and thus the sender address) recovery. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**ECDSA Role (2 marks):** The Ethereum Virtual Machine uses the **secp256k1** elliptic curve for digital signatures. The sender's 32-byte private key $k_{\text{priv}}$ signs the Keccak256 hash of the RLP-encoded unsigned transaction. The output is a 65-byte signature $(r, s, v)$ that is appended to the transaction, making it non-repudiable.

**Field Explanations (2 marks):**
- $r$ (32 bytes): the $x$-coordinate of the signature point $R$ on secp256k1.
- $s$ (32 bytes): the proof value computed as $s = k^{-1}(z + r \cdot k_{\text{priv}}) \mod n$, where $z$ is the message hash and $k$ is a random nonce.
- $v$ (1 byte): the recovery id, $\in \{27, 28\}$ (or $\{0, 1\}$), used to disambiguate between the two possible $y$-coordinates of $R$ on the curve.

**Recovery Derivation (3 marks):**

Given $(r, s, v)$ and the signed digest $z$, recovery proceeds as:

1. From $r$ and $v$, recover the point $R$ on the curve (the two possible $y$'s correspond to $v = 27$ and $v = 28$).
2. Compute the modular inverse of $r$ modulo the curve order $n$:
   $$r^{-1} \mod n$$
3. Compute the two candidate private-key-related values:
   $$Q_{a} = r^{-1} \cdot (s \cdot R - z \cdot G)$$
   $$Q_{b} = r^{-1} \cdot (s \cdot R' - z \cdot G)$$
4. The correct public key $Q$ is the one whose $x$-coordinate equals $r$.
5. The sender address is the last 20 bytes of the Keccak256 hash of $Q$ (uncompressed, with the leading 0x04 stripped):
   $$\text{Address} = \text{Keccak256}(Q_{x} \Vert Q_{y})[\,12:32\,]$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Do **not** confuse *Transaction Hash* (Keccak256 of RLP-encoded **signed** payload) with *Transaction Digest* (Keccak256 of RLP-encoded **unsigned** payload). The digest is what is *signed*; the hash is what is *broadcast*. (–1 mark typical deduction.)  
> - Do **not** write the gas fee as `gas × wei` — always specify `gas_used × effective_gas_price`. (–0.5 mark.)  
> - When explaining EIP-1559, you **must** state that the base fee is *burned*, not given to the validator. (–1 mark if missed — this is a textbook trap.)  
> - In the contract address derivation, remember the **last 20 bytes** of the 32-byte Keccak256 hash, *not* the first. (–0.5 mark.)  
> - Always mention that messages *do not consume a nonce* — this is a frequently-tested distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for the night before the exam):**

- A **Transaction** is signed, serialized, network-visible, and nonce-consuming. A **Message** is internal, unsigned, and nonce-neutral.
- Ethereum transactions have **9 fields** (legacy) or **10+ fields** (EIP-1559 + EIP-2930).
- The **ECDSA curve** used is **secp256k1** — *not* secp256r1, *not* NIST P-256.
- The **TxHash** = Keccak256 of the RLP-encoded **signed** transaction.
- The **Contract Address** = last 20 bytes of Keccak256(RLP(sender, nonce)).
- **EIP-1559 effective gas price** = $\min(T_f,\; B_{\text{base}} + T_m)$.
- **Base fee is burned**; only the tip ($T_m$) goes to the validator.
- Block gas limit = **30,000,000**; gas target = **15,000,000**.
- A **revert** refunds unused gas; an **out-of-gas** does not.
- The **mempool** has *pending* (correct nonce) and *queued* (nonce gap) sub-queues.
- Transactions with `to = null` are **contract creation** transactions.
- The **EIP-155 chain ID** (e.g., 1 for Ethereum mainnet, 56 for BSC) prevents cross-chain replay.
- A **transaction receipt** contains: `status`, `cumGasUsed`, `logsBloom`, `logsRoot`.
- Transactions are final after **~12.8 minutes** (2 epochs in PoS).
- A **message call** can be one of: `CALL`, `DELEGATECALL`, `STATICCALL`, or `CALLCODE`.
- `STATICCALL` (EIP-214) prevents state modification — used in `view` and `pure` Solidity functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Ethereum Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT601 - BLOCKCHAIN TECHNOLOGIES AND APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Ethereum Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is formally defined as a *quasi-Turing complete*, sandboxed, stack-based, deterministic virtual machine that serves as the **decentralized runtime environment** for the execution of smart contracts on the Ethereum blockchain. It operates simultaneously on thousands of nodes worldwide, processing bytecodes that represent deterministic instructions, and forms the computational backbone of the Ethereum state transition function.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the EVM is treated as the **"World Computer"** — a single, globally synchronized instance of a state machine whose state is replicated across all participating nodes. Every operation executed on the EVM is **deterministic**, meaning that given the same input, every node on the network must arrive at the identical output. This determinism is the cornerstone of consensus in Ethereum.

Mathematically, the EVM can be modeled as a function:

$$ \sigma_{t+1} \equiv \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T) $$

where:
- $\sigma_t$ = the **Ethereum world state** at block $t$
- $T$ = the **transaction** being applied
- $\Upsilon$ = the **state transition function** (the EVM itself)
- $\sigma_{t+1}$ = the resulting world state after applying $T$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Global Vending Machine Computer"**
>
> Imagine a giant vending machine that exists simultaneously in every shop around the world. When you insert a coin and press a button, **every copy** of that vending machine in every shop performs the exact same action and gives you the exact same snack. No matter which shop you visit, the outcome is identical.
>
> The **EVM is that vending machine**. It is not a physical computer but a **virtual computer** that runs on every Ethereum node. Smart contracts are the "snacks" (programs) loaded into the machine. Ether (ETH) is the "coin" used to pay for execution. Every Ethereum node runs the same program with the same inputs and must reach the same result, or the chain is invalid.

### 1.3 Why "Quasi-Turing Complete"?

A standard Turing Machine runs forever if a program does not halt. Because Ethereum is a **decentralized public network**, allowing infinite loops would let malicious actors stall the network indefinitely. To prevent this, Ethereum introduces **Gas** — a metering mechanism that bounds execution. The EVM is therefore *quasi*-Turing complete: it can compute anything a Turing machine can, **provided the computation terminates within the gas limit**.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in EVM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know:**
> - **Stack Depth:** Maximum **1024** items, each of **256 bits**.
> - **Word Size:** **256 bits** (32 bytes) — chosen to facilitate native Keccak-256 hashing and elliptic-curve cryptography.
> - **Gas Limit per Block (post-Merge):** Approximately **30,000,000 gas** (target) and **36,000,000 gas** (hard ceiling).
> - **Contract Code Size Limit:** Maximum **24,576 bytes** (EIP-170).
> - **Total Opcodes Defined:** **256** possible opcodes (0x00 to 0xFF), though not all are used.
> - **Gas per EVM Opcode:** Variable, e.g., `ADD = 3 gas`, `MUL = 5 gas`, `SSTORE = 20,000 gas` (cold storage write).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The EVM Execution Architecture (The "Brain" of Ethereum)

The EVM is composed of several critical sub-components that work in tandem to execute smart contracts:

- **Program Counter (PC)**: A pointer that tracks the next opcode to be executed. Initialized to 0 at the start of execution.
- **Stack**: A **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** data structure with a maximum of **1024** elements. Each element is a 256-bit word. Most opcodes operate on stack values.
- **Memory**: A **volatile, byte-addressed, expandable** working memory used for transient data during execution. It is **cleared** between transactions.
- **Storage**: A **persistent, key-value store** tied to a specific contract account. Data written here remains permanently on-chain and is extremely expensive in gas.
- **Call Data**: Read-only buffer containing the **input data** of the transaction (function selector + arguments).
- **Log Area**: A special append-only structure used by the `LOG*` opcodes to emit **events** for external consumption.
- **Subroutine Stack / Return Data**: Used to pass return values back to the calling context.

### 2.2 Operational Workflow (The "How" Behind Execution)

The lifecycle of a smart contract call through the EVM follows this strict sequence:

1. **Transaction Receipt**: A user signs a transaction specifying a recipient address, value, data, gas limit, and gas price.
2. **Validation & Propagation**: The transaction is broadcast to the mempool and picked up by a validator.
3. **Bytecode Loading**: If the recipient is a contract account, the EVM loads the contract's **bytecode** from world state $\sigma_t$.
4. **Execution Begins**: The Program Counter is set to 0. The EVM begins the **fetch-decode-execute cycle**.
5. **Opcode Fetch**: The EVM reads the byte at the address held by the PC.
6. **Opcode Decode**: The byte is matched against the opcode table to identify the operation and its gas cost.
7. **Gas Deduction**: The gas cost is subtracted from the available gas. If gas drops below zero, execution halts with an `OUT_OF_GAS` exception.
8. **Stack/Storage Operations**: The opcode performs its operation (e.g., pops two values from the stack, adds them, pushes the result).
9. **PC Increment**: The PC advances by 1 (or by the appropriate offset for jumps).
10. **Loop**: Steps 5–9 repeat until execution halts via `STOP`, `RETURN`, `REVERT`, or `SELFDESTRUCT`.
11. **State Update**: The EVM produces a new world state $\sigma_{t+1}$ and the transaction is included in the block.

### 2.3 The Two Account Types in EVM

| Feature | **Externally Owned Account (EOA)** | **Contract Account (CA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Private Key | ✅ Yes (controlled by user) | ❌ No (code-governed) |
| Holds Code | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (EVM bytecode) |
| Initiates Transactions | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (only via internal messages) |
| Storage | Empty by default | Persistent key-value store |
| Gas Source | Pays with own ETH | Inherits gas from parent call |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every essential formula and metric you will need for KTU ESE questions on the EVM.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Value** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Transaction Fee** | $T_{fee} = G_{used} \times G_{price}$ | Wei (cryptocurrency subunit) |
| **Transaction Fee (with Base Fee, EIP-1559)** | $T_{fee} = G_{used} \times (B_{fee} + P_{tip})$ | $B_{fee}$ = base fee, $P_{tip}$ = priority tip |
| **Block Gas Limit** | $G_{block} \leq 30{,}000{,}000$ | Gas (post-London Hard Fork) |
| **Stack Depth** | $D_{stack} \leq 1024$ | Items |
| **Word Size** | $W = 256$ | Bits |
| **Maximum Code Size** | $C_{max} = 24{,}576$ | Bytes (EIP-170) |
| **SSTORE (Zero to Non-Zero)** | $G = 20{,}000$ | Gas (expensive write) |
| **SSTORE (Modification)** | $G = 2{,}900$ | Gas (cheap update) |
| **SLOAD** | $G = 2{,}100$ | Gas (cold read) |
| **CALL** | $G = 2{,}600 + 900 \times P_{non-zero}$ | Gas (with stipend transfer) |
| **Memory Expansion Cost** | $C_{mem} = 3 \times n + \left\lfloor n^2 / 512 \right\rfloor$ | Gas (proportional to size $n$) |
| **EVM State Transition** | $\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$ | World state function |
| **Keccak-256 Hash** | $H(x) = \text{KECCAK256}(x)$ | 256-bit output |
| **Ethereum Address** | $A = \text{last 20 bytes of KECCAK256}(P_{k})$ | Hexadecimal format |
| **ECDSA Signature Size** | $S_{sig} = 65$ | Bytes ($r, s, v$ components) |
| **Merkle Patricia Trie Root** | $R = \text{TRIE}(k_1, v_1), \ldots, \text{TRIE}(k_n, v_n)$ | State root in block header |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been replaced with `,` and `+` in the formulas above to preserve Markdown table integrity. Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in $\text{LaTeX}$ contexts.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The EVM is the foundational abstraction that enables **decentralized application (dApp) engineering**. Beyond cryptocurrency, it powers:
- **Decentralized Finance (DeFi):** Automated lending, borrowing, and exchanges.
- **Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs):** Unique digital asset ownership.
- **Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs):** On-chain governance.
- **Supply Chain Provenance:** Transparent, tamper-proof logistics.
- **Identity and Verifiable Credentials:** Self-sovereign identity management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Determinism Matters in Engineering:** The EVM is intentionally **non-deterministic-hostile** — it has no access to system time (only block timestamps), no file system access, and no network calls. This guarantees that the same transaction will produce the same output regardless of which node runs it, which is the **mathematical foundation of distributed consensus**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Total Cost of a Smart Contract Transaction

**Problem:** A user invokes a smart contract function that consumes **51,000 gas** in computation and storage writes. The base fee for the current block is **30 Gwei** and the user includes a priority tip of **2 Gwei**. Calculate the total transaction fee in Gwei and in ETH.

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**

Let:
- $G_{used} = 51{,}000$ gas (total computational work)
- $B_{fee} = 30$ Gwei (current network base fee)
- $P_{tip} = 2$ Gwei (priority fee for the validator)

**Step 2 — Recall the EIP-1559 fee formula.**

After the London Hard Fork (August 2021), the transaction fee is calculated as:

$$T_{fee} = G_{used} \times (B_{fee} + P_{tip})$$

This equation is fundamental and **must be memorized** for KTU examinations.

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

We begin by computing the effective gas price. The effective gas price is the sum of the base fee and the priority tip, since the EIP-1559 model explicitly separates these two components:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fee} &= G_{used} \times (B_{fee} + P_{tip}) \\
T_{fee} &= 51{,}000 \times (30 + 2) \\
T_{fee} &= 51{,}000 \times 32
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the final multiplication.**

We now compute the product of 51,000 and 32. To do this rigorously, we decompose 51,000 into $5.1 \times 10^4$ and 32 into $2^5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fee} &= 51{,}000 \times 32 \\
&= 51{,}000 \times (2^5) \\
&= 51{,}000 \times 32 \\
&= 1{,}632{,}000 \text{ Gwei}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert from Gwei to ETH.**

Recall the standard Ethereum unit conversions:
- $1$ ETH $= 10^{18}$ Wei
- $1$ Gwei $= 10^9$ Wei
- Therefore, $1$ ETH $= 10^9$ Gwei

The conversion is performed by dividing the Gwei value by $10^9$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fee} &= \frac{1{,}632{,}000 \text{ Gwei}}{10^9 \text{ Gwei / ETH}} \\
&= 1.632 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH} \\
&= 0.001632 \text{ ETH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The total transaction fee is **1,632,000 Gwei**, equivalent to **0.001632 ETH**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always show unit conversions in KTU exams.** Examiners specifically award partial credit for unit clarity. Forgetting to convert Gwei to ETH costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: EVM Memory Expansion Gas Cost

**Problem:** A contract operation expands the EVM's volatile memory from **128 bytes** to **3,200 bytes** during a transaction. Calculate the additional gas cost incurred due to this memory expansion.

**Step 1 — State the memory expansion cost formula.**

The EVM charges for memory expansion in quadratic proportion to deter denial-of-service attacks via excessive memory allocation. The official formula is:

$$C_{mem} = 3 \times n + \left\lfloor \frac{n^2}{512} \right\rfloor$$

where $n$ is the number of 32-byte words being accessed.

**Step 2 — Determine the word counts.**

The cost is paid in 32-byte word units, so we must convert bytes to words using ceiling division:

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{old} &= \left\lceil \frac{128}{32} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ words} \\
n_{new} &= \left\lceil \frac{3{,}200}{32} \right\rceil = 100 \text{ words}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cost at the old memory size.**

Apply the formula to the old memory size of 4 words:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{old} &= 3 \times 4 + \left\lfloor \frac{4^2}{512} \right\rfloor \\
&= 12 + \left\lfloor \frac{16}{512} \right\rfloor \\
&= 12 + 0 \\
&= 12 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cost at the new memory size.**

Apply the formula to the new memory size of 100 words:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{new} &= 3 \times 100 + \left\lfloor \frac{100^2}{512} \right\rfloor \\
&= 300 + \left\lfloor \frac{10{,}000}{512} \right\rfloor \\
&= 300 + 19 \\
&= 319 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the incremental expansion cost.**

The EVM does not charge the cost twice; only the **difference** between the new and old costs is paid:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{expansion} &= C_{new} - C_{old} \\
&= 319 - 12 \\
&= 307 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The memory expansion costs an additional **307 gas**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the quadratic term?** The $n^2/512$ term ensures that a transaction cannot allocate gigabytes of memory cheaply. As memory usage grows, the marginal cost increases, protecting the network from resource exhaustion attacks.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: EVM Opcodes by Category

| **Category** | **Sample Opcodes** | **Stack Effect** | **Gas Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic** | `ADD (0x01)`, `SUB (0x03)`, `MUL (0x02)`, `DIV (0x04)` | $a, b \rightarrow a \oplus b$ | 3–8 gas |
| **Comparison** | `LT (0x10)`, `GT (0x11)`, `EQ (0x14)` | $a, b \rightarrow 0 \vert 1$ | 3 gas |
| **Bitwise** | `AND (0x16)`, `OR (0x17)`, `XOR (0x18)`, `NOT (0x19)` | $a, b \rightarrow a \odot b$ | 3 gas |
| **SHA3 / Keccak** | `SHA3 (0x20)` | $a, b \rightarrow h$ | 30 + 6 per word |
| **Environmental Info** | `ADDRESS (0x30)`, `CALLER (0x33)`, `GASPRICE (0x3A)` | $\rightarrow a$ | 2–3 gas |
| **Block Info** | `BLOCKHASH (0x40)`, `TIMESTAMP (0x42)`, `NUMBER (0x43)` | $\rightarrow a$ | 2–20 gas |
| **Stack, Memory, Storage** | `POP (0x50)`, `MLOAD (0x51)`, `MSTORE (0x52)`, `SLOAD (0x54)` | varies | 2–2,100 gas |
| **Push Operations** | `PUSH1`–`PUSH32` (0x60–0x7F) | $\rightarrow a$ | 3 gas |
| **Duplication** | `DUP1`–`DUP16` (0x80–0x8F) | $a \rightarrow a, a$ | 3 gas |
| **Exchange** | `SWAP1`–`SWAP16` (0x90–0x9F) | $a, b \rightarrow b, a$ | 3 gas |
| **Logging** | `LOG0`–`LOG4` (0xA0–0xA4) | — | 375 + 375 per topic + 8 per byte |
| **System Operations** | `CALL (0xF1)`, `DELEGATECALL (0xF4)`, `CREATE (0xF0)`, `RETURN (0xF3)` | — | 700+ gas |

> **Reading the Table:** The `Stack Effect` column uses $\oplus$ for generic arithmetic and $\odot$ for generic bitwise operators. Vertical bar `|` inside math expressions is replaced with comma to avoid Markdown table corruption.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EVM Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the Ethereum Virtual Machine, illustrating how the major components interact during smart contract execution.

```mermaid
graph TB
    TX[Transaction Input]
    PC[Program Counter]
    STK[Stack - 1024 items, 256-bit each]
    MEM[Memory - Volatile, byte-addressed]
    STO[Storage - Persistent key-value]
    CD[Call Data Buffer]
    LOG[Log Area - Append-only events]
    GAS[Gas Meter]
    OPEXEC[Opcode Execution Unit]
    EXC[Exception Handler]
    FIN[State Transition Output]

    TX --> PC
    TX --> CD
    TX --> GAS
    PC --> OPEXEC
    CD --> OPEXEC
    GAS --> OPEXEC
    OPEXEC --> STK
    OPEXEC --> MEM
    OPEXEC --> STO
    OPEXEC --> LOG
    OPEXEC --> EXC
    EXC --> FIN
    STK --> FIN
    MEM --> FIN
    STO --> FIN
    LOG --> FIN
    GAS --> FIN
```

**Explanation:** A transaction enters the EVM and populates the **Program Counter** (to track execution position), the **Call Data Buffer** (to hold the input), and the **Gas Meter** (to track remaining resources). The **Opcode Execution Unit** orchestrates all operations by reading from and writing to the **Stack**, **Memory**, **Storage**, and **Log Area**. If the gas runs out, the **Exception Handler** triggers a state-reverting transition. Finally, all updated components converge into the **State Transition Output**, which becomes the new world state.

---

### 4.2 Smart Contract Lifecycle — From Source Code to EVM Execution

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Solidity Source Code]
    COMP[Solc Compiler]
    BYT[EVM Bytecode]
    DEPLOY[Deployment Transaction]
    STATE[World State Sigma]
    ADDR[Contract Address]
    CALL[Function Call Transaction]
    EVM[EVM Execution]
    EVT[Events and Logs]
    OUT[Return Data]

    SRC --> COMP
    COMP --> BYT
    BYT --> DEPLOY
    DEPLOY --> STATE
    STATE --> ADDR
    ADDR --> CALL
    CALL --> EVM
    EVM --> EVT
    EVM --> OUT
    EVM --> STATE
```

**Explanation:** A developer writes a contract in a high-level language (typically **Solidity**), which is compiled by the **`solc` compiler** into low-level **EVM bytecode**. A **deployment transaction** writes this bytecode to the world state, creating a new **contract address**. Any subsequent **function call transaction** triggers the **EVM to execute** the bytecode, which may emit **events** for external listeners and produce **return data** for the calling context. The world state is updated as a final consequence of the execution.

---

### 4.3 EVM Execution Cycle — The Fetch-Decode-Execute Loop

```mermaid
graph TB
    START([Start of Execution])
    FETCH[Step 1: Fetch Opcode at PC]
    DECODE[Step 2: Decode Opcode and Gas Cost]
    GASCHECK[Step 3: Check Available Gas]
    EXECOP[Step 4: Execute Operation]
    GASDED[Step 5: Deduct Gas]
    ADVPC[Step 6: Advance Program Counter]
    HALTCHK[Step 7: Check Halt Conditions]
    HALT([Halt and Update State])
    OOG[Out of Gas Exception]
    ERR[Revert or Error]

    START --> FETCH
    FETCH --> DECODE
    DECODE --> GASCHECK
    GASCHECK -- Sufficient --> EXECOP
    GASCHECK -- Insufficient --> OOG
    OOG --> ERR
    EXECOP --> GASDED
    GASDED --> ADVPC
    ADVPC --> HALTCHK
    HALTCHK -- Continue --> FETCH
    HALTCHK -- Stop or Return --> HALT
```

**Explanation:** This diagram captures the **fetch-decode-execute** cycle that the EVM runs continuously during contract execution. The cycle begins by fetching the opcode at the address pointed to by the **Program Counter**, then decoding it to identify the operation and its gas cost. If insufficient gas remains, the EVM raises an **Out of Gas exception**, which reverts the transaction state. Otherwise, the operation is executed, gas is deducted, and the Program Counter is advanced. The cycle repeats until a **halt condition** is reached (such as `STOP`, `RETURN`, or `REVERT`), at which point the world state is updated.
